concepts.concept: PHP API up to PHP 8.1 version

references.title: Language Reference
references.summary: This page is the Language Reference section of the PHP manual, providing an index of core language topics such as basic syntax, types, variables, constants, expressions, operators, control structures, functions, classes and objects, namespaces, enumerations, errors, exceptions, fibers, generators, and attributes. It also lists predefined variables, predefined exceptions, predefined interfaces and classes, predefined attributes, context options and parameters, and supported protocols and wrappers. The page serves as a navigational hub to the detailed subsections of PHP's language specification.

references.title: Basic syntax
references.summary: This Language Reference page covers PHP basic syntax topics such as PHP tags, escaping from HTML, instruction separation and comments. It serves as an overview entry in the PHP manual linking to related sections like types, variables, expressions, operators and control structures. The page is part of the PHP documentation’s Language Reference.

references.title: Types
references.summary: Documentation of PHP's type system within the Language Reference. The page lists and explains PHP types and related topics, including NULL, booleans, integers, floats, strings, arrays, objects, enumerations, resources, callables, mixed, void, never, relative class types, singleton types, iterables, type declarations, and type juggling.

references.title: Type declarations
references.summary: Explains PHP type declarations for function parameters, return values, class properties, and supported type forms. For the SFCE 6 PHP 8.1 cap, this page is relevant for scalar, nullable, iterable, object, union, intersection, mixed, never, static return, void, strict typing, and compatibility rules.

references.title: Type Juggling
references.summary: Documents PHP's automatic type conversion behavior in numeric, string, logical, integral/string, comparative, and function contexts. This page is relevant for understanding when values are coerced, when the original value's type is unchanged, and when PHP raises type errors instead of converting.

references.title: Variables
references.summary: Documentation for PHP variables covering basic usage and concepts. The page includes sections on predefined variables, variable scope, variable variables, and variables from external sources.

references.title: Constants
references.summary: Constants are immutable, case-sensitive identifiers declared with `const` or `define()`, and `define()`'s case-insensitive option was removed in PHP 8.0. Constants live in global scope, magic constants are the exception to the case rule, and `constant()` retrieves a constant by its (possibly dynamic) name.

references.title: Expressions
references.summary: This page explains that expressions are PHP's fundamental building blocks — anything that has a value — and gives basic forms such as constants, variables and function calls. It documents expression-oriented features including that assignments evaluate to a value, pre- and post-increment semantics, comparison and strict equivalence operators, combined operator-assignment, the ternary conditional operator, expression statements, and automatic boolean conversion for truth-value testing.

references.title: Operators
references.summary: This page defines PHP operators, explaining that they take one or more values (expressions) and yield another value. It describes operator arity (unary, binary, ternary), and notes that a full list of PHP operators and an explanation of operator precedence and associativity are provided in the Operator Precedence section.

references.title: Assignment Operators
references.summary: Explains PHP assignment operators, including simple assignment, assignment by reference, compound assignments, and the null coalescing assignment operator ??=. It documents that assignment expressions evaluate to the assigned value and that ??= is equivalent to assigning the result of the null coalescing expression.

references.title: Control Structures
references.summary: Reference page listing PHP control structures and their variants. It covers conditional statements (if, else, elseif), looping constructs (while, do-while, for, foreach), flow control keywords (break, continue, return, goto), switch and match, declare, and inclusion directives (require, include, require_once, include_once), as well as alternative syntax for control structures.

references.title: Functions
references.summary: This manual page describes PHP functions and related language features. It covers user-defined functions, function parameters and arguments, return values, variable and internal (built-in) functions, anonymous functions, arrow functions, and first-class callable syntax. The page is part of the Language Reference and links to examples and contribution options.

references.title: Generators
references.summary: Documentation page for PHP Generators within the Language Reference. It provides a generators overview, generator syntax, and a comparison of generators with Iterator objects.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 5.6.x to PHP 7.0.x
references.summary: Migration guide from PHP 5.6 to PHP 7.0 documenting backward-incompatible changes, new features, deprecated features, changed and new functions, new classes/interfaces, new global constants, SAPI changes, and removed extensions/SAPIs. The release focused on removing previously deprecated functionality and improving language consistency.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 7.0.x to PHP 7.1.x
references.summary: Migration guide from PHP 7.0 to PHP 7.1 documenting new features, new functions, new global constants, backward-incompatible changes, deprecated features, and changed functions. Notable additions include nullable type declarations, void return type, iterable pseudo-type, multi-catch via `|`, and visibility modifiers for class constants.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 7.1.x to PHP 7.2.x
references.summary: Migration guide from PHP 7.1 to PHP 7.2 listing new features, new functions and global constants, backward-incompatible changes, and deprecated features. Notable additions include the `object` parameter and return type, parameter type widening (covariance), `Argon2` password hashes, and the abstract-method-override rule.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 7.2.x to PHP 7.3.x
references.summary: Migration guide from PHP 7.2 to PHP 7.3 listing new features, new functions and global constants, backward-incompatible changes, and deprecated features. Notable additions include flexible heredoc/nowdoc syntax, `array_key_first()`/`array_key_last()`, and a trailing comma in function calls.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 7.3.x to PHP 7.4.x
references.summary: Migration guide from PHP 7.3 to PHP 7.4 covering new features, new classes/interfaces, new functions and global constants, backward-incompatible changes, deprecated features, and removed extensions. Notable additions include typed properties, arrow functions, the spread operator inside arrays, the null coalescing assignment `??=`, and weak references.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 7.4.x to PHP 8.0.x
references.summary: Migration guide from PHP 7.4 to PHP 8.0 covering new features, new classes/interfaces, backward-incompatible changes, and deprecated features. Major additions include union types, named arguments, attributes, constructor property promotion, the `match` expression, the nullsafe operator `?->`, and JIT compilation.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 8.0.x to PHP 8.1.x
references.summary: Migration guide from PHP 8.0 to PHP 8.1 covering new features, new classes/interfaces, new functions and global constants, backward-incompatible changes, and deprecated features. Major additions include enumerations, readonly properties, first-class callable syntax, the `never` return type, intersection types, final class constants, and fibers.

references.title: Changelog
references.summary: Lists changes to functions of bundled PHP extensions across PHP versions. The page documents per-version modifications such as deprecated functions and parameters, return type changes, added or optional parameters, new exceptions or ValueError/TypeError behaviors, and other function- and API-level behavior changes.

references.title: Security
references.summary: This PHP manual page collects security guidance for PHP applications, covering general considerations and deployment modes (CGI and Apache module), session and filesystem security, database security and SQL injection, error reporting, and handling user-submitted data. It also lists security-related features and topics such as HTTP authentication, cookies, sessions, file uploads, using remote files, persistent connections, and includes a user-contributed notes section with practical advice (for example, protecting include files and using .htaccess).

references.title: Introduction
references.summary: This page introduces PHP's object model and lists key OOP features such as visibility, abstract and final classes and methods, magic methods, interfaces, and cloning. It explains that PHP variables hold object references rather than copies and links to related topics like Objects and References and the Userland Naming Guide.

references.title: The Basics
references.summary: Covers PHP class declaration, instantiation with `new`, properties, methods, and the `$this` pseudo-variable. Documents inheritance with `extends`, the `::class` name resolution, and the nullsafe operator `?->` for safe member access on null.

references.title: Properties
references.summary: Properties are declared with at least one modifier (visibility, `static`, or `readonly`) and may include a type declaration (since PHP 7.4); typed properties must be initialized before access or an `Error` is thrown. Readonly properties (PHP 8.1) can be initialized only once from their declaring scope, cannot have default values, and are not supported on static properties.

references.title: Class Constants
references.summary: Describes how to declare and use class constants in PHP, noting their default public visibility and that they are allocated once per class rather than per instance. The page covers visibility modifiers for constants (PHP 7.1) and final constants preventing redefinition by child classes (PHP 8.1). It also shows examples of ::class, constant expressions, visibility behavior, late-static binding differences, and fetching constants via variables.

references.title: Autoloading Classes
references.summary: PHP autoloading uses `spl_autoload_register()` to register one or more callbacks that load classes, interfaces, traits, and enumerations on demand. The legacy `__autoload()` was removed in PHP 8.0, and throwing exceptions from an autoload function is discouraged because it prevents other registered autoloaders from running.

references.title: Constructors and Destructors
references.summary: `__construct` is invoked on instantiation and `__destruct` runs when the last reference is released or during shutdown; parent constructors and destructors are not called implicitly when a child redefines them. PHP 8.0+ supports constructor property promotion, and throwing exceptions from a destructor during shutdown is unsafe.

references.title: Visibility
references.summary: Defines member visibility in PHP using public, protected, and private for properties, methods, and (since PHP 7.1) class constants, with undeclared members defaulting to public. The page documents property and method access rules and notes that objects of the same class can access each other's private and protected members.

references.title: Object Inheritance
references.summary: Subclasses inherit public and protected methods, properties, and constants from a parent class, while private members are not visible and may be reimplemented in children. Overriding visibility may be relaxed but generally not restricted (constructors are the exception), readonly and read-write properties cannot be interchanged, and the `#[\ReturnTypeWillChange]` attribute suppresses internal-class return-type deprecation notices.

references.title: Scope Resolution Operator (::)
references.summary: The Scope Resolution Operator (::), also called Paamayim Nekudotayim or double-colon, is used to access a class constant, a static property, or a static method on a class or one of its parents. Static properties and methods can be overridden via late static binding, and when referencing from outside a class you may use the class name or a variable containing the class name (the variable value cannot be a keyword such as self, parent or static). Inside class definitions the special keywords self, parent and static are used to access properties or methods from the current or parent scope.

references.title: Static Keyword
references.summary: The `static` keyword declares static methods and properties that are reached without instantiating the class; static methods cannot use `$this`, and calling a non-static method statically throws an `Error` since PHP 8.0. Static properties must be accessed with `::` rather than `->`, and the class may be referenced via a class-name variable (but not via the keywords `self`, `parent`, or `static`).

references.title: Anonymous classes
references.summary: This page documents PHP anonymous classes: lightweight one-off class declarations that can be instantiated inline, accept constructor arguments, extend other classes, implement interfaces, and use traits like normal classes. It describes scoping rules for nested anonymous classes, notes that all objects from the same anonymous-class declaration share the same class identity, and explains that the engine assigns an implementation-detail name.

references.title: Overloading
references.summary: Overloading in PHP lets classes dynamically create and handle properties and methods via magic methods that are invoked for inaccessible or non-existing members. Property overloading uses __set, __get, __isset and __unset; method overloading uses __call and __callStatic. All overloading methods must be public, none of their arguments can be passed by reference, and property overloading only works in object (not static) context.

references.title: Object Iteration
references.summary: This page documents how PHP objects can be iterated (for example with foreach). By default foreach iterates over an object’s visible properties; iterating from within object scope can expose protected and private properties. The page includes a simple example demonstrating iteration and links to related interfaces and features such as Iterator, IteratorAggregate, SPL Iterators, and Generators.

references.title: Magic Methods
references.summary: This page documents PHP's magic methods (methods starting with __), listing their names and describing required visibility and signature rules. It explains specific behaviors and signatures for serialization (__serialize, __unserialize, __sleep, __wakeup), string conversion (__toString), callable objects (__invoke), export/import (__set_state), and debugging output (__debugInfo), including version notes, warnings about visibility and type declarations, and example usage for each method.

references.title: Final Keyword
references.summary: Documents the final keyword, which prevents child classes from overriding a method or constant when prefixed with final, and states that a final class cannot be extended. Includes examples for final methods, final classes, and final constants (since PHP 8.1.0). Notes that, as of PHP 8.0.0, private methods may not be declared final except for the constructor.

references.title: Object Cloning
references.summary: Describes how to create copies of objects in PHP using the clone keyword and that cloning performs a shallow copy of an object’s properties. After cloning, if a __clone() method is defined it is called to allow adjustment of the new object; properties that are references remain references unless explicitly cloned inside __clone(). The page includes examples showing forced cloning of contained objects and accessing members of a freshly cloned object in a single expression.

references.title: Comparing Objects
references.summary: Describes how PHP compares objects: the equality operator (==) considers two objects equal if they have the same attributes and values (values compared with ==) and are instances of the same class, while the identity operator (===) requires both variables to reference the exact same instance of the same class. The page shows example code illustrating differences between == and === and notes that equality comparison is recursive for object properties and that extensions can define their own rules for (==).

references.title: Function parameters and arguments - Named Arguments
references.summary: Describes named arguments in PHP, where callers pass values by parameter name instead of position. The section covers order-independent named arguments, skipping optional parameters, the rule that named arguments must follow positional arguments, duplicate-parameter errors, and interaction with argument unpacking.

references.title: Late Static Bindings
references.summary: Describes PHP's late static bindings feature, which lets code reference the class that was actually called at runtime using static:: and get_called_class(). Explains differences and limitations compared to self:: and __CLASS__, shows behavior in static and non-static contexts, and demonstrates forwarding versus non-forwarding calls with concrete examples and output. The page includes practical notes and user-contributed examples for using static:: with properties, methods, constructors, and inheritance.

references.title: Objects and references
references.summary: Explains that PHP object variables hold an object identifier rather than the object itself, so assigning, returning or passing an object copies that identifier (not an alias) and multiple variables can point to the same object. It distinguishes this from PHP references (aliases created with &), shows how mutating an object through a copied identifier affects all identifiers for that object while reassigning an identifier does not, and provides examples demonstrating these behaviors.

references.title: Object Serialization
references.summary: serialize() returns a string containing a byte-stream representation of a PHP value; unserialize() can recreate the original variable values. Serializing an object saves its properties and the class name but not its methods, so the class definition must be available (for example via include or spl_autoload_register()) when unserializing; otherwise PHP will create a __PHP_Incomplete_Class object that stores the original class name in __PHP_Incomplete_Class_Name. Objects can customize serialization and restoration with the __sleep() and __wakeup() magic methods, with __sleep() allowing a subset of properties to be serialized.

references.title: Covariance and Contravariance
references.summary: Describes PHP's support for covariance and contravariance: partial contravariance was introduced in PHP 7.2 and full covariance/contravariance in PHP 7.4. Covariance allows a child method to return a more specific type than its parent, while contravariance allows a child method to accept a less specific parameter type; the page illustrates both with Animal/Cat/Dog factories and an eat(Food) example.

references.title: OOP Changelog
references.summary: This page lists changes to PHP's object-oriented programming model organized by PHP version, documenting added features, changed behaviors, deprecations, and incompatibilities. For the PHP 8.1 cap, high-value entries include the nullsafe operator ?-> (8.0.0), final class constants and overridable interface constants (8.1.0), typed properties and limited variance (7.4.0), and many earlier additions and behavioral changes.

references.title: Namespaces
references.summary: Documentation for PHP namespaces including defining namespaces and sub-namespaces, declaring multiple namespaces in one file, and basic usage. It covers name resolution rules, the namespace keyword and __NAMESPACE__, aliasing/importing with use, global-space behavior and fallback, and includes user-contributed examples showing practical uses (aliases, closures, and traits).

references.title: Namespaces overview
references.summary: Explains PHP namespaces (available since PHP 5.3 and in PHP 7/8), describing them as a way to group related classes, interfaces, functions and constants and to prevent name collisions with internal or third-party symbols. It also covers aliasing/shortening of long names to improve readability and gives basic namespace syntax with examples. Notes include that namespace names are case-insensitive and that the top-level name PHP (and compound names starting with it) is reserved for internal use.

references.title: Defining namespaces
references.summary: Explains how PHP resolves names in namespaces using an analogy to filesystem paths and describes three resolution forms: unqualified names (resolved to the current namespace), qualified names (resolved relative to the current namespace), and fully qualified names with a leading backslash (resolved to the literal global name). Notes that unqualified functions and constants fall back to global definitions if a namespaced one is not defined, and shows examples for classes, functions and constants and how to access global elements with a leading backslash (for example, \strlen, \Exception).

references.title: Using namespaces: Aliasing/Importing
references.summary: Describes how PHP namespaces support aliasing and importing using the use operator to import constants, functions, classes, interfaces, traits, enums and namespaces. It documents syntax and examples for simple and grouped use statements, notes that importing is performed at compile time and affects unqualified/qualified names but not fully qualified names, and explains scoping rules (use must be in global scope or inside a namespace block and imports are per-file).

references.title: Object Interfaces
references.summary: Interfaces are declared with the `interface` keyword, all their methods are implicitly public, and a class adopts them with `implements`; interfaces may extend other interfaces. Implementing methods must be signature-compatible with covariance and contravariance rules, and parameter names should match because of named arguments introduced in PHP 8.0.

references.title: Predefined Interfaces and Classes
references.summary: This page lists PHP's predefined interfaces and reserved classes, including Traversable, Iterator, IteratorAggregate, Throwable, Countable, ArrayAccess, Serializable, Closure, stdClass, Generator, Fiber, WeakReference, WeakMap, Stringable, UnitEnum, BackedEnum, SensitiveParameterValue and __PHP_Incomplete_Class. It references the SPL interfaces and reserved classes and includes a user note that extensions can also provide implementable interfaces such as JsonSerializable.

references.title: Type Operators
references.summary: This page documents PHP's instanceof operator and its behavior. It shows how instanceof checks whether a variable is an instantiated object of a class, including inherited classes and implemented interfaces, how negation works, and examples of using objects or string class names. It also notes that non-objects return false, constants on the left were allowed starting in PHP 7.3.0, arbitrary expressions wrapped in parentheses are supported as of PHP 8.0.0, and that a functional variant exists with is_a().

references.title: Function parameters and arguments - Variable-length argument lists
references.summary: Explains variadic parameter lists in PHP using the ... token. The section covers collecting extra arguments into an array, unpacking arrays or Traversable values into calls, type declarations before variadic parameters, and passing variadic arguments by reference.

references.title: Anonymous functions
references.summary: Anonymous functions (closures) are instances of the internal `Closure` class and may be used as callbacks or assigned to variables. The `use` clause imports parent-scope variables by value or by reference, `$this` is auto-bound when the closure is declared inside a class, and a `static` anonymous function opts out of that binding.

references.title: Closure
references.summary: Documentation for the Closure class, the object type used to represent anonymous functions in PHP. It lists the class synopsis and methods for manipulating closures, including bind, bindTo, call, fromCallable and a private constructor; notes that an __invoke method exists for consistency; and includes a changelog item about Closure::__debugInfo output.

references.title: Arrow functions
references.summary: Arrow functions (PHP 7.4+) are concise single-expression closures written as `fn(args) => expr` and are instances of `Closure`. They capture parent-scope variables automatically by value (so outer values cannot be modified from inside), and they support full function signatures including types, defaults, variadics, and by-reference parameters or returns.

references.title: Class Abstraction
references.summary: Describes PHP abstract classes and abstract methods within the PHP 8.1 cap: abstract classes cannot be instantiated, and any class with at least one abstract method must itself be declared abstract. Abstract methods declare only a signature and visibility and must be implemented by child classes following inheritance and signature compatibility rules. The page provides examples of valid abstract method declarations and concrete implementations.

references.title: Exceptions
references.summary: Covers PHP's exception model including throwing and catching objects that implement Throwable, try/catch/finally semantics, and exception propagation up the call stack. Notes language features: throw is an expression (PHP 8.0+), catch can specify multiple types with | (PHP 7.1+) and may omit the exception variable (PHP 8.0+). Describes finally interactions with return and exceptions, global handlers via set_exception_handler(), and converting errors to ErrorException, with numerous examples.

references.title: Errors
references.summary: This page introduces PHP error handling and explains that PHP reports errors, warnings, and notices for common coding and runtime problems. It emphasizes detecting and handling these errors to make debugging easier and includes sections such as Basics and Errors in PHP 7.

references.title: Errors - Basics
references.summary: Describes how PHP reports errors via internal error conditions, each carrying a type. Explains that without a custom handler, behavior is governed by the error_reporting, display_errors, log_errors, and error_log php.ini directives, with E_ALL recommended in development and display_errors disabled in production. Notes that user-defined error handlers can be registered with set_error_handler() to customize handling, though some error types cannot be handled this way.

references.title: Errors - Errors in PHP 7
references.summary: PHP 7 reports most fatal-class errors by throwing `Error` exceptions, which propagate like normal exceptions and fall back to `set_exception_handler()` if uncaught. The `Error` hierarchy does not extend `Exception`, so `catch (Exception $e)` does not catch `Error` instances; subclasses include `ArithmeticError`, `DivisionByZeroError`, `AssertionError`, `ParseError`, `TypeError`, `ArgumentCountError`, `ValueError`, `UnhandledMatchError`, and `FiberError`.

references.title: Class Exception
references.summary: Exception is the base class for all user exceptions in PHP (documented for PHP 5, 7, and 8). The page lists the class synopsis including properties (message, code, file, line, previous, trace, string) and methods such as __construct, getMessage, getPrevious, getCode, getFile, getLine, getTrace, getTraceAsString, __toString, and __clone, and describes that the exception properties reflect where the exception was created.

references.title: Class Error
references.summary: Error is the base class for all internal PHP errors (available in PHP 7 and PHP 8). The page documents the class synopsis, its properties (message, code, file, line, previous, string, trace) and methods including __construct, getters like getMessage/getFile/getTrace, getTraceAsString, __toString and the private __clone method.

references.title: Interface Throwable
references.summary: Throwable is the base interface for any object that can be thrown via a throw statement (including Error and Exception) and is available since PHP 7. PHP classes cannot implement Throwable directly and must instead extend Exception. The interface defines methods such as getMessage, getCode, getFile, getLine, getTrace, getTraceAsString, getPrevious and __toString, and as of PHP 8 Throwable implements Stringable.

references.title: Traits
references.summary: Traits provide a mechanism for code reuse in PHP's single-inheritance model, allowing sets of methods, properties, static members, and abstract members to be inserted into multiple independent classes; traits cannot be instantiated directly and enable horizontal composition of behavior. Method precedence is: methods in the current class override trait methods, which override inherited methods; naming conflicts between traits must be resolved explicitly with insteadof and can be aliased or have visibility changed with as. The documentation also covers traits composed from other traits, abstract trait members, property compatibility rules, and the PHP 8.1 deprecation of calling static trait members directly on a trait.

references.title: Membership
references.summary: This manual page lists PHP extensions and categorizes them under Membership, including Core Extensions (part of the PHP core), Bundled Extensions, External Extensions (require external libraries to compile), and PECL Extensions (available from PECL). The page shows alphabetical and state-based views and provides examples of extensions in each category such as JSON, GD, cURL, OpenSSL, and many PECL packages. It also includes links for reporting documentation issues and contributing.

references.title: function extension_loaded
references.summary: Describes the extension_loaded(string $extension): bool function that checks if a PHP extension is loaded. The extension name parameter is case-insensitive and the function returns true if the named extension is loaded, false otherwise. The page shows a usage example and links to related functions such as get_loaded_extensions(), get_extension_funcs(), phpinfo(), dl(), and function_exists().

references.title: function get_loaded_extensions
references.summary: Describes the get_loaded_extensions(bool $zend_extensions = false): array function which returns an indexed array of the names of all modules compiled and loaded in the PHP interpreter. The optional zend_extensions parameter, when true, restricts the result to Zend extensions; it defaults to false to return regular extensions. The page includes a usage example showing printed output and links to related functions such as extension_loaded() and get_extension_funcs().

references.title: function get_extension_funcs
references.summary: get_extension_funcs returns the names of all functions defined in a specified PHP extension. Its signature is get_extension_funcs(string $extension): array|false; the extension name must be provided in lowercase and the function returns an array of function names or false if the extension name is not valid. The manual notes that FALSE is also returned when an extension defines no functions (for example an extension that only provides classes).

references.title: ReflectionExtension::getFunctions
references.summary: ReflectionExtension::getFunctions() returns an associative array of ReflectionFunction objects for each function defined in the extension, with array keys being the function names. The method takes no parameters and returns an empty array if the extension defines no functions. The page includes a usage example showing ReflectionExtension('SimpleXML')->getFunctions() and notes availability in PHP 5, PHP 7 and PHP 8.

references.title: Datastructures
references.summary: This page lists and describes the SPL (Standard PHP Library) datastructures grouped by underlying implementation: Doubly Linked Lists (SplDoublyLinkedList, SplStack, SplQueue), Heaps (SplHeap, SplMaxHeap, SplMinHeap, SplPriorityQueue), Arrays (SplFixedArray, ArrayObject) and a Map implementation (SplObjectStorage). It notes that PHP native arrays are actually ordered hashtables and that ArrayObject can wrap PHP arrays into an object.

references.title: Iterators
references.summary: This manual page documents the SPL iterators in PHP, providing a set of iterator classes used to traverse objects and structures. It lists available iterator classes (for example ArrayIterator, RecursiveIteratorIterator, DirectoryIterator, FilterIterator, RegexIterator and others) and shows the SPL iterators class tree and table of contents for each iterator type.

references.title: Interfaces
references.summary: Documentation for the SPL interfaces in PHP, listing iterator-related interfaces (OuterIterator, RecursiveIterator, SeekableIterator) and the observer pattern interfaces (SplObserver, SplSubject). The page states that SPL provides these interfaces to enhance iterators and to implement the Observer design pattern and links to related predefined interfaces and classes.

references.title: Exceptions
references.summary: This page documents the SPL set of predefined Exceptions in PHP. It lists the available exception classes (for example LogicException and RuntimeException and their subclasses such as BadFunctionCallException, InvalidArgumentException, OutOfBoundsException, OverflowException, RangeException, UnderflowException, and UnexpectedValueException) and shows the class tree indicating which SPL exceptions extend Exception. The page also links to related predefined exceptions and notes for contributing fixes to the documentation.

references.title: SPL Functions
references.summary: This PHP manual page is a function reference for the Standard PHP Library (SPL), listing available SPL functions such as class_implements, iterator_to_array, spl_autoload_register, spl_classes, spl_object_hash and others. It provides a table of contents linking to SPL-related interfaces, datastructures, iterators, file handling and functions, and includes user-contributed notes with code examples and usage tips.

references.title: File Handling
references.summary: Documentation for PHP's SPL file handling provides reference information on file-related classes. It lists and describes SplFileInfo, SplFileObject, and SplTempFileObject and explains that SPL supplies a number of classes to work with files. The page serves as a table of contents and entry point to SPL file handling functionality in the function reference.

references.title: Symfony and HTTP Fundamentals - Requests and Responses in HTTP
references.summary: Introduces HTTP as a text-based language two machines use to communicate, with the client sending a request and the server returning a response. This page frames the request-response model that the entire Symfony framework is built around.

references.title: Symfony and HTTP Fundamentals - Step 1: The Client Sends a Request
references.summary: Explains the structure of a raw HTTP request including the start line with method and URI, the most common methods (`GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, `DELETE`), and the role of headers such as `Host`, `Accept`, and `User-Agent`. This is the canonical Symfony explanation of what a client actually puts on the wire.

references.title: Symfony and HTTP Fundamentals - Step 2: The Server Returns a Response
references.summary: Shows what an HTTP response looks like, including the status line with version and code, response headers, and the body. It connects the abstract request-response cycle to the concrete bytes Symfony's `Response` object models.

references.title: Symfony and HTTP Fundamentals - The Journey from the Request to the Response
references.summary: Describes the front controller pattern and how Symfony turns an incoming HTTP request into a response by booting the kernel, dispatching events, and invoking a controller. This is the Symfony-specific view of the client-server interaction.

references.title: An overview of HTTP (MDN)
references.summary: A practical overview of HTTP as a stateless, application-layer client-server protocol, covering the roles of clients, servers, intermediaries, and the request-response message model. Useful background before writing code that consumes or emits HTTP traffic.

references.title: HTTP messages (MDN)
references.summary: Describes the structure of HTTP request and response messages, including start lines, status lines, header fields, blank-line separators, and optional bodies. This is the wire-format reference behind Symfony's `Request` and `Response` abstractions.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Terminology and Core Concepts
references.summary: Defines the normative vocabulary of HTTP: user agents, origin servers, intermediaries, gateways, proxies, tunnels, caches, resources, and representations. These are the terms that the rest of HTTP semantics builds on when describing client-server interactions.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Message Abstraction
references.summary: Specifies HTTP messages abstractly as either requests or responses composed of control data, header fields, content, and optional trailer fields. Separates the semantics of a message from its on-the-wire framing.

references.title: HTTP response status codes (MDN)
references.summary: Indexes HTTP response status codes grouped by class: `1xx` informational, `2xx` successful, `3xx` redirection, `4xx` client error, and `5xx` server error. Useful for choosing the correct code and understanding what each communicates to the client.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Status Codes
references.summary: Defines the status code model, including interim and final responses, the five status code classes, reason phrases, and the registry for additional codes. This is the normative basis for response semantics across HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - 1xx Informational
references.summary: Specifies the `1xx` interim response class, including `100 Continue` for clients sending bodies after `Expect: 100-continue` and `101 Switching Protocols` for protocol upgrades. Clarifies that interim responses are non-final and may precede a final response.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - 3xx Redirection
references.summary: Specifies the redirection class including `301 Moved Permanently`, `302 Found`, `303 See Other`, `304 Not Modified`, `307 Temporary Redirect`, and `308 Permanent Redirect`. Distinguishes which codes preserve the request method and which allow the client to switch to `GET`.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - 4xx Client Error
references.summary: Specifies the client-error class including `400 Bad Request`, `401 Unauthorized`, `403 Forbidden`, `404 Not Found`, `405 Method Not Allowed` with required `Allow` header, `409 Conflict`, `410 Gone`, `412 Precondition Failed`, `415 Unsupported Media Type`, and `422 Unprocessable Content`. Distinguishes authentication (`401`) from authorization (`403`).

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - 5xx Server Error
references.summary: Specifies the server-error class including `500 Internal Server Error`, `501 Not Implemented`, `502 Bad Gateway`, `503 Service Unavailable`, and `504 Gateway Timeout`. Explains when servers should advertise transient unavailability with `503` and use `Retry-After`.

references.title: HTTP 304 Not Modified (MDN)
references.summary: Describes the `304 Not Modified` response sent for conditional `GET`/`HEAD` requests when the cached representation is still fresh. Lists which headers must be present, the rule that the response has no body, and how it interacts with `ETag`/`If-None-Match` and `Last-Modified`/`If-Modified-Since`.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - 2xx Successful
references.summary: Specifies the `2xx` successful response class, including `200 OK` (request succeeded with a representation), `201 Created` (resource created and identified by `Location` or the request URI), `202 Accepted` (request accepted for processing but not yet completed), `204 No Content` (success with no body and no representation change), and `205 Reset Content`. Defines that `204` and `304` responses must not include a message body, while `200` and `201` do carry one.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Request
references.summary: Introduces the `Symfony\Component\HttpFoundation\Request` class as the object-oriented wrapper around PHP superglobals. Shows how to obtain a request from globals with `Request::createFromGlobals()` and lists the public attributes (`request`, `query`, `cookies`, `files`, `server`, `headers`).

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Accessing Request Data
references.summary: Documents the `ParameterBag`/`InputBag` API exposed by `request`, `query`, `cookies`, `files`, and `server`, including `get()`, `has()`, `all()`, and the typed filters `getAlpha()`, `getAlnum()`, `getBoolean()`, `getDigits()`, `getInt()`, `filter()`. Also covers the `attributes` bag and reading the raw body with `getContent()` or `toArray()` for JSON.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Identifying a Request
references.summary: Explains how to distinguish requests by inspecting the path info via `Request::getPathInfo()`. For a URL like `http://example.com/blog/index.php/post/hello-world`, the path info is `/post/hello-world`.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Simulating a Request
references.summary: Shows `Request::create()` for building a synthetic `Request` object in tests or sub-requests, with URI, method, and parameter arguments. Also documents `Request::overrideGlobals()`, `duplicate()`, and `initialize()` for modifying request data after creation.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Processing HTTP Headers
references.summary: Covers header parsing utilities provided by `Symfony\Component\HttpFoundation\HeaderUtils`, including `split()`, `combine()`, `toString()`, `quote()`, `unquote()`, and `parseQuery()`. Notes that `parseQuery()` preserves dots in keys, unlike PHP's `parse_str()` which replaces `.` with `_`.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Overriding the Request
references.summary: Documents `Request::setFactory()` for registering a custom `Request` subclass factory. This is useful when migrating from legacy systems that need additional methods or a different default behavior on the request object.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Anonymizing IP Addresses
references.summary: Documents `IpUtils::anonymize()` for masking IPv4 and IPv6 addresses before logging or storing them. Useful for HTTP request data handling in applications that need to comply with user-protection regulations and reduce personally identifiable network information.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Response
references.summary: Documents the `Symfony\Component\HttpFoundation\Response` class, including the constructor signature `(content, status, headers)` and the response API (`setContent()`, `setStatusCode()`, `setCharset()`). Explains the public `headers` property as a `ResponseHeaderBag` and that Symfony assumes UTF-8 by default.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Sending the Response
references.summary: Documents the `prepare()` and `send()` methods used to finalize and emit a response. Explains that `prepare(Request $request)` adjusts the response to fix incompatibilities with the HTTP specification (for example a wrong `Content-Type` header) before `send()` is called.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Redirecting the User
references.summary: Describes the `RedirectResponse` class, whose constructor accepts a target URL and sets the `Location` header. Covers the typical pattern of returning a redirect from a controller without manual header manipulation.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Streaming a Response
references.summary: Documents `StreamedResponse`, whose content is produced by a PHP callable that echoes chunks instead of building a full string in memory. Notes the interaction with `flush()`, `ob_flush()`, and the `X-Accel-Buffering: no` header for nginx.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Serving Files
references.summary: Describes `BinaryFileResponse` and `HeaderUtils::makeDisposition()` for building `Content-Disposition` headers. Covers automatic handling of `Range`/`If-Range`, `X-Sendfile`/`X-Accel-Redirect` offloading via `BinaryFileResponse::trustXSendfileTypeHeader()`, and `deleteFileAfterSend()`.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Creating a JSON Response
references.summary: Documents `JsonResponse`, which sets `Content-Type: application/json`, JSON-encodes the payload, and provides `setData()`, `setEncodingOptions()`, and `JsonResponse::fromJsonString()` for already-encoded payloads. Warns about JSON Hijacking and recommends an associative array as the outermost structure.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Generating Relative and Absolute URLs
references.summary: Documents `UrlHelper` for resolving relative URLs against the current request to produce absolute ones (`getAbsoluteUrl()`) and for generating relative paths from absolute URLs (`getRelativePath()`). Useful when emitting URLs into responses without coupling to routing.

references.title: HTTP request methods (MDN)
references.summary: Indexes the standard HTTP methods (`GET`, `HEAD`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, `CONNECT`, `OPTIONS`, `TRACE`, `PATCH`) and summarizes whether each is safe, idempotent, and cacheable. Useful as a quick reference for choosing the right method for an operation.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Methods Overview
references.summary: Introduces HTTP methods as case-sensitive tokens that define the primary semantics of a request, with an extensible registry. Frames the model before individual method semantics are defined.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Common Method Properties
references.summary: Defines the *safe*, *idempotent*, and *cacheable* properties and lists which methods have which property. Establishes that `GET` and `HEAD` are safe, `GET`/`HEAD`/`PUT`/`DELETE`/`OPTIONS`/`TRACE` are idempotent, and `GET`/`HEAD`/`POST` are cacheable when explicitly allowed.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Method Definitions
references.summary: Defines the eight standard HTTP methods (`GET`, `HEAD`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, `CONNECT`, `OPTIONS`, `TRACE`) including their semantics, allowed bodies, and required behaviors. This is the authoritative source for what each method means.

references.title: HTTP GET method (MDN)
references.summary: Describes `GET` as a safe, idempotent, cacheable method used to retrieve a representation of the target resource. Notes the rule that `GET` requests should not have a request body and that responses are cacheable by default unless explicitly forbidden.

references.title: HTTP POST method (MDN)
references.summary: Describes `POST` as a non-safe, non-idempotent method used to submit data to be processed by the target resource. Lists typical content types (`application/x-www-form-urlencoded`, `multipart/form-data`, `application/json`) and explains how `POST` is cacheable only with explicit cache headers.

references.title: HTTP PUT method (MDN)
references.summary: Describes `PUT` as an idempotent method that creates or replaces the target resource with the request payload. Contrasts `PUT` with `POST` and `PATCH` and notes that responses are not cacheable.

references.title: HTTP DELETE method (MDN)
references.summary: Describes `DELETE` as an idempotent method that requests removal of the target resource. Lists common response codes (`200`, `202`, `204`) depending on whether the deletion is synchronous, asynchronous, or returns no content.

references.title: HTTP PATCH method (MDN)
references.summary: Describes `PATCH` as a non-idempotent (in general) method that applies a partial update to the target resource. Notes that the request body must use a patch document format and that `PATCH` is not cacheable.

references.title: RFC 5789: PATCH Method for HTTP
references.summary: Defines the `PATCH` method as a way to apply a partial set of changes to a resource using a patch document. Specifies error codes such as `409 Conflict` and `415 Unsupported Media Type` and clarifies that `PATCH` is neither safe nor inherently idempotent.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Setting Cookies
references.summary: Documents how to attach cookies to a Symfony response via `$response->headers->setCookie(Cookie::create(...))`, the `Cookie::create()` factory and `with*()` builder methods, and the `clearCookie()` API for deletion. Also covers `Cookie::fromString()` for parsing a raw `Set-Cookie` header value.

references.title: Using HTTP cookies (MDN)
references.summary: Explains the cookie lifecycle from `Set-Cookie` to `Cookie`, the difference between session cookies and persistent cookies (those with `Expires` or `Max-Age`), the `Domain`/`Path` scoping rules, and the security attributes `Secure`, `HttpOnly`, and `SameSite`. Covers tracking, third-party cookies, and prefixes such as `__Secure-`/`__Host-`.

references.title: Set-Cookie header (MDN)
references.summary: Reference for the `Set-Cookie` response header, including all attributes: `Expires`, `Max-Age`, `Domain`, `Path`, `Secure`, `HttpOnly`, and `SameSite=Strict|Lax|None`. Documents the rules for which attributes the user agent honors.

references.title: RFC 6265: HTTP State Management Mechanism
references.summary: Normative specification of HTTP cookies, including the syntax of `Set-Cookie` and `Cookie`, attribute parsing, expiry semantics, and the same-origin and path scoping rules. Defines the algorithm a user agent uses to decide whether to send a stored cookie with a request.

references.title: Framework Configuration Reference - session
references.summary: Reference for the `framework.session` options that control the session cookie. Documents `cookie_lifetime` (defaults to PHP's `session.cookie_lifetime`, `0` means a browser-session cookie), `cookie_samesite` (defaults to `'lax'`, accepts `'none'`, `'strict'`, `'lax'`, or `null`), `cookie_secure` (defaults to `'auto'`, sending only over HTTPS for HTTPS requests), and `cookie_httponly` (defaults to `true` to block JavaScript access).

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Managing the HTTP Cache
references.summary: Documents the cache-related response API: `setPublic()`, `setPrivate()`, `expire()`, `setExpires()`, `setMaxAge()`, `setSharedMaxAge()`, `setTtl()`, `setClientTtl()`, `setLastModified()`, `setEtag()`, `setVary()`, and `setCache()`. Also documents `isNotModified($request)` for serving `304` responses based on `ETag`/`Last-Modified` validators.

references.title: RFC 9111: HTTP Caching
references.summary: Normative specification of HTTP caching, including freshness, validation, response cacheability, request and response directives in `Cache-Control`, and the `Vary` header. Defines the rules for shared (proxy) versus private (browser) caches and how `s-maxage` and `private` interact.

references.title: HTTP caching (MDN)
references.summary: Practical overview of HTTP caching: cache locations (browser, shared proxies, CDNs), freshness via `Cache-Control: max-age` and `Expires`, validation via `ETag`/`If-None-Match` and `Last-Modified`/`If-Modified-Since`, and `Vary`-based content negotiation. Useful before reading RFC 9111.

references.title: Cache-Control header (MDN)
references.summary: Reference for `Cache-Control` directives in both requests and responses, including `no-store`, `no-cache`, `private`, `public`, `max-age`, `s-maxage`, `must-revalidate`, `stale-while-revalidate`, and `immutable`. Explains the precedence between `Cache-Control` and the older `Expires` header.

references.title: ETag header (MDN)
references.summary: Describes `ETag` as an opaque validator the server attaches to a representation, the difference between strong and weak (`W/...`) ETags, and how clients use `If-None-Match` to revalidate cached responses. Used for conditional `GET` and optimistic concurrency control with `If-Match`.

references.title: Working with Server Side Includes
references.summary: **Server Side Includes (SSI)** are similar to ESI but processed by the **web server** (e.g. Apache `mod_include`) rather than a reverse proxy. Symfony exposes `framework.ssi: { enabled: true }` and the `{{ render_ssi(...) }}` Twig function; output is `<!--#include virtual="..."-->` directives.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Accessing Accept-* Headers Data
references.summary: Documents `Request::getAcceptableContentTypes()`, `getLanguages()`, `getCharsets()`, and `getEncodings()`, all returning items ordered by descending quality. Covers the lower-level `AcceptHeader` utility class for parsing arbitrary `Accept-*` headers and reading `q` and other parameters via `AcceptHeaderItem::getQuality()` and `getAttribute()`.

references.title: Content negotiation (MDN)
references.summary: Describes proactive content negotiation driven by `Accept`, `Accept-Language`, `Accept-Encoding`, and `Accept-Charset`, plus reactive negotiation via `300 Multiple Choices`. Explains the role of `Vary` for caches when responses depend on negotiation.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Content Negotiation
references.summary: Defines proactive and reactive content negotiation, the role of quality values (`q=`), and how servers select among representations. Describes how `Vary` informs caches that a response varies based on certain request fields.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Accept
references.summary: Specifies the `Accept` request header: media-range syntax with type and subtype, optional `q` quality factor, accept-extensions, and the rule that absence of `Accept` implies any media type is acceptable. Defines preference ordering by descending `q`.

references.title: Accept header (MDN)
references.summary: Reference for the `Accept` request header used to advertise acceptable response media types with optional quality values (e.g. `text/html;q=0.9, */*;q=0.5`). Explains how multiple types are weighed and matched against server-side representations.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Safe Content Preference
references.summary: Documents Symfony's support for the HTTP safe content preference defined by RFC 8674. Shows how `Request::preferSafeContent()` detects a user agent's preference and how `Response::setContentSafe()` marks that the response respected it.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Accept-Encoding
references.summary: Specifies the `Accept-Encoding` request header used to advertise acceptable content codings (e.g. `gzip`, `br`, `deflate`, `identity`). Defines `q=` quality preferences, the special `*` token, and the rule that an absent `Accept-Encoding` allows any coding while an empty value implies `identity` only. A response that varies on this header should list it in `Vary` so caches store distinct entries per coding.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Accessing Accept-* Headers Data
references.summary: Documents `Request::getLanguages()` for reading the user's accepted languages from the `Accept-Language` header, ordered by descending quality. Also covers `AcceptHeader::fromString($request->headers->get('Accept-Language'))` for direct quality-aware parsing.

references.title: Controllers - The Request and Response Object
references.summary: Shows controllers receiving the `Request` object via type-hint and using `getPreferredLanguage(['en', 'fr'])` to pick the best language from the `Accept-Language` header against a list of supported locales. Also lists `query`, `request`, `server`, `files`, and `cookies` accessors used alongside locale detection.

references.title: How to Work with the User's Locale
references.summary: Explains that the request locale is read with `Request::getLocale()` and changed with `Request::setLocale()`, but that calling `setLocale()` from the controller is too late for the translator. Recommends a `kernel.request` listener that runs before `LocaleListener` for reliable locale assignment.

references.title: How to Work with the User's Locale - The Locale and the URL
references.summary: Recommends carrying the locale in the URL via the special `_locale` routing parameter (e.g. `/{_locale}/contact`) so the matched locale is automatically set on the `Request`. Explains why session-driven locale switching breaks the rule that a URL identifies one resource.

references.title: Accept-Language header (MDN)
references.summary: Reference for the `Accept-Language` request header used by clients to advertise natural-language preferences with optional quality values. Explains language tag syntax (`en`, `en-US`, `en-GB`) and how servers should pick the best available match.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Accept-Language
references.summary: Specifies the `Accept-Language` header, including language-range syntax, `q` factors, the `*` wildcard, and the rule that an explicitly listed language with `q=0` is excluded. Defines the algorithm for matching against available language tags.

references.title: HTTP Client - Basic Usage
references.summary: Shows how to obtain an `HttpClientInterface` via `HttpClient::create()` or autowiring and how to call `request($method, $url, $options)` to perform a request. Introduces the response API as lazily-evaluated and asynchronous by default.

references.title: HTTP Client - Configuration
references.summary: Documents the `framework.http_client` configuration: `default_options`, `max_host_connections`, `mock_response_factory`, and named scoped clients. Explains how options set at the client level are merged with per-request options.

references.title: HTTP Client - Scoping Client
references.summary: Shows how to define scoped HTTP clients in `framework.http_client.scoped_clients` so that `base_uri`, headers, and authentication apply automatically to a regex-matched set of URLs. Each scope becomes its own injectable service.

references.title: HTTP Client - Authentication
references.summary: Documents the `auth_basic`, `auth_bearer`, and `auth_ntlm` request options for sending HTTP authentication credentials. Explains how they translate to `Authorization` headers and that `auth_ntlm` requires the cURL transport.

references.title: HTTP Client - Query String Parameters
references.summary: Documents the `query` request option for appending parameters to a URL without manual encoding, including handling of arrays and nested data. Explains that values already present in the URL are merged with `query` rather than replaced.

references.title: HTTP Client - Headers
references.summary: Documents the `headers` request option for sending custom request headers, supporting both indexed `Name: value` strings and the `name => value` map form. Notes that `Content-Type` is set automatically when using `body` or `json` options.

references.title: HTTP Client - Uploading Data
references.summary: Documents the `body` and `json` request options for submitting payloads, including support for raw strings, arrays (form-encoded), iterables/generators (chunked), and resources. Explains that `json` JSON-encodes the value and sets the proper `Content-Type`.

references.title: HTTP Client - Cookies
references.summary: Explains that the HTTP Client does not handle cookies automatically and that callers must use the `Cookie` header in `headers` or build a `CookieJar` themselves. Shows the typical pattern when a stateful API is being consumed.

references.title: HTTP Client - Redirects
references.summary: Documents the `max_redirects` option, which defaults to `20`, and explains how to disable redirect following with `0`. Notes that the final response object always reflects the last response in the chain and that `Authorization` and cookie headers may be stripped on cross-host redirects.

references.title: HTTP Client - Retry Failed Requests
references.summary: Documents the `RetryableHttpClient` decorator and `framework.http_client.retry_failed`, which retry idempotent requests on transport errors and 423/425/429/502/503/504 responses. Explains backoff via `GenericRetryStrategy` and customization via `RetryStrategyInterface`.

references.title: HTTP Client - HTTP Proxies
references.summary: Documents the `proxy` and `no_proxy` options for routing requests through forward proxies. Explains that Symfony HttpClient honors standard proxy environment variables from the operating system and supports CIDR notation in `no_proxy`.

references.title: HTTP Client - Processing Responses
references.summary: Documents `getStatusCode()`, `getHeaders()`, `getContent()`, `toArray()`, and `getInfo()` for inspecting responses. Explains that `getContent()` and similar methods throw `HttpExceptionInterface` on non-2xx status by default unless `false` is passed.

references.title: HTTP Client - Streaming Responses
references.summary: Documents `HttpClientInterface::stream()`, which yields `ChunkInterface` instances for one or many responses. Useful for processing large downloads incrementally without buffering the full body in memory.

references.title: HTTP Client - Handling Exceptions
references.summary: Defines three exception families implementing `ExceptionInterface`: `HttpExceptionInterface` (3xx-5xx status codes), `TransportExceptionInterface` (lower-level transport errors), and `DecodingExceptionInterface` (response could not be decoded). Explains that `getHeaders()`, `getContent()`, and `toArray()` throw on 3xx-5xx unless `false` is passed to opt out.

references.title: HTTP Client - Concurrent Requests
references.summary: Shows how to fire multiple requests in parallel by simply calling `request()` several times, since responses are lazy. Explains that iterating over `stream($responses)` allows progressing whichever response has data first, enabling true concurrency without explicit promises.

references.title: HTTP Client - Caching Requests and Responses
references.summary: Documents `CachingHttpClient`, which decorates an `HttpClientInterface` with an HTTP-cache-aware `StoreInterface` so that fresh responses are served from local cache and stale responses are revalidated. Useful for reducing load on idempotent upstream APIs.

references.title: HTTP Client - Testing
references.summary: Documents `MockHttpClient` and `MockResponse`, which let tests stub HTTP responses without real network calls. Explains the constructor that takes a callable or array of `MockResponse` and the `getRequestUrl()`/`getRequestOptions()` accessors for asserting outgoing requests.

references.title: HTTP Client - SSRF (Server-side request forgery) Handling
references.summary: Explains the risk of making HTTP client requests to user-provided URIs and how SSRF can reach internal hosts. Documents `NoPrivateNetworkHttpClient`, which decorates another client and blocks requests to private networks by default or to configured CIDR ranges.

references.title: HTTP Client - Dealing with Network Timeouts
references.summary: Documents the `timeout` request option, stream timeouts, non-blocking monitoring with timeout `0`, and the distinction between idle timeouts and maximum request duration. Clarifies that large transfers may run for a long time as long as the transaction remains active.

references.title: HTTP Client - Dealing with Network Errors
references.summary: Explains that network failures such as broken pipes and DNS errors are reported as `TransportExceptionInterface`. Because responses are lazy, these exceptions can occur both when calling `request()` and later when reading response status, headers, or content.

references.title: HTTP Client - PSR-18 and PSR-17
references.summary: Documents the `Psr18Client` adapter that exposes Symfony's HTTP client through the PSR-18 client interface. Also covers its PSR-17 factory support and the need for `psr/http-client` plus a PSR-17 implementation such as `nyholm/psr7`.

references.title: HTTP Client - Consuming Server-Sent Events
references.summary: Shows how `EventSourceHttpClient` consumes server-sent event streams served as `text/event-stream`. Documents the `ServerSentEvent` chunk type and the `getArrayData()` helper for JSON event payloads.

references.title: Upgrading Existing Applications to Symfony Flex
references.summary: Symfony Flex is the Composer-based system that installs Symfony applications and recipes for third-party packages. The page describes the recommended directory structure (`assets/`, `bin/`, `config/`, `public/`, `src/`, `templates/`, `tests/`, `translations/`, `var/`, `vendor/`) and the manual steps required to convert a legacy Symfony 3 application to Flex.

references.title: Upgrading Existing Applications to Symfony Flex - Customizing Flex Paths
references.summary: Lists the configurable Flex paths declared in `composer.json` under `extra`: `bin-dir`, `config-dir`, `src-dir`, `var-dir`, and `public-dir`. The example shows `src-dir: src/App` to make Flex copy PHP classes under `src/App` instead of `src`.

references.title: How To Configure and Use Flex Private Recipe Repositories
references.summary: Explains how Flex recipes are JSON manifests stored in a Git repository alongside an `index.json` file, and how `composer.json`'s `extra.symfony.endpoint` configures Flex to fetch recipes from private GitHub or GitLab repositories. Useful for questions about how Flex installs and configures bundles for a project.

references.title: Installing & Setting up the Symfony Framework - Installing Packages
references.summary: Explains Symfony Flex as the Composer plugin that modifies `require`, `update`, and `remove` to automate common package setup tasks. It introduces recipes, the `symfony.lock` file (which must be committed to the repository), the main and contrib recipe repositories, and packs as Composer metapackages unpacked by Flex.

references.title: Symfony Documentation
references.summary: The **Symfony documentation index** — entry point that organizes the docs into Getting Started, Configuration, Forms, Doctrine, Security, Cache, Console, HTTP Cache, Logging, Mailer, Notifier, Performance, Profiler, Sessions, Testing, Validation, plus the bundles and components reference. The page also links the Quick Tour and the Symfony Flex tutorials that drive the recipe-based workflow.

references.title: Flex: Compose your Application
references.summary: **Symfony Flex** is the Composer plugin that turns `composer require <alias>` into a fully-wired feature: it expands aliases (e.g. `mailer`, `orm`, `messenger`) to the right packages and applies a **recipe** that registers the bundle in `bundles.php`, copies default config under `config/packages/`, registers env vars in `.env`, and adds default routes. Recipes are tracked in `symfony.lock`.

references.title: Symfony Code License
references.summary: Symfony source code is released under the MIT license. The page reproduces the full MIT license text and links to the licenses governing the documentation and the Symfony trademarks.

references.title: Coding Standards - License
references.summary: Symfony's coding standards require every PHP source file to carry the MIT license header at the top of the file, before the namespace declaration. This rule applies to all contributed code in the Symfony core repositories.

references.title: Symfony 6.0 LICENSE file (GitHub)
references.summary: The canonical LICENSE file in the 6.0 branch of the `symfony/symfony` repository, granting the MIT license rights with the standard `(c) 2004-present Fabien Potencier` copyright notice.

references.title: The Contracts Component
references.summary: The Contracts component packages a set of small interfaces (cache, event-dispatcher, http-client, service, translation, deprecation) extracted from Symfony components so they can be required and implemented independently. The page documents the design principles (one sub-namespace per domain, BC with the Symfony components, listing implementations under Composer's `provide`) and how Contracts differ from PHP-FIG PSRs.

references.title: How to Install and Use the Symfony Components
references.summary: Each Symfony component is published as its own Composer package (for example `symfony/finder`) and can be used standalone in any PHP project after requiring `vendor/autoload.php`. Useful for questions about installing components without the full framework.

references.title: The Asset Component
references.summary: The **Asset** component manages URL generation and **versioning** of web assets such as CSS, JavaScript and images. Versioning strategies (e.g. `StaticVersionStrategy`, `JsonManifestVersionStrategy`) append a fingerprint or read manifest entries; named "packages" let you mix several strategies (e.g. local files vs. CDN).

references.title: The Cache Component
references.summary: The **Cache** component implements both **PSR-6** (`CacheItemPoolInterface`) and the Symfony **Cache Contracts** (`CacheInterface`). It ships ready-to-use adapters for filesystem, Redis, APCu, Memcached, and PDO; supports tag-based invalidation, and provides cache-stampede protection via locking and early expiration.

references.title: The Config Component
references.summary: The **Config** component provides utilities to **define and validate configuration** trees. It supplies a `ConfigurationInterface`, a `TreeBuilder` DSL for declaring nodes, multiple loaders (YAML, XML, PHP, INI, directory) and resource trackers used by the kernel's container cache to invalidate when config files change.

references.title: The Console Component
references.summary: The **Console** component eases the creation of **command-line interfaces**. Around `Application` and `Command`, it provides input/output abstractions, helpers (progress bar, table, question, formatter), styling (`SymfonyStyle`), event hooks (`ConsoleEvents::COMMAND`, `TERMINATE`, `ERROR`, `SIGNAL`), and easily testable commands via `CommandTester`.

references.title: The Ldap Component
references.summary: The **Ldap** component connects to an **LDAP server** (OpenLDAP, Active Directory). It exposes a typed `Ldap` client wrapping `bind()`, `query()`, `add()`, `update()` and `delete()` operations and integrates with the Security component for LDAP authentication and user provisioning.

references.title: The Lock Component
references.summary: The **Lock** component provides advisory **locks** to grant exclusive access to a shared resource. Stores include `FlockStore` (filesystem flock), `RedisStore`, `MongoDbStore`, `PdoStore`, `SemaphoreStore`. Locks can be **blocking** or **non-blocking**, support **TTL with auto-release**, and may be **shared** (read) or **exclusive** (write).

references.title: The PropertyAccess Component
references.summary: The **PropertyAccess** component reads from and writes to objects/arrays using a **string property path** (e.g. `address.zipCode`, `[items][0].name`). It looks up public properties, getters/setters/issers/hassers, magic accessors (`__get`/`__set`), and array offsets, and powers form data binding and the `Expression` validation constraint.

references.title: The PropertyInfo Component
references.summary: The **PropertyInfo** component returns metadata about an object's properties — list, type, description, read/write visibility — drawn from multiple sources: PHP attributes, PHPDoc, Doctrine, Reflection, and Serializer metadata. It powers the Form and Serializer components when they need to know property types automatically.

references.title: The Semaphore Component
references.summary: The **Semaphore** component manages **counting semaphores**, a generalisation of locks that allows up to *N* concurrent holders rather than just one. Stores include `RedisStore`, `MongoDbStore`, etc.; like the Lock component it supports TTL and automatic release, and is useful for capping concurrency to an external resource.

references.title: The UID Component
references.summary: The **UID** component provides PHP value objects for unique identifiers, namely **UUID** (`Uuid`, `UuidV1`/`V3`/`V4`/`V5`/`V6`/`V7`/`V8`) and **ULID** (`Ulid`). It includes encoders to base32/58/RFC 4122 string representations and custom Doctrine types so UIDs can be persisted as binary or string columns.

references.title: Loading Resources
references.summary: Explains how the **Validator** component loads constraint metadata from external **resources** — annotations/attributes (`AttributeLoader`), XML files (`XmlFileLoader`), YAML files (`YamlFileLoader`) — through `ValidatorBuilder`. Multiple loaders can be chained via `LoaderChain`.

references.title: The VarExporter Component
references.summary: The **VarExporter** component exports any **serializable** PHP value to plain PHP source code via `VarExporter::export()`. Unlike `serialize()`, the output is human-readable and instantiable without a `unserialize()` call. It also instantiates and populates objects **without invoking their constructor** (`Instantiator::instantiate()`) and supports **lazy ghost** and **lazy proxy** objects since 6.2/7.0.

references.title: The Workflow Component
references.summary: The **Workflow** component provides finite state machines and workflows for any PHP object. It distinguishes **state machines** (a single state at a time) from **workflows** (multiple "places" in parallel via Petri nets). Transitions can be guarded by events (`workflow.<name>.guard.<transition>`), audited via the marking store, and visualised through `bin/console workflow:dump`.

references.title: The Yaml Component
references.summary: The **Yaml** component parses YAML strings into PHP arrays (`Yaml::parse()`, `Yaml::parseFile()`) and dumps PHP arrays back into YAML (`Yaml::dump()`). Parser flags `Yaml::PARSE_OBJECT_FOR_MAP`, `PARSE_DATETIME`, `PARSE_CUSTOM_TAGS`, `PARSE_CONSTANT` opt-in to richer PHP types; dumper flags such as `DUMP_OBJECT_AS_MAP` mirror them.

references.title: Pushing Data to Clients Using the Mercure Protocol
references.summary: **Mercure** is an open protocol — built on Server-Sent Events — for pushing real-time updates from server to client. The MercureBundle integrates a `HubInterface`, a JWT-based publisher and a Twig helper. Clients subscribe via an `EventSource` to a topic URL; the server publishes `Update` messages through `$hub->publish(...)`.

references.title: Creating and Sending Notifications
references.summary: The **Notifier** component is an abstraction over many delivery channels — SMS, chat (Slack, Discord, Teams, …), email (via the Mailer bridge), push and browser notifications. A `Notification` is dispatched to one or more `Recipient`s through registered transports identified by DSNs (`twilio://`, `slack://`, etc.).

references.title: Rate Limiter
references.summary: A **rate limiter** controls how often an event (HTTP request, login attempt, expensive job) may happen. Symfony ships three policies — **fixed window**, **sliding window** and **token bucket** — each with different burst/precision trade-offs. Limiters are configured under `framework.rate_limiter` and consumed via `RateLimiterFactory::create($key)`.

references.title: Asset Preloading and Resource Hints with WebLink
references.summary: The **WebLink** component manages **`Link` HTTP headers** (RFC 5988) used by browsers and HTTP/2 servers for **preload**, **preconnect**, **dns-prefetch**, **prefetch**, and **prerender** resource hints. It exposes Twig helpers (`{{ preload(...) }}`, `{{ preconnect(...) }}`, …) so templates can declare hints inline; the bundle's response listener writes the matching `Link` header.

references.title: The DependencyInjection Component
references.summary: The **DependencyInjection** component is the basis of Symfony's service container. It defines services and their dependencies (autowiring, autoconfiguration, factories, decorators), supports compiler passes for low-level container customization, and exposes loaders that read service definitions from YAML, XML, PHP, and PHP attributes.

references.title: The Form Component
references.summary: The **Form** component creates HTML forms and handles their submitted data. It binds form types to data objects through configurable data mappers, renders fields through themable templates, and integrates with the Validator and PropertyAccess components for validation and read/write access. The framework wires it into the FrameworkBundle, but the component is also usable stand-alone.

references.title: The Messenger Component
references.summary: The **Messenger** component dispatches messages to handlers through a configurable bus and ships them to transports (in-memory, AMQP, Doctrine, Redis, etc.) for asynchronous processing. It distinguishes the **command bus**, **query bus**, and **event bus** patterns and supports middleware, retries, failure transports, and message stamps.

references.title: The OptionsResolver Component
references.summary: The **OptionsResolver** component is an enhanced replacement for PHP's `array_replace_recursive()` for processing options arrays. It supports **defaults**, **required** options, **type checks**, **value validation**, **deferred normalizers**, **deprecation messages**, and **nested option sets**. Symfony's Form types use it for their `configureOptions()` method.

references.title: The PSR-7 Bridge
references.summary: The **PSR-7 Bridge** converts between Symfony's `Request`/`Response` objects and **PSR-7** `RequestInterface` / `ResponseInterface` instances. The bridge is delivered as `symfony/psr-http-message-bridge` and integrates with any PSR-17 factory implementation to produce PSR-7 messages.

references.title: The String Component
references.summary: The **String** component provides an object-oriented API for **Unicode strings**. It exposes three classes — `ByteString` (raw bytes), `CodePointString` (Unicode code points), `UnicodeString` (grapheme clusters) — with chainable methods (`upper()`, `lower()`, `truncate()`, `slug()`, `chunk()`, `padBoth()`, etc.). The static `u()`, `b()`, `s()` shortcuts construct the matching object.

references.title: The Validator Component
references.summary: The **Validator** component implements `Symfony\Component\Validator\Validator\ValidatorInterface` to validate values and objects against constraints described as PHP attributes, YAML, XML, or PHP. The result is a `ConstraintViolationListInterface` of typed violations with paths, messages, codes, and parameters.

references.title: Validator Component Metadata
references.summary: Explains the **metadata model** behind constraint loading. Constraints are attached to a `ClassMetadata` (or its `PropertyMetadata` / `GetterMetadata` children) at boot via metadata loaders (PHP attribute, XML, YAML). At runtime the validator walks the metadata graph to evaluate constraints in the requested validation groups.

references.title: The Serializer Component
references.summary: The **Serializer** component normalizes objects into arrays (and back) and (de)serializes them through pluggable encoders (`JsonEncoder`, `XmlEncoder`, `YamlEncoder`, `CsvEncoder`).
