Normalizers (`ObjectNormalizer`, `GetSetMethodNormalizer`, `ArrayDenormalizer`, etc.) handle the object⇄array step, while metadata loaders read class-level grouping and discriminator information.

references.title: The PSR-7 Bridge
references.summary: The `symfony/psr-http-message-bridge` package converts HttpFoundation `Request`/`Response` objects to and from PSR-7 messages and back. Conversion in either direction goes through `PsrHttpFactory` (HttpFoundation -> PSR-7) and `HttpFoundationFactory` (PSR-7 -> HttpFoundation), and a separate PSR-7/PSR-17 implementation such as `nyholm/psr7` is required.

references.title: Symfony Bridges (6.0 source)
references.summary: Source tree listing every official Symfony Bridge shipped on the 6.0 branch (Doctrine, Monolog, PsrHttpMessage, ProxyManager, PhpUnit, Twig). Bridges glue Symfony components to third-party libraries; unlike components, they cannot be used without that target library.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Use the Default Directory Structure
references.summary: Recommends following Symfony's default directory layout (`bin/`, `config/packages/`, `config/routes/`, `public/`, `src/Controller/`, `src/Entity/`, `templates/`, `tests/`, `translations/`, `var/cache/`, `var/log/`, `vendor/`). The structure is flat and not coupled to Symfony itself.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Don't Create any Bundle to Organize your Application Logic
references.summary: Application code should live under the `App\` namespace in `src/`, not inside bundles. Bundles are only intended for reusable, distributable code; PHP namespaces are the right tool for organizing application-specific logic.

references.title: How to Override Symfony's default Directory Structure
references.summary: Explains which Symfony directories can be relocated and how: cache and log directories via `Kernel::getCacheDir()`/`getLogDir()` (or `APP_CACHE_DIR`/`APP_LOG_DIR` env vars), templates via `twig.default_path`, translations via `framework.translator.default_path`, public dir via `extra.public-dir`, vendor via `config.vendor-dir`, and dotenv via `extra.runtime.dotenv_path`. The `config/` directory itself is hardcoded and cannot be overridden.

references.title: The Bundle System
references.summary: Explains bundles as plugin-like packages that provide reusable code and features, while application code should no longer be organized as bundles. It shows that bundles are enabled per environment in `config/bundles.php`, with Flex automating that file in default applications.

references.title: The Architecture
references.summary: Quick-tour article that explains the **canonical Symfony directory layout** — `bin/`, `config/`, `public/`, `src/`, `templates/`, `tests/`, `translations/`, `var/`, `vendor/` — and how the kernel boots from `public/index.php` through `Kernel`, `Container`, then to controllers via the routing/firewall/argument-resolver pipeline.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component
references.summary: The HttpKernel component implements the request-handling pipeline. The `HttpKernel::handle()` method takes a `Request` and produces a `Response` by dispatching a fixed sequence of kernel events that allow listeners to short-circuit the flow or modify the result.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component - HttpKernel: Driven by Events
references.summary: Describes the kernel event sequence: `kernel.request`, controller resolution, `kernel.controller`, controller arguments resolution, controller call, optional `kernel.view` (when the controller does not return a `Response`), `kernel.response`, `kernel.terminate`, with `kernel.exception` triggered on any thrown exception.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component - Sub Requests
references.summary: `HttpKernel::handle()` accepts a request type argument: the constants `HttpKernelInterface::MAIN_REQUEST` and `HttpKernelInterface::SUB_REQUEST` distinguish the main request from internal sub-requests (used for example by `render()` Twig calls or fragment URIs).

references.title: Understanding how the Front Controller, Kernel and Environments Work together
references.summary: Explains how `public/index.php` (the front controller) bootstraps autoload and runtime, instantiates the application `Kernel` with an environment and debug flag, then delegates request handling to it. Defines what the Kernel class does and how environments select cache, log and configuration directories.

references.title: Building your own Framework with the MicroKernelTrait - The Methods of a "Micro" Kernel
references.summary: Documents the three methods required by a kernel using `MicroKernelTrait`: `registerBundles()`, `configureContainer()`, and `configureRoutes()`. This is useful for understanding how a compact Symfony kernel defines bundles, services, configuration, and routes.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component - Handling Exceptions: the kernel.exception Event
references.summary: When an exception escapes from the controller, HttpKernel dispatches a `kernel.exception` event with an `ExceptionEvent`. Listeners can call `setResponse()` on the event to convert the exception into a `Response`; if no listener does so, the exception is rethrown by the kernel.

references.title: Built-in Symfony Events - kernel.exception
references.summary: Reference entry for `KernelEvents::EXCEPTION`, dispatched whenever any exception is thrown during request handling. Used by Symfony to format exception responses and by user listeners to map domain exceptions to HTTP responses.

references.title: Events and Event Listeners
references.summary: Framework-level guide for registering listeners and subscribers. Includes a complete `ExceptionListener` example using `ExceptionEvent::getThrowable()` and `setResponse()`, and shows how to choose listener priorities so that custom error handling runs before or after Symfony's defaults.

references.title: The EventDispatcher Component
references.summary: Describes the EventDispatcher component as an implementation of the Mediator/Observer patterns. Covers events, the central dispatcher object, registering listeners with `addListener($eventName, $callable, $priority)` and subscribers via `addSubscriber()`.

references.title: The EventDispatcher Component - Using Event Subscribers
references.summary: Event subscribers implement `EventSubscriberInterface::getSubscribedEvents()`, which returns an array mapping event names to a method name, a `[method, priority]` pair, or a list of such pairs. The dispatcher registers all of those listeners automatically when the subscriber is added.

references.title: The EventDispatcher Component - Stopping Event Flow/Propagation
references.summary: Listeners can stop further propagation of the current event by calling `Event::stopPropagation()`. After dispatch, callers can detect that with `Event::isPropagationStopped()`. Useful for short-circuiting kernel events such as `kernel.request`.

references.title: Events and Event Listeners
references.summary: Framework-level guide. Covers the `kernel.event_listener` and `kernel.event_subscriber` service tags, the optional `event`, `method`, `priority` and `dispatcher` tag attributes, the rule that the `__invoke()` method is called when no `method` is given, and how to register listeners via the `#[AsEventListener]` PHP attribute.

references.title: Built-in Symfony Events
references.summary: Reference of every Symfony kernel event in dispatch order: `kernel.request`, `kernel.controller`, `kernel.controller_arguments`, `kernel.view`, `kernel.response`, `kernel.finish_request`, `kernel.terminate`, `kernel.exception`. Each entry lists the event class and use cases.

references.title: The Generic Event Object
references.summary: The base `Event` class deliberately exposes no payload, encouraging applications to write **typed event subclasses**. As a quick alternative, `GenericEvent` carries a `subject`, an associative `arguments` map, and array-access to those arguments, making it convenient for one-off events or third-party libraries that don't ship dedicated event classes.

references.title: The Immutable Event Dispatcher
references.summary: The **`ImmutableEventDispatcher`** wraps another dispatcher to forbid registering or removing listeners and subscribers at runtime. It is useful in long-running workers / library APIs where the event-listener graph must be **frozen** to prevent late mutations.

references.title: The Traceable Event Dispatcher
references.summary: The **`TraceableEventDispatcher`** wraps another dispatcher and records, for each dispatched event, which listeners were called, in what order, and how long they took (via `Stopwatch`). It powers the WebProfiler's **Events** panel.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices
references.summary: Index of Symfony's official best practices for application development, covering project creation, configuration, business logic, controllers, templates, forms, internationalization, security, web assets, and tests.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Use Environment Variables for Infrastructure Configuration
references.summary: Recommends using environment variables (and `.env` files) for values that change between machines (database URLs, mailer DSNs, etc.), and using parameters for values that change application behavior but stay the same across machines.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Make your Controller Extend the AbstractController Base Controller
references.summary: Recommends extending `AbstractController` so that controllers can use shortcuts such as `render()`, `redirectToRoute()`, `denyAccessUnlessGranted()`, and the `$this->container->get()` accessor for the small allowlist of services exposed by `getSubscribedServices()`.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Use Attributes or Annotations to Configure Routing, Caching, and Security
references.summary: Recommends using PHP attributes (or Doctrine annotations on PHP < 8) such as `#[Route]`, `#[Cache]`, and `#[IsGranted]` directly on controller actions instead of YAML/XML/PHP configuration files, so the configuration lives next to the code it applies to.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Define a Single Firewall
references.summary: Recommends a single firewall for most applications, using `access_control` rules under `security.access_control` to restrict areas, instead of stacking multiple firewalls. Multiple firewalls are appropriate only when the authentication systems are genuinely different (for example a stateless API alongside a session-based UI).

references.title: The Release Process - Development
references.summary: Each minor or major version has a six-month development cycle split into four months of development (new features) and two months of stabilization (bug fixes, ecosystem catch-up). During development, features can be reverted if they will not be ready or stable enough for the final release.

references.title: Maintenance
references.summary: Defines what is allowed in patch (bug-fix) releases: bug fixes with new tests, support for newer PHP versions, translations, ICU updates. Forbids new features, security hardening, BC breaks (except to fix a security issue), new public API, new configuration options, and changes to exception messages.

references.title: Experimental Features
references.summary: Features marked `@experimental` are explicitly excluded from Symfony's BC promise. They can only be experimental for a single minor version (extendable case-by-case), can never be introduced in an LTS release, and the changelog must document any breaking changes plus an upgrade path.

references.title: Our Backward Compatibility Promise
references.summary: Symfony follows Semantic Versioning: only major releases (5.0, 6.0, 7.0...) may break BC, and the BC promise applies to all minor releases of the same major branch. Code marked `@internal`, `@experimental`, classes under `*\Tests\`, and BC breaks required to fix security issues are explicitly excluded from the promise.

references.title: Our Backward Compatibility Promise - Using our Interfaces
references.summary: Application code may type-hint Symfony interfaces and call any of their methods with full BC guarantees, except for interfaces tagged `@internal`. When implementing a Symfony interface, the application is also free to add arguments with defaults, add a return type, etc., without losing BC.

references.title: Our Backward Compatibility Promise - Using our Classes
references.summary: Symfony guarantees BC for instantiating classes, type-hinting against them, calling public methods, accessing public properties, and (when extending) overriding public/protected members. It does *not* guarantee BC when adding new properties or new methods that may collide with subclass code, or when accessing private members via reflection.

references.title: Our Backward Compatibility Promise - Making Code Changes in a Backward Compatible Way
references.summary: Step-by-step recipe for adding a new argument to a public method without breaking BC: declare the argument as a comment in the signature, document it in PHPDoc, retrieve it via `func_num_args()`/`func_get_arg()`, and trigger a deprecation when the argument is missing. The argument is uncommented only in the next major version.

references.title: Experimental Features
references.summary: Features marked `@experimental` are explicitly outside the BC promise. They are limited to a single minor version, are never introduced into an LTS release, and any breaking changes must be explained in the changelog with an upgrade path.

references.title: Conventions - Deprecating Code
references.summary: Defines how Symfony deprecates code: deprecations may only land on the next minor version, never on a brand-new class/method. A `@deprecated` PHPDoc states the version and replacement, and `trigger_deprecation('symfony/package', 'X.Y', 'message')` from `symfony/deprecation-contracts` raises the runtime warning. The CHANGELOG, UPGRADE-X.Y and UPGRADE-X+1.0 files must all be updated in the same pull request.

references.title: Conventions - Removing Deprecated Code
references.summary: Deprecated code can only be removed in the next major version (`6.0`, `7.0`, ...), which means roughly every two years. The removal must be recorded in the CHANGELOG of the affected component.

references.title: symfony/deprecation-contracts v3.0.0 (GitHub)
references.summary: The package that ships the `trigger_deprecation()` function used by Symfony to raise deprecation notices in a way that is silent unless the `E_USER_DEPRECATED` error reporting bit is enabled (typically by the PHPUnit Bridge or by Symfony's own deprecation tooling).

references.title: How to Override any Part of a Bundle
references.summary: Documents the supported override points for third-party bundles: templates under `templates/bundles/<BundleName>/`, services through service decoration or compiler passes, Doctrine mapped superclasses, validation groups (you cannot remove constraints, only add them), and translations by reusing the bundle's translation domain.

references.title: How to Load Service Configuration inside a Bundle
references.summary: Bundles load their own service configuration through a DI Extension class living under `<Bundle>\DependencyInjection\<Bundle>Extension`, whose alias is the snake-cased bundle name without the `Bundle` suffix (for example `AcmeHelloExtension` → `acme_hello`). The `load()` method reads the bundle configuration and registers services in the container.

references.title: How to Simplify Configuration of Multiple Bundles
references.summary: Bundles can implement `PrependExtensionInterface::prepend()` to inject configuration into other bundles before their own extension load runs, which is the canonical way for one bundle to override another bundle's defaults.

references.title: How to Override Symfony's default Directory Structure
references.summary: Explains how to relocate cache, log, templates, translations, public, vendor, and dotenv directories. The `config/` directory is hardcoded and cannot be overridden.

references.title: The Release Process
references.summary: Symfony follows Semantic Versioning with a strict time-based schedule: a patch every month, a minor every six months (May and November), and a major every two years in November of odd years. The same minor cycle has four months of development followed by two months of stabilization.

references.title: The Release Process - Maintenance
references.summary: Each major branch contains five minor versions (X.0..X.4). The last minor of a branch is the LTS. Standard versions receive bug fixes and security fixes for 8 months each, while LTS versions receive 3 years of bug fixes and 4 years of security fixes.

references.title: The Release Process - Backward Compatibility
references.summary: When a major version is being prepared, Symfony develops the new major (e.g. 6.0) and the previous LTS (e.g. 5.4) in parallel: both ship the same new features, but the LTS keeps the deprecated APIs while the new major removes them, so projects can clean up deprecations on the LTS before upgrading.

references.title: Maintenance
references.summary: Patch versions are restricted to bug fixes plus a small set of allowed changes (PHP/OS support, translations, external data updates). New features, security hardening, new configuration options, new dependencies, and changes to exception messages are not allowed in patch releases.

references.title: The Contracts Component
references.summary: Symfony Contracts are small, domain-specific abstraction packages (cache, event-dispatcher, http-client, service, translation, deprecation) extracted from the components. Implementations declare themselves via Composer's `provide` key using the `symfony/*-implementation` convention.

references.title: The DependencyInjection Component
references.summary: Symfony's DependencyInjection container is fully PSR-11 compliant: it implements `Psr\Container\ContainerInterface`. The page documents how to define services in PHP and how to use the container as a PSR-11 container in non-Symfony applications.

references.title: Coding Standards
references.summary: Symfony's coding standards are based on PSR-1, PSR-2, PSR-4 and PSR-12. The `PHP CS Fixer` tool is used to enforce them, and any contribution is expected to pass it before being merged.

references.title: HTTP Client - PSR-18 and PSR-17
references.summary: The Symfony HttpClient ships a `Psr18Client` adapter that implements PSR-18 (`Psr\Http\Client\ClientInterface`) and the PSR-17 message factories so that any PSR-18-compatible code can run on top of Symfony's HttpClient.

references.title: PSRs - PHP-FIG
references.summary: PHP-FIG's catalog of PHP Standards Recommendations. Useful as the normative source for PSR-3 (logger), PSR-6/PSR-16 (cache), PSR-7/PSR-15/PSR-17/PSR-18 (HTTP messages, server middleware, factories, client) and PSR-11 (container) referenced by Symfony components.

references.title: Adapters For Interoperability between PSR-6 and PSR-16 Cache
references.summary: The **Cache** component bridges **PSR-6** (`CacheItemPoolInterface`) and **PSR-16** (`SimpleCacheInterface`) in both directions. `Psr16Cache` wraps a PSR-6 pool to expose the simpler PSR-16 API; `Psr6Cache` (a.k.a. `SimpleCacheAdapter`) wraps a PSR-16 cache to expose the richer PSR-6 API.

references.title: The PSR-7 Bridge
references.summary: The **PSR-7 Bridge** (`symfony/psr-http-message-bridge`) converts between Symfony `Request`/`Response` and **PSR-7** message objects (`RequestInterface`, `ResponseInterface`, `ServerRequestInterface`, `StreamInterface`). It depends on a PSR-17 factory implementation to produce concrete PSR-7 instances.

references.title: Coding Standards - Naming Conventions
references.summary: Defines the casing rules: camelCase for variables and methods, snake_case for configuration parameters and Twig variables, SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE for constants, UpperCamelCase for class/interface/trait/enum names and enum cases, plus suffixes such as `Interface`, `Trait`, `Exception` and the `Abstract` prefix for abstract classes.

references.title: Coding Standards - Service Naming Conventions
references.summary: Service identifiers should match the FQCN of their class (for example `App\EventSubscriber\UserSubscriber`). When several services share a class, the FQCN identifies the main one and the others use lowercase, dot-separated identifiers (for example `fos_user.something.service_name`).

references.title: Conventions - Naming a Method
references.summary: When a class has a single 'main' relation, normalized verbs are used (`get`, `set`, `has`, `all`, `replace`, `remove`, `clear`, `add`, `count`, etc.). For other relations, a suffix is appended (`getXXX`, `setXXX`, `removeXXX`, ...). `setXXX()` may add new entries while `replaceXXX()` must throw on unknown keys.

references.title: Conventions - Naming Commands and Options
references.summary: Symfony Console commands and their options must be named and described using the English imperative mood (for example `run` rather than `runs`, `list` rather than `lists`), to stay consistent with traditional Unix command-line interfaces.

references.title: Best Practices for Reusable Bundles - Bundle Name
references.summary: Defines reusable bundle naming rules: the namespace must follow PSR-4, end with a short name ending in `Bundle`, and the bundle class name should be short, descriptive, prefixed by the vendor or category, and suffixed with `Bundle`. The section also explains the conventional bundle alias, such as `acme_blog` for `AcmeBlogBundle`.

references.title: Controller - A Basic Controller
references.summary: Introduces the usual Symfony controller shape: a controller can be any PHP callable, but is commonly a method on a controller class. The example also shows Symfony's convention of suffixing controller classes with `Controller` and returning a `Response` object from the action method.

references.title: Controller - Mapping a URL to a Controller
references.summary: Shows how Symfony maps a URL path to a controller method by combining route configuration with controller code. This section reinforces the controller action naming pattern and the relationship between routes and controller callables.

references.title: Controller - The Base Controller Class & Services
references.summary: Explains Symfony's optional `AbstractController` base class and the helper methods it provides to controller classes. It is relevant for exam questions about extending the base controller and using helpers such as `render()` in Symfony 6.0 controllers.

references.title: Controller - Generating URLs
references.summary: `AbstractController::generateUrl()` is a helper for generating a URL from a route name and parameter values. It is relevant for controller questions because it avoids manually building route URLs inside controller actions.

references.title: Controller - Rendering Templates
references.summary: `AbstractController::render()` renders a Twig template and wraps the result in a `Response` object. This section is useful for controller questions about returning HTML responses from template rendering.

references.title: Controller - Fetching Services
references.summary: Symfony injects services into controller action arguments by type-hinting a service class or interface. The section also points to `debug:autowiring` for discovering type-hintable services and service binding for exact argument values.

references.title: Controller - The Request object as a Controller Argument
references.summary: Shows how a controller receives the current `Request` object by adding a type-hinted `Request` argument to the action method. This section is useful for understanding how controllers read query parameters, headers, and uploaded files through HttpFoundation request APIs.

references.title: Controller - The Request and Response Object
references.summary: The controller chapter summarizes common `Request` accessors for query parameters, form data, server variables, uploaded files, cookies, and headers. It also explains that header names are normalized when read through HttpFoundation.

references.title: Controller - The Request and Response Object
references.summary: Describes the controller contract of returning a `Response` object and shows how response headers are managed through the `headers` property. It also introduces common controller response helpers and response classes such as JSON and file responses.

references.title: Controller - Returning JSON Response
references.summary: `AbstractController::json()` returns a `JsonResponse` and automatically encodes the provided data. Symfony uses the serializer service when available, otherwise it falls back to PHP's `json_encode()` function.

references.title: Controller - Streaming File Responses
references.summary: `AbstractController::file()` returns a `BinaryFileResponse` for serving files from controller actions. The helper can force downloads, rename files, or display them inline with `ResponseHeaderBag::DISPOSITION_INLINE`.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Setting Cookies
references.summary: Explains how response cookies are created and attached through the response header bag in HttpFoundation. It also covers clearing cookies and using immutable `Cookie` methods such as `withValue()` and `withExpires()` to build cookie instances.

references.title: Sessions - Basic Usage
references.summary: Shows how Symfony exposes the session through the `Request` object and the `RequestStack` service. This is relevant for controller questions about retrieving the session with `$request->getSession()` and avoiding premature session access.

references.title: Sessions - Flash Messages
references.summary: Explains flash messages as one-time session messages intended for user notifications after a request cycle. It shows controller usage with `addFlash()` and template retrieval through `app.flashes()`.

references.title: Controller - Redirecting
references.summary: Covers the controller helpers for route-based and absolute URL redirects. The section also shows status-code customization, route parameters, preserving query strings, redirecting to the current route, and the security caveat for unchecked external redirects.

references.title: How to Forward Requests to another Controller
references.summary: Explains internal controller forwarding with `AbstractController::forward()`. It distinguishes forwarding from browser redirects by describing the internal sub-request and the `Response` returned by the target controller.

references.title: Controller - Managing Errors and 404 Pages
references.summary: Shows how a controller generates a 404 response by throwing the exception created with `createNotFoundException()`. It also explains Symfony's status-code behavior for `HttpException` instances versus generic exceptions.

references.title: How to Upload Files
references.summary: Walks through handling an uploaded file in a Symfony controller after a form is submitted and validated. It covers `FileType`, unmapped form fields, `UploadedFile`, safe filename generation, `guessExtension()`, and moving the uploaded file to a configured directory.

references.title: How to Upload Files - Creating an Uploader Service
references.summary: Shows how to extract upload logic from a controller into a dedicated service. The service receives the target directory and `SluggerInterface`, moves the `UploadedFile`, and returns the generated safe filename.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Rendering a Template Directly from a Route
references.summary: Describes Symfony's built-in `TemplateController` for rendering static templates directly from route configuration. It is relevant for built-in controller questions because the route defaults define the template, status code, cache settings, and optional context without a custom controller action.

references.title: Routing - Redirecting to URLs and Routes Directly from a Route
references.summary: Symfony's built-in `RedirectController` can redirect from route configuration without writing a custom controller action. Route defaults can point to another route or to a path and can control permanence, query preservation, and method preservation.

references.title: Routing - Creating Routes
references.summary: Symfony routes can be configured with PHP attributes or in YAML, XML, and PHP files. All supported routing formats provide the same features and performance, while attributes keep route metadata next to the controller code.

references.title: Routing - Creating Routes as Attributes
references.summary: PHP attributes define routes directly next to controller code in Symfony 6.0 applications. Attribute route loading requires importing the controller directory with `type: attribute`, which lets Symfony scan PHP classes for route metadata.

references.title: Routing - Creating Routes in YAML, XML or PHP Files
references.summary: Symfony can also define routes in separate YAML, XML, or PHP configuration files. These formats associate route names and paths with controller callables, and XML or PHP route files require enabling those formats in the kernel configuration.

references.title: Routing - Route Groups and Prefixes
references.summary: Route groups allow shared routing configuration such as URL prefixes, name prefixes, and requirements. In Symfony 6.0, attributes put common configuration on the controller class, while imported route files configure shared options on the import.

references.title: Routing - Parameters Validation
references.summary: Route parameters accept any value unless a requirement narrows what may match. Symfony uses PHP regular expressions in the `requirements` option, and requirements can also be inlined in the route parameter syntax.

references.title: Routing - Priority Parameter
references.summary: Symfony evaluates routes in their configured order, which can matter when broad parameterized routes overlap specific paths. Attribute and annotation routes can use the integer `priority` parameter so higher-priority routes are evaluated before lower-priority ones.

references.title: Routing - Slash Characters in Route Parameters
references.summary: Route parameters do not match slash characters by default because slashes separate URL path segments. Symfony can allow slashes by using a more permissive requirement such as `.+`, but this interacts with multiple parameters and the special `_format` parameter.

concepts.concept: Set default values to URL parameters

references.title: Routing - Optional Parameters
references.summary: A route parameter is required unless the route defines a default value for it. Symfony supports defaults through controller arguments, the `defaults` route option, or inline parameter syntax such as `{page<\d+>?1}`.

references.title: Routing - Extra Parameters
references.summary: Route defaults may define values that are not part of the route path itself. Symfony stores these extra defaults as request attributes and can pass them to controller arguments when the route matches.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs
references.summary: Symfony routing is bidirectional: routes match incoming URLs and can also generate URLs from route names and parameter values. Extra parameters that are not placeholders in the route path are appended to the generated URL as query string parameters.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs in Controllers
references.summary: Controllers extending `AbstractController` can generate URLs with the `generateUrl()` helper. The helper accepts route parameters and can produce different reference types, including absolute URLs via `UrlGeneratorInterface::ABSOLUTE_URL`.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs in Services
references.summary: Services should generate routes by depending on Symfony's routing generator service. With autowiring, type-hinting `UrlGeneratorInterface` gives access to the `generate()` method outside controllers.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs in Commands
references.summary: Console commands can generate URLs through the router, but they do not have an HTTP request context. Symfony uses `http://localhost/` for absolute URLs unless the router `default_uri` option configures the command request context.

references.title: Routing - Checking if a Route Exists
references.summary: Dynamic code should avoid loading the full route collection just to check whether a route exists. Symfony recommends attempting URL generation and catching `RouteNotFoundException` when the route name is absent.

references.title: Routing - Forcing HTTPS on Generated URLs
references.summary: Generated URLs use the current request scheme by default, or `http` in console contexts. Symfony routes can require HTTPS with the `schemes` option, which also enforces the scheme for incoming requests.

references.title: Routing - Redirecting to URLs and Routes Directly from a Route
references.summary: Symfony can trigger redirects from routing configuration by using `RedirectController` instead of writing a controller action. Redirect defaults can target a route or path, choose permanent redirects, preserve query parameters, and preserve the HTTP method.

references.title: Routing - Redirecting URLs with Trailing Slashes
references.summary: Symfony normalizes trailing slashes for routes on `GET` and `HEAD` requests. When the requested slash variant differs from the configured route path, Symfony redirects permanently to the matching variant.

references.title: Routing - Special Parameters
references.summary: Symfony routes recognize special parameters such as `_controller`, `_format`, `_fragment`, and `_locale`. These parameters influence controller selection, request format, URL fragments, and locale handling.

references.title: Routing - Getting the Route Name and Parameters
references.summary: Symfony stores matched routing metadata in request attributes. Controllers, services, and templates can read `_route`, `_route_params`, and other attributes from the current `Request`.

references.title: Routing - Stateless Routes
references.summary: Routes can declare `stateless: true` to indicate that the session should not be used for matching that request. In debug mode, unexpected session usage on a stateless route triggers an `UnexpectedSessionUsageException`; otherwise Symfony logs a warning.

references.title: Routing - Sub-Domain Routing
references.summary: Routes can restrict matching to a specific HTTP host with the `host` option. Host patterns may include parameters, defaults, and requirements, and tests must send the expected `Host` header for subdomain routes to match.

references.title: Routing - Matching Expressions
references.summary: Routes can use the `condition` option for arbitrary matching logic written with Symfony ExpressionLanguage syntax. Matching expressions can access the routing request context, the current Request object, and environment variables.

references.title: Routing - Matching HTTP Methods
references.summary: Symfony routes match any HTTP verb by default. The `methods` option restricts a route to specific verbs such as `GET`, `HEAD`, or `PUT`, and Symfony forms can use the `_method` hidden field for method override.

references.title: How to Work with the User's Locale - The Locale and the URL
references.summary: Symfony can derive the current request locale from the special `_locale` route parameter. When a localized URL such as `/fr/contact` matches, the locale is automatically set on the Request and can be read with `Request::getLocale()`.

references.title: Routing - Localized Routes (i18n)
references.summary: Localized routes let a single route define different paths, prefixes, or hosts per locale. When Symfony matches a localized route, it automatically uses the matched locale for the current request.

references.title: Routing - Debugging Routes
references.summary: Symfony provides console commands for diagnosing routing behavior. `debug:router` lists application routes in evaluation order, while `router:match` shows which route matches a given URL.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Twig Templating Language
references.summary: Introduces Twig as Symfony's template engine and explains the core syntax constructs for output, logic, and comments. It also covers why PHP code is not written directly inside Twig templates and how filters, tags, and functions support template logic.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Template Naming
references.summary: Symfony recommends snake_case file and directory names for templates and a two-extension naming convention. The first extension (e.g. `html`, `xml`, `txt`) declares the final format the template will produce, while the second `.twig` extension marks the file as a Twig template.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - The App Global Variable
references.summary: Symfony injects an `app` variable (`Symfony\Bridge\Twig\AppVariable`) into every Twig template automatically. It exposes `app.user`, `app.request`, `app.session`, `app.flashes`, `app.environment`, `app.debug` and `app.token` to read the current security, request, session and environment context from any template.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Output Escaping
references.summary: Explains Symfony's default Twig autoescaping behavior and how it protects rendered templates from cross-site scripting attacks. It also identifies when the `raw` filter disables escaping for trusted HTML content.

references.title: Twig Configuration Reference (TwigBundle)
references.summary: Configures the **TwigBundle**: `default_path` (where to look for templates), `paths` (additional namespaces), `auto_reload` (recompile templates when sources change in dev), `strict_variables` (fail on undefined variables), and `autoescape_service` (replace the default file-extension-based auto-escape strategy with a custom service).

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Template Inheritance and Layouts
references.summary: Describes Twig template inheritance, parent layouts, child templates, and block overriding in Symfony applications. It explains the recommended multi-level layout structure and the restriction that child templates using `extends` must place content inside blocks.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - The App Global Variable
references.summary: Covers Symfony's automatically injected `app` Twig variable and the request, session, user, environment, debug, token, and flash data it exposes. This is relevant for templates that need application context without explicit controller variables.

references.title: How to Inject Variables Automatically into all Templates
references.summary: Explains how to define custom Twig globals with the `twig.globals` configuration option. It also covers the tradeoff of exposing services as globals, because such services are instantiated when Twig loads.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - Functions
references.summary: Documents Symfony-provided Twig functions such as `path()`, `url()`, `asset()`, `csrf_token()`, `render()`, and `controller()`. These functions connect templates to routing, assets, security, and controller fragments.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - Filters
references.summary: Documents Symfony-provided Twig filters such as `trans`, `humanize`, `yaml_encode`, `yaml_dump`, and debugging-oriented filters. It clarifies how filters transform a value before rendering or further processing.

references.title: How to Write a custom Twig Extension
references.summary: A custom **Twig extension** subclasses `AbstractExtension` and exposes filters via `getFilters()` (`new TwigFilter(...)`), functions via `getFunctions()` (`new TwigFunction(...)`), tests via `getTests()`, and operators via `getOperators()`. Symfony auto-registers any service tagged `twig.extension` when autoconfigure is on.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Including Templates
references.summary: Explains how to extract repeated Twig markup into template fragments and include those fragments with `include()`. It also covers variable visibility and passing renamed variables to included templates.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Twig Templating Language
references.summary: Introduces Twig control syntax in Symfony templates, including the `{% ... %}` construct used for conditionals and loops. It gives examples of template logic such as `if` blocks and explains that PHP code is not run directly inside Twig templates.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Linking to Pages
references.summary: Explains how Symfony templates generate links from route names and parameters with `path()` and `url()`. It distinguishes relative paths from absolute URLs and shows how route changes are reflected automatically in generated template links.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Rendering a Template in Controllers
references.summary: Shows how controllers render Twig templates with `AbstractController::render()` and pass variables into the template context. It also contrasts `render()` returning a `Response` with `renderView()` returning template contents.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Embedding Controllers
references.summary: From a Twig template, the `render()` and `controller()` functions execute another Symfony controller as a sub-request and inject its response, which is different from the `include()` function that only inlines another template's contents. The dedicated `/_fragment` URL is configured under `framework.fragments.path` to serve fragments referenced via `controller()`.

references.title: Translations - Translations in Templates
references.summary: Explains Twig translation support through the `trans` tag and `trans` filter, including parameters, domains, locales, and escaping differences. This section is central for rendering translated template text correctly.

references.title: Translations - Message Format
references.summary: Explains variable placeholders and plural forms in translated messages. It covers ICU MessageFormat support and the `%count%` fallback mechanism for selecting plural forms when ICU syntax is not used.

references.title: ICU MessageFormat — Pluralization
references.summary: ICU MessageFormat uses a **`{count, plural, ...}`** select to handle plural forms — categories include `zero`, `one`, `two`, `few`, `many`, `other`, plus exact matches like `=0` or `=1`. Symfony picks the right branch based on the supplied numeric parameter, taking the locale's plural rules into account.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Twig Templating Language
references.summary: Explains that Twig uses `{{ ... }}` to display variables or evaluated expressions inside template output. This is the base syntax for inserting dynamic values into rendered HTML or other text formats.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Linking to CSS, JavaScript and Image Assets
references.summary: Explains how the `asset()` Twig function generates public paths for static assets such as images, stylesheets, and scripts. It also covers portability across host roots or subdirectories and using `absolute_url()` when absolute asset URLs are needed.

references.title: Front-end Tools: Handling CSS & JavaScript
references.summary: Overview of the **front-end stack** Symfony recommends: **AssetMapper** (no Node.js required, importmaps + JSDelivr), **Webpack Encore** (Node.js / Webpack), **StimulusBundle**, **UX components**, and the legacy **Asset** component. Compares trade-offs and links the dedicated guides for each tool.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - The Dump Twig Utilities
references.summary: Explains Symfony's Twig integration for the VarDumper component through the `dump` tag and `dump()` function. It distinguishes dumping to the Web Debug Toolbar from dumping into page output and notes that these utilities are only available in `dev` and `test` environments.

references.title: Forms - Creating Forms in Controllers
references.summary: Controllers extending AbstractController can build a form fluently with createFormBuilder($data) and chain ->add('field', SomeType::class) calls before calling getForm(). Without AbstractController, the form.factory service exposes a createBuilder() method that does the same thing.

references.title: Forms - Creating Form Classes
references.summary: Reusable forms are defined in dedicated classes that extend AbstractType and implement buildForm() to declare fields. AbstractController provides createForm(FormType::class, $data) to instantiate such a form, and binding the form to a class is usually completed by setting the data_class option.

references.title: Forms - Processing Forms
references.summary: The recommended Symfony pattern uses a single controller action that calls $form->handleRequest($request) and then guards work behind isSubmitted() && isValid(). On a successful submission the controller persists the data and redirects to follow the POST/redirect/GET pattern.

references.title: Forms - Changing the Action and HTTP Method
references.summary: Form action URLs and HTTP methods are configured with setAction()/setMethod() on the builder, with the action and method options at createForm() time, or directly in Twig via form() / form_start(). When the form's method is PUT, PATCH or DELETE Symfony submits over POST and adds a hidden _method field that requires http_method_override to be enabled.

references.title: How to Use the submit() Function to Handle Form Submissions
references.summary: Form::submit() is an alternative to handleRequest() that gives explicit control over the submitted payload, which is useful for JSON or PATCH style requests. Its second boolean argument $clearMissing controls whether missing fields are set to null (true) or kept untouched (false), as needed for PATCH semantics.

references.title: How to Choose Validation Groups Based on the Clicked Button
references.summary: When a form has multiple submit buttons, the validation groups can depend on which button was clicked: pass a callable to the `validation_groups` option of the **submit** button, returning the appropriate group(s). The `Form::isClicked()` check on the parent form decides which group set is applied.

references.title: How to Disable the Validation of Submitted Data
references.summary: Setting the form's `validation_groups` option to **`false`** disables validation entirely on submission — the form's data is mapped back to the model but no constraints are evaluated. Useful for "draft" submissions or wizard "back" buttons. Pass `[]` instead to keep validation on but limit it to the empty group set.

references.title: How to Dynamically Configure Form Validation Groups
references.summary: When the validation-group choice depends on logic that doesn't fit a closure, set `validation_groups` to a **callable service** (FQCN string or `\Closure(FormInterface): array|string`). The service typically implements `__invoke(FormInterface $form): array` and is resolved through the DI container.

references.title: Configuring Validation Groups in Forms
references.summary: When the underlying class declares constraints across **validation groups**, the form must list them via the `validation_groups` option (string, array, or callable). Without a callable, the value is a static set; a closure receives the `FormInterface` and may return groups dynamically based on submitted data.

references.title: How to Create a Custom Form Field Type
references.summary: Symfony form types are PHP classes that implement FormTypeInterface, with AbstractType being the usual base class for application form types. Custom types can extend built-in behavior with getParent(), define fields in buildForm(), expose template variables in buildView(), and configure options with configureOptions().

references.title: How to Customize Form Rendering - Form Rendering Functions
references.summary: Twig form helpers can render an entire form or individual form parts, including labels, widgets, help text, and errors. When rendering fields manually, form_rest() is the recommended way to output any fields that have not already been rendered, including the automatic CSRF token field.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes
references.summary: Form themes control the Twig blocks used to render Symfony forms and can be applied globally or to a single form. Symfony provides built-in themes such as form_div_layout.html.twig and Bootstrap themes, and multiple themes can be layered with later themes overriding earlier ones.

references.title: Bootstrap 4 Form Theme
references.summary: Built-in Twig theme that renders forms with **Bootstrap 4** markup. Activate it project-wide by setting `twig.form_themes: ["bootstrap_4_layout.html.twig"]`, or per-template with `{% form_theme form 'bootstrap_4_layout.html.twig' %}`. A horizontal variant (`bootstrap_4_horizontal_layout.html.twig`) is also shipped.

references.title: How to Implement CSRF Protection - CSRF Protection in Symfony Forms
references.summary: Symfony Form component forms include CSRF tokens by default and validate them automatically. The token is stored in a hidden field named _token by default, while csrf_protection, csrf_field_name, and csrf_token_id allow per-form customization.

references.title: How to Upload Files
references.summary: Symfony file uploads commonly use a FileType field marked as unmapped so the uploaded file is not read from or written directly to the entity property. After a valid submission, the UploadedFile object is retrieved from the form field, moved to a configured directory, and the entity stores the generated file name rather than the file contents.

references.title: Form Types Reference - Supported Field Types
references.summary: Symfony forms are composed of fields built from field types such as TextType and ChoiceType. The form types reference groups the native field types available in Symfony, including text, choice, date/time, boolean, file, hidden, button, and compound form types.

references.title: ChoiceType Field - Field Options
references.summary: ChoiceType lets the user pick one or several values from a list. Its `choices`, `multiple`, `expanded`, `choice_label`, `choice_value`, and `placeholder` options control the available choices, the rendered widget (select, radio buttons or checkboxes), and the empty/default option.

references.title: CollectionType Field - Field Options
references.summary: CollectionType renders a collection of fields based on the `entry_type` option. The `allow_add`, `allow_delete`, `by_reference`, and `prototype` options control whether new items can be appended, existing items removed, how the underlying object is updated, and whether a JavaScript-friendly prototype row is exposed.

references.title: EntityType Field - Field Options
references.summary: EntityType is a specialized ChoiceType backed by Doctrine. Its required `class` option points at the entity, while `choice_label`, `query_builder`, and `em` configure the displayed label, the choices to load, and the entity manager used to retrieve them.

references.title: DateType Field - Field Options
references.summary: DateType renders a date field as either three select widgets, three text inputs, or a single HTML5 date input. The `widget`, `format`, `html5`, and `input` options control the rendered widget, the date format used by `IntlDateFormatter`, whether HTML5 types are emitted, and the format of the value stored on the underlying object.

references.title: BirthdayType Field
references.summary: A specialization of `DateType` for **birth dates**. The defaults narrow the year range to the **last 120 years**, which is the only behavioural difference from `DateType`. Like its parent, it can be rendered as three select boxes or one/three text boxes depending on the `widget` option.

references.title: ButtonType Field
references.summary: A simple, non-responsive `<button>` element. It is the parent of the `SubmitType` and `ResetType` button fields and is **not mapped** to a property of the underlying data object — buttons exist only in the form, not in the model.

references.title: CheckboxType Field
references.summary: Renders a single `<input type="checkbox">`. The submitted value becomes `true` when the box is checked and `false` otherwise. The `false_values` option lists the strings that should be interpreted as the unchecked state (default `[null]`).

references.title: ColorType Field
references.summary: Text field rendered as `<input type="color">`. The `html5` option (default `false`) toggles a strict format check: when `true`, only valid HTML5 simple colors (lowercase 7-character hex like `#abcdef`) are accepted, otherwise any string passes through.

references.title: CountryType Field
references.summary: A specialized `ChoiceType` listing every world country. The choice keys are localized country names in the user's locale and the values are **ISO 3166-1 alpha-2** codes by default. Setting the `alpha3` option to `true` switches the values to the three-letter alpha-3 codes.

references.title: CurrencyType Field
references.summary: A specialized `ChoiceType` listing currencies as **3-letter ISO 4217** codes (e.g. `EUR`, `USD`). Choice labels are localized currency names rendered in the user's locale; the choices come from the **Intl** component, not a hard-coded list.

references.title: DateIntervalType Field
references.summary: Field for capturing a span of time as a `DateInterval` (years, months, days, hours, minutes, seconds). The `widget` option (`single_text`, `text`, `choice`) controls rendering, and `with_*` flags (e.g. `with_years`, `with_hours`, `with_invert`) toggle which interval components are exposed. The `input` option chooses the model type (`dateinterval` default, `string`, `array`).

references.title: DateTimeType Field
references.summary: Field that combines a date and time into one form. It can render as one `<input type="datetime-local">`, a single text box, or five select fields, controlled by `date_widget` and `time_widget`. The `format` option uses **`IntlDateFormatter`** constants (e.g. `IntlDateFormatter::SHORT`) when `widget` is `single_text`; the `input` option chooses the PHP model type (`datetime` default, `datetime_immutable`, `string`, `timestamp`, `array`).

references.title: EmailType Field
references.summary: Text field rendered as **`<input type="email">`**. It does **not** validate the submitted address by itself — pair it with the `Email` validation constraint when validation is required. It inherits options from its parent `TextType`.

references.title: FormType Field
references.summary: Root form type that **every other form type** inherits from. Its options (e.g. `data_class`, `empty_data`, `mapped`, `required`, `label`, `attr`, `constraints`, `error_bubbling`, `inherit_data`, `trim`, `validation_groups`) are therefore available on all form types.

references.title: IntegerType Field
references.summary: Renders an HTML5 `<input type="number">` and parses the submitted string into a PHP `int`. The `rounding_mode` option chooses between modes such as `IntegerType::ROUND_DOWN` (the default truncation), `ROUND_FLOOR`, `ROUND_HALF_UP`, etc.; `grouping` controls thousands separators in display.

references.title: LanguageType Field
references.summary: A specialized `ChoiceType` listing **Unicode language identifiers** (e.g. `fr`, `zh-Hant`). With `alpha3: true` it instead lists ISO 639-2 alpha-3 codes (`fra`); `choice_self_translation` (default `false`) renders each label in its own language instead of the user's locale.

references.title: LocaleType Field
references.summary: A specialized `ChoiceType` listing all **locales** (language+region pairs such as `en_GB`, `fr_FR`). The labels are localized into the user's locale and the choices come from the Intl component.

references.title: MoneyType Field
references.summary: Renders an HTML5 `<input type="number">` (or text input when `html5: false`) for monetary input. The `currency` option (default `EUR`) decides which currency symbol is rendered next to the field; `scale` (default `2`) sets the number of fraction digits, and `divisor` (default `1`) lets you store an integer cents value while displaying the correct decimal amount.

references.title: NumberType Field
references.summary: Renders a text input for numeric data. Options include `scale` (number of fraction digits, default `null` = locale dependent), `rounding_mode` (`NumberType::ROUND_HALF_UP`, `ROUND_DOWN`, `ROUND_FLOOR`, …), `grouping` (thousands separators), and `html5` (when `true`, renders `<input type="number">` instead of a text input).

references.title: PasswordType Field
references.summary: Renders an `<input type="password">`. By default the submitted value is **not re-rendered** when validation fails; the `always_empty` option (default `true`) controls this behaviour. The field inherits all `TextType` options.

references.title: PercentType Field
references.summary: Renders a text input for percentage values. The `type` option chooses between `fractional` (default — `0.95` is rendered as `95%`), `integer` (`95` is rendered as `95%`), or `decimal`. The `symbol` option (default `%`) controls the suffix and `scale` sets the number of fraction digits.

references.title: RadioType Field
references.summary: Renders a single `<input type="radio">`. It inherits from `CheckboxType` and is rarely used directly — `ChoiceType` with `expanded: true` and `multiple: false` builds groups of radio buttons automatically.

references.title: RangeType Field
references.summary: Field rendered as an HTML5 `<input type="range">` slider. The `attr` option is typically used to pass the `min`, `max`, and `step` HTML attributes that define the slider's allowed range and increment.

references.title: ResetType Field
references.summary: A button rendered as `<button type="reset">` that resets all fields of the surrounding form to their original values. It inherits from `ButtonType` and is **not mapped** to the underlying data.

references.title: SearchType Field
references.summary: Text field rendered as **`<input type="search">`** (the search variant of a text input — some browsers add a clear "x" icon and dedicated search styling). It otherwise behaves like `TextType`.

references.title: TelType Field
references.summary: Text field rendered as **`<input type="tel">`**. The submitted value is **not validated** — phone-number formats vary too widely between countries — so pair the field with a custom validator if validation is required.

references.title: TextType Field
references.summary: The most basic input field, rendered as `<input type="text">`. It is the parent of many specialized text-based types (`EmailType`, `PasswordType`, `UrlType`, `SearchType`, `TelType`, `ColorType`, `RangeType`, `TextareaType`).

references.title: TextareaType Field
references.summary: Renders an HTML `<textarea>`. It inherits from `TextType` and is therefore a multi-line variant of the basic text field.

references.title: TimeType Field
references.summary: Field for time input. The `widget` option chooses between `single_text` (a `<input type="time">` or text), `text` (two/three text inputs), or `choice` (two/three select boxes). `with_seconds` (default `false`) toggles the seconds component, and `input` chooses the PHP model type (`datetime` default, `datetime_immutable`, `string`, `timestamp`, `array`).

references.title: TimezoneType Field
references.summary: A specialized `ChoiceType` listing every IANA timezone identifier (e.g. `Europe/Paris`). With `intl: true` (default `false`), the choices come from the **Intl** component; with `intl: false`, they come from `DateTimeZone::listIdentifiers()`.

references.title: UrlType Field
references.summary: Text field rendered as `<input type="url">`. The `default_protocol` option (default `"http"`) is **prepended automatically** to submitted values that lack a protocol; setting it to `null` disables that behaviour.

references.title: WeekType Field
references.summary: Field for **ISO 8601 week numbers** such as `1984-W05`. The `widget` option chooses between `single_text` (one `<input type="week">`), `text` (two text boxes), or `choice` (two select boxes). `input` chooses the model type (`array` default, `string`, plus the years range supplied via the `years` option for the choice widget).

references.title: FileType Field
references.summary: Renders an `<input type="file">` for **file uploads**. It binds to a `Symfony\Component\HttpFoundation\File\UploadedFile` (or `null`). Pair it with the `File` or `Image` validation constraint to enforce mime types, size, and dimensions; the `multiple` option lets the field accept several files at once.

references.title: HiddenType Field
references.summary: Renders an `<input type="hidden">`. Used to ship form data that should not be edited by the user (e.g. an entity id). The `error_bubbling` option defaults to `true` for this type, so validation errors attach to the parent form rather than the hidden field itself.

references.title: RepeatedType Field
references.summary: A field group that **renders two identical fields** whose values must match — typical for password/email confirmation. The `type` option (default `TextType`) chooses the underlying type and the `first_options` / `second_options` options configure each rendering. The validation message comes from `invalid_message`.

references.title: SubmitType Field
references.summary: A button rendered as `<button type="submit">`. Inherits from `ButtonType` and is **not mapped** to any property of the underlying data. The button exposes `isClicked()` (`Symfony\Component\Form\ClickableInterface`), which is useful when a form has several submit buttons.

references.title: How to Use Data Transformers
references.summary: Data transformers convert a single form field value between the model representation and the representation displayed or submitted by the form. Symfony supports CallbackTransformer for simple transformations and custom DataTransformerInterface implementations for reusable or more complex conversion logic.

references.title: How to Use Data Transformers - Creating the Transformer
references.summary: A reusable data transformer implements `Symfony\Component\Form\DataTransformerInterface` with two methods: `transform()` converts the model value into the value rendered by the form, and `reverseTransform()` converts the submitted value back into the model value. Throwing a `TransformationFailedException` from `reverseTransform()` triggers a validation error displayed via the field's `invalid_message` option.

references.title: How to Use Data Transformers - About Model and View Transformers
references.summary: A form has three data formats (model, normalized and view) and two kinds of transformers wired between them. Model transformers are added with `addModelTransformer()` and convert between model and norm data; view transformers are added with `addViewTransformer()` and convert between norm and view data.

references.title: When and How to Use Data Mappers
references.summary: **Data mappers** are the bridge between the form's view data and the underlying data class. Implement `DataMapperInterface::mapDataToForms()` (model → child fields) and `mapForms()`/`mapFormsToData()` (child fields → model) to control the mapping when the default behaviour does not fit (immutable value objects, arrays, custom shapes).

references.title: Form Events
references.summary: The Symfony Form component dispatches events during form population and submission so applications can alter data or fields at specific workflow points. PRE_SET_DATA and POST_SET_DATA run around setData(), while PRE_SUBMIT, SUBMIT, and POST_SUBMIT run during handleRequest() or submit().

references.title: Form Events - The Form Workflow
references.summary: Two events run around `Form::setData()`: `FormEvents::PRE_SET_DATA` fires at the beginning (with `null` as model, normalized and view data) and `FormEvents::POST_SET_DATA` fires at the end. Three events run during `handleRequest()` or `submit()`: `FormEvents::PRE_SUBMIT`, `FormEvents::SUBMIT` and `FormEvents::POST_SUBMIT`. During `PRE_SET_DATA`, `Form::setData()` is locked and `FormEvent::setData()` must be used instead.

references.title: How to Dynamically Modify Forms Using Form Events - Customizing your Form Based on the Underlying Data
references.summary: A form can be reshaped depending on the model data passed to it by listening to `FormEvents::PRE_SET_DATA` and adding or removing fields on the form returned by `FormEvent::getForm()`. This is the recommended way to make a field appear only when the underlying object is new (or has any other state-dependent shape).

references.title: How to Dynamically Modify Forms Using Form Events - Dynamic Generation for Submitted Forms
references.summary: When the form shape depends on the value the user just submitted, an event listener should be attached to `FormEvents::PRE_SUBMIT`. At that point, `FormEvent::getData()` returns the raw submitted array, which the listener can use to add or replace fields on the form before binding.

references.title: How to Create a Form Type Extension
references.summary: Form type extensions modify existing form field types globally or add shared behavior to several related types. A type extension usually extends AbstractTypeExtension, declares affected types with getExtendedTypes(), and is registered as a service tagged with form.type_extension.

references.title: How to Create a Form Type Extension - Defining the Form Type Extension
references.summary: A form type extension typically extends `AbstractTypeExtension` (or implements `FormTypeExtensionInterface`) and must implement the static `getExtendedTypes()` method, which returns an iterable of fully qualified form type class names. Extensions can override `buildForm()`, `buildView()`, `configureOptions()` and `finishView()` to add behavior to every instance of the targeted types.

references.title: How to Create a Form Type Extension - Registering your Form Type Extension as a Service
references.summary: Form type extensions must be registered as services tagged with `form.type_extension`. With the default `services.yaml` configuration, autoconfiguration applies the tag automatically; an optional `priority` attribute (default `0`) controls the order in which multiple extensions for the same type are loaded.

references.title: Creating a custom Type Guesser
references.summary: A **type guesser** infers the form type, required flag, max length and pattern from class metadata — Symfony ships a guesser based on Validator constraints, but you can register your own by implementing `FormTypeGuesserInterface` and tagging it `form.type_guesser`. Each guess returns a `TypeGuess`/`ValueGuess` carrying a `Guess::HIGH_CONFIDENCE` / `MEDIUM_CONFIDENCE` / `LOW_CONFIDENCE` score; the guess with the highest confidence wins.

references.title: Validation
references.summary: Symfony's Validator component validates PHP objects against declarative constraints based on the JSR-303 Bean Validation specification. Constraints can be defined via PHP attributes, YAML or XML files in config/validator/, or PHP code via a static loadValidatorMetadata() method on the class.

references.title: Validation - Using the Validator Service
references.summary: The validator service implements ValidatorInterface and is autowired by its type-hint. Calling validate() on an object reads its mapped constraints and returns a ConstraintViolationList; an empty list means the object is valid, while a non-empty list contains one ConstraintViolation per failed assertion.

references.title: Validation - Validation Callables
references.summary: Validation::createCallable() and Validation::createIsValidCallable() turn a list of constraints into a closure usable wherever a validation callback is expected, such as Console question validators or OptionsResolver value validators. The first throws ValidationFailedException on failure, the second returns false.

references.title: The Validator Component
references.summary: Outside Symfony applications, Validation::createValidator() builds a Validator that can validate scalar values and arrays directly against a list of Constraint objects. Validating mapped objects requires configuring the validator with metadata loaders via Validation::createValidatorBuilder().

references.title: The Validator Component - Metadata
references.summary: ClassMetadata represents all configured constraints for a class. The loadValidatorMetadata(ClassMetadata $metadata) static method registers constraints with addPropertyConstraint(), addGetterConstraint(), and addConstraint(), corresponding to property, getter, and class targets.

references.title: The Validator Component - Loading Resources
references.summary: The ValidatorBuilder configures loaders that read constraint metadata: StaticMethodLoader (via addMethodMapping), YamlFileLoader, XmlFileLoader, and AnnotationLoader (enabled with enableAnnotationMapping). Loaders chain via LoaderChain, results can be cached with a PSR-6 pool through setMappingCache(), and a custom factory can be injected via setMetadataFactory().

references.title: Validation Constraints Reference
references.summary: Index of constraints shipped with Symfony, grouped by category: basic, string, comparison, number, date, choice, file, financial/identifier, and other constraints. Each constraint links to a dedicated reference page describing its options and behavior.

references.title: NotBlank
references.summary: Validates that a value is not equal to a blank string, an empty array, false, or (by default) null. The allowNull option flips the null behavior so null values are accepted, which is what differentiates it from NotNull.

references.title: NotNull
references.summary: Validates that a value is strictly not equal to null. Unlike NotBlank, an empty string or empty array passes NotNull because they are not null.

references.title: IsTrue
references.summary: Validates that a value is exactly true, the integer 1, or the string '1'. It is most powerful when applied to a getter such as isPasswordSafe() so the assertion runs against the method's return value.

references.title: Type
references.summary: Validates that the value matches a given PHP type. The type option accepts a fully qualified class name, a PHP datatype checked by an is_*() function (string, int, array, etc.), or a ctype name (alpha, digit, etc.); it can also be an array of accepted types.

references.title: Length
references.summary: Validates that the string length is between min and max, or exactly equal to the exactly option (which is the default option). It also exposes a charset option used with mb_check_encoding() and mb_strlen() to compute the length.

references.title: Email
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid email address. The mode option chooses between html5, strict (RFC 5322 via egulias/email-validator), and the deprecated loose pattern; the default mode is configured by framework.validation.email_validation_mode.

references.title: Url
references.summary: Validates that a value is a syntactically valid URL string. It does not perform DNS resolution or HTTP requests; checking that the host actually exists requires PHP's checkdnsrr().

references.title: Regex
references.summary: Validates that a value matches a regular expression pattern. The match option (default true) can be flipped to false to assert that a string must NOT match the pattern.

references.title: Choice
references.summary: Ensures the value is one of a fixed set of valid choices, supplied either via the choices option or a callback (a method name, a [Class, method] pair, or a Closure). With multiple: true the input is treated as an array and min/max bound the number of selected items.

references.title: Range
references.summary: Validates that a number or DateTime is between min and max. When both bounds are set the message is notInRangeMessage; min/max may also be date strings accepted by the DateTime constructor (including relative formats), and minPropertyPath/maxPropertyPath read bounds from another property of the same object.

references.title: Count
references.summary: Validates that an array or Countable object contains a number of elements between min and max (or exactly equal to a target). Unlike Length, Count operates on element count rather than string length.

references.title: Collection
references.summary: Applies different constraints to specific keys of an array or array-like object (Traversable + ArrayAccess). The fields option maps keys to nested constraints, and allowMissingFields and allowExtraFields control whether absent or unexpected keys are accepted.

references.title: All
references.summary: Applies the same set of constraints to every element of an array or Traversable. Each entry must individually satisfy every nested constraint.

references.title: Unique
references.summary: Validates that all elements of an array or Traversable are unique. Comparison is strict by default (so '7' and 7 are different); the normalizer option can change how elements are compared.

references.title: UniqueEntity
references.summary: Validates that one or more fields of a Doctrine entity are unique among all rows of that entity's table. It is a class-level constraint shipped by symfony/doctrine-bridge and lives in Symfony\Bridge\Doctrine\Validator\Constraints.

references.title: AtLeastOneOf
references.summary: Combines several constraints with logical OR semantics: the value is valid as soon as at least one nested constraint passes, and validation stops on the first satisfied constraint.

references.title: Compound
references.summary: Cannot be used directly. Subclasses extend Compound and implement getConstraints() to bundle a reusable set of constraints (for example a PasswordRequirements rule combining NotBlank, Type, Length, and NotCompromisedPassword) that can be applied like any other constraint.

references.title: File
references.summary: Validates that a value is a string path to an existing file or a Symfony File/UploadedFile object. Common options include maxSize (with k/M/Ki/Mi suffixes) and mimeTypes, with dedicated messages for size, mime-type, upload errors, and missing files.

references.title: NotCompromisedPassword
references.summary: Checks that a password has not appeared in haveibeenpwned.com data breaches. It uses k-anonymity: the password is hashed with SHA-1 and only the first bytes are sent, so the raw password never leaves the application.

references.title: Email - mode option
references.summary: The Email mode option selects the validation algorithm: html5 (matches the HTML5 input[type=email] regex), strict (RFC 5322 via egulias/email-validator), or loose (legacy simple regex). The constants Email::VALIDATION_MODE_* expose these values.

references.title: Valid
references.summary: Cascades validation into an embedded object so its own constraints are evaluated together with the parent. Without Valid on a related property, validating the parent leaves the child's constraints unchecked; the traverse option (default true) controls whether values inside a Traversable are traversed.

references.title: Callback
references.summary: Runs custom validation logic by invoking a method, an array callable, or a Closure during validation. The callback receives an ExecutionContextInterface (and the validated object as a first argument when static or a closure) and adds violations through the context's buildViolation() API; global functions and service methods are not supported.

references.title: Date
references.summary: Validates that a value is a string (or stringable object) following the YYYY-MM-DD format. Empty values are considered valid, so combine it with NotBlank or NotNull when the field is required.

references.title: DateTime
references.summary: Validates that a value is a string matching a DateTime::createFromFormat() pattern; the default format is Y-m-d H:i:s. The format option accepts any pattern supported by DateTime::createFromFormat().

references.title: Time
references.summary: Validates that a value is a string in the HH:MM:SS format. Empty values are accepted, so pair it with NotBlank or NotNull if the field cannot be missing.

references.title: Image
references.summary: Extends the File constraint with image-specific defaults (mimeTypes set to image/*) and additional options for width, height, pixel count, and aspect ratio (minWidth, maxWidth, minHeight, maxHeight, minPixels, maxPixels, minRatio, maxRatio). It also provides allowLandscape, allowPortrait, allowSquare, and a detectCorrupted option that uses imagecreatefromstring() to flag corrupted files.

references.title: Expression
references.summary: Runs a Symfony ExpressionLanguage expression and considers the value valid only when the expression evaluates to true (using == not ===). Inside the expression, this is the validated object and value is the property value when the constraint is attached to a property; the values option injects extra named variables.

references.title: Sequentially
references.summary: Applies a list of constraints step-by-step on the same value and stops at the first failure. This avoids cascading useless or expensive checks (such as Regex or a remote service call) when an earlier constraint like Type or Length already failed.

references.title: Ip
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid IP address. The version option selects the format: 4 (default) or 6, plus combined forms such as all, and variants suffixed with _no_priv, _no_res, or _public to exclude private, reserved, or both ranges.

references.title: Json
references.summary: Validates that a string contains syntactically valid JSON. It only checks parse-ability and does not validate the structure or content against any schema.

references.title: Uuid
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid RFC 4122 UUID. The strict option (default true) enforces the canonical 8-4-4-4-12 hyphenated format, and versions restricts the accepted UUID versions (1-6, with constants like Uuid::V4_RANDOM); empty values are considered valid.

references.title: EqualTo
references.summary: Validates that a value is equal to a configured value or to the value at a given propertyPath. Comparison uses ==, so 3 and "3" are considered equal; use IdenticalTo for strict === comparison.

references.title: NotEqualTo
references.summary: Validates that a value is not equal to a configured value or to the value at a given propertyPath. Comparison uses !=, so 3 and "3" are still considered equal; use NotIdenticalTo to compare with !==.

references.title: IdenticalTo
references.summary: Validates that a value is identical to another value (compared with ===), so both the value and the type must match. Unlike EqualTo, the integer 3 and the string "3" are not considered identical.

references.title: NotIdenticalTo
references.summary: Validates that a value is not identical to another value, comparing with !==. The integer 3 and the string "3" are considered not identical because their types differ; use NotEqualTo for loose != comparison.

references.title: LessThan
references.summary: Validates that the value is strictly less than a comparison value (or another property's value via propertyPath). It also accepts DateTime objects compared against any string accepted by the DateTime constructor (for example today or +5 hours).

references.title: LessThanOrEqual
