references.summary: Validates that the value is less than or equal to a comparison value or another property's value via propertyPath. Like LessThan, it can compare DateTime objects against date strings accepted by the DateTime constructor.

references.title: GreaterThan
references.summary: Validates that the value is strictly greater than a comparison value or another property's value via propertyPath. It also handles DateTime comparisons against any date string accepted by the DateTime constructor.

references.title: GreaterThanOrEqual
references.summary: Validates that the value is greater than or equal to a comparison value or another property's value via propertyPath. Like its strict counterpart, it can compare DateTime objects against date strings accepted by the DateTime constructor.

references.title: Blank
references.summary: Validates that a value is blank, meaning equal to an empty string or null. To require strictly null, use IsNull; Blank is not the strict opposite of NotBlank because the two constraints define blankness differently.

references.title: Negative
references.summary: Validates that a value is a negative number, strictly less than zero. Zero itself is rejected, so use NegativeOrZero when zero must also be accepted.

references.title: Positive
references.summary: Validates that a value is a positive number, strictly greater than zero. Zero is rejected; use PositiveOrZero when zero is also a valid value.

references.title: Language
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid Unicode language identifier such as fr or zh-Hant. The alpha3 option (default false) switches the check from ISO 639-1 two-letter codes to ISO 639-2 (2T) three-letter codes.

references.title: Bic
references.summary: Validates that a value matches the **Business Identifier Code (BIC)** format. The `iban` and `ibanPropertyPath` options additionally cross-check that the BIC's country code matches the country code of a paired IBAN. The `mode` option accepts `Bic::VALIDATION_MODE_STRICT` (default) or `Bic::VALIDATION_MODE_CASE_INSENSITIVE`, which uppercases the value before validating.

references.title: CardScheme
references.summary: Validates that a value is a credit-card number that matches one of the configured card networks. The `schemes` option is **required** and accepts a string or array of constants like `VISA`, `MASTERCARD`, `AMEX`, `DISCOVER`, `DINERS`, `JCB`, `MAESTRO`, `MIR`, `UATP`, `CHINA_UNIONPAY`, `INSTAPAYMENT`, `LASER`. Empty values are valid; combine with `NotBlank` if needed.

references.title: Cidr
references.summary: Validates that a string is a valid **CIDR** (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) notation. By default both IPv4 and IPv6 are accepted; the `version` option restricts which IP version is allowed and supports the same range tokens as the `Ip` constraint (e.g. `4`, `6`, `all`, `4_no_priv`). The `netmaskMin` and `netmaskMax` options bound the netmask value, with `netmaskMax` defaulting to `32` for IPv4 or `128` for IPv6.

references.title: Country
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid country code. By default it expects an **ISO 3166-1 alpha-2** two-letter code (e.g. `FR`); enabling the `alpha3` option switches to the **ISO 3166-1 alpha-3** three-letter code (e.g. `FRA`). Empty values are valid; combine with `NotBlank` to forbid them.

references.title: CssColor
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid CSS color. The `formats` option restricts which CSS color notations are accepted; constants such as `Assert\CssColor::HEX_LONG`, `HEX_SHORT`, `BASIC_NAMED_COLORS`, `EXTENDED_NAMED_COLORS`, `SYSTEM_COLORS`, `KEYWORDS`, `RGB`, `RGBA`, `HSL`, and `HSLA` enumerate the allowed formats. Without `formats`, any of those formats is accepted.

references.title: Currency
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **3-letter ISO 4217** currency code (e.g. `EUR`, `USD`). It applies to a property or method and rejects values that are not registered ISO 4217 codes. Empty values are accepted; pair with `NotBlank` to forbid them.

references.title: DivisibleBy
references.summary: Validates that a number is divisible by another value. The `value` option (or constructor argument) sets the divisor and may be an integer or float (e.g. `0.25` for quarter-step weights). It supports `propertyPath` to compare against another property of the same object.

references.title: ExpressionLanguageSyntax
references.summary: Validates that a string contains a syntactically valid Symfony **ExpressionLanguage** expression. The `allowedVariables` option restricts which variables the expression may reference; setting it to `null` (the default) allows any variables. Useful when end-users write expressions stored in configuration.

references.title: Hostname
references.summary: Validates that a string is a valid hostname. Internally it uses `filter_var()` with `FILTER_VALIDATE_DOMAIN`. The `requireTld` option (default `true`) requires a top-level domain such as `example.com`; when set to `false`, single-label hostnames such as `example` are accepted. RFC 2606 reserved TLDs (`.example`, `.invalid`, `.localhost`, `.test`) are rejected.

references.title: IBAN
references.summary: Validates that a value is a syntactically valid **International Bank Account Number (IBAN)**. The validator transparently strips various whitespace characters (regular, non-breaking, narrow non-breaking) before validating, so the same input may be accepted while still failing strict downstream APIs. Empty values are considered valid.

references.title: IsFalse
references.summary: Validates that a value is **`false`**, exactly the integer `0`, or exactly the string `"0"`. Often used on boolean getter methods (e.g. `isStateInvalid()`) so that the surrounding object is valid only when the getter returns one of those falsy values. `null` is treated as valid.

references.title: IsNull
references.summary: Validates that a value is exactly `null`. To accept the empty string in addition to `null`, use `Blank`; to accept any non-`null` value, use `NotNull`.

references.title: Isbn
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **International Standard Book Number (ISBN)**. The `type` option selects between `ISBN_10`, `ISBN_13`, or no value (which accepts either). Different message options (`isbn10Message`, `isbn13Message`, `bothIsbnMessage`) tailor the error per mode.

references.title: Isin
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **International Securities Identification Number (ISIN)** — a 12-character alphanumeric code identifying a security. It applies to a property or method.

references.title: Issn
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **International Standard Serial Number (ISSN)**. The `caseSensitive` option (default `false`) controls whether a final `x` checksum digit must be uppercase. The `requireHyphen` option (default `false`) requires the canonical `XXXX-XXXX` form.

references.title: Locale
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **locale** identifier (a language code optionally combined with a region/script, e.g. `en`, `fr_FR`, `zh_Hant`). It applies to a property or method.

references.title: Luhn
references.summary: Validates that a numeric string passes the **Luhn algorithm** checksum, used as a quick sanity check on credit-card-like numbers before sending them to a payment gateway. It does not check that the number belongs to a real account or matches a specific scheme — combine it with `CardScheme` for that.

references.title: NegativeOrZero
references.summary: Validates that a value is a **negative number or zero**. Use `Negative` instead if zero must be rejected.

references.title: PositiveOrZero
references.summary: Validates that a value is a **positive number or zero**. Use `Positive` instead when zero must be rejected.

references.title: Timezone
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid timezone identifier (e.g. `Europe/Paris`). The `zone` option restricts the timezone group via `DateTimeZone` constants such as `DateTimeZone::EUROPE`. The `countryCode` option further restricts to identifiers of a specific country (paired with `zone: DateTimeZone::PER_COUNTRY`). The `intlCompatible` option requires the value to also be valid under the Intl extension.

references.title: Traverse
references.summary: Class-level constraint that asks the validator to traverse the object as a `Traversable` and validate every yielded element. Useful when an object is iterable but its inner data is not exposed through public properties or accessors. It only applies to a class.

references.title: UserPassword
references.summary: Validates that a string equals the **currently authenticated user's password**. Typically used in a "change password" form where the user must re-enter their old password. It only applies when there is an authenticated user and a configured password hasher.

references.title: Validation - Constraint Targets
references.summary: Explains where Symfony constraints can be applied: properties, getter methods, or an entire class. This is exam-relevant for choosing the correct validation scope for property values, derived rules, and object-wide invariants.

references.title: How to Validate Raw Values (Scalar Values and Arrays)
references.summary: Shows how to validate scalar values and arrays directly with `ValidatorInterface::validate()` instead of validating a mapped object. It also demonstrates array validation with `Collection` and confirms that validation returns a `ConstraintViolationList`.

references.title: How to Apply only a Subset of all Your Validation Constraints (Validation Groups)
references.summary: Explains how to assign constraints to named groups and validate only a subset of constraints by passing group names to the validator. It distinguishes the `Default` group, class-name groups, custom groups, and embedded-object behavior.

references.title: How to Sequentially Apply Validation Groups
references.summary: Shows how `GroupSequence` validates groups in a configured order and stops later groups when an earlier group fails. It also explains why a group sequence must use the class-name group instead of `Default` to avoid recursive validation.

references.title: How to Sequentially Apply Validation Groups - Group Sequence Providers
references.summary: Describes dynamic validation sequences through `GroupSequenceProviderInterface` and the `GroupSequenceProvider` marker. The provider's `getGroupSequence()` method can return simple or nested group arrays to control which groups run together and which run sequentially.

references.title: Sequentially
references.summary: Explains the `Sequentially` constraint for applying several constraints to one property or method step by step. It stops validation for that property once the first nested violation is raised, which is narrower than a full object-level group sequence.

references.title: Callback
references.summary: Documents the built-in `Callback` constraint for custom validation logic attached to a class, property, or method. Callback methods receive an execution context and add violations themselves instead of returning a boolean.

references.title: Callback - The Callback Method
references.summary: Shows how an instance callback receives `ExecutionContextInterface` and uses the context to attach violations to specific fields. This is relevant for exam questions about callback signatures and error path assignment.

references.title: Callback - External Callbacks and Closures
references.summary: Explains supported callback formats beyond instance methods, including static array callables and closures. It also states the limitations around global functions and service methods, which require a custom constraint instead.

references.title: How to Handle Different Error Levels
references.summary: A constraint can attach an arbitrary **payload** (any value) that propagates to the violation. The conventional pattern is a **severity payload** like `["severity" => "error"]` or `["severity" => "warning"]`, used by templates to render distinct UI for each severity level. Read the value back via `ConstraintViolation::getConstraint()->payload`.

references.title: How to Translate Validation Constraint Messages
references.summary: Validation messages are translated through the **`validators`** translation domain. Add a `validators.<locale>.xliff` file (or YAML/PHP) under `translations/` with a `<source>` matching the constraint message and a `<target>` for the translation. Custom messages support placeholders like `{{ value }}` and `{{ limit }}` that the translator preserves.

references.title: How to Create a Custom Validation Constraint - Creating the Validator itself
references.summary: Shows how a custom constraint validator uses `$this->context->buildViolation()` to create violations during `validate()`. The builder accepts the error message, supports parameters such as `setParameter()`, and persists the violation only when `addViolation()` is called.

references.title: Service Container - Service Container
references.summary: Introduces Symfony services as useful objects managed by the service container. It explains that the container centralizes object construction, supports application architecture, and avoids constructing services until they are requested.

references.title: Service Container - Fetching and using Services
references.summary: Shows how services are obtained from the container by type-hinting their class or interface in controller actions or constructors. Symfony's autowiring resolves the dependency from the service ID, and `debug:autowiring` lists the type-hints supported out of the box.

references.title: How to Debug the Service Container & List Services - How to Debug the Service Container & List Services
references.summary: Documents the `debug:container` console command to list every public and private service together with its PHP class. The `--show-hidden` flag exposes services whose ID starts with a dot, and passing a service ID prints its definition (with `--show-arguments` for constructor arguments).

references.title: Container Building Workflow
references.summary: Walks through how the container is **built** in the framework: extensions load YAML/XML/PHP into a `ContainerBuilder`, compiler passes run in their priority/types (`PassConfig::TYPE_BEFORE_OPTIMIZATION`, `TYPE_OPTIMIZE`, `TYPE_BEFORE_REMOVING`, `TYPE_REMOVE`, `TYPE_AFTER_REMOVING`), and the result is dumped to PHP for production use.

references.title: How to Configure a Service with a Configurator
references.summary: A **service configurator** is a callable invoked **after** the service is instantiated but before it is handed back to the consumer — typical use case: register handlers/transformers on the new service. It is declared with the `configurator` key (YAML) or `#[AsConfigurator]`/`->configurator(...)` (PHP), and accepts `[Class::class, "method"]`, `'service::method'`, or a closure.

references.title: Service Container - Fetching and using Services
references.summary: Explains that Symfony applications start with many built-in services already registered in the container. It shows how services can be injected by type-hint and how to discover autowirable types or full service IDs with console debug commands.

references.title: Built-in Symfony Service Tags - Built-in Symfony Service Tags
references.summary: Catalogs the service tags exposed by Symfony components and the integrations they unlock, such as `console.command`, `kernel.event_listener`, `kernel.event_subscriber`, `twig.extension`, `security.voter`, `validator.constraint_validator`, `form.type`, `serializer.normalizer`, and `monolog.logger`. The page also shows the `debug:container --tags` and `debug:container --tag=<name>` commands for inspecting tagged services.

references.title: How to Retrieve the Request from the Service Container
references.summary: The `request` service is removed in modern Symfony — to access the **current `Request`** in a service, inject `RequestStack` and call `getCurrentRequest()` (or `getMainRequest()` for the master request). Inject `RequestStack`, never the request itself, because the latter changes during the request lifecycle (sub-requests, ESI fragments).

references.title: Service Container - Service Parameters
references.summary: Describes container parameters and service references inside service configuration. It covers YAML service-reference syntax, parameter accessor methods, the flat nature of parameter names, and the rule that parameters can only be set before compilation.

references.title: Service Container - Binding Arguments by Name or Type
references.summary: Explains the `bind` keyword under `_defaults` to inject scalar values or services into any constructor argument that matches a name (e.g. `$adminEmail`), a type (e.g. `Psr\Log\LoggerInterface`), or both at once (e.g. `Psr\Log\LoggerInterface $requestLogger`). Bindings can also use `!tagged_iterator` to inject collections of tagged services.

references.title: Using Parameters within a Dependency Injection Class
references.summary: Explains how to use container parameters such as `%kernel.debug%` from inside a bundle's `Extension::load()` to drive **configuration-time** decisions. Container parameters resolve only at compile time, so to read them in `load()` you must inject them via `$container->getParameter(...)` (the `%...%` placeholder syntax expands later).

references.title: How to work with Service Definition Objects
references.summary: A **`Definition`** describes how the container should build a service: class, arguments, calls, configurator, public/private, lazy, decorator, factory, parent, etc. It is the in-memory representation manipulated by compiler passes; the container reads `Definition` objects when generating the optimised dumped container.

references.title: How to Inject Values Based on Complex Expressions
references.summary: Use the **`@=`** prefix in YAML/PHP to inject the result of an **ExpressionLanguage** expression as a service argument. Variables `service('id')`, `parameter('name')`, and any container parameter are accessible inside the expression — handy for selecting a value based on `kernel.debug` or combining configuration.

references.title: Service Container - Creating/Configuring Services in the Container
references.summary: Shows how application classes become services in Symfony and how the default `services.yaml` loads classes from `src/`. It explains the `resource` and `exclude` options, default autowiring and autoconfiguration, and that later service definitions replace earlier ones.

references.title: Service Container - Limiting Services to a specific Symfony Environment
references.summary: Describes the `#[When]` PHP attribute from `Symfony\Component\DependencyInjection\Attribute` for restricting service registration to selected kernel environments. The attribute takes an `env:` argument, can be repeated on the same class, and the class is only registered as a service when the active environment matches.

references.title: How to Inject Instances into the Container - How to Inject Instances into the Container
references.summary: Documents synthetic services, which are services injected at runtime instead of being instantiated by the container. Such definitions use `synthetic: true`, omit a class, and the actual instance is provided through `Container::set()`; the `kernel` service is the canonical example.

references.title: Service Aliases and Public/Private Services
references.summary: Documents **service aliases** (`Alias` definitions, declared with `alias:` or `#[Autowire(service: ...)]`) and the **public vs private** flag. Symfony 4+ services are **private by default**, meaning `$container->get()` cannot retrieve them at runtime — only DI injection works. Make a service public when external code or templates need to fetch it.

references.title: Service Method Calls and Setter Injection
references.summary: Defines how to call methods after instantiation (**setter injection**): YAML's `calls:` key, PHP's `->call('method', [args])`, the `#[Required]` attribute (autowiring calls all `#[Required]` methods automatically), and `returns_clone` to chain a method that returns `$this` and have its return value discarded.

references.title: How to Import Configuration Files/Resources
references.summary: YAML/XML/PHP service files include other configuration **resources** through the `imports:` key (YAML) or `<imports>` block (XML). Globs and relative paths are supported, and `ignore_errors: not_found` lets you import a file only if it exists. Resources are tracked so the container is rebuilt when any of them change.

references.title: Types of Injection
references.summary: Compares the three injection styles: **constructor injection** (recommended default), **setter / method injection** (for optional dependencies), and **property injection** (rare, for legacy code). Constructor injection is preferred because it makes mandatory dependencies explicit and the resulting object is fully usable from the moment it is constructed.

references.title: Lazy Services
references.summary: Marking a service `lazy: true` makes the container inject a **virtual proxy** (since 7.x via PHP 8.4 `\ReflectionClass::newLazyProxy()` natively, otherwise via `symfony/proxy-manager-bridge`). The real instance is built only on first method call, which is useful for breaking circular dependencies and avoiding the cost of building heavy services that may never be used.

references.title: How to Make Service Arguments/References Optional
references.summary: When a referenced service may be absent, use the **`?`** prefix in YAML/XML (`@?logger`) or the `IGNORE_ON_INVALID_REFERENCE` / `NULL_ON_INVALID_REFERENCE` constants in PHP definitions. The container then injects `null` (or skips the call) instead of throwing if the dependency is missing.

references.title: How to Manage Common Dependencies with Parent Services
references.summary: A **parent service** is an abstract template definition (`abstract: true`) whose arguments, tags, and method calls are inherited by child definitions through the `parent:` key. Children may override individual arguments by index; parent services were widely used before autowiring became the norm.

references.title: Service Subscribers & Locators
references.summary: A **service subscriber** declares a list of services it may need by implementing `ServiceSubscriberInterface::getSubscribedServices()`; the container injects a **`ServiceLocator`** holding only those services (lazy by default). This lets a class declare optional or rarely-used dependencies without inflating its constructor signature.

references.title: How to Define Non-Shared Services
references.summary: By default services are **shared** (the same instance is returned every time). Set `shared: false` to make the container build a **new instance on each retrieval** — useful for stateful workers or message handlers that must not leak state between dispatches.

references.title: How to Decorate Services - How to Decorate Services
references.summary: Explains how service decoration replaces a service while keeping the original service available to the decorator. It covers the `decorates` option, the generated inner service reference, explicit injection of the decorated service, and custom inner names.

references.title: How to Decorate Services - Decoration Priority
references.summary: Documents the `decoration_priority` option for chaining multiple decorators of the same service. Its value is an integer (default `0`) and higher priorities are applied earlier, so a decorator with priority 5 wraps one with priority 1 in the final compiled container.

references.title: How to Decorate Services - Stacking Decorators
references.summary: Describes `stack` definitions as an alternative to decoration priorities: each frame of the stack decorates the next, and the topmost frame becomes the public service. Frames may be inline services, references, or child definitions, and stacks themselves only support `public` and `deprecated` attributes.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - How to Work with Service Tags
references.summary: Introduces service tags as markers that let Symfony or bundles process services in special ways. It shows explicit tag configuration, explains autoconfigured tags, and describes custom tags as inputs for compiler passes.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - Reference Tagged Services
references.summary: Shows how to inject every service that carries a given tag as an iterable using the `!tagged_iterator` YAML shortcut, the `<argument type="tagged_iterator">` XML form, the `tagged_iterator()` PHP configurator helper, or the `#[TaggedIterator]` PHP attribute applied directly on a constructor argument.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - Tagged Services with Index
references.summary: Explains the `index_by` and `default_index_method` options that turn a tagged iterator into a keyed collection. Each tag attribute (e.g. `key`) provides the index, services may implement `getDefaultIndexName()` to supply it implicitly, and the same options exist on `#[TaggedIterator]` and `#[TaggedLocator]` (`indexAttribute`, `defaultIndexMethod`).

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - The #[AsTaggedItem] attribute
references.summary: Documents the `#[AsTaggedItem]` PHP attribute which sets both the priority and the index of a tagged item directly on the service class. The attribute accepts `index:` and `priority:` arguments and replaces the need for static `getDefaultPriority()` or `getDefaultIndexName()` methods.

references.title: How to Create Friendly Configuration for a Bundle - How to Create Friendly Configuration for a Bundle
references.summary: Explains semantic bundle configuration: users configure high-level bundle options while the bundle extension converts them into services, parameters, and definitions. It covers DI aliases, configuration trees, `processConfiguration()`, and using merged configuration values inside an extension.

references.title: How to Create Friendly Configuration for a Bundle - Processing the $configs Array
references.summary: Shows how a bundle's `Configuration` class implements `ConfigurationInterface::getConfigTreeBuilder()` and uses `TreeBuilder` plus node builders (`arrayNode`, `scalarNode`, `integerNode`) to define the configuration schema. Inside the extension's `load()` method, `processConfiguration()` validates, normalizes, and merges every `$configs` array, and the resulting values can override service definitions through `ContainerBuilder::getDefinition()->replaceArgument()`.

references.title: Using a Factory to Create Services - Using a Factory to Create Services
references.summary: Shows how Symfony service definitions can delegate object creation to factory callables. It covers static factories, factory services, invokable factories, and how factory-returned objects determine the actual service class.

references.title: Using a Factory to Create Services - Static Factories
references.summary: Demonstrates configuring a static factory with `factory: ['Class', 'method']` (or `<factory class="..." method="..."/>` in XML). When the factory class is the same as the service class, only `method` needs to be set, and the configured `class` of the service does not affect the resulting object since the actual class is the one returned by the factory.

references.title: Using a Factory to Create Services - Invokable Factories
references.summary: Explains how factories with an `__invoke()` method are wired by passing only the factory service reference (e.g. `factory: '@App\Email\InvokableNewsletterManagerFactory'`), without naming a method. Arguments to the factory call can still be autowired when autowiring is enabled.

references.title: How to Work with Compiler Passes - How to Work with Compiler Passes
references.summary: Explains that compiler passes can manipulate service definitions registered in the container. It shows registration from the kernel `build()` method, using the kernel as a compiler pass, and the common use case of processing tagged services.

references.title: How to Work with Compiler Passes - Working with Compiler Passes in Bundles
references.summary: Documents how a bundle's main `Bundle` class registers compiler passes inside its `build(ContainerBuilder $containerBuilder)` method using `addCompilerPass()`. The page also notes the convention that custom service tag names are prefixed with the bundle name (lowercase, underscores) followed by a dot.

references.title: Compiling the Container
references.summary: The `ContainerBuilder::compile()` method runs all registered **compiler passes** in five phases — `BEFORE_OPTIMIZATION`, `OPTIMIZE`, `BEFORE_REMOVING`, `REMOVE`, `AFTER_REMOVING` — to resolve parameters, validate definitions, remove unused services, and dump an optimised PHP container. Compilation is one-way: once compiled, the container is frozen.

references.title: Defining Services Dependencies Automatically (Autowiring) - Defining Services Dependencies Automatically (Autowiring)
references.summary: Describes Symfony autowiring as automatic dependency injection based on constructor and method type-hints. It explains exact type-hint matching, aliases for non-class service IDs, actionable exceptions, and the absence of runtime overhead in the compiled container.

references.title: Defining Services Dependencies Automatically (Autowiring) - Dealing with Multiple Implementations of the Same Type
references.summary: Shows how to disambiguate autowiring when several services implement the same interface. A default alias maps the interface to one implementation, named autowiring aliases of the form `Interface $variableName` pick a different implementation by argument name, and the `#[Target]` attribute (`Symfony\Component\DependencyInjection\Attribute\Target`) selects an implementation by alias regardless of the argument name.

references.title: Defining Services Dependencies Automatically (Autowiring) - Autowiring other Methods (e.g. Setters and Public Typed Properties)
references.summary: Explains setter and property injection driven by the `#[Required]` attribute from `Symfony\Contracts\Service\Attribute\Required`. Symfony automatically calls every method tagged with `#[Required]` after instantiation and autowires its arguments, and the same attribute can be applied to public typed properties.

references.title: Security - Authenticating Users
references.summary: Index of the authenticator mechanisms shipped with the SecurityBundle: form login, JSON login, HTTP Basic, login link, X.509 client certificates, remote users, and custom authenticators. Lists which firewall key activates each one and how Symfony handles the authentication flow under a firewall.

references.title: Security - Form Login
references.summary: Describes how the form_login authenticator processes a POST submission to check_path, reads the _username and _password parameters, loads the user via the configured user provider, and either authenticates or redirects back to login_path. Also covers AuthenticationUtils for retrieving the last error and last submitted username.

references.title: Security - JSON Login
references.summary: Explains the json_login authenticator: a client posts a JSON body with username and password keys to check_path with Content-Type application/json, the security system authenticates the user and runs the controller, and on bad credentials returns HTTP 401 Unauthorized. Mentions the #[CurrentUser] controller attribute used to access the authenticated user.

references.title: Security - HTTP Basic
references.summary: Documents the http_basic firewall key: Symfony emits a WWW-Authenticate response header with the configured realm to trigger the browser dialog, then verifies the credentials. Notes that the Symfony logout cannot terminate an HTTP Basic session because the browser keeps re-sending credentials.

references.title: Security - X.509 Client Certificates
references.summary: Explains the x509 firewall authenticator that relies on web-server-level TLS client authentication. Symfony reads the certificate distinguished name from the SSL_CLIENT_S_DN_Email server parameter, falling back to parsing emailAddress= from SSL_CLIENT_S_DN, and uses the resulting email as the user identifier loaded by the configured provider.

references.title: Security - Remote Users
references.summary: Documents the remote_user firewall key for pre-authenticated users coming from web-server modules such as Kerberos. The authenticator reads the REMOTE_USER server variable as the user identifier and loads the matching user through the configured provider.

references.title: Login Link - Using the Login Link Authenticator
references.summary: Describes the login_link firewall key for passwordless magic-link authentication. Configuration requires a check_route and signature_properties to build a signed URL that uniquely identifies the user; when the user clicks the link, the authenticator intercepts the check route and authenticates the user without running a controller.

references.title: Security - Limiting Login Attempts
references.summary: Describes the login_throttling firewall option that uses the Rate Limiter component to block brute-force login attempts. The default policy allows 5 login attempts per minute per IP+username and 5 * max_attempts per IP; the max_attempts and interval keys, or a custom limiter service ID, can be set to change the policy.

references.title: Customizing the Form Login Authenticator Responses
references.summary: Reference page for tuning the form_login authenticator: default_target_path sets the fallback URL after success, always_use_default_target_path forces it, target_path_parameter and failure_path_parameter customize the request fields used to control redirects, and failure_path / failure_forward control where the user is sent after a failed attempt.

references.title: Security - Access Control (Authorization)
references.summary: Defines authorization as the process of deciding whether the current user may access a resource (URL, controller, model, method call). Distinguishes between assigning roles to a user and requiring an attribute to access a resource.

references.title: Security - Add Code to Deny Access
references.summary: Lists the two ways to deny access in Symfony: declarative URL-pattern protection via access_control in security.yaml, and imperative checks inside a controller, service, or template. Introduces denyAccessUnlessGranted(), isGranted(), the Security helper service, and the is_granted() Twig function.

references.title: Security - Securing Controllers and Other Code
references.summary: Shows how to deny access from a controller with denyAccessUnlessGranted(). When access is refused Symfony throws AccessDeniedException, which causes a redirect to the login form for unauthenticated users or a 403 page for users without the required attribute. Also covers using the Security service or AuthorizationCheckerInterface in any service.

references.title: Security - Checking if a User is Logged In
references.summary: Documents the special authorization attributes IS_AUTHENTICATED_FULLY (full session login), IS_AUTHENTICATED_REMEMBERED (logged in either fully or via a remember-me cookie), IS_REMEMBERED (only via remember-me cookie), and IS_IMPERSONATOR (the current user is impersonating another). They can be used wherever roles are accepted.

references.title: Security - Allowing Unsecured Access
references.summary: Explains how to permit anonymous access to specific routes by using the PUBLIC_ACCESS attribute in access_control rules. This is the recommended way to whitelist routes such as the login form while keeping authentication required for the rest of the protected area.

references.title: Using Expressions in Security Access Controls
references.summary: Describes how isGranted() and denyAccessUnlessGranted() accept an Expression object whose body uses variables such as user, role_names, object/subject, token and trust_resolver, plus helper functions like is_authenticated(), is_remember_me(), is_fully_authenticated(), and is_granted(). role_names contains hierarchical roles but excludes the IS_AUTHENTICATED_* attributes.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference (SecurityBundle) - Configuration
references.summary: This reference summarizes the main SecurityBundle configuration keys, including basic options and links to advanced sections for access control, password hashers, firewalls, providers, and role hierarchy. It is useful for exam questions about where security behavior is configured.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference (SecurityBundle) - firewalls
references.summary: The firewalls option defines the authentication mechanism used for each URL or URL pattern of the application. Each firewall is identified by a name and supports a pattern key that is a regular expression matched against the incoming request URL.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference (SecurityBundle) - providers
references.summary: The providers option defines how application users are loaded, for example from a database, an LDAP server, a configuration file or a chain of providers. Each provider is referenced by name from a firewall's provider key.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference (SecurityBundle) - role_hierarchy
references.summary: The role_hierarchy option lets you declare role inheritance rules instead of attaching many roles to each user. Roles are static strings and the hierarchy is resolved by Symfony when checking access through the security helpers.

references.title: User Providers
references.summary: This page explains that user providers reload users from storage by a user identifier such as an email address or username. It covers Symfony's built-in entity, LDAP, memory, and chain providers.

references.title: User Providers - Entity User Provider
references.summary: The entity user provider loads users from a database using Doctrine. It is configured under providers with an entity entry that takes the user class and the property used as the user identifier; an optional manager_name selects a non-default Doctrine entity manager.

references.title: User Providers - Chain User Provider
references.summary: The chain user provider combines two or more configured providers under a chain entry that lists their names. Symfony queries the listed providers in order and stops at the first one that returns a user.

references.title: User Providers - Creating a Custom User Provider
references.summary: A custom user provider implements UserProviderInterface (and optionally PasswordUpgraderInterface) and is registered in security.yaml under providers using the id key pointing to its service ID. The class must implement loadUserByIdentifier(), refreshUser() and supportsClass().

references.title: Authenticating against an LDAP server
references.summary: Combines the LDAP component with the Security stack: `ldap` user provider fetches users from an LDAP directory, while `form_login_ldap` and `http_basic_ldap` authenticate against the same directory using the user-supplied password. The provider supports DN templates and search filters scoped per organisational unit.

references.title: Security - The Firewall
references.summary: This section describes firewalls as the authentication systems that define which parts of an application are secured and how users authenticate. It explains that only one firewall is active for each request and that Symfony chooses the first matching firewall pattern.

references.title: How to Restrict Firewalls to a Request
references.summary: Firewalls decide whether they handle a request through a request matcher built from configuration options such as pattern, host and methods, or by a custom service. The first firewall whose matcher accepts the request handles it, and a final firewall without any matcher acts as a catch-all.

references.title: How to Restrict Firewalls to a Request - Restricting by Host
references.summary: The host firewall option is a regular expression matched against the request host name. When set, the firewall is initialized only for requests whose host matches the expression; otherwise subsequent firewalls are evaluated.

references.title: How to Restrict Firewalls to a Request - Restricting by Service
references.summary: When pattern, host and methods are not enough, the request_matcher option points to a service implementing RequestMatcherInterface. The firewall is initialized only when this service accepts the current request.

references.title: How to Impersonate a User
references.summary: Enables admin user impersonation under a firewall via `switch_user: { provider: ..., role: ROLE_ALLOWED_TO_SWITCH }`. Append `?_switch_user=username` to any URL to assume the target user, `?_switch_user=_exit` to switch back. The `IS_IMPERSONATOR` and `ROLE_PREVIOUS_ADMIN` security attributes mark the impersonation state.

references.title: How to Add "Remember Me" Login Functionality
references.summary: The **`remember_me`** firewall option keeps a user authenticated across sessions through a signed cookie. Two strategies are available: the **signature-based** default (HMAC over user properties) and the **token-based** strategy that stores opaque tokens in a `RememberMeHandler` (e.g. Doctrine).

references.title: How to Create and Enable Custom User Checkers
references.summary: A **user checker** runs additional checks during authentication (`checkPreAuth()` before credentials are verified) and after a successful login (`checkPostAuth()`). Implement `UserCheckerInterface` and assign it per firewall via `user_checker: <service-id>`. Use it for things like account expiration, two-factor enforcement, or domain allowlists.

references.title: Security - The User
references.summary: This section explains that Symfony permissions are linked to a user object and that a security user class must implement UserInterface. It also shows the generated user entity shape, including identifier, roles, and password-related methods.

references.title: Security - Registering the User: Hashing Passwords
references.summary: Users that authenticate with a password must additionally implement PasswordAuthenticatedUserInterface, whose getPassword() method returns the stored hashed password. The same class is then referenced under password_hashers to select the algorithm Symfony uses to hash and verify credentials.

references.title: Security - Loading the User: The User Provider
references.summary: The user provider loads users from a storage based on a user identifier returned by getUserIdentifier(). On stateful firewalls the provider also refreshes the user from storage at the beginning of each request and can de-authenticate the user if core data changed.

references.title: User Providers - Creating a Custom User Provider
references.summary: A custom user provider implements UserProviderInterface with loadUserByIdentifier(), refreshUser() and supportsClass(). It can also implement PasswordUpgraderInterface to persist a rehashed password through upgradePassword() when Symfony detects a stronger algorithm is available.

references.title: Password Hashing and Verification
references.summary: This page explains how Symfony's PasswordHasher component hashes and verifies passwords and how password hashers are configured with the password_hashers option. It also describes supported algorithms such as auto, bcrypt, sodium, and PBKDF2.

references.title: Password Hashing and Verification - The Bcrypt Password Hasher
references.summary: The Bcrypt hasher produces 60-character hashes that include their salt automatically. Its only configuration option is cost, an integer between 4 and 31 (default 13); each increment doubles the time required to hash a password.

references.title: Password Hashing and Verification - Password Migration
references.summary: Password migration uses the migrate_from option to keep legacy hashers available while moving new hashes to a stronger algorithm. When a user logs in with a legacy hash, Symfony validates it with the old algorithm and automatically rehashes the password with the new one.

references.title: Password Hashing and Verification - Upgrade the Password
references.summary: On successful login, Symfony rehashes the password if a better algorithm is available. To persist the new hash with Doctrine, the UserRepository must implement PasswordUpgraderInterface; custom authenticators must include PasswordCredentials in the security passport for the upgrade to run.

references.title: Security - Roles
references.summary: This section explains how Symfony obtains roles from the User object's getRoles() method and that generated users are commonly guaranteed ROLE_USER. It also states that Symfony roles must start with the ROLE_ prefix and can be organized through role hierarchy.

references.title: Security - Hierarchical Roles
references.summary: Role inheritance is configured under role_hierarchy: parent roles list the roles they inherit, so a user with ROLE_ADMIN automatically gains ROLE_USER. The hierarchy is only honoured by the security helpers (isGranted(), denyAccessUnlessGranted()), not by reading $user->getRoles() directly.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference (SecurityBundle) - role_hierarchy
references.summary: The role_hierarchy configuration key declares static role inheritance rules under the security configuration. Storing the hierarchy in a database is not supported; dynamic role rules must be implemented with a custom security voter.

references.title: How Does the Security access_control Work?
references.summary: This page explains how Symfony evaluates access_control entries for incoming requests. It covers matching options such as path, IP, port, host, and methods, then describes enforcement with roles, allow_if, and requires_channel.

references.title: How Does the Security access_control Work? - Matching Options
references.summary: Each access_control entry builds a RequestMatcher from path (regular expression), ip/ips (with netmask support), port (integer), host (regular expression) and methods. Options that are not set match any value, and only the first matching entry is used.

references.title: How Does the Security access_control Work? - Access Enforcement
references.summary: Once an entry matches, Symfony enforces access through roles (denied if the user lacks the required role), allow_if (denied if the expression is false) and requires_channel (redirected when the channel does not match). With the default affirmative strategy, defining both roles and allow_if grants access if either condition succeeds.

references.title: How Does the Security access_control Work? - Forcing a Channel (http, https)
references.summary: The requires_channel option forces a request to be served over a specific channel such as https. When the incoming channel does not match, Symfony redirects the user to the same URL using the configured channel.

references.title: How to Customize Access Denied Responses
references.summary: Throwing `AccessDeniedException` in a controller or voter triggers Symfony's access-denied flow. The response depends on whether a user is authenticated: **anonymous** users are redirected to the firewall's `entry_point`; **authenticated** users get a `403`. Override the response with the `access_denied_handler` firewall option (a service implementing `AccessDeniedHandlerInterface`).

references.title: How to Write a Custom Authenticator
references.summary: This page explains how custom authenticators implement AuthenticatorInterface or extend AbstractAuthenticator. It describes supports(), authenticate(), passport creation, success and failure handling, and firewall registration through custom_authenticators.

references.title: How to Write a Custom Authenticator - Security Passports
references.summary: A passport carries the user that will be authenticated together with credentials. The default Passport requires a UserBadge (which loads the user via the user provider) plus credentials such as PasswordCredentials or CustomCredentials.

references.title: How to Write a Custom Authenticator - Self Validating Passport
references.summary: When no credential check is needed (for example with API tokens), the authenticator can return a SelfValidatingPassport that only requires a UserBadge and optional badges.

references.title: How to Write a Custom Authenticator - Passport Badges
references.summary: Badges attach extra security data to a passport. Built-in badges include RememberMeBadge, PasswordUpgradeBadge, CsrfTokenBadge and PreAuthenticatedUserBadge; PasswordUpgradeBadge is added automatically when the passport carries PasswordCredentials.

references.title: How to Use Voters to Check User Permissions
references.summary: This page explains voters as reusable permission checks used by isGranted(), denyAccessUnlessGranted(), and access controls. It also covers voter interfaces and access decision strategies such as affirmative, consensus, unanimous, and priority.

references.title: How to Use Voters to Check User Permissions - The Voter Interface
references.summary: A custom voter implements VoterInterface or extends the Voter abstract class, which only requires implementing supports(string $attribute, mixed $subject) and voteOnAttribute(string $attribute, mixed $subject, TokenInterface $token). Voters that implement CacheableVoterInterface let the access decision manager skip them when the attribute or subject type is not supported.

references.title: How to Use Voters to Check User Permissions - Changing the Access Decision Strategy
references.summary: Symfony provides four built-in access decision strategies: affirmative (default, grants access if any voter grants), consensus (majority wins, ties resolved by allow_if_equal_granted_denied), unanimous (denies if any voter denies) and priority (uses the first non-abstaining voter by service priority). When all voters abstain, the decision falls back to allow_if_all_abstain (default false).

references.title: HTTP Cache - Caching on the Shoulders of Giants
references.summary: Introduces HTTP caching as full response caching that can bypass the Symfony application entirely on later requests. It explains that Symfony relies on standard HTTP caching semantics, including expiration and validation models.

references.title: HTTP Cache - Caching with a Gateway Cache
references.summary: Explains gateway caches as intermediaries between clients and the Symfony application. It identifies reverse proxy caches such as Varnish, Squid in reverse proxy mode, and Symfony's reverse proxy as HTTP gateway caches.

references.title: HTTP Cache - Symfony Reverse Proxy
references.summary: Shows how Symfony can act as a PHP-based reverse proxy by enabling the `framework.http_cache` option in the production environment. It also describes the `X-Symfony-Cache` trace header and related trace configuration.

references.title: HTTP Cache Expiration
references.summary: Explains the expiration caching model, where a fresh cached response is returned without hitting the application until it expires. It frames expiration as the efficient and straightforward HTTP caching model to use whenever possible.

references.title: HTTP Cache Expiration - Expiration with the Cache-Control Header
references.summary: Shows how `Cache-Control` directives are produced from Symfony response methods such as `setPublic()` and `setMaxAge()`. It also notes the behavioral difference between `setSharedMaxAge()` and combining `public` with `max-age`.

references.title: HTTP Cache Expiration - Expiration with the Expires Header
references.summary: Explains the `Expires` response header and the `Response::setExpires()` method. It covers GMT conversion, clock-skew concerns, and the precedence of `Cache-Control` freshness directives over `Expires`.

references.title: HTTP Cache Validation
references.summary: Introduces validation caching, where the cache stores a response but asks the application whether it is still valid on later requests. It explains that a valid cached response should be confirmed with a `304 Not Modified` response and no content.

references.title: HTTP Cache Validation - Validation with the ETag Header
references.summary: Explains `ETag` validators as application-generated fingerprints for resource representations. It shows how `Response::setEtag()` and `Response::isNotModified()` work with `If-None-Match` to produce a `304` response.

references.title: HTTP Cache Validation - Validation with the Last-Modified Header
references.summary: Explains `Last-Modified` validation using the resource representation's last update time. It shows how `Response::setLastModified()` and `Response::isNotModified()` compare against `If-Modified-Since`.

references.title: HTTP Cache Validation - Optimizing your Code with Validation
references.summary: Shows how to perform cheap validation before generating expensive response content. It describes how `Response::isNotModified()` sets status `304`, removes content, and strips headers that must not appear on `304` responses.

references.title: Varying the Response for HTTP Cache
references.summary: Explains that HTTP caches normally use the URI as the cache key and need the `Vary` header when different request header values create different representations. It shows how Symfony's `Response::setVary()` manages one or more varying request headers.

references.title: HTTP Cache - HTTP Caching and User Sessions
references.summary: Explains Symfony's default protection for responses generated while a session is active. When a session starts, Symfony turns the response into a private non-cacheable response to avoid exposing private user information through caches.

references.title: How to Use Varnish to Speed up my Website
references.summary: Explains that Symfony's HTTP cache behavior is based on standard HTTP cache headers. It describes Varnish as an HTTP accelerator that can replace Symfony's gateway cache and serve cached content quickly.

references.title: How to Use Varnish to Speed up my Website - Cookies and Caching
references.summary: Describes how cookies and basic authentication commonly prevent reverse proxies from caching a request. It shows that irrelevant frontend cookies should be removed from the `Cookie` header so cacheable pages are not unnecessarily bypassed.

references.title: Cache Invalidation
references.summary: Explains explicit gateway cache invalidation for content that changes before a long cache lifetime expires. It covers the `PURGE` convention, protecting purge requests, and alternatives such as refreshing, banning, and cache tagging.

references.title: Working with Edge Side Includes
references.summary: Introduces Edge Side Includes as a way to cache page fragments with a different strategy from the main page. It explains that Symfony implements the ESI `include` tag and that each ESI tag points to a fully qualified fragment URL.

references.title: Working with Edge Side Includes - Using ESI in Symfony
references.summary: Shows how to enable ESI in Symfony and use the `render_esi()` Twig function for embedded controllers or URLs. It explains Symfony's fallback to inline rendering when no ESI-capable gateway cache is available.

references.title: How to Use Varnish to Speed up my Website - Enable Edge Side Includes (ESI)
references.summary: Explains the Varnish configuration needed to process Symfony-generated ESI tags. It covers the `Surrogate-Capability` request header and the `Surrogate-Control` response check used to enable ESI processing.

references.title: Using Console Commands, Shortcuts and Built-in Commands - Built-in Commands
references.summary: Symfony Console applications provide built-in commands such as `list` and `help`. This section explains how those commands expose registered command names, standard options, and command-specific help.

references.title: Console Commands - Creating a Command
references.summary: Custom Symfony commands are PHP classes that extend the Console `Command` class and implement their work in `execute()`. The section shows the default command name, input/output method signature, and the standard command exit constants.

references.title: Console Commands - Command Lifecycle
references.summary: Symfony commands can define `initialize()`, `interact()`, and `execute()` lifecycle methods. The lifecycle determines where setup, interactive prompting, and required command logic belong.

references.title: How to Style a Console Command
references.summary: `Symfony\Component\Console\Style\SymfonyStyle` wraps `InputInterface` and `OutputInterface` to produce consistently styled console output. It exposes titling helpers (`title()`, `section()`), content helpers (`text()`, `listing()`, `table()`), result helpers (`success()`, `warning()`, `error()`, `info()`), and user input helpers (`ask()`, `askHidden()`, `confirm()`, `choice()`).

references.title: How to Call Other Commands
references.summary: Shows how a **command can run another command** from inside its own `execute()` (or `__invoke()`) — useful for "meta" commands that orchestrate a series of build/cache/migration commands. The recipe uses `$this->getApplication()->find()->run()` with an `ArrayInput`; output can be muted with a `NullOutput`.

references.title: How to Call a Command from a Controller
references.summary: Shows how to invoke a console command from an HTTP **controller** — by autowiring `KernelInterface`, building an `Application($kernel)`, calling `setAutoExit(false)`, and passing an `ArrayInput`. The captured output (e.g. via `BufferedOutput`) can then be inspected or returned in the response.

references.title: How to Hide Console Commands
references.summary: Explains how to keep a command **hidden** from the `list` output: set the `hidden` attribute on `#[AsCommand(name: ..., hidden: true)]`, or call `$this->setHidden(true)` in `configure()`. Hidden commands are still executable when invoked by name.

references.title: How to Define Commands as Services
references.summary: When using the default `services.yaml` (autoconfigure on), command classes that extend `Command` or carry `#[AsCommand]` are automatically tagged `console.command`. The article covers manual registration when autoconfigure is off, including how to set the `command` attribute on the tag to define the command name.

references.title: Prevent Running the Same Console Command Multiple Times
references.summary: Adds the **`LockableTrait`** to a command and uses `$this->lock()` / `$this->release()` to guarantee that **only one instance** of the command runs at a time on the same machine. Internally it uses the `Lock` component (default store: `FlockStore`); pass a name to `lock()` to share the lock between commands.

references.title: Console Commands - Configuring the Command
references.summary: Symfony commands can define description, help text, options, and arguments in the `configure()` method. This section also explains why static command metadata can avoid unnecessary command instantiation for command listings.

references.title: Console Commands - Registering the Command
references.summary: Commands are registered with the application either through the `AsCommand` attribute or by registering them as tagged services. The section is relevant for choosing command names, descriptions, aliases, and service registration mechanisms.

references.title: How to Make Commands Lazily Loaded
references.summary: Lazy command loading registers a `CommandLoaderInterface` (e.g. `FactoryCommandLoader` or `ContainerCommandLoader`) on the `Application` via `setCommandLoader()`. Each command is only instantiated when it is actually run, which avoids the cost of constructing every command just to list them or to dispatch a single one.

references.title: Console Input (Arguments & Options) - Using Command Arguments
references.summary: Console arguments are ordered values that follow the command name and may be required, optional, or array-valued. The section explains how to declare arguments and retrieve them through the input object.

references.title: Console Input (Arguments & Options) - Using Command Options
references.summary: Console options are unordered, optional switches introduced with dashes. This section covers option modes such as required values, optional values, arrays, booleans, and negatable options.

references.title: Understanding how Console Arguments and Options Are Handled
references.summary: Detailed coverage of how the Console parser treats arguments and options — short vs long flags, value-required vs value-optional vs no-value modes, array arguments, the `--` token, equals-sign vs space, and the underlying **docopt**-inspired conventions. Helps reason about edge cases like options whose value starts with a dash.

references.title: Console Commands - Console Output
references.summary: Symfony commands receive an `OutputInterface` in `execute()` for writing messages to the console. The section distinguishes `writeln()` from `write()` and shows output generated from strings, arrays, and iterators.

references.title: Console Commands - Output Sections
references.summary: Console output sections are independent regions that can be cleared or overwritten. They are created through `ConsoleOutput::section()` and are useful for dynamic output such as multiple progress bars.

references.title: Console Commands - Console Input
references.summary: Symfony commands receive an `InputInterface` for accessing argument and option values. This section shows command input being declared with `addArgument()` and read with `getArgument()` during execution.

references.title: How to Color and Style the Console Output
references.summary: Outputs can include **inline tags** like `<info>...</info>`, `<comment>...</comment>`, `<error>...</error>` or fully-customised styles via `OutputFormatterStyle`. Hyperlinks use `<href=https://example.com>label</href>` (works in supporting terminals). New styles are added via `OutputFormatter::setStyle()` or by passing them to the `Application` constructor.

references.title: The Console Helpers
references.summary: The Console component includes helper classes for common command-line tasks. The helper index links to formatting, process, progress bar, question, table, debug formatter, and cursor helpers.

references.title: Question Helper
references.summary: The `QuestionHelper` asks users for interactive input through `ask()`. It works with `InputInterface`, `OutputInterface`, and question objects such as `Question`, `ConfirmationQuestion`, and `ChoiceQuestion`.

references.title: Progress Bar
references.summary: The `ProgressBar` helper displays progress for long-running commands. It can be started, advanced, finished, updated with a maximum step count, or used to iterate over iterable data automatically.

references.title: Table
references.summary: The `Table` helper renders tabular data in console output. It supports headers, rows, separators, titles, styles, and explicit or maximum column widths.

references.title: Question Helper - Asking the User for Confirmation
references.summary: `ConfirmationQuestion` asks a yes/no question and returns a boolean. Its constructor accepts the question text, a default value returned when the user presses Enter, and an optional regular expression that matches affirmative answers (defaulting to one that accepts answers starting with `y`).

references.title: Question Helper - Let the User Choose from a List of Answers
references.summary: `ChoiceQuestion` restricts the answer to a predefined list of strings. The constructor accepts the question, the array of choices and an optional default key; `setMultiselect(true)` lets the user pick several values separated by commas, while `setErrorMessage()` customizes the error shown when the answer is invalid.

references.title: Cursor Helper
references.summary: The **`Cursor`** helper moves the terminal cursor to arbitrary positions and clears parts of the screen. Methods include `moveUp()`, `moveDown()`, `moveLeft()`, `moveRight()`, `moveToColumn()`, `moveToPosition()`, `savePosition()`, `restorePosition()`, `clearLine()`, `clearOutput()`, `clearScreen()`, `hide()`, `show()`.

references.title: Debug Formatter Helper
references.summary: The **`DebugFormatterHelper`** prints structured debug output for an external program (e.g. a `Process` or HTTP request) using a coloured, indented stream that distinguishes start/output/error/finish events. It is typically used together with the `Process` component to live-stream a sub-process's stdout/stderr.

references.title: Formatter Helper
references.summary: The **`FormatterHelper`** wraps text with predefined or custom **`OutputFormatterStyle`** styles. Methods include `formatSection()` (label + message), `formatBlock()` (multi-line block, optional `large` mode), and `truncate()` (cut to a maximum length with a configurable suffix).

references.title: Process Helper
references.summary: The **`ProcessHelper`** runs an external command via the `Process` component while reporting its progress through the `DebugFormatterHelper`. The `run()` method accepts a `Process` instance or a command line, an output, an optional error message, an optional callback for streaming and a `verbosity` threshold below which output is hidden.

references.title: Using Events
references.summary: Console applications can hook into command execution through Symfony events by attaching an event dispatcher to the `Application`. Console events are emitted only for the main command being executed.

references.title: Using Events - The `ConsoleEvents::COMMAND` Event
references.summary: `ConsoleEvents::COMMAND` is dispatched just before a command runs and exposes the command, input, output, and application. A listener can disable command execution and cause the application to return the disabled-command code.

references.title: Using Events - The `ConsoleEvents::ERROR` Event
references.summary: `ConsoleEvents::ERROR` is dispatched when an exception is thrown by a command or a console event listener. Listeners can inspect the output, command, exit code, and current error, and can replace the error.

references.title: Using Events - The `ConsoleEvents::TERMINATE` Event
references.summary: `ConsoleEvents::TERMINATE` runs after command execution and is useful for cleanup shared across commands. It also runs after `ConsoleEvents::ERROR` when an exception occurs and allows listeners to change the exit code.

references.title: Using Events - The `ConsoleEvents::SIGNAL` Event
references.summary: `ConsoleEvents::SIGNAL` is dispatched when command execution is interrupted by an operating-system signal. Symfony commands can also handle signals directly by implementing `SignalableCommandInterface`.

references.title: Verbosity Levels
references.summary: Symfony console commands support quiet, normal, verbose, very verbose, and debug verbosity levels. The section maps CLI flags and `SHELL_VERBOSITY` values to `OutputInterface` constants and shows how commands can conditionally write messages.

references.title: Testing - Unit Tests
references.summary: How Symfony treats unit tests as standard PHPUnit tests for individual classes or methods. This section is relevant for exam questions about test placement, naming conventions, autoloading, and running specific PHPUnit test files or directories.

references.title: Testing - The PHPUnit Testing Framework
references.summary: How Symfony integrates with PHPUnit, with the `symfony/test-pack` Flex recipe and the `phpunit.xml.dist` configuration file at the project root. Test classes end with `Test` (e.g. `BlogControllerTest`) and live under `tests/`, and the suite is executed by `./bin/phpunit` (or `vendor/bin/phpunit`).

references.title: Testing - Integration Tests
references.summary: How Symfony's `KernelTestCase` boots the kernel for tests that exercise multiple services together. The kernel class is read from the `KERNEL_CLASS` env var (or overridden via `getKernelClass()`/`createKernel()`), and the kernel is rebooted between tests so each one runs in isolation.

references.title: Testing - Application Tests
references.summary: How Symfony application tests exercise the complete application by making simulated HTTP requests and checking responses. This section is relevant for exam questions about functional test workflow, `WebTestCase`, and the relationship between PHPUnit and Symfony's browser-like test client.

references.title: Testing - Write Your First Application Test
references.summary: How a typical Symfony functional test extends `WebTestCase`, calls `static::createClient()` to obtain a test client, and uses `request()` to drive controllers. The returned `Crawler` enables Symfony assertions such as `assertResponseIsSuccessful()` and `assertSelectorTextContains()`.

references.title: Testing - Set-up your Test Environment
references.summary: How tests run in the `test` environment using configuration from `config/packages/test/`. The kernel is booted via `bootKernel()` and you can override the environment or debug mode (e.g. `bootKernel(['environment' => 'my_test_env', 'debug' => false])`).

references.title: How to Simulate HTTP Authentication in a Functional Test
references.summary: Symfony >= 5.1 exposes `WebTestCase::createClient()->loginUser($user)` to log in **without** going through the form login flow. The method writes a session token directly. For HTTP Basic firewalls use `client->setServerParameter('PHP_AUTH_USER', ...)` / `PHP_AUTH_PW` instead.

references.title: Testing - Making Requests
references.summary: How Symfony's test client simulates browser-like HTTP requests into an application. This section is relevant for exam questions about `request()` arguments, returned crawler objects, redirect handling, browser history, and request isolation.

references.title: The BrowserKit Component
references.summary: How the BrowserKit component simulates a web browser independently of Symfony tests, exposing `AbstractBrowser` for `request()`, link clicks and form submissions. The Symfony test client extends this component and reuses its API for application tests.

references.title: The BrowserKit Component - Making Requests
references.summary: How `AbstractBrowser::request()` performs HTTP method/URL requests and returns a `Crawler` instance. The component also exposes `jsonRequest()` (sets `CONTENT_TYPE` and `HTTP_ACCEPT` to JSON) and `xmlHttpRequest()` (adds the `HTTP_X_REQUESTED_WITH` header) as shortcuts.

references.title: The BrowserKit Component - Clicking Links
references.summary: How `AbstractBrowser::clickLink()` simulates clicking a link by its text content and issues the corresponding HTTP request. The lower-level `click()` method accepts a `Symfony\Component\DomCrawler\Link` object obtained via `selectLink('...')->link()`.

references.title: The BrowserKit Component - Submitting Forms
references.summary: How `AbstractBrowser::submitForm()` selects a form by button text/id/value/name and posts overridden field values, optionally changing the HTTP method or `$_SERVER` parameters. The lower-level `submit()` accepts a `Symfony\Component\DomCrawler\Form` object obtained via `selectButton('...')->form()`.

references.title: The DOM Crawler
references.summary: How the DOM Crawler traverses HTML or XML returned by the Symfony test client. This section is relevant for exam questions about selecting nodes, following links and forms, and extracting text, attributes, or arrays of node data.

references.title: The DomCrawler Component - Node Filtering
references.summary: How `Crawler::filterXPath()` selects nodes with XPath while `Crawler::filter()` accepts CSS selectors when the CssSelector component is installed. Filtering returns a new `Crawler` instance, and `reduce()` accepts an anonymous function to keep or drop nodes (returning `false` removes them).

references.title: The DomCrawler Component - Accessing Node Values
references.summary: How to read information from nodes: `nodeName()`, `text()` (with optional default and trim flag), `innerText()`, `attr()` and `extract()` (which accepts pseudo attributes `_text` and `_name`). The `each()` method runs an anonymous callback on every node and returns an array of results.

references.title: The CssSelector Component
references.summary: How the CssSelector component converts CSS selectors into XPath expressions via `CssSelectorConverter::toXPath()`. It enables CSS-style filtering inside the DomCrawler's `filter()` method and explains which selectors (e.g. `:hover`, `:visited`, pseudo-elements) cannot be translated.

references.title: How to Use the Profiler in a Functional Test - Testing the Profiler Information
references.summary: How functional tests can enable Symfony profiler collection for a request and inspect the resulting profile. This section is relevant for exam questions about `enableProfiler()`, `getProfile()`, collectors, and profiler tokens in failing assertions.

references.title: How to Use the Profiler in a Functional Test
references.summary: Why functional tests should normally test the response only, but may inspect profiler data to enforce metrics such as DB query counts or framework time. The profile object obtained from `$client->getProfile()` exposes all data collectors registered by Symfony.

references.title: How to Use the Profiler in a Functional Test - Enabling the Profiler in Tests
references.summary: How profiling is disabled by default in tests for performance and how `framework.profiler.collect: true` enables it globally; otherwise call `$client->enableProfiler()` before a request to collect data only for the next request. After the request, call `$profile->getCollector('db')` or `$profile->getCollector('time')` and `$profile->getToken()` for diagnostics.

references.title: Testing - Retrieving Services in the Test
references.summary: How integration tests boot the Symfony kernel and access services through the test container. This section is relevant for exam questions about `KernelTestCase`, `bootKernel()`, `static::getContainer()`, and access to public and non-removed private services.

references.title: Testing - Integration Tests
references.summary: How `KernelTestCase` boots the Symfony Kernel via `self::bootKernel()` so a test can fetch services from the dependency injection container. The `KERNEL_CLASS` env var (or the overridable `getKernelClass()`/`createKernel()` methods) determines which kernel class is instantiated.

references.title: Testing - Mocking Dependencies
references.summary: How to swap a service in the test container using `$container->set(ServiceId::class, $mock)`. Private aliases must first be made public in `config/services_test.yaml` (under the `test` environment) so the replacement is honored when other services are resolved.

references.title: Testing - Sending Custom Headers
references.summary: How to configure default headers and server parameters on a Symfony test client. Pass headers (e.g. `HTTP_HOST`, `HTTP_USER_AGENT`) as the second argument of `createClient()` or override them per request via the fifth `request()` argument; custom header names follow RFC 3875 (uppercase, dashes replaced by underscores, prefixed with `HTTP_`, e.g. `HTTP_X_SESSION_TOKEN`).

references.title: How to Test the Interaction of several Clients
references.summary: How to create multiple clients with `static::createClient()` to simulate cross-client interactions. Calling `$client->insulate()` configures a client to run each request in a dedicated, clean PHP process (avoiding global-state side effects); insulated clients are slower and require serializable state.

references.title: How to Customize the Bootstrap Process before Running Tests
references.summary: PHPUnit's `bootstrap.php` (default `tests/bootstrap.php`) sets up the Symfony environment before any test runs — it includes Composer's autoloader and may reset the test database. The `bootstrap` attribute of `phpunit.xml.dist` chooses the file. Custom bootstrap is the place to install fixtures, clear `var/cache/test`, or pre-warm caches.

references.title: Testing - Accessing Internal Objects
references.summary: How the Symfony test client exposes internal objects after a request. `$client->getHistory()` and `$client->getCookieJar()` return BrowserKit collections; `$client->getRequest()`/`getResponse()` return the HttpKernel request and response, while `$client->getInternalRequest()`/`getInternalResponse()` return the BrowserKit ones, and `$client->getCrawler()` returns the parsed DOM Crawler.

references.title: Testing - Reporting Exceptions
references.summary: How Symfony's test client by default catches exceptions thrown during request handling so it can build an error response. Calling `$client->catchExceptions(false)` disables this behavior so the underlying exception bubbles up and is reported by PHPUnit, simplifying debugging of failing application tests.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge
references.summary: What Symfony's PHPUnit Bridge provides for test suites, including deprecation reporting and helpers for time-, DNS-, and class-existence-sensitive tests. This section is relevant for exam questions about `simple-phpunit`, deprecation reports, native-function mocks, and compatibility helpers.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge - Usage
references.summary: How to install the bridge, run tests via `vendor/bin/simple-phpunit` (a wrapper around the original PHPUnit binary), and how to register the `Symfony\Bridge\PhpUnit\SymfonyTestsListener` listener in `phpunit.xml.dist` to receive deprecation reports when not using `simple-phpunit`. The deprecation report classifies notices as Unsilenced, Legacy, and Remaining/Other.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge - Time-sensitive Tests
references.summary: How `Symfony\Bridge\PhpUnit\ClockMock` mocks PHP's built-in `time()`, `microtime()`, `sleep()`, `usleep()`, `gmdate()` and `date()`. Add the `@group time-sensitive` annotation to the class/method (or call `ClockMock::register()`/`ClockMock::withClockMock(true)`) so time advances instantly without waiting.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge - DNS-sensitive Tests
references.summary: How `Symfony\Bridge\PhpUnit\DnsMock` mocks PHP DNS functions such as `checkdnsrr`, `dns_check_record`, `getmxrr`, `gethostbyname` and `dns_get_record`. Tests opt in via the `@group dns-sensitive` annotation and configure responses with `DnsMock::withMockedHosts(['example.com' => [['type' => 'A', 'ip' => '1.2.3.4']]])`.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge - Mark Tests as Legacy
references.summary: How Symfony's PHPUnit Bridge distinguishes tests that intentionally exercise deprecated behavior. This section is relevant for exam questions about `@group legacy`, `Legacy` class prefixes, and `testLegacy*()` method names.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge - Configuration
references.summary: How the `SYMFONY_DEPRECATIONS_HELPER` environment variable controls deprecation handling. Setting it to a regular expression (enclosed in `/`) makes the test suite stop on a matching deprecation, useful when inspecting the stack trace of a particular deprecated call.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge - Making Tests Fail
references.summary: How `SYMFONY_DEPRECATIONS_HELPER` accepts URL-encoded thresholds such as `max[total]=320`, `max[self]=0`, `max[direct]=0` and `max[indirect]=...`. By default any non-legacy, non-silenced deprecation fails the suite (`max[total]=0`); `max[self]=0` is recommended for libraries to ignore vendor deprecations while still policing first-party code.
