references.summary: Documents the `timeout` request option, stream timeouts, non-blocking monitoring with timeout `0`, and the distinction between idle timeouts and maximum request duration. Clarifies that large transfers may run for a long time as long as the transaction remains active.

references.title: HTTP Client - Dealing with Network Errors
references.summary: Explains that network failures such as broken pipes and DNS errors are reported as `TransportExceptionInterface`. Because responses are lazy, these exceptions can occur both when calling `request()` and later when reading response status, headers, or content.

references.title: HTTP Client - PSR-18 and PSR-17
references.summary: Documents the `Psr18Client` adapter that exposes Symfony's HTTP client through the PSR-18 client interface. Also covers its PSR-17 factory support and the need for `psr/http-client` plus a PSR-17 implementation such as `nyholm/psr7`.

references.title: HTTP Client - Consuming Server-Sent Events
references.summary: Shows how `EventSourceHttpClient` consumes server-sent event streams served as `text/event-stream`. Documents the `ServerSentEvent` chunk type and the `getArrayData()` helper for JSON event payloads.

references.title: Upgrading Existing Applications to Symfony Flex
references.summary: Symfony Flex is the Composer-based system that installs Symfony applications and recipes for third-party packages. The page describes the recommended directory structure (`bin/`, `config/`, `public/`, `src/`, `templates/`, `tests/`, `translations/`, `var/`, `vendor/`) and the steps required to convert legacy Symfony applications to it.

references.title: Upgrading Existing Applications to Symfony Flex - Customizing Flex Paths
references.summary: Lists the configurable Flex paths declared in `composer.json` under `extra`: `bin-dir`, `config-dir`, `src-dir`, `var-dir`, and `public-dir`. Useful for exam questions about adapting Symfony's directory layout to legacy or shared-host environments.

references.title: How To Configure and Use Flex Private Recipe Repositories
references.summary: Explains how Flex recipes are JSON manifests stored in a Git repository alongside an `index.json` file, and how `composer.json`'s `extra.symfony.endpoint` configures Flex to fetch recipes from private GitHub or GitLab repositories. Useful for questions about how Flex installs and configures bundles for a project.

references.title: Installing & Setting up the Symfony Framework - Installing Packages
references.summary: Explains Symfony Flex as the Composer plugin that modifies `require`, `update`, and `remove` to automate common package setup tasks. It introduces recipes, `symfony.lock`, the main and contrib recipe repositories, and packs as Composer metapackages unpacked by Flex.

references.title: Symfony Documentation
references.summary: The **Symfony documentation index** — entry point that organizes the docs into Getting Started, Configuration, Forms, Doctrine, Security, Cache, Console, HTTP Cache, Logging, Mailer, Notifier, Performance, Profiler, Sessions, Testing, Validation, plus the bundles and components reference. The page also links the Quick Tour and the Symfony Flex tutorials that drive the recipe-based workflow.

references.title: Flex: Compose your Application
references.summary: **Symfony Flex** is the Composer plugin that turns `composer require <alias>` into a fully-wired feature: it expands aliases (e.g. `mailer`, `orm`, `messenger`) to the right packages and applies a **recipe** that registers the bundle in `bundles.php`, copies default config under `config/packages/`, registers env vars in `.env`, and adds default routes. Recipes are tracked in `symfony.lock`.

references.title: Symfony Code License
references.summary: Symfony source code is released under the MIT license. The page reproduces the full MIT license text and links to the licenses governing the documentation and the Symfony trademarks.

references.title: Coding Standards - License
references.summary: Symfony's coding standards require every PHP source file to carry the MIT license header at the top of the file, before the namespace declaration. This rule applies to all contributed code in the Symfony core repositories.

references.title: Symfony 7.0 LICENSE file (GitHub)
references.summary: Points to the canonical LICENSE file in the 7.0 branch of the `symfony/symfony` repository. It grants the MIT license rights with the standard `(c) 2004-present Fabien Potencier` copyright notice.

references.title: The Contracts Component
references.summary: The Contracts component packages a set of small interfaces (cache, event-dispatcher, http-client, service, translation, deprecation) extracted from Symfony components so they can be required and implemented independently. The page documents the design principles (one sub-namespace per domain, BC with the Symfony components, listing implementations under Composer's `provide`) and how Contracts differ from PHP-FIG PSRs.

references.title: How to Install and Use the Symfony Components
references.summary: Each Symfony component is published as its own Composer package (for example `symfony/finder`) and can be used standalone in any PHP project after requiring `vendor/autoload.php`. Useful for questions about installing components without the full framework.

references.title: The Asset Component
references.summary: The **Asset** component manages URL generation and **versioning** of web assets such as CSS, JavaScript and images. Versioning strategies (e.g. `StaticVersionStrategy`, `JsonManifestVersionStrategy`) append a fingerprint or read manifest entries; named "packages" let you mix several strategies (e.g. local files vs. CDN).

references.title: The Config Component
references.summary: The **Config** component provides utilities to **define and validate configuration** trees. It supplies a `ConfigurationInterface`, a `TreeBuilder` DSL for declaring nodes, multiple loaders (YAML, XML, PHP, INI, directory) and resource trackers used by the kernel's container cache to invalidate when config files change.

references.title: The Console Component
references.summary: The **Console** component eases the creation of **command-line interfaces**. Around `Application` and `Command`, it provides input/output abstractions, helpers (progress bar, table, question, formatter), styling (`SymfonyStyle`), event hooks (`ConsoleEvents::COMMAND`, `TERMINATE`, `ERROR`, `SIGNAL`), and easily testable commands via `CommandTester`.

references.title: The CssSelector Component
references.summary: The **CssSelector** component converts CSS selectors (e.g. `div.menu > a:first-child`) into **XPath** expressions, so they can be used to query an XML/HTML document with PHP's native XPath engine (e.g. via `DomCrawler`).

references.title: The Ldap Component
references.summary: The **Ldap** component connects to an **LDAP server** (OpenLDAP, Active Directory). It exposes a typed `Ldap` client wrapping `bind()`, `query()`, `add()`, `update()` and `delete()` operations and integrates with the Security component for LDAP authentication and user provisioning.

references.title: The Lock Component
references.summary: The **Lock** component provides advisory **locks** to grant exclusive access to a shared resource. Stores include `FlockStore` (filesystem flock), `RedisStore`, `MongoDbStore`, `PdoStore`, `SemaphoreStore`. Locks can be **blocking** or **non-blocking**, support **TTL with auto-release**, and may be **shared** (read) or **exclusive** (write).

references.title: The PropertyAccess Component
references.summary: The **PropertyAccess** component reads from and writes to objects/arrays using a **string property path** (e.g. `address.zipCode`, `[items][0].name`). It looks up public properties, getters/setters/issers/hassers, magic accessors (`__get`/`__set`), and array offsets, and powers form data binding and the `Expression` validation constraint.

references.title: The PropertyInfo Component
references.summary: The **PropertyInfo** component returns metadata about an object's properties — list, type, description, read/write visibility — drawn from multiple sources: PHP attributes, PHPDoc, Doctrine, Reflection, and Serializer metadata. It powers the Form and Serializer components when they need to know property types automatically.

references.title: The Semaphore Component
references.summary: The **Semaphore** component manages **counting semaphores**, a generalisation of locks that allows up to *N* concurrent holders rather than just one. Stores include `RedisStore`, `MongoDbStore`, etc.; like the Lock component it supports TTL and automatic release, and is useful for capping concurrency to an external resource.

references.title: The UID Component
references.summary: The **UID** component provides PHP value objects for unique identifiers, namely **UUID** (`Uuid`, `UuidV1`/`V3`/`V4`/`V5`/`V6`/`V7`/`V8`) and **ULID** (`Ulid`). It includes encoders to base32/58/RFC 4122 string representations and custom Doctrine types so UIDs can be persisted as binary or string columns.

references.title: Loading Resources
references.summary: Explains how the **Validator** component loads constraint metadata from external **resources** — annotations/attributes (`AttributeLoader`), XML files (`XmlFileLoader`), YAML files (`YamlFileLoader`) — through `ValidatorBuilder`. Multiple loaders can be chained via `LoaderChain`.

references.title: The VarExporter Component
references.summary: The **VarExporter** component exports any **serializable** PHP value to plain PHP source code via `VarExporter::export()`. Unlike `serialize()`, the output is human-readable and instantiable without a `unserialize()` call. It also instantiates and populates objects **without invoking their constructor** (`Instantiator::instantiate()`) and supports **lazy ghost** and **lazy proxy** objects since 6.2/7.0.

references.title: The Workflow Component
references.summary: The **Workflow** component provides finite state machines and workflows for any PHP object. It distinguishes **state machines** (a single state at a time) from **workflows** (multiple "places" in parallel via Petri nets). Transitions can be guarded by events (`workflow.<name>.guard.<transition>`), audited via the marking store, and visualised through `bin/console workflow:dump`.

references.title: The Yaml Component
references.summary: The **Yaml** component parses YAML strings into PHP arrays (`Yaml::parse()`, `Yaml::parseFile()`) and dumps PHP arrays back into YAML (`Yaml::dump()`). Parser flags `Yaml::PARSE_OBJECT_FOR_MAP`, `PARSE_DATETIME`, `PARSE_CUSTOM_TAGS`, `PARSE_CONSTANT` opt-in to richer PHP types; dumper flags such as `DUMP_OBJECT_AS_MAP` mirror them.

references.title: Pushing Data to Clients Using the Mercure Protocol
references.summary: **Mercure** is an open protocol — built on Server-Sent Events — for pushing real-time updates from server to client. The MercureBundle integrates a `HubInterface`, a JWT-based publisher and a Twig helper. Clients subscribe via an `EventSource` to a topic URL; the server publishes `Update` messages through `$hub->publish(...)`.

references.title: Creating and Sending Notifications
references.summary: The **Notifier** component is an abstraction over many delivery channels — SMS, chat (Slack, Discord, Teams, …), email (via the Mailer bridge), push and browser notifications. A `Notification` is dispatched to one or more `Recipient`s through registered transports identified by DSNs (`twilio://`, `slack://`, etc.).

references.title: Rate Limiter
references.summary: A **rate limiter** controls how often an event (HTTP request, login attempt, expensive job) may happen. Symfony ships three policies — **fixed window**, **sliding window** and **token bucket** — each with different burst/precision trade-offs. Limiters are configured under `framework.rate_limiter` and consumed via `RateLimiterFactory::create($key)`.

references.title: Asset Preloading and Resource Hints with WebLink
references.summary: The **WebLink** component manages **`Link` HTTP headers** (RFC 5988) used by browsers and HTTP/2 servers for **preload**, **preconnect**, **dns-prefetch**, **prefetch**, and **prerender** resource hints. It exposes Twig helpers (`{{ preload(...) }}`, `{{ preconnect(...) }}`, …) so templates can declare hints inline; the bundle's response listener writes the matching `Link` header.

references.title: The DependencyInjection Component
references.summary: The **DependencyInjection** component is the basis of Symfony's service container. It defines services and their dependencies (autowiring, autoconfiguration, factories, decorators), supports compiler passes for low-level container customization, and exposes loaders that read service definitions from YAML, XML, PHP, and PHP attributes.

references.title: The Form Component
references.summary: The **Form** component creates HTML forms and handles their submitted data. It binds form types to data objects through configurable data mappers, renders fields through themable templates, and integrates with the Validator and PropertyAccess components for validation and read/write access. The framework wires it into the FrameworkBundle, but the component is also usable stand-alone.

references.title: The Messenger Component
references.summary: The **Messenger** component dispatches messages to handlers through a configurable bus and ships them to transports (in-memory, AMQP, Doctrine, Redis, etc.) for asynchronous processing. It distinguishes the **command bus**, **query bus**, and **event bus** patterns and supports middleware, retries, failure transports, and message stamps.

references.title: The OptionsResolver Component
references.summary: The **OptionsResolver** component is an enhanced replacement for PHP's `array_replace_recursive()` for processing options arrays. It supports **defaults**, **required** options, **type checks**, **value validation**, **deferred normalizers**, **deprecation messages**, and **nested option sets**. Symfony's Form types use it for their `configureOptions()` method.

references.title: The PSR-7 Bridge
references.summary: The **PSR-7 Bridge** converts between Symfony's `Request`/`Response` objects and **PSR-7** `RequestInterface` / `ResponseInterface` instances. The bridge is delivered as `symfony/psr-http-message-bridge` and integrates with any PSR-17 factory implementation to produce PSR-7 messages.

references.title: The String Component
references.summary: The **String** component provides an object-oriented API for **Unicode strings**. It exposes three classes — `ByteString` (raw bytes), `CodePointString` (Unicode code points), `UnicodeString` (grapheme clusters) — with chainable methods (`upper()`, `lower()`, `truncate()`, `slug()`, `chunk()`, `padBoth()`, etc.). The static `u()`, `b()`, `s()` shortcuts construct the matching object.

references.title: The Validator Component
references.summary: The **Validator** component implements `Symfony\Component\Validator\Validator\ValidatorInterface` to validate values and objects against constraints described as PHP attributes, YAML, XML, or PHP. The result is a `ConstraintViolationListInterface` of typed violations with paths, messages, codes, and parameters.

references.title: Validator Component Metadata
references.summary: Explains the **metadata model** behind constraint loading. Constraints are attached to a `ClassMetadata` (or its `PropertyMetadata` / `GetterMetadata` children) at boot via metadata loaders (PHP attribute, XML, YAML). At runtime the validator walks the metadata graph to evaluate constraints in the requested validation groups.

references.title: The DomCrawler Component
references.summary: The **DomCrawler** component traverses HTML and XML documents through an immutable `Crawler` object built from a string, a DOM document, or a server response. It supports CSS selectors (via the CssSelector component), XPath, and form submission emulation, and is the engine behind Symfony's functional test client.

references.title: The PSR-7 Bridge
references.summary: The `symfony/psr-http-message-bridge` package converts HttpFoundation `Request`/`Response` objects to and from PSR-7 messages and back. Conversion in either direction goes through `PsrHttpFactory` (HttpFoundation -> PSR-7) and `HttpFoundationFactory` (PSR-7 -> HttpFoundation), and a separate PSR-7/PSR-17 implementation such as `nyholm/psr7` is required.

references.title: Symfony Bridges (7.0 source)
references.summary: Source tree listing every official Symfony Bridge shipped on the 7.0 branch (Doctrine, Monolog, PsrHttpMessage, ProxyManager, PhpUnit, Twig). Bridges glue Symfony components to third-party libraries; unlike components, they cannot be used without that target library.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Use the Default Directory Structure
references.summary: Recommends following Symfony's default directory layout (`bin/`, `config/packages/`, `config/routes/`, `public/`, `src/Controller/`, `src/Entity/`, `templates/`, `tests/`, `translations/`, `var/cache/`, `var/log/`, `vendor/`). The structure is flat and not coupled to Symfony itself.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Don't Create any Bundle to Organize your Application Logic
references.summary: Application code should live under the `App\` namespace in `src/`, not inside bundles. Bundles are only intended for reusable, distributable code; PHP namespaces are the right tool for organizing application-specific logic.

references.title: How to Override Symfony's default Directory Structure
references.summary: Explains which Symfony directories can be relocated and how: cache and log directories via `Kernel::getCacheDir()`/`getLogDir()` (or `APP_CACHE_DIR`/`APP_LOG_DIR` env vars), templates via `twig.default_path`, translations via `framework.translator.default_path`, public dir via `extra.public-dir`, vendor via `config.vendor-dir`, and dotenv via `extra.runtime.dotenv_path`. The `config/` directory itself is hardcoded and cannot be overridden.

references.title: The Bundle System
references.summary: Explains bundles as plugin-like packages that provide reusable code and features, while application code should no longer be organized as bundles. It shows that bundles are enabled per environment in `config/bundles.php`, with Flex automating that file in default applications.

references.title: The Architecture
references.summary: Quick-tour article that explains the **canonical Symfony directory layout** — `bin/`, `config/`, `public/`, `src/`, `templates/`, `tests/`, `translations/`, `var/`, `vendor/` — and how the kernel boots from `public/index.php` through `Kernel`, `Container`, then to controllers via the routing/firewall/argument-resolver pipeline.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component
references.summary: The HttpKernel component implements the request-handling pipeline. The `HttpKernel::handle()` method takes a `Request` and produces a `Response` by dispatching a fixed sequence of kernel events that allow listeners to short-circuit the flow or modify the result.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component - The Request-Response Lifecycle
references.summary: Describes the kernel event sequence for request handling. The flow includes `kernel.request`, controller resolution, `kernel.controller`, controller arguments resolution, controller call, optional `kernel.view`, `kernel.response`, and `kernel.terminate`, with `kernel.exception` triggered on any thrown exception.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component - Sub Requests
references.summary: Explains the request type argument accepted by `HttpKernel::handle()`. The constants `HttpKernelInterface::MAIN_REQUEST` and `HttpKernelInterface::SUB_REQUEST` distinguish the main request from internal sub-requests, such as those used by `render()` Twig calls or fragment URIs.

references.title: Understanding how the Front Controller, Kernel and Environments Work together
references.summary: Explains how `public/index.php` (the front controller) bootstraps autoload and runtime, instantiates the application `Kernel` with an environment and debug flag, then delegates request handling to it. Defines what the Kernel class does and how environments select cache, log and configuration directories.

references.title: Building your own Framework with the MicroKernelTrait - The Methods of a "Micro" Kernel
references.summary: Documents the three methods required by a kernel using `MicroKernelTrait`: `registerBundles()`, `configureContainer()`, and `configureRoutes()`. This is useful for understanding how a compact Symfony kernel defines bundles, services, configuration, and routes.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component - Handling Exceptions: the kernel.exception Event
references.summary: When an exception escapes from the controller, HttpKernel dispatches a `kernel.exception` event with an `ExceptionEvent`. Listeners can call `setResponse()` on the event to convert the exception into a `Response`; if no listener does so, the exception is rethrown by the kernel.

references.title: Built-in Symfony Events - kernel.exception
references.summary: Reference entry for `KernelEvents::EXCEPTION`, dispatched whenever any exception is thrown during request handling. Used by Symfony to format exception responses and by user listeners to map domain exceptions to HTTP responses.

references.title: Events and Event Listeners
references.summary: Framework-level guide for registering listeners and subscribers. Includes a complete `ExceptionListener` example using `ExceptionEvent::getThrowable()` and `setResponse()`, and shows how to choose listener priorities so that custom error handling runs before or after Symfony's defaults.

references.title: The EventDispatcher Component
references.summary: Describes the EventDispatcher component as an implementation of the Mediator/Observer patterns. Covers events, the central dispatcher object, registering listeners with `addListener($eventName, $callable, $priority)` and subscribers via `addSubscriber()`.

references.title: The EventDispatcher Component - Using Event Subscribers
references.summary: Event subscribers implement `EventSubscriberInterface::getSubscribedEvents()`, which returns an array mapping event names to a method name, a `[method, priority]` pair, or a list of such pairs. The dispatcher registers all of those listeners automatically when the subscriber is added.

references.title: The EventDispatcher Component - Stopping Event Flow/Propagation
references.summary: Listeners can stop further propagation of the current event by calling `Event::stopPropagation()`. After dispatch, callers can detect that with `Event::isPropagationStopped()`. Useful for short-circuiting kernel events such as `kernel.request`.

references.title: Events and Event Listeners
references.summary: Framework-level guide. Covers the `kernel.event_listener` and `kernel.event_subscriber` service tags, the optional `event`, `method`, `priority` and `dispatcher` tag attributes, the rule that the `__invoke()` method is called when no `method` is given, and how to register listeners via the `#[AsEventListener]` PHP attribute.

references.title: Built-in Symfony Events
references.summary: Reference of every Symfony kernel event in dispatch order: `kernel.request`, `kernel.controller`, `kernel.controller_arguments`, `kernel.view`, `kernel.response`, `kernel.finish_request`, `kernel.terminate`, `kernel.exception`. Each entry lists the event class and use cases.

references.title: The Generic Event Object
references.summary: The base `Event` class deliberately exposes no payload, encouraging applications to write **typed event subclasses**. As a quick alternative, `GenericEvent` carries a `subject`, an associative `arguments` map, and array-access to those arguments, making it convenient for one-off events or third-party libraries that don't ship dedicated event classes.

references.title: The Immutable Event Dispatcher
references.summary: The **`ImmutableEventDispatcher`** wraps another dispatcher to forbid registering or removing listeners and subscribers at runtime. It is useful in long-running workers / library APIs where the event-listener graph must be **frozen** to prevent late mutations.

references.title: The Traceable Event Dispatcher
references.summary: The **`TraceableEventDispatcher`** wraps another dispatcher and records, for each dispatched event, which listeners were called, in what order, and how long they took (via `Stopwatch`). It powers the WebProfiler's **Events** panel.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices
references.summary: Indexes Symfony's official best practices for application development. It covers project creation, configuration, business logic, controllers, templates, forms, internationalization, security, web assets, and tests.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Use Environment Variables for Infrastructure Configuration
references.summary: Covers the best-practice split between environment variables and container parameters. Environment variables and `.env` files are used for values that change between machines, while parameters are used for values that change application behavior but stay the same across machines.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Make your Controller Extend the AbstractController Base Controller
references.summary: Covers the best-practice role of `AbstractController` in application controllers. Extending it provides shortcuts such as `render()`, `redirectToRoute()`, `denyAccessUnlessGranted()`, and the `$this->container->get()` accessor for the small allowlist of services exposed by `getSubscribedServices()`.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Use Attributes to Configure Routing, Caching, and Security
references.summary: Covers the best-practice preference for PHP attributes on controller actions. Attributes such as `#[Route]`, `#[Cache]`, and `#[IsGranted]` keep configuration next to the code it applies to instead of in separate YAML/XML/PHP configuration files.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Define a Single Firewall
references.summary: Recommends a single firewall for most applications, using `access_control` rules under `security.access_control` to restrict areas, instead of stacking multiple firewalls. Multiple firewalls are appropriate only when the authentication systems are genuinely different (for example a stateless API alongside a session-based UI).

references.title: The Release Process - Development
references.summary: Each minor or major version has a six-month development cycle split into four months of development (new features) and two months of stabilization (bug fixes, ecosystem catch-up). During development, features can be reverted if they will not be ready or stable enough for the final release.

references.title: Maintenance
references.summary: Defines what is allowed in patch (bug-fix) releases: bug fixes with new tests, support for newer PHP versions, translations, ICU updates. Forbids new features, security hardening, BC breaks (except to fix a security issue), new public API, new configuration options, and changes to exception messages.

references.title: Experimental Features
references.summary: Features marked `@experimental` are explicitly excluded from Symfony's BC promise. They can only be experimental for a single minor version (extendable case-by-case), can never be introduced in an LTS release, and the changelog must document any breaking changes plus an upgrade path.

references.title: Our Backward Compatibility Promise
references.summary: Symfony follows Semantic Versioning: only major releases (5.0, 6.0, 7.0...) may break BC, and the BC promise applies to all minor releases of the same major branch. Code marked `@internal`, `@experimental`, classes under `*\Tests\`, and BC breaks required to fix security issues are explicitly excluded from the promise.

references.title: Our Backward Compatibility Promise - Using our Interfaces
references.summary: Application code may type-hint Symfony interfaces and call any of their methods with full BC guarantees, except for interfaces tagged `@internal`. When implementing a Symfony interface, the application is also free to add arguments with defaults, add a return type, etc., without losing BC.

references.title: Our Backward Compatibility Promise - Using our Classes
references.summary: Symfony guarantees BC for instantiating classes, type-hinting against them, calling public methods, accessing public properties, and (when extending) overriding public/protected members. It does *not* guarantee BC when adding new properties or new methods that may collide with subclass code, or when accessing private members via reflection.

references.title: Our Backward Compatibility Promise - Making Code Changes in a Backward Compatible Way
references.summary: Step-by-step recipe for adding a new argument to a public method without breaking BC: declare the argument as a comment in the signature, document it in PHPDoc, retrieve it via `func_num_args()`/`func_get_arg()`, and trigger a deprecation when the argument is missing. The argument is uncommented only in the next major version.

references.title: Experimental Features
references.summary: Features marked `@experimental` are explicitly outside the BC promise. They are limited to a single minor version, are never introduced into an LTS release, and any breaking changes must be explained in the changelog with an upgrade path.

references.title: Conventions - Deprecating Code
references.summary: Defines how Symfony deprecates code: deprecations may only land on the next minor version, never on a brand-new class/method. A `@deprecated` PHPDoc states the version and replacement, and `trigger_deprecation('symfony/package', 'X.Y', 'message')` from `symfony/deprecation-contracts` raises the runtime warning. The CHANGELOG, UPGRADE-X.Y and UPGRADE-X+1.0 files must all be updated in the same pull request.

references.title: Conventions - Removing Deprecated Code
references.summary: Deprecated code can only be removed in the next major version (`6.0`, `7.0`, ...), which means roughly every two years. The removal must be recorded in the CHANGELOG of the affected component.

references.title: symfony/deprecation-contracts (GitHub)
references.summary: Covers the package that ships the `trigger_deprecation()` function used by Symfony to raise deprecation notices. These notices stay silent unless the `E_USER_DEPRECATED` error reporting bit is enabled, typically by the PHPUnit Bridge or by Symfony's own deprecation tooling.

references.title: How to Override any Part of a Bundle
references.summary: Documents the supported override points for third-party bundles. These include templates under `templates/bundles/<BundleName>/`, services through service decoration or compiler passes, Doctrine mapped superclasses, validation groups, and translations by reusing the bundle's translation domain.

references.title: How to Load Service Configuration inside a Bundle
references.summary: Explains how bundles load their own service configuration. They can use `AbstractBundle::loadExtension()` for new bundles, called only at compile time, or a traditional DI Extension class under `<Bundle>\DependencyInjection\<Bundle>Extension` whose alias is the snake-cased bundle name without the `Bundle` suffix.

references.title: How to Simplify Configuration of Multiple Bundles
references.summary: Explains how bundles can inject configuration into other bundles before their own extension load runs. They can implement `PrependExtensionInterface::prepend()` or use `AbstractBundle::prependExtension()`, which is the canonical way for one bundle to override another bundle's defaults.

references.title: How to Override Symfony's default Directory Structure
references.summary: Explains how to relocate cache, log, templates, translations, public, vendor, and dotenv directories. The `config/` directory is hardcoded and cannot be overridden.

references.title: The Release Process
references.summary: Symfony follows Semantic Versioning with a strict time-based schedule: a patch every month, a minor every six months (May and November), and a major every two years in November of odd years. The same minor cycle has four months of development followed by two months of stabilization.

references.title: The Release Process - Maintenance
references.summary: Each major branch contains five minor versions (X.0..X.4). The last minor of a branch is the LTS. Standard versions receive bug fixes and security fixes for 8 months each, while LTS versions receive 3 years of bug fixes and 4 years of security fixes.

references.title: The Release Process - Backward Compatibility
references.summary: When a major version is being prepared, Symfony develops the new major (e.g. 7.0) and the previous LTS (e.g. 6.4) in parallel: both ship the same new features, but the LTS keeps the deprecated APIs while the new major removes them, so projects can clean up deprecations on the LTS before upgrading.

references.title: Maintenance
references.summary: Patch versions are restricted to bug fixes plus a small set of allowed changes (PHP/OS support, translations, external data updates). New features, security hardening, new configuration options, new dependencies, and changes to exception messages are not allowed in patch releases.

references.title: The Contracts Component
references.summary: Symfony Contracts are small, domain-specific abstraction packages (cache, event-dispatcher, http-client, service, translation, deprecation) extracted from the components. Implementations declare themselves via Composer's `provide` key using the `symfony/*-implementation` convention.

references.title: The DependencyInjection Component
references.summary: Symfony's DependencyInjection container is fully PSR-11 compliant: it implements `Psr\Container\ContainerInterface`. The page documents how to define services in PHP and how to use the container as a PSR-11 container in non-Symfony applications.

references.title: Coding Standards
references.summary: Symfony's coding standards are based on PSR-1, PSR-2, PSR-4 and PSR-12. The `PHP CS Fixer` tool is used to enforce them, and any contribution is expected to pass it before being merged.

references.title: HTTP Client - PSR-18 and PSR-17
references.summary: Documents the `Psr18Client` adapter shipped by Symfony HttpClient. The adapter implements PSR-18 (`Psr\Http\Client\ClientInterface`) and PSR-17 message factories so that PSR-18-compatible code can run on top of Symfony's HttpClient.

references.title: PSRs - PHP-FIG
references.summary: PHP-FIG's catalog of PHP Standards Recommendations. Useful as the normative source for PSR-3 (logger), PSR-6/PSR-16 (cache), PSR-7/PSR-15/PSR-17/PSR-18 (HTTP messages, server middleware, factories, client) and PSR-11 (container) referenced by Symfony components.

references.title: Adapters For Interoperability between PSR-6 and PSR-16 Cache
references.summary: The **Cache** component bridges **PSR-6** (`CacheItemPoolInterface`) and **PSR-16** (`SimpleCacheInterface`) in both directions. `Psr16Cache` wraps a PSR-6 pool to expose the simpler PSR-16 API; `Psr6Cache` (a.k.a. `SimpleCacheAdapter`) wraps a PSR-16 cache to expose the richer PSR-6 API.

references.title: The PSR-7 Bridge
references.summary: The **PSR-7 Bridge** (`symfony/psr-http-message-bridge`) converts between Symfony `Request`/`Response` and **PSR-7** message objects (`RequestInterface`, `ResponseInterface`, `ServerRequestInterface`, `StreamInterface`). It depends on a PSR-17 factory implementation to produce concrete PSR-7 instances.

references.title: Coding Standards - Naming Conventions
references.summary: Defines Symfony's naming and casing rules. It covers camelCase for variables and methods, snake_case for configuration parameters and Twig variables, SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE for constants, UpperCamelCase for class/interface/trait/enum names and enum cases, plus suffixes such as `Interface`, `Trait`, `Exception` and the `Abstract` prefix for abstract classes.

references.title: Coding Standards - Service Naming Conventions
references.summary: Service identifiers should match the FQCN of their class (for example `App\EventSubscriber\UserSubscriber`). When several services share a class, the FQCN identifies the main one and the others use lowercase, dot-separated identifiers (for example `fos_user.something.service_name`).

references.title: Conventions - Naming a Method
references.summary: When a class has a single 'main' relation, normalized verbs are used (`get`, `set`, `has`, `all`, `replace`, `remove`, `clear`, `add`, `count`, etc.). For other relations, a suffix is appended (`getXXX`, `setXXX`, `removeXXX`, ...). `setXXX()` may add new entries while `replaceXXX()` must throw on unknown keys.

references.title: Conventions - Naming Commands and Options
references.summary: Explains the naming style for Symfony Console commands and their options. They must be named and described using the English imperative mood, for example `run` rather than `runs` and `list` rather than `lists`, to stay consistent with traditional Unix command-line interfaces.

references.title: Best Practices for Reusable Bundles - Bundle Name
references.summary: Defines reusable bundle naming rules: the namespace must follow PSR-4, end with a short name ending in `Bundle`, and the bundle class name should be short, descriptive, prefixed by the vendor or category, and suffixed with `Bundle`. The section also explains the conventional bundle alias, such as `acme_blog` for `AcmeBlogBundle`.

references.title: Controller - A Basic Controller
references.summary: Describes the conventions for writing a Symfony controller class. The class is suffixed with Controller by convention, the controller is usually a public method on that class, and the action receives a Request and returns a Response object.

references.title: How to Define Controllers as Services - Invokable Controllers
references.summary: Describes how a controller can define a single action through the __invoke() method following the ADR (Action-Domain-Responder) pattern. Routing then references the class itself (without ::method) as the _controller value.

references.title: How to Define Controllers as Services
references.summary: Explains controller registration as services and the use of the AsController PHP attribute and the controller.service_arguments tag, which let services be injected as action arguments. With the default services.yaml, controllers extending AbstractController are registered automatically.

references.title: Controller - The Base Controller Class & Services
references.summary: Introduces Symfony\Bundle\FrameworkBundle\Controller\AbstractController as the optional base controller class that exposes shortcut helpers such as render(), generateUrl(), redirectToRoute() and createNotFoundException(). Extending it gives controllers access to a service locator with the most common services.

references.title: Controller - Fetching Services
references.summary: Explains that controllers can receive services through type-hinted action arguments (autowiring) and that the Autowire attribute lets you target a specific service id or container parameter. Constructor injection also works for controllers that are services.

references.title: Controller - Accessing Configuration Values
references.summary: Documents the AbstractController::getParameter() helper which fetches any configuration parameter (such as kernel.project_dir) from the service container. Useful inside controllers extending AbstractController.

references.title: Controller - Sending Early Hints
references.summary: Describes the AbstractController::sendEarlyHints() helper. It returns a 103 Early Hints informational response with Link headers so the browser can preload assets while the controller is still building the final Response.

references.title: How to Define Controllers as Services - Alternatives to base Controller Methods
references.summary: Explains that extending AbstractController is optional. Controllers can be plain classes that receive services through dependency injection and use them directly (e.g. injecting Twig\Environment instead of calling $this->render()).

references.title: Controller - The Request object as a Controller Argument
references.summary: Explains that Symfony injects the current Request into a controller action by adding an argument type-hinted with Symfony\Component\HttpFoundation\Request. The Request gives access to query parameters, headers, files and other input.

references.title: Controller - Mapping Query Parameters Individually
references.summary: Documents the MapQueryParameter attribute, which automatically populates a controller action argument from a single query string parameter. It also covers the filter option using PHP FILTER_VALIDATE_* constants.

references.title: Controller - Mapping The Whole Query String
references.summary: Documents the MapQueryString attribute which deserializes and validates the entire query string into a typed DTO. Validation failures default to HTTP 404 but can be customized with validationGroups and validationFailedStatusCode.

references.title: Controller - Mapping Request Payload
references.summary: Documents the MapRequestPayload attribute which maps the request body (e.g. JSON, form data) into a typed DTO. The default validation failed status code is 422 and acceptFormat, validationGroups and a custom resolver can be configured.

references.title: Controller - The Request and Response Object
references.summary: Covers common Request methods used in controllers, including query/getPayload to read GET/POST data. It also shows server, files, cookies and headers ParameterBag accessors, plus helpers like isXmlHttpRequest() and getPreferredLanguage().

references.title: Controller - Rendering Templates
references.summary: Documents AbstractController::render() which renders a Twig template and wraps its output in a Response object that the controller returns. The second argument provides template variables.

references.title: Controller - Returning JSON Response
references.summary: Documents the AbstractController::json() shortcut. It returns a JsonResponse with the right Content-Type, accepting optional status, headers and serializer context. When the serializer is enabled it is used; otherwise data is encoded with json_encode().

references.title: Controller - Streaming File Responses
references.summary: Documents AbstractController::file() which returns a BinaryFileResponse to serve a file. By default the file is sent as an attachment, but DISPOSITION_INLINE can render it inline and a custom file name can be provided.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Response
references.summary: Describes Symfony\Component\HttpFoundation\Response and how to set a status code via the constructor or setStatusCode(). It also covers reading and writing headers via the public headers ResponseHeaderBag and producing the final HTTP response with send().

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Setting Cookies
references.summary: Documents how to set and clear response cookies through the public Response::$headers ResponseHeaderBag. setCookie() takes a Symfony\Component\HttpFoundation\Cookie instance, clearCookie() removes a cookie, and Cookie::create() / Cookie::fromString() / fluent with*() methods build Cookie objects.

references.title: Controller - The Request and Response Object
references.summary: Shows that incoming cookies are accessible from a controller via $request->cookies->get('NAME'). The cookies property is a ParameterBag exposing the COOKIE values that were sent with the request.

references.title: Sessions - Basic Usage
references.summary: Explains that the session is reached from a controller via $request->getSession() (with a Request type-hint) or via the RequestStack service in services. The Symfony session replaces direct use of $_SESSION and native session_*() functions.

references.title: Sessions - Session Attributes
references.summary: Documents the SessionInterface attribute API: set(), get() with an optional default, has() and remove(). Sessions are automatically started whenever you read or check session data, so anonymous endpoints should avoid touching the session.

references.title: Sessions
references.summary: Top-level overview of the HttpFoundation session subsystem in Symfony. Sessions are lazily started — they are only opened when code reads from or writes to the session — and they replace the native $_SESSION superglobal usage.

references.title: Sessions - Flash Messages
references.summary: Defines flash messages as session messages meant to be displayed exactly once: they vanish from the session as soon as they are retrieved. They are stored under arbitrary keys (notice, warning, error...) in the FlashBag and read in templates via app.flashes().

references.title: Controller - Managing the Session
references.summary: Documents the AbstractController::addFlash() shortcut. It pushes a flash message under a given type into the session FlashBag and is equivalent to $request->getSession()->getFlashBag()->add($type, $message).

references.title: Controller - Redirecting
references.summary: Documents the AbstractController::redirectToRoute() and redirect() helpers. redirectToRoute() targets a named route (with optional route parameters and HTTP status), and redirect() targets an arbitrary URL. Both return a RedirectResponse. The default status is 302 and 301 produces a permanent redirect.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Redirecting the User
references.summary: Describes Symfony\Component\HttpFoundation\RedirectResponse, the dedicated Response subclass used to send a Location header to the client. redirectToRoute() in the AbstractController is a shortcut around new RedirectResponse(generateUrl(...)).

references.title: How to Forward Requests to another Controller
references.summary: Documents AbstractController::forward(). Unlike a redirect, forward() makes an internal sub-request, dispatches it to the target controller and returns the resulting Response without changing the URL in the browser. Arguments are matched by name to the target method's parameters.

references.title: Controller - Managing Errors and 404 Pages
references.summary: Documents how to trigger a 404 from a controller. AbstractController::createNotFoundException() returns a NotFoundHttpException whose status becomes 404 when thrown. Other HttpException subclasses set the response status; non-HttpException throwables become 500.

references.title: How to Customize Error Pages - Overriding the Default Error Templates
references.summary: Explains the template-naming logic used by TwigErrorRenderer to render production error pages: it first looks for templates/bundles/TwigBundle/Exception/error<status>.html.twig (e.g. error404.html.twig) and falls back to error.html.twig if no status-specific template exists.

references.title: How to Customize Error Pages - Example 404 Error Template
references.summary: Shows the layout and variables (status_code, status_text, exception) that the error template receives. It is rendered for production 404 pages and can extend the application's base layout to keep the look and feel consistent.

references.title: How to Customize Error Pages
references.summary: Top-level overview of how Symfony renders error pages. In production a generic page is shown; in development a detailed exception page is displayed. Customization is possible via templates, custom error controller, kernel.exception listeners or Serializer normalizers for non-HTML output.

references.title: How to Upload Files
references.summary: Walks through processing file uploads in a controller. Files reach the controller as Symfony\Component\HttpFoundation\File\UploadedFile objects, must be validated (e.g. with the File constraint), and are typically renamed (using SluggerInterface and uniqid) and moved to the target directory with UploadedFile::move().

references.title: How to Upload Files - Creating an Uploader Service
references.summary: Recommends extracting upload logic into a dedicated service (e.g. FileUploader) injected into the controller. The service receives the UploadedFile, generates a safe unique filename and calls $file->move($targetDirectory, $fileName), keeping controllers thin.

references.title: Routing - Redirecting to URLs and Routes Directly from a Route
references.summary: Documents Symfony\Bundle\FrameworkBundle\Controller\RedirectController. Wired as the controller of a route, it redirects to another route (route default) or absolute path/URL (path default). Supports permanent (301), keepQueryParams, keepRequestMethod (307/308) and ignoreAttributes.

references.title: Templates - Rendering a Template Directly from a Route
references.summary: Documents Symfony\Bundle\FrameworkBundle\Controller\TemplateController. It renders a Twig template directly from the route definition with no application controller. Defaults are template (path), statusCode, maxAge/sharedAge for HTTP cache, private flag and an optional context array.

references.title: Extending Action Argument Resolving
references.summary: Explains that controller action arguments are resolved by HttpKernel's ArgumentResolver, which delegates to a chain of value resolvers. Custom resolvers can be created and registered to extend how arguments are filled in.

references.title: Extending Action Argument Resolving - Built-In Value Resolvers
references.summary: Lists Symfony's built-in HttpKernel resolvers for backed enums, request payloads, request attributes, dates, Request objects, services, sessions, defaults, UIDs and variadic arguments. It also identifies additional resolvers provided by Security, Doctrine and PSR-7 integration, including the conditions and failure behavior they use.

references.title: Extending Action Argument Resolving - Managing Value Resolvers
references.summary: Explains that for each argument every resolver tagged with controller.argument_value_resolver is tried in priority order until one returns a value. The ValueResolver attribute can target a specific resolver by name or disable it for that argument.

references.title: Extending Action Argument Resolving - Adding a Custom Value Resolver
references.summary: Describes how to write a custom resolver implementing ValueResolverInterface::resolve(), which receives the current Request and an ArgumentMetadata. The method must return an iterable: an empty array if it cannot resolve the argument, or one or more values otherwise (variadic arguments need multiple).

references.title: Extending Action Argument Resolving - controller.argument_value_resolver
references.summary: Explains that services implementing ValueResolverInterface are auto-tagged with controller.argument_value_resolver. The tag accepts a priority and a name; for reference, RequestAttributeValueResolver runs at priority 100, so resolvers reading request attributes should use 100 or higher and others should use a lower priority.

references.title: Extending Action Argument Resolving - controller.targeted_value_resolver
references.summary: Documents the controller.targeted_value_resolver tag and the AsTargetedValueResolver attribute. A targeted resolver only runs when explicitly selected by the ValueResolver attribute on a controller argument, instead of being part of the regular chain.

references.title: Routing - Creating Routes as Attributes
references.summary: Defining routes with the #[Route] PHP attribute next to controller actions. Symfony recommends attributes because the route and the controller live in the same place; routes are loaded by importing controller classes with type 'attribute' in config/routes/attributes.yaml. The route name argument is the unique identifier used later to generate URLs.

references.title: Routing - Creating Routes in YAML, XML or PHP Files
references.summary: Defining routes in dedicated YAML files using path and controller keys, where controller has the format 'controller_class::method_name' (and the method part is omitted when the action is implemented as __invoke()). By default Symfony loads routes defined in YAML and PHP formats; XML must be enabled explicitly in the kernel.

references.title: Routing - Route Groups and Prefixes
references.summary: Sharing options across a set of routes. With attributes, common configuration is placed on a #[Route] attribute attached to the controller class; with YAML, common options are passed when importing the resource (prefix, name_prefix, requirements, trailing_slash_on_root, exclude). The trailing_slash_on_root option controls whether an empty imported path becomes '/blog/' or '/blog'.

references.title: Routing - Getting the Route Name and Parameters
references.summary: Once a route matches, Symfony stores the route name and the matched parameters as request attributes. The current route name is available via the request attribute '_route' and the matched parameters via '_route_params'. In templates, the Twig 'app' global variable exposes 'app.current_route' and 'app.current_route_parameters'.

references.title: Routing - Priority Parameter
references.summary: Routes are evaluated in definition order, which can be hard to control for PHP attributes. The `priority` argument lets attribute routes with higher priority be sorted before lower-priority routes; omitted priority defaults to `0`.

references.title: Routing - Route Aliasing
references.summary: Route aliases let multiple route names point to the same route, so both names generate the same URL. Symfony supports aliases in YAML and PHP route configuration, which is useful while renaming public route names.

references.title: Routing - Deprecating Route Aliases
references.summary: A route alias can be marked deprecated with package, version, and message metadata. This lets applications keep an old route name temporarily while emitting a deprecation notice when the alias is used.

references.title: Routing - Parameters Validation
references.summary: Restricting route parameters with the `requirements` option, which uses PHP regular expressions to constrain matched values. The `Requirement` enum collects common patterns (digits, dates, UUIDs); requirements can be inlined per parameter using the `{name<regex>}` syntax. Setting the `utf8` route option to true makes `.` match any UTF-8 character instead of a single byte.

references.title: Routing - Slash Characters in Route Parameters
references.summary: By default route parameters cannot contain `/` because slashes separate URL segments. Allowing slashes requires a permissive requirement such as `.+`. When the route also includes the special `{_format}` parameter, `.+` is unsafe because it greedily eats dots; `[^.]+` should be used instead.

references.title: Routing - Parameter Conversion
references.summary: Symfony can convert route parameters into typed controller objects, such as resolving a `BlogPost` argument from a `{slug}` route parameter. If no matching object is found, Symfony automatically generates a 404 response.

concepts.concept: Set default values to URL parameters

references.title: Routing - Optional Parameters
references.summary: Making a route parameter optional by giving it a default value. With attributes the default lives in the controller method signature; with YAML/PHP files it is set with the `defaults` option. Inline syntax `{name?value}` defines a default; `{name?}` defaults to null. Adding `!` before the parameter name (e.g. `{!page}`) forces the default value to always be included in generated URLs.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs
references.summary: The router is bidirectional: it matches URLs to controllers and generates URLs for routes by name. Route names must therefore be unique; when not declared explicitly Symfony auto-generates a name based on the FQCN, and an alias is added for invokable controllers and for any method that defines exactly one route.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs in Controllers
references.summary: Controllers extending `AbstractController` use `generateUrl($name, $parameters, $referenceType)`. The third argument selects the URL form via the `UrlGeneratorInterface` constants such as `ABSOLUTE_URL` and `ABSOLUTE_PATH` (the default). Extra parameters that do not match a route placeholder are appended to the URL as query string entries.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs in Services
references.summary: Services generate URLs by injecting `Symfony\Component\Routing\Generator\UrlGeneratorInterface` and calling its `generate()` method. The signature mirrors `generateUrl()` in controllers: route name, parameters, and an optional reference type.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs in Templates
references.summary: Twig provides the `path()` and `url()` functions to build a relative path and an absolute URL respectively for a named route. They follow the same parameter rules as the controller and service helpers.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs in Commands
references.summary: Commands run outside an HTTP request, so by default absolute URLs use `http://localhost/`. Configure `framework.router.default_uri` to set the base URI used by URL generation in CLI contexts; this also drives the asset request context unless overridden via `asset.request_context.base_path` and `asset.request_context.secure`.

references.title: Routing - Checking if a Route Exists
references.summary: Avoid `Router::getRouteCollection()` for existence checks because it rebuilds the routing cache. Instead, attempt to generate the URL and catch `Symfony\Component\Routing\Exception\RouteNotFoundException`, which is thrown when the route name is unknown.

references.title: Routing - Forcing HTTPS on Generated URLs
references.summary: Generated URLs reuse the current request scheme by default. Setting the `schemes` option on a route forces a specific scheme (e.g. `['https']`); the same option applies to incoming requests and triggers a redirect when the scheme does not match. The `router.request_context.scheme` parameter sets the scheme used outside HTTP, e.g. in console commands.

references.title: Routing - Signing URIs
references.summary: The `Symfony\Component\HttpFoundation\UriSigner` service produces and verifies signed URIs. `sign()` appends a `_hash` query parameter derived from the URI's contents; `check()` re-validates that hash, and `checkRequest()` validates the signature directly from a `Request` object.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs in JavaScript
references.summary: When JavaScript is embedded in a Twig template, Symfony routes can be generated with the `path()` and `url()` Twig functions. The resulting value should be escaped with `escape("js")` before being inserted into JavaScript code.

references.title: Routing - Redirecting to URLs and Routes Directly from a Route
references.summary: FrameworkBundle's `RedirectController` lets a route delegate to another route or absolute URL without writing a controller. The defaults `route` (named target) or `path` (absolute path/URL), `permanent`, `keepQueryParams`, `keepRequestMethod`, and `ignoreAttributes` configure the redirect. By default redirects use 302; `permanent: true` switches to 301; `keepRequestMethod: true` upgrades the status to 307 (temporary) or 308 (permanent).

references.title: Routing - Redirecting URLs with Trailing Slashes
references.summary: Symfony automatically reconciles routes that differ only by a trailing slash for `GET` and `HEAD` requests by issuing a 301 redirect. A request to `/foo` for a route declared as `/foo/` is redirected to `/foo/`, and vice versa, so a single canonical URL is served.

references.title: Routing - Special Parameters
references.summary: Symfony reserves the parameters `_controller`, `_format`, `_fragment`, and `_locale` for internal use. `_controller` selects which controller runs, `_format` sets the request format on the `Request` object (e.g. `json` -> `application/json`), `_fragment` sets the URL fragment, and `_locale` sets the current locale. Routes can also use the `format`, `locale` (and so on) sugar names without the leading underscore.

references.title: Routing - Extra Parameters
references.summary: The `defaults` option may declare values for keys that are NOT present as `{placeholders}` in the route path. These extra defaults are passed to the controller as arguments, providing per-route configuration without polluting the URL.

references.title: Routing - Backed Enum Parameters
references.summary: PHP backed enums can be used as route parameter types: when the controller argument is type-hinted with a backed enum, Symfony resolves the URL parameter to the enum case automatically using its scalar value. Backed enums can also be used as default values for parameters.

references.title: Routing - Stateless Routes
references.summary: Setting `stateless: true` on a route declares that the session must not be used while the request matches that route, helping protect HTTP cacheability. If the session is started anyway, Symfony throws `UnexpectedSessionUsageException` when `kernel.debug` is enabled, or logs a warning when it is disabled.

references.title: Routing - Sub-Domain Routing
references.summary: The `host` route option restricts a route to one or more host names, enabling sub-domain routing where two routes can share the same path but resolve to different controllers depending on the `Host` header. The host pattern can include `{placeholders}` validated by `requirements`, and can be set globally on a route import. Functional tests must set the `HTTP_HOST` server parameter so the routes match.

references.title: Routing - Matching Expressions
references.summary: The `condition` route option attaches an Expression Language expression that must evaluate to true for the route to match. Available variables are `context` (a `RequestContext`), `request` (the `Request` object), and `params` (the matched route parameters). Available functions include `env()` for environment variable processors and `service()` for services tagged with `routing.condition_service` or annotated with `#[AsRoutingConditionService]`. Conditions are compiled to PHP and ignored during URL generation.

references.title: Routing - Matching HTTP Methods
references.summary: Routes match any HTTP verb by default. The `methods` route option restricts the allowed verbs (e.g. `methods: ['GET', 'HEAD']` in attributes; `methods: GET|HEAD` in YAML). HTML forms only support `GET` and `POST`, so to use other methods through forms Symfony reads the `_method` field if `framework.http_method_override` is `true`.

references.title: Routing - Localized Routes (i18n)
references.summary: Defining a different URL per locale on the same route by passing an array to `path` (e.g. `['en' => '/about-us', 'nl' => '/over-ons']`). When a localized route matches, Symfony uses that locale for the rest of the request automatically. Routes can also be imported with a per-locale `prefix` or `host` to localize entire controller groups.

references.title: Translation - The Locale and the URL
references.summary: The recommended way to identify the user's locale is to put it in the URL using the special `_locale` route parameter. When `_locale` is matched, Symfony automatically calls `Request::setLocale()` and the value can be retrieved later via `Request::getLocale()`. Locale requirements (e.g. `_locale: en|fr|de`) ensure only allowed locales match.

references.title: Translation - Handling the User's Locale
references.summary: The locale of the current request is stored on the `Request` object and is the value returned by `Request::getLocale()`. Setting the locale through `$request->setLocale()` from a controller is too late to affect the translator; use a `kernel.request` listener with a higher priority than `LocaleListener`, or set it via routing/translator service.

references.title: Framework configuration - default_locale
references.summary: `framework.default_locale` defines the locale used when no other mechanism (URL, listener, session) sets it. It is the fallback locale of the request and the translator.

references.title: Framework configuration - enabled_locales
references.summary: `framework.enabled_locales` lists the locales that the application supports. Symfony automatically uses this list as the requirement for the `_locale` routing placeholder, so URLs with non-listed locales return a 404. Defining the enabled locales also helps Symfony optimise locale-aware caches and code generation.

references.title: Framework Configuration Reference (FrameworkBundle)
references.summary: `framework.set_locale_from_accept_language` lets Symfony set the `Request` locale automatically from the `Accept-Language` HTTP header. The option is disabled by default. When a `_locale` request attribute is present, Symfony ignores the `Accept-Language` header for this locale decision.

references.title: Routing - Debugging Routes
references.summary: Symfony ships two console commands to inspect routing: `debug:router` lists every route in the order Symfony evaluates them and accepts a route name (or substring) to print a single route's full configuration. `router:match` takes a URL and reports which route would match it, useful when the wrong controller seems to be invoked. The `--show-aliases` option prints aliases for a route.

references.title: Routing - Troubleshooting
references.summary: Two common error messages explained: `Controller ... requires that you provide a value for the "$slug" argument` happens when the route path lacks a `{slug}` placeholder for a controller argument that has no default value; `Some mandatory parameters are missing ("slug") to generate a URL for route "blog_show"` happens when calling `generateUrl()`/`path()` without supplying a value for a mandatory placeholder.

references.title: Twig 3.8 - Synopsis
references.summary: A Twig template uses three default delimiters: `{{ ... }}` to print an expression, `{% ... %}` to execute a statement (tag), and `{# ... #}` for comments. Anything outside these tags is rendered verbatim. Pinned to Twig 3.8.0 source to avoid later Twig 3.x syntax additions.

references.title: Twig 3.8 - Variables
references.summary: Dot notation such as `foo.bar` uses Twig attribute resolution: array access, public properties, class constants, and public getter-style methods. Subscript notation such as `foo['bar']` uses item access on sequences or mappings and does not perform method lookup. Pinned to Twig 3.8.0 source to avoid later Twig 3.x syntax additions.

references.title: Twig 3.8 - Expressions
references.summary: Twig supports literals (numbers, strings, arrays, hashes), arithmetic, comparison, logical, string concatenation (`~`), and conditional/ternary operators. Filters (`|`), tests (`is`), and function calls combine to form complex expressions. Pinned to Twig 3.8.0 source to avoid later Twig 3.x syntax additions.

references.title: Twig 3.8 - Comments
references.summary: Twig comments are written with `{# ... #}` and are stripped at compile time. Comments may span multiple lines and are useful to disable parts of a template without removing them. Pinned to Twig 3.8.0 source to avoid later Twig 3.x syntax additions.

references.title: Twig 3.8 - Whitespace control
references.summary: Adding `-` immediately inside an opening or closing delimiter (e.g. `{%- ... -%}`) trims whitespace before/after the tag. Twig also offers a global `trim_blocks` and `lstrip_blocks` setting for HTML-friendly output. Pinned to Twig 3.8.0 source to avoid later Twig 3.x syntax additions.

references.title: Twig 3.8 - Setting variables
references.summary: Variables are assigned with `{% set name = value %}` or in block form `{% set name %}...{% endset %}`. The block form captures the rendered output as a string and is useful for building reusable snippets. Pinned to Twig 3.8.0 source to avoid later Twig 3.x syntax additions.

references.title: Twig 3.8 - Named arguments
references.summary: Filters and functions accept named arguments with the `name=value` syntax, allowing callers to skip optional positional arguments and improve readability. For the Twig 3.8 exam cap, prefer the equals separator because colon-separated named arguments were introduced later. Pinned to Twig 3.8.0 source to avoid later Twig 3.x syntax additions.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Output Escaping and XSS Attacks
references.summary: Symfony applications are safe by default because Twig performs automatic output escaping for variables printed with `{{ ... }}`. The page also shows how to disable escaping for trusted HTML using the `raw` filter.

references.title: Twig - escape filter
references.summary: The `escape` filter (alias `e`) escapes a string for safe output in different contexts: `html` (default), `js`, `css`, `url`, `html_attr`. It can take an explicit strategy and is automatically applied when auto-escaping is enabled, but only once per variable.

references.title: Twig - raw filter
references.summary: The `raw` filter marks a value as already safe so that auto-escaping does not transform it. It must be the last filter applied because escaping happens at the end of the filter chain.

references.title: Twig - autoescape tag
references.summary: The `{% autoescape %}` tag enables or disables automatic escaping for a block of template, optionally selecting a strategy such as `html`, `js`, or `false` to disable escaping entirely. Child blocks and included templates inherit the active strategy.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Template Inheritance and Layouts
references.summary: Symfony recommends a three-level inheritance scheme: a `base.html.twig` parent, an intermediate `layout.html.twig`, and per-page templates that extend it. Child templates may only contain `{% block %}` definitions; any markup outside a block raises a `SyntaxError`.

references.title: Twig - extends tag
references.summary: The `{% extends %}` tag declares that a template inherits from another template; it must be the first tag and only one `extends` is allowed per template. Extending templates may only define blocks, optionally overriding parent blocks.

references.title: Twig - block tag
references.summary: The `{% block %}` tag both defines a placeholder in a parent template and overrides it from a child template. Block names must be unique in a single template, and blocks defined in child templates replace parent blocks of the same name.

references.title: Twig - parent() function
references.summary: The `{{ parent() }}` function renders the parent block's content from inside an overriding block. It lets child templates keep default parent output and append or prepend their own block content.

references.title: Twig - use tag
references.summary: The `{% use %}` tag implements horizontal reuse: it imports blocks from another template into the current template without creating a parent/child relationship. Imported blocks can be aliased to avoid name clashes.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - The App Global Variable
references.summary: Symfony injects an `app` variable (instance of `Symfony\Bridge\Twig\AppVariable`) into every Twig template. It exposes `app.user`, `app.request`, `app.session`, `app.flashes`, `app.environment`, `app.debug`, `app.token`, `app.current_route`, `app.current_route_parameters`, `app.locale`, and `app.enabled_locales`.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Global Variables
references.summary: User-defined Twig globals are configured under `twig.globals` and become available in every template. Values prefixed with `@` reference services in the container, and those services are instantiated as soon as Twig is loaded rather than lazily on first use.

references.title: Twig Configuration Reference (TwigBundle) - globals
references.summary: Reference for the `twig.globals` option. It maps variable names to scalar values or service references (`@service_id`) that are exposed to all templates as global variables.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - Functions
references.summary: Symfony provides Twig functions including `render`, `render_esi`, `fragment_uri`, `controller`, `asset`, `csrf_token`, `is_granted`, `path`, `url`, `absolute_url`, `relative_path`, `t`, and `importmap`. These functions integrate components such as HttpKernel, Security, Asset, Routing, and Translation.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - Filters
references.summary: Symfony adds filters such as `humanize`, `trans`, `sanitize_html`, `yaml_encode`, `yaml_dump`, `serialize`, and several WebProfiler formatting helpers. These filters are available wherever the FrameworkBundle's Twig extensions are loaded.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - humanize
references.summary: The `humanize` filter normalizes camelCase, PascalCase, snake_case, kebab-case and spaced strings into a human-readable form by replacing separators with spaces and capitalizing the first character (e.g. `dateOfBirth` becomes `Date of birth`).

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - sanitize_html
references.summary: The `sanitize_html` filter passes the input through the HTML Sanitizer component using a named sanitizer (default `"default"`). It is intended for cleaning user-supplied HTML before output.

references.title: Twig 3.8 - Filters
references.summary: Filters are applied to expressions using the pipe character (`|`) and can be chained; arguments are passed in parentheses. Filter chains evaluate left to right and the special `escape`/`raw` filters interact with auto-escaping rules. Pinned to Twig 3.8.0 source to avoid later Twig 3.x behavior additions.

references.title: Twig 3.8 - Functions
references.summary: Functions are called with arguments using parenthesis syntax inside `{{ ... }}` or `{% ... %}` tags. Twig provides built-in functions such as `range`, `cycle`, `random`, `attribute`, `block`, `parent`, `include`, `source`, `min`, and `max`. Pinned to Twig 3.8.0 source to avoid later Twig 3.x behavior additions.

references.title: Twig 3.8 - length filter
references.summary: The `length` filter returns the number of items of a sequence or the number of characters of a string. For objects, it uses `count()` when the object implements `Countable` and can fall back to the string length of `__toString()`. Pinned to Twig 3.8.0 source to avoid later Twig 3.x behavior additions.

references.title: Twig 3.8 - default filter
references.summary: The `default` filter returns the supplied default value when the value is `undefined` or empty (`null`, `false`, `''`, empty array). To distinguish strict undefined from empty, combine it with the `defined` test. Pinned to Twig 3.8.0 source to avoid later Twig 3.x behavior additions.

references.title: Twig 3.8 - date filter
references.summary: The `date` filter formats a date using the given format string (PHP `date` syntax) and accepts an optional timezone. When no format is provided, Twig uses the format configured globally. Pinned to Twig 3.8.0 source to avoid later Twig 3.x behavior additions.

references.title: Templates - Writing a Twig Extension
references.summary: Shows how to create custom Twig filters and functions by extending `AbstractExtension` and returning `TwigFilter` or `TwigFunction` instances. Also explains service registration with the `twig.extension` tag and the `debug:twig` command for checking registered extensions.

references.title: Templates - Creating Lazy-Loaded Twig Extensions
references.summary: Explains why putting heavy dependencies directly in a Twig extension can hurt rendering performance. Shows the runtime pattern that separates extension definition from implementation so custom filters and functions can be loaded lazily.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Including Templates
references.summary: The `include()` Twig function reuses a template fragment. The included template inherits the parent's variables by default, and additional variables can be supplied as a hash literal in the second argument to rename or extend context.

references.title: Twig - include() function
references.summary: The `include()` function returns the rendered content of a template. It accepts options such as `with_context` (default `true`) to control variable inheritance, `ignore_missing` to skip missing templates without an error, and `sandboxed` to render in sandbox mode.

references.title: Twig - include tag
references.summary: The `{% include %}` tag is the legacy statement form. It supports `with`, `only`, and `ignore missing`. Modern code is encouraged to use the `include()` function expression because it can be combined with filters and other expressions.

references.title: Twig - embed tag
references.summary: The `{% embed %}` tag includes a template like `{% include %}`, but allows the embedding template to override blocks of the embedded template. It combines template inclusion with template-inheritance semantics for reusable fragments that expose blocks.

references.title: Twig - for tag
references.summary: The `{% for %}` tag iterates over arrays, objects implementing `\Traversable`, or numeric ranges. It exposes a special `loop` variable with `index`, `index0`, `revindex`, `revindex0`, `first`, `last`, `length`, and `parent`. An optional `{% else %}` block runs when the sequence is empty.

references.title: Twig - if tag
references.summary: The `{% if %}` tag evaluates a Twig expression and renders the contained block when the result is truthy. It supports `{% elseif %}` and `{% else %}` branches and uses Twig's truthiness rules: `false`, `null`, `0`, `''`, `[]`, and `'0'` are falsy.

references.title: Twig - defined test
references.summary: The `defined` test returns `true` when a variable, array key, or object property exists. Combined with `if`, it lets templates safely guard access to optional context such as `{% if user.email is defined %}`.

references.title: Twig - empty test
references.summary: The `empty` test returns `true` for `null`, `false`, an empty string, an empty array, or a `Countable`/`Traversable` whose `count()` is zero. It is the canonical way to test for missing or empty content in a Twig condition.

references.title: Twig - iterable test
references.summary: The `iterable` test returns `true` if the value is an array or an instance of `\Traversable`. It is useful before applying a `for` loop to a variable that may not be a sequence.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Linking to Pages
references.summary: Use the `path()` Twig function with a route name and optional parameter hash to generate URLs from the routing configuration. The recommended workflow is to never hardcode URLs in templates so the routing config remains the single source of truth.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - path
references.summary: Signature `path(name, parameters = [], relative = false)`. Returns the URL path (without scheme/host) for a route. With `relative=true` it returns a path relative to the current URL.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - url
references.summary: Signature `url(name, parameters = [], schemeRelative = false)`. Returns the absolute URL (scheme + host + path) for a route. With `schemeRelative=true` it returns a protocol-relative URL such as `//example.com/...`.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - absolute_url
