references.summary: Signature `absolute_url(path)`. Converts a relative path or asset URL into an absolute URL using the current request's scheme and host. It is commonly combined with the `asset()` function to generate fully qualified asset URLs.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - relative_path
references.summary: Signature `relative_path(path)`. Returns a path relative to the current URL given an absolute URL. For example, on `http://example.com/products/hover-board`, `relative_path('http://example.com/human.txt')` produces `../human.txt`.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Rendering a Template in Controllers
references.summary: `AbstractController::render()` returns a `Response` whose body is the rendered template, while `renderView()` returns the template output as a string. The `#[Template]` attribute on a controller method declares the template, letting the action return only the parameter array.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Embedding Controllers
references.summary: Inside a Twig template, the `render()` function combined with `path()`, `url()`, or `controller()` runs another action and embeds its response inline. The `controller()` form generates a fragment URL so the controller does not need a public route.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Rendering a Template Directly from a Route
references.summary: Static pages can be rendered without a custom controller by configuring a route to use Symfony's `TemplateController` and supplying the template name as a default. Route defaults can also provide context values and a response status for the rendered template.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - render
references.summary: Signature `render(uri, options = [])`. Performs an internal sub-request to the given URI or `ControllerReference` and returns the rendered output. The render strategy is selected via the `strategy` key of the options array.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - controller
references.summary: Signature `controller(controller, attributes = [], query = [])`. Returns a `ControllerReference` to a controller (typically `App\Controller\X::method`) that can be passed to `render()` or `render_esi()`. Lets templates execute non-routed controllers.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - render_esi
references.summary: Signature `render_esi(uri, options = [])`. Behaves like `render()` but emits an Edge Side Includes tag when ESI is enabled, falling back to a regular sub-request otherwise.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - How to Embed Asynchronous Content with hinclude.js
references.summary: Symfony templates can embed fragments asynchronously with `render_hinclude()`, which configures hinclude.js tags for a controller or URL. The fragment can define fallback content through a global `hinclude_default_template`, a per-call `default` template/string, and hinclude.js attributes.

references.title: Translations - Using Twig Filters
references.summary: The `trans` Twig filter translates a string or variable, optionally with placeholder values and an explicit translation domain. The filter respects auto-escaping; chain `|raw` after `|trans` to keep HTML produced by translation untouched.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - trans_default_domain
references.summary: The `{% trans_default_domain domain %}` tag sets the default translation domain for the current template. It is useful when many translations in one template use the same non-default domain.

references.title: Translations - Using Twig Tags
references.summary: The `{% trans %}{% endtrans %}` tag translates static blocks of text and supports `with`, `from`, and `into` clauses to pass parameters and override the domain or locale. Output of the tag is **not** auto-escaped, unlike the `trans` filter.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - trans (filter)
references.summary: Signature `message|trans(arguments = [], domain = null, locale = null)`. Uses the Translator to translate a `string` or `Translatable` value, applying placeholders and overriding the domain/locale when provided.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - t (function)
references.summary: The `t(message, parameters = [], domain = 'messages')` function creates a `TranslatableMessage`. Combined with `|trans` it provides a concise way to defer translation while keeping all the context (message, params, domain) attached to the value.

references.title: ICU MessageFormat - Pluralization
references.summary: Symfony uses the ICU MessageFormat `plural` function for pluralization, with branches such as `=0`, `=1`, `one`, `few`, `many`, and `other`. The pound sign `#` inside a branch is replaced by the current count and pluralization rules vary by locale.

references.title: Translations - Message Format
references.summary: Messages with variables and pluralization use the ICU MessageFormat syntax (delegated to PHP's `MessageFormatter`). The same translator API handles both simple placeholder substitution and ICU `select`/`plural` blocks.

references.title: Twig - String interpolation
references.summary: String interpolation works only inside double-quoted strings and uses the `#{...}` syntax to embed any valid Twig expression: `"foo #{bar} baz"`. Single-quoted strings are taken literally and never interpolated.

references.title: Twig - Literals
references.summary: Twig string literals can be written with single or double quotes. Both support `\\` and the matching quote escape, and double-quoted strings additionally process `\n`, `\t`, and `#{...}` interpolation.

references.title: Twig - Other operators
references.summary: The `~` operator concatenates string values, converting non-strings to strings. It is the canonical alternative to interpolation when joining several Twig expressions and avoids the cost of evaluating a double-quoted string.

references.title: Twig - format filter
references.summary: The `format` filter formats a string using the same rules as PHP's `sprintf()` (e.g. `'%s is %d'|format(name, age)`). It is useful for parameterized strings where positional placeholders are clearer than concatenation or interpolation.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Linking to CSS, JavaScript and Image Assets
references.summary: The `asset()` Twig function generates the public URL of a static file (under `public/`). It accounts for the application installation path so the same template works whether the app is mounted at the root or in a subdirectory.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - asset
references.summary: Signature `asset(path, packageName = null)`. Returns the public path for an asset, optionally using a named asset package. Cache busting is configured via `assets.version`, `assets.version_strategy`, or `assets.json_manifest_path`.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - asset_version
references.summary: Signature `asset_version(packageName = null)`. Returns the current version string of an asset package, useful when constructing custom asset URLs or cache busting outside the standard `asset()` flow.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - importmap
references.summary: The `importmap()` Twig function outputs the import map and module preload tags used by the AssetMapper component. It enables browsers to resolve bare module specifiers to mapped asset URLs and load the configured JavaScript entrypoint.

references.title: AssetMapper: Modern Asset Management with PHP
references.summary: AssetMapper serves frontend assets directly via native ES modules and import maps without a Node-based bundler. Templates use `{{ importmap('app') }}` to expose the import map and inject preloaded module URLs.

references.title: Front-end Tools: Handling CSS & JavaScript
references.summary: Overview of the **front-end stack** Symfony recommends: **AssetMapper** (no Node.js required, importmaps + JSDelivr), **Webpack Encore** (Node.js / Webpack), **StimulusBundle**, **UX components**, and the legacy **Asset** component. Compares trade-offs and links the dedicated guides for each tool.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - The Dump Twig Utilities
references.summary: When `symfony/debug-bundle` is installed, Twig templates expose a `dump()` function and a `{% dump %}` tag. The function dumps inline; the tag forwards the dump to the Web Debug Toolbar instead. Both are available only in the `dev` and `test` environments and trigger a PHP error in `prod`.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Linting Twig Templates
references.summary: The `lint:twig` console command checks templates for syntax errors. It accepts file or directory arguments, supports `--show-deprecations` to surface deprecated API usage, and `--format=github` for CI-friendly annotations.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Inspecting Twig Information
references.summary: The `debug:twig` console command lists available Twig functions, filters, tests, tags, and global variables. With a template path argument it shows which physical file Twig will load and supports `--filter=` to narrow the output.

references.title: Twig - dump function
references.summary: The `dump()` Twig function prints the structure of one or more variables. With no arguments it dumps every variable in the current context. It is provided by Twig's debug extension and only operates when debug mode is enabled.

references.title: Forms - Building Forms
references.summary: Symfony exposes a form builder that describes form fields with a fluent interface and produces the actual form object used to render and process data. The builder is the entry point for both inline and class-based form creation.

references.title: Forms - Creating Forms in Controllers
references.summary: Controllers extending AbstractController create a form via createFormBuilder(), chaining add() calls and finishing with getForm(). Controllers that do not extend AbstractController must inject the form.factory service and call its createBuilder() method instead.

references.title: Forms - Creating Form Classes
references.summary: Form classes implement FormTypeInterface, but Symfony recommends extending AbstractType and defining the fields in buildForm(). Controllers instantiate them through createForm(); declaring the data_class option binds the form to a specific entity, which is required when forms are embedded.

references.title: Forms - Passing Options to Forms
references.summary: Custom options are passed as the third argument of createForm() and must be declared in the form type's configureOptions() method via OptionsResolver. The resolver can also enforce types with setAllowedTypes() and reject unknown options at runtime.

references.title: Forms - Changing the Action and HTTP Method
references.summary: By default a Symfony form posts to the same URL with method POST. setAction() and setMethod() on the builder, or the action and method form options, override this. PUT, PATCH or DELETE methods rely on a hidden _method field and require http_method_override to be enabled.

references.title: Forms - Changing the Form Name
references.summary: The default form and field name is derived from the type class name. FormFactoryInterface::createNamed() lets you choose a custom name, and an empty string suppresses the prefix entirely so field names are not nested under it.

references.title: How to Use a Form without a Data Class
references.summary: When createFormBuilder() receives an array (or no data) and no data_class option is set, getData() returns an associative array keyed by field name. Constraints can still be attached at field level or via the form's constraints option.

references.title: How to Configure empty Data for a Form Class
references.summary: The empty_data option provides the data used when a form is submitted without any bound data. By default it is null, or a new instance of data_class created via the no-arg constructor; it can be overridden with an explicit object or a closure that builds it lazily from submitted form data.

references.title: How to Embed Forms
references.summary: Symfony forms can embed another form type so one form edits fields belonging to related objects. The embedded object's property should use the Valid constraint when its own validation constraints must cascade during parent form validation.

references.title: How to Reduce Code Duplication with inherit_data
references.summary: The inherit_data option lets an embedded form read and write the same data object as its parent form. This is useful for extracting repeated field definitions into a reusable child form, but forms using inherit_data cannot have *_SET_DATA event listeners.

references.title: Forms - Form Type Options
references.summary: Covers common form field options such as `required` and `label`. The `required` option controls browser-side required attributes and does not perform server-side validation without constraints such as `NotBlank` or `NotNull`.

references.title: Forms - Client-Side HTML Validation
references.summary: Generated Symfony forms use HTML5 validation attributes such as `required` to trigger browser validation. Client-side validation can be disabled with `novalidate` on the form or `formnovalidate` on the submit button, which is useful when testing server-side constraints.

references.title: Forms - Processing Forms
references.summary: The recommended pattern is a single controller action that builds the form, calls handleRequest($request), and branches on isSubmitted() and isValid(). Passing the form (not its view) to render() automatically returns HTTP 422 on invalid submissions, and a successful submission should redirect to avoid double posts.

references.title: Forms - Validating Forms
references.summary: isValid() is a shortcut that asks whether the underlying data object passes its validation constraints after the form has applied submitted values. Constraints can be declared on the entity (PHP attributes, YAML, XML) or on the form via the constraints option, and both styles can be combined.

references.title: How to Use the submit() Function to Handle Form Submissions
references.summary: FormInterface::submit() submits an explicit array (or scalar for individual fields) instead of relying on handleRequest(). Its second argument $clearMissing defaults to true; passing false (typically for PATCH) keeps non-submitted fields untouched and only validates the fields that were actually sent.

references.title: How to Submit a Form with Multiple Buttons
references.summary: Several SubmitType buttons can coexist in one form. ClickableInterface::isClicked() on a button or Form::getClickedButton() identifies which one was pressed, allowing the controller to branch (e.g. "save" vs. "save and add") based on the user choice.

references.title: How to Choose Validation Groups Based on the Clicked Button
references.summary: Buttons accept their own validation_groups option. Setting validation_groups to false on a button (e.g. a wizard's "previous step") suppresses constraint validation when that button is clicked, while basic integrity checks (file size, type coercion) still run.

references.title: How to Choose Validation Groups Based on the Submitted Data
references.summary: validation_groups can be set to a [Class, 'staticMethod'] callback or to a closure receiving the FormInterface, returning an array of group names computed from $form->getData(). Default constraints are not implicit and must be re-added (e.g. ['Default', 'person']) if they should still apply.

references.title: How to Define the Validation Groups to Use
references.summary: The validation_groups option restricts validation to the named groups. Specifying a custom group such as 'registration' uses only that group; combining with 'Default' (e.g. ['Default', 'registration']) re-includes ungrouped constraints.

references.title: How to Disable the Validation of Submitted Data
references.summary: Setting validation_groups to false disables constraint validation entirely for the form. Basic integrity checks still run, and submission of extra fields is governed separately by the allow_extra_fields option.

references.title: How to Dynamically Configure Form Validation Groups
references.summary: Advanced validation group selection can be delegated to an invokable service when a simple callback is not enough. The resolver service receives the FormInterface instance and returns the array of validation groups used by the form validator.

references.title: Forms - Form Types
references.summary: In Symfony, individual fields, field groups and entire forms are all "form types". The component ships dozens of built-in types and you can create custom ones; debug:form lists all registered types, type extensions and guessers.

references.title: Creating Form Types Based on Symfony Built-in Types
references.summary: Custom types extend AbstractType and override getParent() to return an existing type's FQCN, e.g. ChoiceType::class. configureOptions() then customizes the parent's options. Symfony chains parent methods (buildForm/buildView/etc.) before the child's own methods.

references.title: Custom Form Field Type - Configuration Options
references.summary: configureOptions() leverages OptionsResolver to set defaults, restrict types via setAllowedTypes(), restrict values via setAllowedValues(), and transform inputs via setNormalizer(). Custom options are then read from the $options array passed to buildForm() and buildView().

references.title: How to Create a Custom Form Field Type
references.summary: FormTypeInterface defines the main hooks used by custom types, including getParent(), configureOptions(), buildForm(), buildView(), finishView() and getBlockPrefix(). getBlockPrefix() is the hook to override when a custom type's Twig block name would clash with a built-in type.

references.title: Creating a custom Type Guesser
references.summary: A type guesser implements FormTypeGuesserInterface (guessType, guessRequired, guessMaxLength, guessPattern). guessType() returns a TypeGuess with a type FQCN, options array, and a confidence constant from Guess (LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH/VERY_HIGH); the guess with the highest confidence wins. Services tagged with form.type_guesser are registered automatically.

references.title: FormType Field
references.summary: FormType is the root parent of every form type and exposes shared options (compound, data_class, empty_data, error_bubbling, mapped, required, validation_groups, etc.). BaseType supplies further inherited options (attr, block_prefix, disabled, label, priority) but is not usable on its own.

references.title: Forms - Form Type Guessing
references.summary: If validation constraints are present on the data class, omitting the second argument of add() (or passing null) lets Symfony guess the field type. With null as the second argument you can still supply field options as the third argument.

references.title: How to Access Services or Config from Inside a Form
references.summary: In Symfony 7, form classes are services by default and can receive dependencies through service autowiring. For values that should vary per form instance, custom form options remain the appropriate way to pass configuration into the form type.

references.title: How to Create a Custom Form Field Type - Creating the Form Type Template
references.summary: Shows how a custom form type can define a dedicated Twig template and register it through `twig.form_themes`. Explains block-prefix based fragment names and why overriding `getBlockPrefix()` avoids collisions with built-in form types.

references.title: How to Create a Custom Form Field Type - Passing Variables to the Form Type Template
references.summary: Explains how custom form types pass variables to their Twig fragments by using `buildView()` and the `FormView` variables array. These variables can depend on form options or independent runtime values.

references.title: Forms - Rendering Forms
references.summary: Controllers pass either the form or its createView() output to render(). In a Twig template, {{ form(form) }} prints the entire form including the surrounding <form> tags, default method POST, and the same target URL by default.

references.title: How to Customize Form Rendering - Form Rendering Functions
references.summary: form_start(), form_end(), form_errors(), form_row() and the per-part form_label(), form_widget(), form_help() helpers let you control rendering granularity. form_rest() renders any unrendered fields and is recommended to make sure the auto-added _token CSRF field is not forgotten.

references.title: How to Customize Form Rendering - Form Field Helpers
references.summary: field_name(), field_value(), field_label(), field_help(), field_errors() and field_choices() return raw form values without surrounding HTML. They are useful when a template hand-writes each tag instead of relying on the standard form rendering helpers or a form theme.

references.title: How to Customize Form Rendering - Form Rendering Variables
references.summary: Twig form helpers accept a variables argument such as {'attr': {'class': 'task_field'}}. These variables are not applied recursively to children when calling form_widget(form). Each field exposes its variables (id, name, label, etc.) via form.field.vars.

references.title: How to Customize Form Rendering - Functions
references.summary: form_start() emits the opening <form> tag including the right method, action and an enctype of multipart/form-data when the form has file fields. form_end() also calls form_rest() unless render_rest is false. form_parent() returns the parent FormView, preferred over form.parent which fails if a child field is named parent.

references.title: How to Customize Form Rendering - Tests
references.summary: Twig tests selectedchoice(value) and rootform are used in form themes. selectedchoice tests if a choice equals or belongs to the selected value(s); rootform reliably checks whether a form view has no parent, even if the form contains a child field named parent.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes
references.summary: Built-in themes include form_div_layout.html.twig (default), form_table_layout.html.twig, bootstrap_3/4/5_layout.html.twig (and horizontal variants), foundation_5/6_layout.html.twig and tailwind_2_layout.html.twig. Themes are configured globally via twig.form_themes or per-form via the form_theme Twig tag.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes - Applying Themes to all Forms
references.summary: twig.form_themes accepts an array of themes; the order matters because each theme overrides previous ones, so the most important theme must be at the end of the list. The default theme used when nothing is configured is form_div_layout.html.twig.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes - Applying Themes to Single Forms
references.summary: {% form_theme form 'theme.html.twig' %} applies a theme only to the form variable in the current template. Multiple themes can be supplied with the with [...] syntax, and a child form view can have its own theme by passing form.child to form_theme.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes - Disabling Global Themes for Single Forms
references.summary: Adding the only keyword (e.g. {% form_theme form with [...] only %}) suppresses all globally configured themes for the form. To still inherit blocks from a built-in theme, the custom theme should reuse it via Twig's use, not extends.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes - Form Fragment Naming
references.summary: Fragments follow the type_part pattern (e.g. textarea_widget) to override every field of a type, or _id_part (e.g. _product_description_widget) to override one specific field. The block_name option renames the field id segment, and block_prefix gives the field a custom fragment family without subclassing the type.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes - Creating a Form Theme in the same Template as the Form
references.summary: {% form_theme form _self %} tells Twig to look for overridden form blocks within the current template. This requires the template to extend another (so block resolution works) and limits reuse to the current file only.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes - Creating a Form Theme in a Separate Template
references.summary: A reusable form theme can live in its own Twig template and define blocks that follow Symfony's form fragment naming rules. The theme is then enabled globally with twig.form_themes or applied to selected forms with the form_theme tag.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes - Reusing Parts of a Built-In Form Theme
references.summary: A custom theme can import blocks from a built-in theme with Twig's use tag instead of redefining every form fragment. Inside an overridden block, parent() can reuse the original block content when making small changes such as wrapping the generated HTML.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes - Customizing the Form Validation Errors
references.summary: Validation errors are rendered by form_errors() through the form_errors Twig block in the active form theme. The compound variable distinguishes errors attached to an entire form from errors attached to an individual field.

references.title: Bootstrap 5 Form Theme
references.summary: Setting twig.form_themes to bootstrap_5_layout.html.twig (or applying it via {% form_theme %}) renders forms with Bootstrap 5 markup. Errors are placed after the input element; for checkbox/radio fields the label is rendered by form_widget(), so a separate form_label() prints nothing.

references.title: Tailwind CSS Form Theme
references.summary: tailwind_2_layout.html.twig provides a minimal Tailwind-based form theme. It can be used globally or with the form_theme tag, and is intended to be customized with project-specific class lists rather than reused as-is.

references.title: Bootstrap 4 Form Theme
references.summary: Built-in Twig theme that renders forms with **Bootstrap 4** markup. Activate it project-wide by setting `twig.form_themes: ["bootstrap_4_layout.html.twig"]`, or per-template with `{% form_theme form 'bootstrap_4_layout.html.twig' %}`. A horizontal variant (`bootstrap_4_horizontal_layout.html.twig`) is also shipped.

references.title: How to Implement CSRF Protection
references.summary: Cross-Site Request Forgery exploits the trust the browser has in an authenticated session to perform unintended actions. The standard countermeasure is anti-CSRF tokens: a per-user, per-form value that the legit server validates so requests originating from another site are rejected.

references.title: How to Implement CSRF Protection - Installation
references.summary: CSRF support requires the symfony/security-csrf package and is toggled with the framework.csrf_protection option. Tokens are stored in the session, so a session is started automatically when a CSRF-protected form is rendered, which means such pages cannot be fully cached.

references.title: CSRF Protection in Symfony Forms
references.summary: Symfony forms enable CSRF protection by default. The hidden field defaults to _token and can be customized globally via framework.form.csrf_protection.field_name or per-form via the csrf_protection, csrf_field_name and csrf_token_id options of the form type.

references.title: Generating and Checking CSRF Tokens Manually
references.summary: Plain HTML forms can use the csrf_token() Twig function with an arbitrary token id to render a hidden token field. The controller validates it via AbstractController::isCsrfTokenValid($id, $submittedToken), passing the same id used to generate the token.

references.title: CSRF Tokens and Compression Side-Channel Attacks
references.summary: Symfony prepends a random mask to each CSRF token and uses it to scramble the value. This mitigates BREACH/CRIME-style compression side-channel attacks against HTTPS that try to recover token bytes from response sizes.

references.title: FileType Field
references.summary: FileType renders an <input type="file"> and after submission its data is an UploadedFile (or array of them when multiple is true). Its parent type is FormType and the default data_class is HttpFoundation\File\File.

references.title: FileType Field - Basic Usage
references.summary: $form['attachment']->getData() returns an UploadedFile, which exposes move($targetDirectory, $name) to relocate the temporary upload. The original client-supplied name is unsafe (user-controlled) so guessExtension() should be combined with a controlled file name when persisting the file.

references.title: FileType Field - Field Options
references.summary: Setting multiple to true lets the user pick more than one file at once and changes the submitted data to an array of UploadedFile objects. The data_class option can be replaced (default File) so that bound objects represent the upload.

references.title: How to Upload Files
references.summary: When the upload field stores only a filename in the entity, the FileType is added with mapped: false so Symfony does not attempt to read/write the property. Validation is supplied via the constraints option (e.g. File constraint with maxSize and mimeTypes), and the controller moves the UploadedFile to a target directory after slugifying the original name.

references.title: Form Types Reference
references.summary: Symfony groups built-in form types into families such as text fields, choice fields, date/time fields, field groups, hidden fields, buttons and the base FormType. The reference page is the authoritative index for the available built-in field type classes in Symfony 7.

references.title: ChoiceType Field (select drop-downs, radio buttons & checkboxes)
references.summary: ChoiceType requires choices or choice_loader. The choices array maps human labels (keys) to the values stored in PHP, and the rendering changes between <select>, radios and checkboxes via the multiple and expanded options. choice_label, choice_value and group_by accept property paths or callables for object-based choices.

references.title: EntityType Field
references.summary: EntityType extends ChoiceType to load options from a Doctrine entity given by the class option. choice_label picks the displayed property; query_builder customizes the fetched set, and combining multiple/expanded with a Doctrine relation is what enables many-to-many checkbox/select pickers.

references.title: EnumType Field
references.summary: EnumType extends ChoiceType for backed PHP enums. The class option points to the enum FQCN and Symfony exposes every case as a choice. Implementing TranslatableInterface on the enum customizes the rendered labels, since enum case names cannot contain spaces.

references.title: RepeatedType Field
references.summary: RepeatedType creates two identical sub-fields whose values must match (e.g. password confirmation). The type option chooses the inner type (defaults to TextType), options is shared between the two children, while first_options and second_options customize each side; first_name/second_name rename the children, mapped is forced to true.

references.title: CollectionType Field
references.summary: CollectionType renders a dynamic list of children built from entry_type. allow_add and allow_delete control whether unrecognised submitted entries are appended and whether missing existing entries are removed; the prototype option lets JavaScript clone an empty entry to add new rows on the client side.

references.title: SubmitType Field
references.summary: SubmitType is rendered as a <button type="submit"> and exposes ClickableInterface::isClicked() so a controller can tell which button submitted a multi-button form. The validate option (default true) allows toggling browser-side HTML validation.

references.title: HiddenType Field
references.summary: HiddenType renders an <input type="hidden">. Its required default is false (hidden fields cannot use HTML5 required) and error_bubbling defaults to true so validation errors surface on the parent form instead of being lost on a non-visible field.

references.title: DateType Field
references.summary: DateType edits date values and can render either a single text input or three choice fields depending on the widget option. The input option controls the underlying PHP data format, such as DateTime, DateTimeImmutable, string, timestamp or array.

references.title: CheckboxType Field
references.summary: CheckboxType renders a single checkbox for boolean data. A checked box maps to true, an unchecked box maps to false, and the false_values option can define additional submitted values interpreted as false.

references.title: MoneyType Field
references.summary: MoneyType renders a text input specialized for money values and displays a currency symbol based on the currency option. The divisor option converts between stored integer-like values and the user-facing amount, which is useful when storing cents to avoid rounding errors.

references.title: How to Embed a Collection of Forms
references.summary: CollectionType can embed many child forms so a single parent form edits related objects. Dynamic add/remove workflows use options such as allow_add, allow_delete, prototype and by_reference together with entity adder/remover methods.

references.title: BirthdayType Field
references.summary: A specialization of `DateType` for **birth dates**. The defaults narrow the year range to the **last 120 years**, which is the only behavioural difference from `DateType`. Like its parent, it can be rendered as three select boxes or one/three text boxes depending on the `widget` option.

references.title: ButtonType Field
references.summary: A simple, non-responsive `<button>` element. It is the parent of the `SubmitType` and `ResetType` button fields and is **not mapped** to a property of the underlying data object — buttons exist only in the form, not in the model.

references.title: ColorType Field
references.summary: Text field rendered as `<input type="color">`. The `html5` option (default `false`) toggles a strict format check: when `true`, only valid HTML5 simple colors (lowercase 7-character hex like `#abcdef`) are accepted, otherwise any string passes through.

references.title: CountryType Field
references.summary: A specialized `ChoiceType` listing every world country. The choice keys are localized country names in the user's locale and the values are **ISO 3166-1 alpha-2** codes by default. Setting the `alpha3` option to `true` switches the values to the three-letter alpha-3 codes.

references.title: CurrencyType Field
references.summary: A specialized `ChoiceType` listing currencies as **3-letter ISO 4217** codes (e.g. `EUR`, `USD`). Choice labels are localized currency names rendered in the user's locale; the choices come from the **Intl** component, not a hard-coded list.

references.title: DateIntervalType Field
references.summary: Field for capturing a span of time as a `DateInterval` (years, months, days, hours, minutes, seconds). The `widget` option (`single_text`, `text`, `choice`) controls rendering, and `with_*` flags (e.g. `with_years`, `with_hours`, `with_invert`) toggle which interval components are exposed. The `input` option chooses the model type (`dateinterval` default, `string`, `array`).

references.title: DateTimeType Field
references.summary: Field that combines a date and time into one form. It can render as one `<input type="datetime-local">`, a single text box, or five select fields, controlled by `date_widget` and `time_widget`. The `format` option uses **`IntlDateFormatter`** constants (e.g. `IntlDateFormatter::SHORT`) when `widget` is `single_text`; the `input` option chooses the PHP model type (`datetime` default, `datetime_immutable`, `string`, `timestamp`, `array`).

references.title: EmailType Field
references.summary: Text field rendered as **`<input type="email">`**. It does **not** validate the submitted address by itself — pair it with the `Email` validation constraint when validation is required. It inherits options from its parent `TextType`.

references.title: FormType Field
references.summary: Root form type that **every other form type** inherits from. Its options (e.g. `data_class`, `empty_data`, `mapped`, `required`, `label`, `attr`, `constraints`, `error_bubbling`, `inherit_data`, `trim`, `validation_groups`) are therefore available on all form types.

references.title: IntegerType Field
references.summary: Renders an HTML5 `<input type="number">` and parses the submitted string into a PHP `int`. The `rounding_mode` option chooses between modes such as `IntegerType::ROUND_DOWN` (the default truncation), `ROUND_FLOOR`, `ROUND_HALF_UP`, etc.; `grouping` controls thousands separators in display.

references.title: LanguageType Field
references.summary: A specialized `ChoiceType` listing **Unicode language identifiers** (e.g. `fr`, `zh-Hant`). With `alpha3: true` it instead lists ISO 639-2 alpha-3 codes (`fra`); `choice_self_translation` (default `false`) renders each label in its own language instead of the user's locale.

references.title: LocaleType Field
references.summary: A specialized `ChoiceType` listing all **locales** (language+region pairs such as `en_GB`, `fr_FR`). The labels are localized into the user's locale and the choices come from the Intl component.

references.title: NumberType Field
references.summary: Renders a text input for numeric data. Options include `scale` (number of fraction digits, default `null` = locale dependent), `rounding_mode` (`NumberType::ROUND_HALF_UP`, `ROUND_DOWN`, `ROUND_FLOOR`, …), `grouping` (thousands separators), and `html5` (when `true`, renders `<input type="number">` instead of a text input).

references.title: PasswordType Field
references.summary: Renders an `<input type="password">`. By default the submitted value is **not re-rendered** when validation fails; the `always_empty` option (default `true`) controls this behaviour. The field inherits all `TextType` options.

references.title: PercentType Field
references.summary: Renders a text input for percentage values. The `type` option chooses between `fractional` (default — `0.95` is rendered as `95%`), `integer` (`95` is rendered as `95%`), or `decimal`. The `symbol` option (default `%`) controls the suffix and `scale` sets the number of fraction digits.

references.title: RadioType Field
references.summary: Renders a single `<input type="radio">`. It inherits from `CheckboxType` and is rarely used directly — `ChoiceType` with `expanded: true` and `multiple: false` builds groups of radio buttons automatically.

references.title: RangeType Field
references.summary: Field rendered as an HTML5 `<input type="range">` slider. The `attr` option is typically used to pass the `min`, `max`, and `step` HTML attributes that define the slider's allowed range and increment.

references.title: ResetType Field
references.summary: A button rendered as `<button type="reset">` that resets all fields of the surrounding form to their original values. It inherits from `ButtonType` and is **not mapped** to the underlying data.

references.title: SearchType Field
references.summary: Text field rendered as **`<input type="search">`** (the search variant of a text input — some browsers add a clear "x" icon and dedicated search styling). It otherwise behaves like `TextType`.

references.title: TelType Field
references.summary: Text field rendered as **`<input type="tel">`**. The submitted value is **not validated** — phone-number formats vary too widely between countries — so pair the field with a custom validator if validation is required.

references.title: TextType Field
references.summary: The most basic input field, rendered as `<input type="text">`. It is the parent of many specialized text-based types (`EmailType`, `PasswordType`, `UrlType`, `SearchType`, `TelType`, `ColorType`, `RangeType`, `TextareaType`).

references.title: TextareaType Field
references.summary: Renders an HTML `<textarea>`. It inherits from `TextType` and is therefore a multi-line variant of the basic text field.

references.title: TimeType Field
references.summary: Field for time input. The `widget` option chooses between `single_text` (a `<input type="time">` or text), `text` (two/three text inputs), or `choice` (two/three select boxes). `with_seconds` (default `false`) toggles the seconds component, and `input` chooses the PHP model type (`datetime` default, `datetime_immutable`, `string`, `timestamp`, `array`).

references.title: TimezoneType Field
references.summary: A specialized `ChoiceType` listing every IANA timezone identifier (e.g. `Europe/Paris`). With `intl: true` (default `false`), the choices come from the **Intl** component; with `intl: false`, they come from `DateTimeZone::listIdentifiers()`.

references.title: UrlType Field
references.summary: Text field rendered as `<input type="url">`. The `default_protocol` option (default `"http"`) is **prepended automatically** to submitted values that lack a protocol; setting it to `null` disables that behaviour.

references.title: WeekType Field
references.summary: Field for **ISO 8601 week numbers** such as `1984-W05`. The `widget` option chooses between `single_text` (one `<input type="week">`), `text` (two text boxes), or `choice` (two select boxes). `input` chooses the model type (`array` default, `string`, plus the years range supplied via the `years` option for the choice widget).

references.title: How to Use Data Transformers
references.summary: Data transformers convert the data of a field between the format used in the application and the format displayed/submitted by the form. They are used internally by many built-in types (e.g. DateType <-> yyyy-MM-dd). When inherit_data is true on a field, transformers attached to it are not applied.

references.title: Transforming Strings Form Data Tags from User Input to an Array
references.summary: CallbackTransformer takes two callables: the first transforms the model value into the format rendered in the form, the second reverses the submitted value back to the model format. addModelTransformer() attaches it to a single field; attaching to the builder itself would apply the transformer to the whole form.

references.title: Data Transformers - Creating the Transformer
references.summary: A custom transformer implements DataTransformerInterface with transform() (model -> form) and reverseTransform() (form -> model). When transform() receives null it must return an empty equivalent (empty string, 0). To trigger a validation error from reverseTransform() throw TransformationFailedException; the message shown to the user comes from the field's invalid_message option (or setInvalidMessage() with placeholders).

references.title: About Model and View Transformers
references.summary: A form has three data layers: model (Form::getData()), norm (normalized middle layer) and view (what is sent to/from the browser). Model transformers convert model <-> norm via addModelTransformer(); view transformers convert norm <-> view via addViewTransformer().

references.title: How to Use Data Mappers
references.summary: Data mappers (DataMapperInterface) write form data back to the underlying object and read it for rendering. The getter and setter form options provide ad hoc per-field mappers; for a fully custom strategy implement DataMapperInterface and call FormBuilder::setDataMapper().

references.title: Form Events
references.summary: Form events are dispatched at well-defined moments of the form lifecycle and let you alter data or fields. Constants live on FormEvents and listener callbacks receive a FormEvent (or a typed subclass like PreSubmitEvent) exposing getForm() and getData()/setData().

references.title: Form Events - The Form Workflow
references.summary: Pre-population (Form::setData()) dispatches PRE_SET_DATA then POST_SET_DATA. Submission (handleRequest()/submit()) dispatches PRE_SUBMIT (request data, may add/remove fields), SUBMIT (normalized data, fields cannot be added/removed), then POST_SUBMIT (denormalized data, no field changes; ValidationListener runs here).

references.title: Form Events - Registering Listeners or Subscribers
references.summary: FormEvents constants map to dispatcher names: form.pre_set_data, form.post_set_data, form.pre_submit, form.submit, form.post_submit. Each event's data column is, respectively, model data, model data, request data, normalized data, view data.

references.title: Form Events - Event Subscribers
references.summary: An event subscriber implements EventSubscriberInterface and groups multiple listeners through getSubscribedEvents(). It is attached to a builder via addEventSubscriber(), in contrast with addEventListener() which attaches a single callable to one event.

references.title: How to Dynamically Modify Forms Using Form Events
references.summary: Dynamic field changes that depend on the bound data use POST_SET_DATA (object-driven) or PRE_SUBMIT (request-driven), where the form structure can still be mutated. Choices that depend on a parent field are reacted to with a PRE_SUBMIT listener attached to the parent.

references.title: How to Create a Form Type Extension
references.summary: Form type extensions modify existing form types globally without subclassing them. They can target a specific type (e.g. add a download feature to FileType) or every type derived from a common parent (e.g. add help text to every text-like field via FormType).

references.title: Defining the Form Type Extension
references.summary: Extensions extend AbstractTypeExtension (or implement FormTypeExtensionInterface) and must implement the static getExtendedTypes(): iterable method. Optional methods buildForm(), buildView(), configureOptions() and finishView() behave just like on a regular form type.

references.title: Registering your Form Type Extension as a Service
references.summary: Extensions are services tagged with form.type_extension; autoconfiguration handles this automatically with the default services.yaml. The optional priority tag attribute (default 0) controls load order, with higher priorities loading earlier.

references.title: Generic Form Type Extensions
references.summary: Returning a parent type from getExtendedTypes() applies the extension to every descendant. Extending FormType targets nearly every built-in type (excluding ButtonType-derived types); custom types that do not extend FormType are not affected. Multiple types can be returned in a single iterable to target several specific types at once.

references.title: Creating a custom Type Guesser
references.summary: A **type guesser** infers the form type, required flag, max length and pattern from class metadata — Symfony ships a guesser based on Validator constraints, but you can register your own by implementing `FormTypeGuesserInterface` and tagging it `form.type_guesser`. Each guess returns a `TypeGuess`/`ValueGuess` carrying a `Guess::HIGH_CONFIDENCE` / `MEDIUM_CONFIDENCE` / `LOW_CONFIDENCE` score; the guess with the highest confidence wins.

references.title: Validation - The Basics of Validation
references.summary: Introduces Symfony validation for plain PHP objects and explains that constraints define the rules an object must satisfy. It shows the supported mapping styles for object constraints, including PHP attributes, YAML, XML, and static PHP metadata.

references.title: Validation - Using the Validator Service
references.summary: Explains how to validate an object with the `validator` service and its `validate()` method. It also describes the returned `ConstraintViolationList` and how violations can be inspected or rendered.

references.title: Validation - Constraint Targets
references.summary: Describes where Symfony validation constraints can be attached on PHP objects: properties, getter methods, and entire classes. It covers property visibility, uninitialized typed properties, getter naming, and class-level validation.

references.title: Validation - Validating Object With Inheritance
references.summary: Explains how Symfony handles validation metadata when an object extends another class. Parent constraints are automatically validated and merged with child constraints, with validation groups offered as the way to choose different behavior.

references.title: Validation Constraints Reference
references.summary: Indexes the built-in Symfony validation constraints grouped as basic, string, comparison, number, date, choice, file, financial/locale, security, identifier, doctrine and other categories. It is the canonical entry point for discovering which constraints ship with the Validator component.

references.title: NotBlank Constraint
references.summary: `NotBlank` rejects blank strings, empty arrays, `false` and (by default) `null`. Its `allowNull` option lets `null` pass without a violation, and a `normalizer` can be applied before checking.

references.title: Choice Constraint
references.summary: `Choice` validates that a value, or each value of an array, is one of a list of allowed `choices`. Choices may be hard-coded or supplied by a `callback` method, and the `multiple`, `min`, and `max` options control validation of array values.

references.title: Length Constraint
references.summary: `Length` checks that a string length is between `min` and `max` (or equals `exactly`). The `countUnit` option selects between counting bytes, code points, or graphemes, and a `charset` option controls the character set used for length checks.

references.title: Type Constraint
references.summary: `Type` validates that a value is of a specific PHP data type, ctype function class, or fully qualified class name. It is meant for untyped properties because typed PHP properties already enforce types before validation runs.

references.title: Email Constraint
references.summary: `Email` validates that a value is a valid email address, with a `mode` option that selects between `html5`, `html5-allow-no-tld`, and `strict` (RFC 5322 via `egulias/email-validator`). Empty values are considered valid unless combined with `NotBlank`.

references.title: Valid Constraint
references.summary: `Valid` cascades validation into a related object stored in a property so its own constraints are also checked. It does not use the `Default` group, so it always runs unless restricted via the `groups` option, and its `traverse` option controls whether `\Traversable` values are iterated.

references.title: Compound Constraint
references.summary: `Compound` is an abstract base used to bundle several constraints into a reusable, named constraint via the `getConstraints()` method. The resulting subclass must be marked with `#[\Attribute]` to be usable as a PHP attribute.

references.title: Sequentially Constraint
references.summary: `Sequentially` runs a list of constraints step by step on the same value and stops on the first violation. It avoids stacking error messages for the same property and prevents heavy checks from running when earlier syntactic checks already failed.

references.title: Collection Constraint
references.summary: `Collection` validates arrays or objects implementing `Traversable` and `ArrayAccess` by applying different constraints to different keys. It can also enforce whether configured keys must be present and whether extra, unrecognized keys are allowed.

references.title: All Constraint
references.summary: `All` applies the same list of nested constraints to every element of an array or `Traversable` value. It is useful when the collection shape is uniform and each item must satisfy the same rules.

references.title: Count Constraint
references.summary: `Count` validates that an array or `Countable` object contains a number of elements within configured bounds. Its `min`, `max`, `exactly`, and `divisibleBy` options express common collection-size rules.

references.title: Regex Constraint
references.summary: `Regex` validates a string with PHP regular expressions through its required `pattern` option. The `match` option can invert the rule so validation fails when the value does match the pattern, and `htmlPattern` controls generated HTML5 pattern validation.

references.title: Url Constraint
references.summary: `Url` validates that a value has valid URL syntax and defaults to accepting `http` and `https` protocols. It does not verify that the URL host exists in DNS, and options such as `protocols` and `relativeProtocol` adjust accepted URL forms.

references.title: File Constraint
references.summary: `File` validates an existing file path, a stringable file path, or an HttpFoundation `File` or `UploadedFile` object. It can enforce file size and extension/media-type rules, with `extensions` preferred over direct `mimeTypes` configuration.

references.title: AtLeastOneOf Constraint
references.summary: `AtLeastOneOf` validates that a value satisfies at least one constraint from a configured list. Validation stops as soon as one nested constraint passes, and failure messages can include the internal constraint errors.

references.title: When Constraint
references.summary: `When` conditionally applies one or more constraints only when an ExpressionLanguage condition evaluates to true. It can be used at class, property, or method scope and exposes variables such as `this` and, for property validation, `value`.

references.title: Unique Constraint
references.summary: `Unique` validates that the elements of an array or `Traversable` collection are not duplicated. By default it compares elements strictly, while the `fields` option can restrict uniqueness checks to selected keys in nested collection items.

references.title: Expression Constraint
references.summary: `Expression` evaluates an ExpressionLanguage expression for complex class, property, or method validation rules. The expression must evaluate to true for validation to pass by default, and the constraint can use variables such as `this`, `value`, and custom `values`.

references.title: UniqueEntity Constraint
references.summary: Validates that one or more fields of a Doctrine entity remain unique in the database. It is applied at class level and can target a single field or a combination of fields with custom error paths and messages.

references.title: Cascade Constraint
references.summary: Validates an object graph by cascading validation into nested object properties. It can exclude selected properties from cascading, which is useful when most but not all nested values should be traversed.

references.title: Bic
references.summary: Validates that a value matches the **Business Identifier Code (BIC)** format. The `iban` and `ibanPropertyPath` options additionally cross-check that the BIC's country code matches the country code of a paired IBAN. The `mode` option accepts `Bic::VALIDATION_MODE_STRICT` (default) or `Bic::VALIDATION_MODE_CASE_INSENSITIVE`, which uppercases the value before validating.

references.title: Blank
references.summary: Validates that a value is blank, meaning equal to an empty string `""` or `null`. It is the inverse use case of `NotBlank`, but the two are not strictly opposites because `Blank` accepts only those two values. To require strictly `null`, use `IsNull` instead.

references.title: CardScheme
references.summary: Validates that a value is a credit-card number that matches one of the configured card networks. The `schemes` option is **required** and accepts a string or array of constants like `VISA`, `MASTERCARD`, `AMEX`, `DISCOVER`, `DINERS`, `JCB`, `MAESTRO`, `MIR`, `UATP`, `CHINA_UNIONPAY`, `INSTAPAYMENT`, `LASER`. Empty values are valid; combine with `NotBlank` if needed.

references.title: Cidr
references.summary: Validates that a string is a valid **CIDR** (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) notation. By default both IPv4 and IPv6 are accepted; the `version` option restricts which IP version is allowed and supports the same range tokens as the `Ip` constraint (e.g. `4`, `6`, `all`, `4_no_priv`). The `netmaskMin` and `netmaskMax` options bound the netmask value, with `netmaskMax` defaulting to `32` for IPv4 or `128` for IPv6.

references.title: Country
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid country code. By default it expects an **ISO 3166-1 alpha-2** two-letter code (e.g. `FR`); enabling the `alpha3` option switches to the **ISO 3166-1 alpha-3** three-letter code (e.g. `FRA`). Empty values are valid; combine with `NotBlank` to forbid them.

references.title: CssColor
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid CSS color. The `formats` option restricts which CSS color notations are accepted; constants such as `Assert\CssColor::HEX_LONG`, `HEX_SHORT`, `BASIC_NAMED_COLORS`, `EXTENDED_NAMED_COLORS`, `SYSTEM_COLORS`, `KEYWORDS`, `RGB`, `RGBA`, `HSL`, and `HSLA` enumerate the allowed formats. Without `formats`, any of those formats is accepted.

references.title: Currency
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **3-letter ISO 4217** currency code (e.g. `EUR`, `USD`). It applies to a property or method and rejects values that are not registered ISO 4217 codes. Empty values are accepted; pair with `NotBlank` to forbid them.

references.title: Date
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid date string in the **`Y-m-d`** format (e.g. `2023-10-18`). It accepts strings or `Stringable` objects; if a custom format is required, use the `DateTime` constraint. Empty values are valid.

references.title: DateTime
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid datetime string. The `format` option (default `Y-m-d H:i:s`) accepts any format string supported by `DateTime::createFromFormat()`. The default message exposes `{{ value }}` and `{{ format }}` placeholders.

references.title: DivisibleBy
references.summary: Validates that a number is divisible by another value. The `value` option (or constructor argument) sets the divisor and may be an integer or float (e.g. `0.25` for quarter-step weights). It supports `propertyPath` to compare against another property of the same object.

references.title: EqualTo
references.summary: Validates that a value is equal to another value using **loose comparison (`==`)**. The reference value is set through the `value` option or constructor argument, or via `propertyPath` to point at another property. Use `IdenticalTo` instead when type-strict comparison is required.

references.title: GreaterThan
references.summary: Validates that a value is **strictly greater than** another value. The compared value can be a literal in the `value` option/constructor argument or another property via `propertyPath`. It also compares `DateTime` objects with any string accepted by the `DateTime` constructor (e.g. `"today"`, `"+5 hours"`, `"today UTC"`).

references.title: GreaterThanOrEqual
references.summary: Validates that a value is **greater than or equal to** another value. Like `GreaterThan`, the comparison value may be supplied via `value` or `propertyPath`, and `DateTime` properties accept any string accepted by the `DateTime` constructor (e.g. `today`, `+5 hours`).

references.title: Hostname
references.summary: Validates that a string is a valid hostname. Internally it uses `filter_var()` with `FILTER_VALIDATE_DOMAIN`. The `requireTld` option (default `true`) requires a top-level domain such as `example.com`; when set to `false`, single-label hostnames such as `example` are accepted. RFC 2606 reserved TLDs (`.example`, `.invalid`, `.localhost`, `.test`) are rejected.

references.title: IBAN
references.summary: Validates that a value is a syntactically valid **International Bank Account Number (IBAN)**. The validator transparently strips various whitespace characters (regular, non-breaking, narrow non-breaking) before validating, so the same input may be accepted while still failing strict downstream APIs. Empty values are considered valid.

references.title: IdenticalTo
references.summary: Validates that a value is identical to another value using **strict comparison (`===`)** — both type and value must match. The compared value comes from the `value` option/constructor argument or `propertyPath`. Use `EqualTo` instead when loose `==` comparison is desired.

references.title: Image
references.summary: Extends the `File` constraint with image-specific defaults — `mimeTypes` defaults to `image/*` — and adds dimension/orientation rules. Options like `minWidth`/`maxWidth`/`minHeight`/`maxHeight` cap pixel dimensions, `minRatio`/`maxRatio` cap the aspect ratio, `minPixels`/`maxPixels` cap the total pixel count, and `allowLandscape`/`allowPortrait`/`allowSquare` reject specific orientations. Setting `detectCorrupted` to `true` runs `imagecreatefromstring()` to detect broken image bytes (requires the GD extension).

references.title: Ip
references.summary: Validates that a string is a valid IP address. The `version` option chooses the accepted address family and range — for example `4`, `6`, `all`, `4_no_priv` (no RFC 1918 ranges), `4_public`, `6_reserved`, `all_public`. The default is `4`, so IPv6 addresses fail unless `version` is changed.

references.title: IsFalse
references.summary: Validates that a value is **`false`**, exactly the integer `0`, or exactly the string `"0"`. Often used on boolean getter methods (e.g. `isStateInvalid()`) so that the surrounding object is valid only when the getter returns one of those falsy values. `null` is treated as valid.

references.title: IsNull
references.summary: Validates that a value is exactly `null`. To accept the empty string in addition to `null`, use `Blank`; to accept any non-`null` value, use `NotNull`.

references.title: IsTrue
references.summary: Validates that a value is **`true`**, exactly the integer `1`, or exactly the string `"1"`. Commonly applied to a boolean getter method to enforce that some derived state is true. `null` is treated as valid.

references.title: Isbn
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **International Standard Book Number (ISBN)**. The `type` option selects between `ISBN_10`, `ISBN_13`, or no value (which accepts either). Different message options (`isbn10Message`, `isbn13Message`, `bothIsbnMessage`) tailor the error per mode.

references.title: Isin
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **International Securities Identification Number (ISIN)** — a 12-character alphanumeric code identifying a security. It applies to a property or method.

references.title: Issn
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **International Standard Serial Number (ISSN)**. The `caseSensitive` option (default `false`) controls whether a final `x` checksum digit must be uppercase. The `requireHyphen` option (default `false`) requires the canonical `XXXX-XXXX` form.

references.title: Json
references.summary: Validates that a string contains syntactically valid **JSON**. It does not validate schema or shape — only that `json_decode()` parses the input without errors.

references.title: Language
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **Unicode language identifier** (e.g. `fr` or `zh-Hant`). With `alpha3` set to `true` it instead expects ISO 639-2 alpha-3 codes (e.g. `fra`).

references.title: LessThan
references.summary: Validates that a value is **strictly less than** another value. As with the other comparison constraints, the comparison target is given by `value` (or constructor argument) or `propertyPath`, and `DateTime` properties may be compared against any string accepted by `new DateTime(...)`.

references.title: LessThanOrEqual
references.summary: Validates that a value is **less than or equal to** another value. Use `LessThan` for a strict bound, or `LessThanOrEqual` when the bound itself must be accepted. Like the other comparison constraints, it accepts both literal values and `propertyPath` references and supports `DateTime` comparisons via `DateTime`-constructor strings.

references.title: Locale
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **locale** identifier (a language code optionally combined with a region/script, e.g. `en`, `fr_FR`, `zh_Hant`). It applies to a property or method.

references.title: Luhn
references.summary: Validates that a numeric string passes the **Luhn algorithm** checksum, used as a quick sanity check on credit-card-like numbers before sending them to a payment gateway. It does not check that the number belongs to a real account or matches a specific scheme — combine it with `CardScheme` for that.

references.title: Negative
references.summary: Validates that a value is a **negative number**, i.e. strictly less than zero. Zero itself is **not** accepted; use `NegativeOrZero` if zero must be allowed.

references.title: NegativeOrZero
references.summary: Validates that a value is a **negative number or zero**. Use `Negative` instead if zero must be rejected.

references.title: NotCompromisedPassword
references.summary: Validates that a password has not appeared in any **haveibeenpwned.com** public data breach. The `threshold` option sets how many leak occurrences are tolerated (default `1`, so any leaked password is rejected). When `skipOnError` is `true`, network failures while contacting the service are silently ignored instead of failing validation.

references.title: NotEqualTo
references.summary: Validates that a value is **not equal** to another value, using PHP loose comparison (`!=`). The reference is supplied via `value` or `propertyPath`. Use `NotIdenticalTo` for strict (`!==`) comparison.

references.title: NotIdenticalTo
references.summary: Validates that a value is **not identical** to another value, using PHP strict comparison (`!==`). It rejects values that match the reference both in type and value, and is paired with `IdenticalTo` for the inverse use case.

references.title: NotNull
references.summary: Validates that a value is **not strictly equal to `null`**. Empty strings, empty arrays, `0`, and `false` are accepted. Use `NotBlank` if those should also be rejected.

references.title: Positive
references.summary: Validates that a value is a **positive number**, i.e. strictly greater than zero. Zero is **not** accepted; use `PositiveOrZero` to allow it.

references.title: PositiveOrZero
references.summary: Validates that a value is a **positive number or zero**. Use `Positive` instead when zero must be rejected.

references.title: Time
references.summary: Validates that a string is a valid time in the **`H:i:s`** format (e.g. `16:27:36`). The `withSeconds` option (default `true`) controls whether the seconds component is required; setting it to `false` accepts the `H:i` form.

references.title: Timezone
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid timezone identifier (e.g. `Europe/Paris`). The `zone` option restricts the timezone group via `DateTimeZone` constants such as `DateTimeZone::EUROPE`. The `countryCode` option further restricts to identifiers of a specific country (paired with `zone: DateTimeZone::PER_COUNTRY`). The `intlCompatible` option requires the value to also be valid under the Intl extension.

references.title: Traverse
references.summary: Class-level constraint that asks the validator to traverse the object as a `Traversable` and validate every yielded element. Useful when an object is iterable but its inner data is not exposed through public properties or accessors. It only applies to a class.

references.title: UserPassword
references.summary: Validates that a string equals the **currently authenticated user's password**. Typically used in a "change password" form where the user must re-enter their old password. It only applies when there is an authenticated user and a configured password hasher.

references.title: UUID
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **UUID** per RFC 4122 by default. The `versions` option restricts which UUID versions are accepted (e.g. `[Uuid::V4_RANDOM, Uuid::V7_UNIX_TIME]`). Setting `strict` to `false` accepts non-RFC formats used by other systems (e.g. PostgreSQL UUIDs without dashes).

references.title: Range
references.summary: Validates that a number or `DateTime` falls between `min` and `max`. Both bounds may be omitted to create open-ended ranges, and date strings accepted by the `DateTime` constructor can be used to compare against time ranges.

references.title: Validation - Properties
references.summary: Describes the property scope of Symfony validation, where constraints are attached directly to class fields. Symfony reads private, protected and public properties through reflection, so visibility does not change which constraints can be applied.

references.title: Validation - Getters
references.summary: Describes the getter scope, where constraints are applied to the return value of methods whose name starts with `get`, `is`, or `has`. In YAML, XML, and static PHP mappings the prefix is omitted from the configured getter name so the constraint can be moved between a property and a getter without code changes.

references.title: Validation - Classes
references.summary: Describes class-level validation, where some constraints apply to the entire object being validated. The `Callback` constraint is the canonical example: methods specified at class level are invoked when the class is validated to perform custom checks.

references.title: Validation - Constraints in Form Classes
references.summary: Explains how to attach validation constraints directly inside a form type via the `constraints` option on each field. This lets a form enforce rules without those rules living on the underlying data class.

references.title: Validation - Validation Callables
references.summary: Documents `Validation::createCallable()` and `Validation::createIsValidCallable()`, which wrap one or more constraints into a closure. The callable form integrates with Console question helpers and the OptionsResolver so constraints can be reused outside the validator service.

references.title: How to Validate Raw Values (Scalar Values and Arrays)
references.summary: Shows how to call `validator->validate($value, $constraint)` directly on scalars, arrays, and structured input. The `Collection`, `All`, and `Optional` constraints make it possible to describe the expected shape of arbitrary array data without a mapped object.

references.title: How to Apply only a Subset of all Your Validation Constraints (Validation Groups)
references.summary: Explains how to organize constraints into named validation groups via the `groups` option and how the `Default` group, the class-name group, and explicit named groups interact. It also documents the `Default` versus class-name distinction for embedded objects validated through `Valid`.

references.title: How to Define the Validation Groups to Use (Forms)
references.summary: Shows how to set the `validation_groups` form option from a controller and from `configureOptions()` of a form type. It also documents how to combine `Default` with custom groups and recommends snake_case names for user-defined groups.

references.title: How to Sequentially Apply Validation Groups
references.summary: Explains the static `GroupSequence` feature, where a class declares an ordered list of groups via `#[Assert\GroupSequence]`, the `group_sequence` mapping key, or `setGroupSequence()`. Subsequent groups in the sequence run only if the preceding group produced no violations, and the class-name group must be used in place of `Default` to avoid recursion.

references.title: Group Sequence Providers
references.summary: Documents how an entity can implement `GroupSequenceProviderInterface` to compute the sequence dynamically at runtime. A flat array stops on the first failing group, while a nested array keeps validating sibling groups inside the same step before moving to the next.

references.title: Advanced Validation Group Provider
references.summary: Shows how to move group-sequence logic into a separate class implementing `GroupProviderInterface` and link it via `#[Assert\GroupSequenceProvider(provider: ...)]`. Such providers are autowired or registered with the `validator.group_provider` tag, decoupling sequence rules from entities.

references.title: Callback Constraint
references.summary: Documents `#[Assert\Callback]`, the constraint that runs custom validation methods on the validated object. The callback option may be a method name, an array callable `[Class, method]`, or a `Closure` (PHP-only mapping), and the constraint can be applied at class, property, or getter level.

references.title: Callback Constraint - The Callback Method
references.summary: Explains the signature of an instance callback `(ExecutionContextInterface $context, mixed $payload)`. The method does not return a value; instead it calls `$context->buildViolation(...)` and `addViolation()` to attach errors, optionally narrowing them to a specific field via `atPath()`.

references.title: Callback Constraint - Static Callbacks
references.summary: Describes static callback methods, which receive the validated value as the first argument followed by the `ExecutionContextInterface` and the payload. Static callbacks must therefore work without an instance and access the validated object through this first parameter.

references.title: Callback Constraint - External Callbacks and Closures
references.summary: Shows how to invoke a static method on another class via an array callable, and how to register a `Closure` as a callback when configuring the constraint in PHP. Global functions and service methods are not supported, and using a `Closure` disables the attribute cache for the class because closures cannot be cached.

references.title: How to Handle Different Error Levels
references.summary: A constraint can attach an arbitrary **payload** (any value) that propagates to the violation. The conventional pattern is a **severity payload** like `["severity" => "error"]` or `["severity" => "warning"]`, used by templates to render distinct UI for each severity level. Read the value back via `ConstraintViolation::getConstraint()->payload`.

references.title: How to Create a Custom Validation Constraint - Creating the Validator Itself
references.summary: Shows the body of a `ConstraintValidator::validate()` method using `$this->context->buildViolation($constraint->message)` to start building a violation. The example calls `setParameter('{{ string }}', $value)` to interpolate placeholders before `addViolation()` registers the violation on the context.

references.title: Callback Constraint - The Callback Method
references.summary: Demonstrates the violation builder fluent API in a `Callback` validator: `$context->buildViolation('...')->atPath('firstName')->addViolation()`. `atPath()` reattributes the violation to a specific property path so form errors render next to the correct field.

references.title: How to Translate Validation Constraint Messages - Custom Translation Domain
references.summary: Shows how a `ConstraintViolationBuilder` chains `setTranslationDomain('validation_errors')` to translate that single violation in a different domain than the framework default. The default domain can also be configured globally via `framework.validation.translation_domain`.

references.title: Service Container
references.summary: Introduces Symfony services and the service container as the centralized object that constructs application objects. Explains that services are created lazily, returned as the same instance on subsequent requests, and accessed via type-hinted dependency injection rather than direct container lookups.

references.title: Service Container - Fetching and using Services
references.summary: Shows how to retrieve services from the container by type-hinting a class or interface in controller actions or service constructors. Introduces the debug:autowiring and debug:container console commands for inspecting available services.

references.title: Service Container - Limiting Services to a specific Symfony Environment
references.summary: Documents the #[When] attribute from the DependencyInjection component, which registers a class as a service only in specific Symfony environments such as dev or test. Multiple #[When] attributes can be combined on the same class.

references.title: Service Container - Public Versus Private Services
references.summary: Defines that all services in Symfony are private by default and cannot be retrieved with $container->get(). Explains how to expose a service publicly with the public option or the #[Autoconfigure(public: true)] attribute, and recommends fetching services via dependency injection instead of public services.

references.title: Service Container - Choose a Specific Service
references.summary: Explains how to explicitly select a particular service among many that share an interface or alias. Shows that the @ prefix in YAML, the type="service" attribute in XML, and the service() function in PHP all reference a service by id rather than passing a string.

references.title: Service Container - Linting Service Definitions
references.summary: Documents the lint:container console command that verifies arguments injected into services match their type declarations. The check is performed by the CheckTypeDeclarationsPass compiler pass, which is disabled during normal compilation for performance reasons.

references.title: Service Container - Injecting a Closure as an Argument
references.summary: Describes how to pass a callable as a constructor argument using YAML's !closure tag, XML's type="closure" attribute, or PHP's closure() function. The referenced service must be invokable so it can be wrapped in a closure for delegated execution.

references.title: Service Container - Remove Services
references.summary: Shows how to remove a service definition from the container in PHP configuration with $services->remove(), so a service is no longer available in a particular environment such as test. The original service definition is dropped at compile time.

references.title: Lazy Services
references.summary: Explains how marking a service as lazy injects a proxy so the real service is instantiated only when first used. Documents the lazy configuration option, #[Autoconfigure(lazy: true)], #[Autowire(..., lazy: true)], and interface proxifying for services whose concrete classes cannot be proxied directly.

references.title: How to Define Non Shared Services
references.summary: Documents that services are shared by default, so repeated retrieval returns the same instance. Shows how to set shared: false, #[Autoconfigure(shared: false)], or the PHP configurator's share(false) to receive a new instance whenever the service is requested.

references.title: Container Building Workflow
references.summary: Walks through how the container is **built** in the framework: extensions load YAML/XML/PHP into a `ContainerBuilder`, compiler passes run in their priority/types (`PassConfig::TYPE_BEFORE_OPTIMIZATION`, `TYPE_OPTIMIZE`, `TYPE_BEFORE_REMOVING`, `TYPE_REMOVE`, `TYPE_AFTER_REMOVING`), and the result is dumped to PHP for production use.

references.title: How to Configure a Service with a Configurator
references.summary: A **service configurator** is a callable invoked **after** the service is instantiated but before it is handed back to the consumer — typical use case: register handlers/transformers on the new service. It is declared with the `configurator` key (YAML) or `#[AsConfigurator]`/`->configurator(...)` (PHP), and accepts `[Class::class, "method"]`, `'service::method'`, or a closure.

references.title: Synthetic Services
references.summary: A **synthetic service** is one that is **not built by the container** but injected at runtime through `Container::set()`.
