Define `synthetic: true` in the service definition; the container then refuses to instantiate it and demands an external `set()` call (e.g. for `kernel`, which the kernel itself injects on boot).

references.title: Service Container - Fetching and using Services
references.summary: Confirms that a fresh Symfony application already exposes many built-in services (logger, request_stack, router.default, etc.) that can be injected by type-hinting their class or interface. Lists the `debug:autowiring` and `debug:container` commands used to discover them.

references.title: How to Debug the Service Container & List Services
references.summary: Documents the `debug:container` command which lists every service registered in the application, with `--show-hidden` to also display services whose id starts with a dot. Single-service inspection accepts the service id as an argument and supports `--show-arguments`.

references.title: How to Debug the Service Container & List Services - Debugging Service Tags
references.summary: Shows how `debug:container --tag=kernel.event_listener` lists every service carrying a given tag. Partial tag prefixes are accepted and trigger an interactive selector; this is the canonical way to discover the built-in tag-driven extension points.

references.title: Built-in Symfony Service Tags
references.summary: Reference list of every framework-recognized service tag (such as `kernel.event_listener`, `kernel.event_subscriber`, `console.command`, `twig.extension`, `validator.constraint_validator`, `messenger.message_handler`). These tags hook custom services into built-in framework subsystems.

references.title: Controller - Fetching Services
references.summary: Lists common built-in services available through type-hints in controller arguments, such as `LoggerInterface`, `RequestStack`, `EventDispatcherInterface`, `RouterInterface`, `MailerInterface`, the validator and the serializer. Recommends `php bin/console debug:autowiring` to find the autowireable services and their aliases.

references.title: Controller - The Base Controller Class & Services
references.summary: Documents helpers from `AbstractController` that proxy to built-in services, including `getParameter()`, `addFlash()`, `redirectToRoute()`, `render()`, `denyAccessUnlessGranted()`, and `dispatchMessage()`. It also covers access to the typed `$this->container` service locator filled with subscribed services.

references.title: Using Parameters within a Dependency Injection Class
references.summary: Documents how parameters such as `kernel.project_dir`, `kernel.cache_dir`, `kernel.environment`, and `kernel.debug` are exposed by FrameworkBundle. It also shows how those parameters can be referenced from configuration files using `%kernel.*%`.

references.title: How to Retrieve the Request from the Service Container
references.summary: The `request` service is removed in modern Symfony — to access the **current `Request`** in a service, inject `RequestStack` and call `getCurrentRequest()` (or `getMainRequest()` for the master request). Inject `RequestStack`, never the request itself, because the latter changes during the request lifecycle (sub-requests, ESI fragments).

references.title: Configuring Symfony - Configuration Parameters
references.summary: Defines container parameters as reusable configuration values declared under the `parameters` key. Documents supported parameter types (strings, booleans, arrays, binary, PHP constants, PHP enums), the `%param%` reference syntax in YAML/XML/PHP, and the convention that parameter names starting with a dot are available only during container compilation.

references.title: Service Container - Service Parameters
references.summary: Explains the difference between regular parameter values and service references. Lists the container's accessor methods `hasParameter()`, `getParameter()` and `setParameter()`, and notes that parameter names are case-sensitive and parameters can only be set before container compilation.

references.title: Configuring Symfony - Accessing Configuration Parameters
references.summary: Shows three ways to read parameters: the `getParameter()` helper on `AbstractController`, explicit injection via the `arguments` configuration key with `%param%` syntax, and injecting `ContainerBagInterface` to retrieve any parameter at runtime. Notes that autowiring does not match parameters by type-hint.

references.title: Environment Variable Processors
references.summary: Documents the env var processor syntax `%env(processor:VAR_NAME)%` used to cast or transform environment variable values when they are referenced as parameters. Lists processors such as `bool`, `int`, `float`, `json`, `resolve`, `csv`, `file`, `default` and chained combinations.

references.title: Configuring Symfony - Configuration Based on Environment Variables
references.summary: Shows the `%env(VAR_NAME)%` syntax that resolves an environment variable at runtime rather than at container compile time. Explains how env vars are loaded from `.env` files and how they integrate with parameter references in service configuration.

references.title: Using Parameters within a Dependency Injection Class
references.summary: Explains how to use container parameters such as `%kernel.debug%` from inside a bundle's `Extension::load()` to drive **configuration-time** decisions. Container parameters resolve only at compile time, so to read them in `load()` you must inject them via `$container->getParameter(...)` (the `%...%` placeholder syntax expands later).

references.title: How to work with Service Definition Objects
references.summary: A **`Definition`** describes how the container should build a service: class, arguments, calls, configurator, public/private, lazy, decorator, factory, parent, etc. It is the in-memory representation manipulated by compiler passes; the container reads `Definition` objects when generating the optimised dumped container.

references.title: How to Inject Values Based on Complex Expressions
references.summary: Use the **`@=`** prefix in YAML/PHP to inject the result of an **ExpressionLanguage** expression as a service argument. Variables `service('id')`, `parameter('name')`, and any container parameter are accessible inside the expression — handy for selecting a value based on `kernel.debug` or combining configuration.

concepts.concept: Services registration (only with YAML and PHP attributes)

references.title: Service Container - Automatic Service Loading in services.yaml
references.summary: Documents the default `config/services.yaml` shipped with Symfony: the `_defaults` block sets `autowire: true` and `autoconfigure: true`, and the `App\` PSR-4 prototype loads every class under `src/` as a service. The `exclude` option keeps non-service paths such as `Entity/`, `DependencyInjection/`, and `Kernel.php` out of the container.

references.title: Service Container - Importing Many Services at once with resource
references.summary: Explains how `resource:` registers all classes in a directory as services using their fully-qualified class name as the service id. Excluded classes are not tracked, slightly improving dev-environment performance, and unused private services are removed during compilation.

references.title: Service Container - Multiple Service Definitions Using the Same Namespace
references.summary: Explains that YAML uses the namespace as a unique key, so multiple registrations under the same namespace require the explicit `namespace:` option with arbitrary keys (for example `command_handlers:` and `event_subscribers:`). This is a YAML-specific limitation.

references.title: Service Container - Explicitly Configuring Services and Arguments
references.summary: Demonstrates registering several services for the same class under custom ids (for example `site_update_manager.superadmin` vs `site_update_manager.normal_users`). Adding an alias keyed by the class makes one of them the default when the class is type-hinted.

references.title: Service Container - Manually Wiring Arguments
references.summary: Shows how to override autowiring by setting `arguments:` on a service. Arguments named with `$argName` map by parameter name; renaming a constructor parameter without updating the wiring throws a clear exception on the next request.

references.title: Service Container - Abstract Service Arguments
references.summary: Documents the `!abstract` YAML tag (and `abstract_arg()` PHP function) used to declare a placeholder argument that must be replaced by a compiler pass or bundle extension. Failing to replace the value at compile time raises a `RuntimeException` at runtime.

references.title: How to Create Service Aliases and Mark Services as Public/Private
references.summary: Documents the `alias` configuration that creates an alternative id for a service, and the `public` / `deprecated` options. Alias visibility independently controls whether the alias itself can be retrieved with `$container->get()`.

references.title: How to Import Configuration Files
references.summary: Documents the `imports:` directive in YAML configuration that loads additional configuration files (YAML, XML or PHP) before the rest of the file is parsed. `ignore_errors: not_found` silently skips missing files; `ignore_errors: true` skips all loading errors.

references.title: How to Manage Common Dependencies with Parent Services
references.summary: Shows how abstract parent service definitions let related services reuse common arguments and method calls. Explains that child services declare parent: ..., can override settings, and do not inherit the parent's shared, abstract, or tags attributes.

references.title: How to Make Service Arguments/References Optional
references.summary: Documents strategies for optional service references so missing dependencies do not break a service definition. Covers null-on-invalid references in XML/PHP and the YAML @? syntax, which ignores a missing dependency and removes a method call when appropriate.

references.title: Service Method Calls and Setter Injection
references.summary: Shows how to configure method calls on services, commonly used for setter injection or optional dependencies. YAML uses the `calls` key, while PHP configuration uses `->call()` on the service definition.

references.title: Service Configurators
references.summary: Explains service configurators, which run custom callable logic after a service has been instantiated and configured. Configurators are useful when complex setup cannot be expressed cleanly as constructor arguments or simple method calls.

references.title: Types of Injection
references.summary: Compares the three injection styles: **constructor injection** (recommended default), **setter / method injection** (for optional dependencies), and **property injection** (rare, for legacy code). Constructor injection is preferred because it makes mandatory dependencies explicit and the resulting object is fully usable from the moment it is constructed.

references.title: Lazy Services
references.summary: Marking a service `lazy: true` makes the container inject a **virtual proxy** (since 7.x via PHP 8.4 `\ReflectionClass::newLazyProxy()` natively, otherwise via `symfony/proxy-manager-bridge`). The real instance is built only on first method call, which is useful for breaking circular dependencies and avoiding the cost of building heavy services that may never be used.

references.title: How to Define Non-Shared Services
references.summary: By default services are **shared** (the same instance is returned every time). Set `shared: false` to make the container build a **new instance on each retrieval** — useful for stateful workers or message handlers that must not leak state between dispatches.

references.title: How to Decorate Services
references.summary: Explains the Decorator pattern in the service container: redefining a service id replaces the previous definition, but `decorates` keeps the original service available under the auto-generated id `.inner` so the new service can wrap it. Documents the `#[AsDecorator]` and `#[AutowireDecorated]` attributes used to declare decorators with PHP attributes and inject the inner service when autowiring cannot resolve it by type-hint alone.

references.title: How to Decorate Services - Decoration Priority
references.summary: Documents the `decoration_priority` option that controls the order in which multiple decorators are applied to the same service. The default priority is 0 and higher priorities are applied **earlier**, producing nesting like `new Baz(new Bar(new Foo()))` with priority 5 > 1.

references.title: How to Decorate Services - Stacking Decorators
references.summary: Describes the `stack` configuration entry that defines an ordered list of services, each decorating the next. Stacks support inlined services, references, and child definitions, and can only carry the `public` and `deprecated` attributes.

references.title: How to Decorate Services - Control the Behavior When the Decorated Service Does Not Exist
references.summary: Documents the `decoration_on_invalid` option with three values: `exception` (default — throws `ServiceNotFoundException`), `ignore` (removes the decorator), and `null` (keeps the decorator and injects null for the missing inner service). The `null` mode usually requires a nullable constructor argument.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags
references.summary: Defines service tags as labels that mark services for special treatment by Symfony or third-party bundles. Shows the YAML, XML and PHP syntax for adding a tag (`tags: ['twig.extension']`) and notes that tags can carry additional attributes beyond the required `name`.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - Autoconfiguring Tags
references.summary: Documents the `_instanceof` configuration key and the `#[AutoconfigureTag]` PHP attribute that automatically tag services implementing a base class or interface. Also shows `registerForAutoconfiguration()` and `registerAttributeForAutoconfiguration()` for kernel/bundle-level autoconfiguration logic.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - Reference Tagged Services
references.summary: Documents YAML's `!tagged_iterator`, XML's `<argument type="tagged_iterator">`, PHP's `tagged_iterator()` and the `#[TaggedIterator]` attribute that inject all services with a tag as an iterable. The `exclude` option removes specific ids and the `exclude_self` option (default `true`) excludes the consuming service when it carries the same tag.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - Tagged Services with Priority
references.summary: Explains the `priority` tag attribute (an integer, default 0). **Higher** priority places the service earlier in the iterable. As an alternative to writing `priority` on every tag, services may declare a static `getDefaultPriority()` method, or the consuming service may set `defaultPriorityMethod` to use a different method name.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - Tagged Services with Index
references.summary: Documents `index_by`/`indexAttribute` and `default_index_method`, which control the keys of the injected iterable. Without configuration, tagged services are indexed by service id; with these options the iterable can be keyed by a per-tag attribute or by a value computed by a static method on the tagged service.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - Create a Compiler Pass
references.summary: Shows the canonical pattern of consuming tagged services from a compiler pass. `findTaggedServiceIds('app.mail_transport')` returns an array keyed by service id whose values are the tag attribute lists, and the pass typically wires each service into a collector via `addMethodCall(...)`.

references.title: Built-in Symfony Service Tags
references.summary: Reference list of every service tag recognized by the core Symfony framework, such as `kernel.event_listener`, `kernel.event_subscriber`, `console.command`, `twig.extension`, `validator.constraint_validator`, `messenger.message_handler`, and many others. Each entry documents the additional attributes the tag accepts.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - The #[AsTaggedItem] attribute
references.summary: Documents the `#[AsTaggedItem]` attribute, which lets a class declare its `index` and/or `priority` for autoconfigured tags directly via the attribute. This removes the need to write a static method or YAML configuration for those tag attributes.

references.title: How to Create Friendly Configuration for a Bundle
references.summary: Explains semantic ("friendly") configuration: bundles expose a high-level configuration tree under a root key (the DI alias), and the bundle's extension class translates that tree into low-level service definitions and parameters at compile time. Lists the two recommended approaches: extending `AbstractBundle` or providing a separate Extension class.

references.title: How to Create Friendly Configuration for a Bundle - Using the AbstractBundle Class
references.summary: Documents the modern approach: a class extending `Symfony\Component\HttpKernel\Bundle\AbstractBundle` defines the configuration tree in `configure(DefinitionConfigurator $definition)` and consumes the merged config in `loadExtension(array $config, ContainerConfigurator $container, ContainerBuilder $builder)`. Both methods run only at compile time.

references.title: How to Create Friendly Configuration for a Bundle - Using the Bundle Extension
references.summary: Describes the traditional approach: a separate Extension class processes configuration in its `load()` method. Notes that the root configuration key is derived from the bundle name (snake-cased, without the `Bundle` suffix), for example `AcmeSocialBundle` becomes `acme_social`.

references.title: How to Create Friendly Configuration for a Bundle - Processing the $configs Array
references.summary: Explains why `load()` receives an array of arrays (one entry per configuration source). Documents how to merge and validate them via `Symfony\Component\Config\Definition\Processor` and a `Configuration` class implementing `ConfigurationInterface`, returning a `TreeBuilder` from `getConfigTreeBuilder()`.

references.title: How to Load Service Configuration inside a Bundle
references.summary: Documents the conventions for the bundle extension class: it must live in `src/DependencyInjection/`, end with `Extension`, and implement the `load(array $configs, ContainerBuilder $container)` method. That method loads service definitions, typically from XML/YAML/PHP resource files.

references.title: Defining and Processing Configuration Values
references.summary: Describes how to model semantic configuration with the Config component's `TreeBuilder`. Covers node types (`scalarNode`, `booleanNode`, `arrayNode`, `enumNode`, etc.), default values, validation rules, prototype arrays, normalization, and `appendChild()` for splitting large definitions across files.

references.title: How to Simplify Configuration of Multiple Bundles
references.summary: Documents the `PrependExtensionInterface::prepend()` method, which lets one bundle's extension push default configuration into other bundles' extensions before any of them load. This is how some bundles configure dependencies (such as `framework.cache`) on behalf of the user.

references.title: Using a Factory to Create Services
references.summary: Introduces the Factory design pattern in the service container. Instead of instantiating the configured class directly, the container calls a factory method to obtain the service object, useful when construction logic is non-trivial.

references.title: Using a Factory - Static Factories
references.summary: Documents how to declare a factory using a class and a **static method**. In YAML the `factory` option accepts a `[ClassName, methodName]` array, and in XML/PHP the equivalent `<factory class="..." method="..."/>` and `->factory([Class::class, 'method'])` syntaxes are used.

references.title: Using a Factory - Using the Class as Factory Itself
references.summary: Shows how to call a static factory method on the **same class** that is being created. The class name in the factory array can be set to `null`, or alternatively the `constructor` option can be used to declare the named static factory method (also exposed via `#[Autoconfigure(constructor: ...)]`).

references.title: Using a Factory - Non-Static Factories
references.summary: Explains how to delegate service creation to a non-static method. The factory itself must first be registered as a service, then the consuming service references it with `factory: ['@FactoryService', 'method']` (or its XML/PHP equivalents).

references.title: Using a Factory - Invokable Factories
references.summary: Documents how a factory class with an `__invoke()` method can be used by referencing only the factory service id without specifying a method name. The container calls the factory as a callback to produce the service.

references.title: Using a Factory - Using Expressions in Service Factories
references.summary: Shows how the `@=` expression syntax (Symfony ExpressionLanguage) can be used in the `factory` option to compute the service dynamically. Expressions can call `parameter()`, `service()`, and `arg()` to produce different services depending on configuration.

references.title: Using a Factory - Passing Arguments to the Factory Method
references.summary: Explains how the `arguments` configuration key supplies positional or named arguments to the factory method. Factory method arguments are autowired when autowiring is enabled, just like constructor arguments.

references.title: How to Work with Compiler Passes
references.summary: Explains that compiler passes manipulate service definitions during container compilation. In a Symfony application, they are registered with `$container->addCompilerPass(new CustomPass())` from the `build()` method of the kernel.

references.title: How to Work with Compiler Passes - Kernel as Compiler Pass
references.summary: Shows that the application kernel can directly implement `CompilerPassInterface` and put logic in `process(ContainerBuilder $container)` instead of registering a separate pass class. This is convenient for small applications that only need one compiler pass.

references.title: How to Work with Compiler Passes - Working with Compiler Passes in Bundles
references.summary: Describes how reusable bundles register compiler passes from the bundle class's `build()` method. Establishes the convention that custom service tags introduced by a bundle should follow the `bundle_name.tag_name` naming pattern.

references.title: Compiling the Container
references.summary: Documents the container compilation phase, which validates definitions, resolves parameters, removes unused or private services, and resolves aliases. Several built-in passes such as `CheckDefinitionValidityPass` are registered automatically before optimization and removal phases.

references.title: Compiling the Container - Creating Separate Compiler Passes
references.summary: Describes implementing `CompilerPassInterface::process(ContainerBuilder $container)` in a dedicated class. Lists the `PassConfig` constants `TYPE_BEFORE_OPTIMIZATION`, `TYPE_OPTIMIZE`, `TYPE_BEFORE_REMOVING`, `TYPE_REMOVE` and `TYPE_AFTER_REMOVING` that determine when the pass runs, and the integer priority that orders passes within a phase (higher runs earlier).

references.title: Compiling the Container - Managing Configuration with Extensions
references.summary: Explains how container `Extension` classes (implementing `ExtensionInterface`) load configuration during compilation. Their `load()` method receives the merged config sections keyed by the extension alias and registers the corresponding services, making them part of the compiled container.

references.title: Compiling the Container - Prepending Configuration Passed to the Extension
references.summary: Describes the `PrependExtensionInterface` and its `prepend(ContainerBuilder $container)` method. The method runs before any extension's `load()` and uses `prependExtensionConfig()` to push configuration into other bundles' extensions.

references.title: Service Tags - Create a Compiler Pass
references.summary: Demonstrates the canonical compiler pass that locates services by tag. The pass calls `$container->findTaggedServiceIds('app.mail_transport')` and wires every result into a collector service via `$definition->addMethodCall('addTransport', [new Reference($id)])`.

references.title: Defining Services Dependencies Automatically (Autowiring)
references.summary: Explains autowiring as the mechanism that reads constructor and method type-hints to inject the matching service automatically. Notes that autowiring uses the compiled container, so it has no runtime cost in production.

references.title: Autowiring - Autowiring Logic Explained
references.summary: Describes the rule that autowiring matches a type-hint to a service whose id is exactly the fully-qualified class or interface name. If no such service id exists, the container throws a clear exception rather than guessing.

references.title: Autowiring - Using Aliases to Enable Autowiring
references.summary: Shows how a service alias whose id matches a class or interface enables autowiring when the actual service id does not. Bundles routinely add such aliases (for example Psr\Log\LoggerInterface aliased to logger) to enable type-hinted injection.

references.title: Autowiring - Dealing with Multiple Implementations of the Same Type
references.summary: Explains named autowiring aliases, where the alias id is the interface followed by an argument name (for example `App\Util\TransformerInterface $shoutyTransformer`). The container picks the matching implementation based on the constructor argument name.

references.title: Autowiring - Fixing Non-Autowireable Arguments
references.summary: Covers the #[Autowire] PHP attribute that injects a specific service id, parameter (`%kernel.project_dir%`), expression, or environment variable into otherwise non-autowireable scalar arguments. Useful when autowiring alone cannot resolve a dependency.

references.title: Autowiring - Autowiring other Methods (e.g. Setters and Public Typed Properties)
references.summary: Shows how the #[Required] attribute (from `Symfony\Contracts\Service\Attribute\Required`) marks setter methods or public typed properties so the container autowires them after instantiation. This enables setter and property injection in addition to constructor injection.

references.title: Autowiring - Generate Closures With Autowiring
references.summary: Documents the #[AutowireServiceClosure] and #[AutowireCallable] attributes which generate closures encapsulating service instantiation or a method call. With `lazy: true`, AutowireCallable defers service instantiation until the closure is first invoked.

references.title: Service Container - Binding Arguments by Name or Type
references.summary: Explains the `bind` configuration key that injects values into any service argument matching a name (`$adminEmail`), a type (`Psr\Log\LoggerInterface`), or both. Bindings under `_defaults` apply to every service defined in the same configuration file.

references.title: Service Container - The autoconfigure Option
references.summary: Describes the autoconfigure option that automatically tags services based on their class hierarchy or PHP attributes (such as #[AsCommand], #[AsEventListener] and #[AsMessageHandler]). Combined with autowiring it eliminates manual tag declarations for many built-in integrations.

references.title: Service Subscribers & Locators
references.summary: Introduces the problem service locators solve: a consumer needs access to several services without instantiating them all eagerly. Recommends locators over injecting the whole container, which would expose every service and force them to be public.

references.title: Service Subscribers & Locators - Defining a Service Subscriber
references.summary: Documents the `Symfony\Contracts\Service\ServiceSubscriberInterface` whose static `getSubscribedServices()` method declares the lazy services. The container injects an instance of `Symfony\Component\DependencyInjection\ServiceLocator` (which implements PSR-11 `ContainerInterface` and `ServiceProviderInterface`).

references.title: Service Subscribers & Locators - Including Services
references.summary: Shows how `getSubscribedServices()` can return service-type strings, key/value pairs to alias services internally, optional services using a leading `?` (e.g. `'?'.LoggerInterface::class`), and how to merge with a parent's subscribed services when extending another subscriber.

references.title: Service Subscribers & Locators - The AutowireLocator and AutowireIterator Attributes
references.summary: Documents the `#[AutowireLocator]` attribute that injects an ad-hoc service locator on a `ContainerInterface` argument. `#[AutowireIterator]` is the iterable counterpart. The attribute can declare aliased keys, optional services with `?`, and `SubscribedService` for custom autowiring.

references.title: Service Subscribers & Locators - Defining a Service Locator
references.summary: Shows the manual definition of a service locator using YAML's `!service_locator`, XML's `<argument type="service_locator">`, or PHP's `service_locator()` function. It also covers the `#[TaggedLocator('app.handler')]` attribute, which builds a locator of all services with a tag.

references.title: Service Subscribers & Locators - Reusing a Service Locator in Multiple Services
references.summary: Documents how to define a stand-alone `Symfony\Component\DependencyInjection\ServiceLocator` service and inject it in multiple consumers. When the default autoconfiguration is disabled, the service must be tagged `container.service_locator`.

references.title: Service Subscribers & Locators - Using Service Locators in Compiler Passes
references.summary: Recommends `Symfony\Component\DependencyInjection\Compiler\ServiceLocatorTagPass::register($container, $services)` for creating a service locator from a compiler pass. The helper returns an argument that can be added to a definition and reuses identical locators across services.

references.title: Security - Authenticating Users
references.summary: Overview of how the SecurityBundle authenticates users on a firewall. Lists the built-in authenticators (form_login, json_login, http_basic, login_link, access_token, x509, remote_user) and links to custom authenticators.

references.title: Security - Form Login
references.summary: Enables the FormLoginAuthenticator with login_path and check_path. The form must POST to check_path with _username and _password fields, and an optional _csrf_token when enable_csrf is true.

references.title: Security - JSON Login
references.summary: Configures the JSON login authenticator with check_path. Clients POST a JSON body with username and password keys to that path; on failure the system returns 401 Unauthorized automatically.

references.title: Security - HTTP Basic
references.summary: Enables HTTP Basic authentication with the http_basic key and a realm. Symfony asks the browser for credentials using the WWW-Authenticate header; logout is not supported because browsers cache credentials.

references.title: Security - Limiting Login Attempts
references.summary: Login throttling uses the Rate Limiter component to restrict failed login attempts (default 5 per minute per IP+username and 5 * max_attempts per IP). max_attempts and interval are configured per firewall, and a custom limiter service can be plugged in via login_throttling.limiter.

references.title: Security - Login Programmatically
references.summary: Use Symfony\Bundle\SecurityBundle\Security::login() to authenticate a user from PHP code. Optional arguments select the authenticator, target firewall and badges; the call returns a redirect Response based on the configured login behavior.

references.title: Security - Logging Out
references.summary: Activating logout under a firewall registers a Symfony-handled route at the configured path. The user is unauthenticated and redirected to the configured target; the auto-generated route name is _logout_<firewallname>.

references.title: Security - Authentication Events
references.summary: Authentication dispatches CheckPassportEvent, AuthenticationTokenCreatedEvent, AuthenticationSuccessEvent, LoginSuccessEvent and LoginFailureEvent in order. Listeners can validate the passport, modify the token or replace the response.

references.title: Security - Access Control (Authorization)
references.summary: Overview of Symfony authorization. After authentication, the user has roles and the application checks them on resources (URLs, controllers, services) using attributes such as roles or IS_AUTHENTICATED_*.

references.title: Security - Add Code to Deny Access
references.summary: Two ways to deny access: access_control rules in security.yaml or code-level checks (denyAccessUnlessGranted, isGranted, the #[IsGranted] attribute). Denying access throws AccessDeniedException; logged-out users are redirected to login, logged-in users see a 403.

references.title: Security - Allowing Unsecured Access (PUBLIC_ACCESS)
references.summary: Explains the PUBLIC_ACCESS security attribute for `access_control` rules. It can whitelist routes such as the login page for unauthenticated users while still requiring authentication on neighboring routes.

references.title: Security - Checking to see if a User is Logged In
references.summary: Special authorization attributes: IS_AUTHENTICATED_FULLY (only fully logged in), IS_AUTHENTICATED_REMEMBERED (also remember-me users), IS_AUTHENTICATED, IS_REMEMBERED (only remember-me) and IS_IMPERSONATOR. They are usable wherever roles are accepted.

references.title: Using Expressions in Security Access Controls
references.summary: isGranted() and #[IsGranted] accept an ExpressionLanguage Expression instead of a role string. Expressions can use user, role_names, object/subject, token and trust_resolver, plus is_authenticated(), is_remember_me(), is_fully_authenticated() and is_granted() helpers.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference (SecurityBundle)
references.summary: Top-level reference for the security configuration tree provided by the SecurityBundle. All options live under the security: key and the actual values can be inspected with config:dump-reference security and debug:config security.

references.title: Security Configuration - Configuration overview
references.summary: Covers the main top-level SecurityBundle configuration options. These include basic options such as access_denied_url, erase_credentials, hide_user_not_found and session_fixation_strategy, plus advanced options such as access_control, hashers, firewalls, providers and role_hierarchy.

references.title: Security Configuration - hide_user_not_found
references.summary: Boolean (default true). When true, missing users surface as the generic BadCredentialsException with the message 'Bad credentials'; when false, a UserNotFoundException is thrown that exposes the requested identifier.

references.title: Security Configuration - session_fixation_strategy
references.summary: Controls how the session is changed at authentication to mitigate session fixation. Valid values are NONE (not recommended), MIGRATE (default - rotates session id while keeping attributes) and INVALIDATE (regenerates the whole session).

references.title: Security Configuration - erase_credentials
references.summary: Boolean (default true). When true, Symfony calls eraseCredentials() on the user object after authentication so that sensitive data such as the plain password is removed from memory.

references.title: Security Configuration - access_denied_url
references.summary: Optional URL to redirect the user to after a 403 response, instead of rendering the default access denied page. A custom access denial handler overrides this option.

references.title: Security Configuration - Firewall Context
references.summary: Each firewall has a context key (defaults to its name) used to scope the security token in the session. Setting the same context value on multiple stateful firewalls lets them share authentication; stateless firewalls cannot share a context.

references.title: Security Configuration - stateless
references.summary: Boolean firewall option that disables the use of the session for authentication. Routes matched by a stateless firewall behave as stateless, which prevents cookie-based persistence between requests.

references.title: Security Configuration - delete_cookies (logout)
references.summary: Per-firewall logout option that deletes the listed cookies (with optional path and domain) when the user logs out. It is configured under firewalls.<name>.logout.delete_cookies.

references.title: Security Configuration - Required Badges
references.summary: Per-firewall list of badge classes that the authenticator's Passport must contain. If a required badge is missing on the validated passport, authentication fails.

references.title: Security - Loading the User: The User Provider
references.summary: User providers (re)load users by a user identifier. They are used both during login (loading the user from an identifier) and at the start of each non-stateless request (refreshing the user from the session). Built-in providers include entity, ldap, memory and chain.

references.title: User Providers - Entity User Provider
references.summary: Loads users from a database via Doctrine using a class and a property. The property is the user identifier; if the lookup needs more flexibility, the repository can implement UserLoaderInterface and the property option must be removed from security.yaml.

references.title: User Providers - Memory User Provider
references.summary: Stores users (identifier, hashed password, roles) directly in security.yaml. Recommended only for prototypes; passwords must already be hashed with the configured algorithm.

references.title: User Providers - Chain User Provider
references.summary: Combines two or more user providers into one. Configured providers are tried in order until one finds the user, which is useful when migrating users between sources.

references.title: User Providers - Creating a Custom User Provider
references.summary: A custom user provider implements UserProviderInterface (loadUserByIdentifier, refreshUser, supportsClass) and is wired by id under providers in security.yaml. PasswordUpgraderInterface enables automatic password rehashing on login.

references.title: Authenticating against an LDAP server
references.summary: Combines the LDAP component with the Security stack: `ldap` user provider fetches users from an LDAP directory, while `form_login_ldap` and `http_basic_ldap` authenticate against the same directory using the user-supplied password. The provider supports DN templates and search filters scoped per organisational unit.

references.title: Security - The Firewall
references.summary: A firewall defines which parts of the application are secured and how users authenticate. Firewalls are matched in order; only the first matching firewall handles a request, and a firewall without pattern matches every URL.

references.title: Security - Fetching the Firewall Configuration for a Request
references.summary: Symfony\Bundle\SecurityBundle\Security exposes getFirewallConfig(Request) to retrieve the FirewallConfig that matched a request. The Security service should be stored, not its result, because authentication may not yet be complete in service constructors.

references.title: How to Restrict Firewalls to a Request
references.summary: Firewalls choose to handle a request via a request matcher. They can be restricted by path (pattern), host, methods, or by a custom service implementing RequestMatcherInterface configured as request_matcher.

references.title: Security Configuration - Firewall Context
references.summary: Each firewall stores its security token under a context key (defaulting to its own name) in the session. Two stateful firewalls that share the same context value share authentication.

references.title: Security Configuration - stateless
references.summary: Boolean firewall option that prevents authentication from being persisted to the session. Routes matched by the firewall behave as stateless when they do not specify their own value.

references.title: How to Impersonate a User
references.summary: Enables admin user impersonation under a firewall via `switch_user: { provider: ..., role: ROLE_ALLOWED_TO_SWITCH }`. Append `?_switch_user=username` to any URL to assume the target user, `?_switch_user=_exit` to switch back. The `IS_IMPERSONATOR` and `ROLE_PREVIOUS_ADMIN` security attributes mark the impersonation state.

references.title: How to Add "Remember Me" Login Functionality
references.summary: The **`remember_me`** firewall option keeps a user authenticated across sessions through a signed cookie. Two strategies are available: the **signature-based** default (HMAC over user properties) and the **token-based** strategy that stores opaque tokens in a `RememberMeHandler` (e.g. Doctrine).

references.title: How to Create and Enable Custom User Checkers
references.summary: A **user checker** runs additional checks during authentication (`checkPreAuth()` before credentials are verified) and after a successful login (`checkPostAuth()`). Implement `UserCheckerInterface` and assign it per firewall via `user_checker: <service-id>`. Use it for things like account expiration, two-factor enforcement, or domain allowlists.

references.title: Security - The User
references.summary: Permissions are linked to a user object that implements UserInterface. The make:user maker generates a User entity with getUserIdentifier(), getRoles() and eraseCredentials(); password handling additionally requires PasswordAuthenticatedUserInterface.

references.title: Security - Understanding how Users are Refreshed from the Session
references.summary: On every request (unless the firewall is stateless), the user is deserialized from the session and refreshed via the user provider. AbstractToken compares getPassword(), getSalt() and getUserIdentifier(); any change de-authenticates the user.

references.title: Security - Comparing Users Manually with EquatableInterface
references.summary: Explains how EquatableInterface customizes user comparison after the user is refreshed from storage. When it is implemented on the user class, Symfony calls isEqualTo() instead of the default field comparison to decide whether the refreshed user matches the one stored in the session.

references.title: How to Create and Enable Custom User Checkers
references.summary: User checkers implement UserCheckerInterface (checkPreAuth and checkPostAuth) and throw subclasses of AccountStatusException to block authentication. They are wired per firewall via user_checker; tagging multiple checkers with security.user_checker.<firewall> enables a chain checker.

references.title: Security - Fetching the User Object
references.summary: AbstractController exposes getUser() which returns the User object or null. From a service, inject Symfony\Bundle\SecurityBundle\Security and call getUser(); in Twig, the current user is available via app.user.

references.title: Security - Fetching the User from a Service
references.summary: Inject Symfony\Bundle\SecurityBundle\Security and call getUser() to retrieve the authenticated user from any service. The Security service should be stored in a field rather than calling getUser() in the constructor, where authentication may not yet be complete.

references.title: Password Hashing - Configuring a Password Hasher
references.summary: The password_hashers option maps a User class (or PasswordAuthenticatedUserInterface) to an algorithm and its options. The auto algorithm picks the best available native algorithm and lets Symfony rehash on login when a stronger one becomes available.

references.title: Password Hashing - Hashing the Password
references.summary: Inject UserPasswordHasherInterface and call hashPassword($user, $plain) to obtain the hash, or isPasswordValid($user, $plain) to verify it. Both pick the algorithm based on the user class configuration in security.yaml.

references.title: Password Hashing - Configure a new Hasher Using migrate_from
references.summary: Use migrate_from to keep verifying old hashes while issuing new hashes with a stronger algorithm. New users are stored with the new hasher; on successful login, users still on a legacy hash are transparently rehashed.

references.title: Password Hashing - Dynamic (named) Password Hashers
references.summary: Define named hashers and pick one dynamically by implementing PasswordHasherAwareInterface::getPasswordHasherName() on the user class. Returning null falls back to the default hasher.

references.title: Password Hashing - Supported Algorithms
references.summary: Built-in algorithms are auto (selects the best available, currently bcrypt), bcrypt (cost 4-31, default 13), sodium (Argon2) and PBKDF2 (legacy, no longer recommended). All of them include their salt inside the resulting hash string.

references.title: Password Hashing - Creating a custom Password Hasher
references.summary: A custom hasher implements PasswordHasherInterface (hash, verify, needsRehash). hash() and verify() must reject passwords longer than 4096 characters (CheckPasswordLengthTrait helps); needsRehash() returning true triggers automatic password migration.

references.title: Security - Roles
references.summary: User roles are returned by getRoles() on the user class. Every role string must start with the ROLE_ prefix; the make:user maker also guarantees that every authenticated user has at least ROLE_USER.

references.title: Security - Hierarchical Roles
references.summary: The role_hierarchy section in security.yaml maps a parent role to one or more child roles that are granted automatically. Hierarchy is only resolved through the security service (isGranted/denyAccessUnlessGranted), not by inspecting $user->getRoles() directly.

references.title: Security - Special Authentication Attributes (IS_AUTHENTICATED_*)
references.summary: IS_AUTHENTICATED_FULLY, IS_AUTHENTICATED_REMEMBERED and IS_AUTHENTICATED behave like roles for access checks, distinguishing fully logged-in users from remember-me users. IS_REMEMBERED matches only remember-me users; IS_IMPERSONATOR matches users currently impersonating someone.

references.title: How Does the Security access_control Work?
references.summary: Each request is matched against access_control entries top-to-bottom; only the first matching rule is used to enforce access. Each entry combines matching options (path, host, ip, methods, etc.) and enforcement options (roles, allow_if, requires_channel).

references.title: access_control - Matching Options
references.summary: An access_control rule can match by path (regex), ip/ips (with netmasks), port, host (regex), methods, route, attributes (request attributes such as _route) and request_matcher service. Symfony composes them through ChainRequestMatcher.

references.title: access_control - Access Enforcement
references.summary: Once a rule matches, enforcement uses roles (deny if missing), allow_if (deny if false) and requires_channel (redirect between http/https). With the default affirmative strategy, access is granted when at least one of roles or allow_if succeeds.

references.title: access_control - Matching by IP
references.summary: The ips option supports IP addresses and subnet masks. A rule restricted by ips only matches when the client IP is in the list; otherwise the engine continues to the next rule, so IP-restricted rules are typically paired with a fallback ROLE_NO_ACCESS rule.

references.title: access_control - Securing by an Expression (allow_if)
references.summary: allow_if accepts an Expression Language expression evaluated against variables like request and user. It is implemented internally by the ExpressionVoter. With the default affirmative strategy, roles and allow_if work like an OR.

references.title: access_control - Restrict to a Port
references.summary: The port option restricts an access_control entry to a specific TCP port (e.g. 8080). It can be combined with the other matching options to differentiate between local development and production access.

references.title: access_control - Forcing a Channel (http, https)
references.summary: requires_channel: https on a matching access_control entry redirects the user from http to https (or vice versa) when the incoming channel does not match. It is the configuration-based equivalent of forcing TLS on certain URLs.

references.title: How to Customize Access Denied Responses
references.summary: Throwing `AccessDeniedException` in a controller or voter triggers Symfony's access-denied flow. The response depends on whether a user is authenticated: **anonymous** users are redirected to the firewall's `entry_point`; **authenticated** users get a `403`. Override the response with the `access_denied_handler` firewall option (a service implementing `AccessDeniedHandlerInterface`).

references.title: How to Write a Custom Authenticator
references.summary: A custom authenticator implements AuthenticatorInterface (typically by extending AbstractAuthenticator). supports() decides whether to handle the request, authenticate() returns a Passport, and onAuthenticationSuccess/Failure shape the HTTP response. Custom authenticators are wired per firewall under custom_authenticators.

references.title: Custom Authenticator - Security Passports
references.summary: A Passport carries the user (via UserBadge) plus credentials such as PasswordCredentials (verified against the configured password hasher) or CustomCredentials (verified by a closure). UserBadge identifiers are limited to 4096 characters to prevent session storage flooding.

references.title: Custom Authenticator - SelfValidatingPassport
references.summary: SelfValidatingPassport carries only a UserBadge and optional badges; it skips credential checks. It is the typical choice for token-based authenticators where the credentials are validated outside Symfony's password mechanism.

references.title: Custom Authenticator - Passport Badges
references.summary: Covers built-in passport badges such as RememberMeBadge, PasswordUpgradeBadge, CsrfTokenBadge, and PreAuthenticatedUserBadge. These badges enable remember-me, password auto-rehashing, CSRF token validation, or skipping the pre-authentication user checker.

references.title: Custom Authenticator - Passport Attributes
references.summary: setAttribute() and getAttribute() let an authenticator pass arbitrary data between authenticate() and createToken(), e.g. an OAuth scope reused by a custom token class.

references.title: How to Use Voters to Check User Permissions
references.summary: Voters centralize permission logic and are invoked on every isGranted()/denyAccessUnlessGranted() call and by access_control rules. The access decision manager combines all voter votes according to the configured strategy.

references.title: Voters - The Voter Interface
references.summary: Custom voters implement Symfony\Component\Security\Core\Authorization\Voter\VoterInterface or extend the abstract Voter, which exposes supports($attribute, $subject) and voteOnAttribute($attribute, $subject, TokenInterface $token). Implementing CacheableVoterInterface lets the access decision manager skip voters that cannot vote on a given attribute/subject.

references.title: Voters - Configuring the Voter
references.summary: A voter is registered by tagging its service with security.voter. With the default services.yaml autoconfiguration, classes implementing VoterInterface (or extending Voter) are tagged automatically.

references.title: Voters - Changing the Access Decision Strategy
references.summary: The access_decision_manager.strategy option chooses among affirmative (default - one grant is enough), consensus (majority wins, ties decided by allow_if_equal_granted_denied), unanimous (no voter may deny) and priority (first non-abstaining voter decides). When all voters abstain, allow_if_all_abstain (default false) decides.

references.title: Voters - Custom Access Decision Strategy
references.summary: Use access_decision_manager.strategy_service to plug in a class implementing AccessDecisionStrategyInterface. Use access_decision_manager.service to replace the entire AccessDecisionManagerInterface implementation.

references.title: Voters - Changing the message and status code returned
references.summary: By default #[IsGranted] denies access with HTTP 403 and the message 'Access Denied'. Passing a custom message and statusCode (e.g. 404) results in an HttpException with that status instead of the default AccessDeniedException flow.

references.title: HTTP Cache - Caching with a Gateway Cache
references.summary: Defines the gateway (reverse proxy) cache as a middleman between the client and the application that stores cacheable responses. Names common gateway caches such as Varnish, Squid in reverse proxy mode, and the built-in Symfony reverse proxy, and notes that gateway caches are also called reverse proxy caches, surrogate caches, or HTTP accelerators.

references.title: HTTP Cache - Symfony Reverse Proxy
references.summary: Describes Symfony's PHP-based reverse proxy (`HttpCache` kernel) and how to enable it via `framework.http_cache: true` in the prod environment. Explains that this is a great way to start but is not as full-featured as Varnish, and that it adds an `X-Symfony-Cache` trace header in debug mode (configurable via `trace_level` and `trace_header`).

references.title: RFC 9111: HTTP Caching - Introduction
references.summary: Normative definition of HTTP caches. Distinguishes private caches (dedicated to a single user, typically the browser cache) from shared caches (caches that serve more than one user, typically proxy/CDN/gateway caches). Defines `Cache-Control: private` and `public` and how each cache type interprets them.

references.title: HTTP caching (MDN) - Kinds of caches
references.summary: Identifies the main kinds of HTTP cache: the browser's private cache, shared proxy caches inside a network, managed shared caches such as CDNs and reverse proxies controlled by the origin, and the local cache used when offline. Explains how the same response can be cached by several layers and which directives target which layer.

references.title: HTTP Cache Expiration - Expiration with the Cache-Control Header
references.summary: Shows how to mark a response as cacheable for a specific duration using the `#[Cache(public: true, maxage: ...)]` attribute or the `setPublic()` and `setMaxAge()` methods, which produce `Cache-Control: public, max-age=N`. Notes that `setSharedMaxAge()` is not equivalent to combining `setPublic()` and `setMaxAge()` because `s-maxage` disables `stale-if-error` reuse for shared caches.

references.title: HTTP Cache Expiration - Expiration with the Expires Header
references.summary: Documents the `Expires` HTTP header as the alternative to `Cache-Control: max-age`. Explains that `setExpires()` and the `expires` option of `#[Cache]` automatically convert dates to GMT, that HTTP/1.0 was vulnerable to clock skew, and that per RFC 7234 the `Expires` value is ignored when `Cache-Control: max-age` or `s-maxage` is also present.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Managing the HTTP Cache
references.summary: Lists the cache-related `Response` API: `setPublic()`, `setPrivate()`, `setMaxAge()`, `setSharedMaxAge()`, `setExpires()`, and `setCache()`. These methods write the appropriate `Cache-Control` directives or the `Expires` header on the response.

references.title: RFC 9111: HTTP Caching - Cache-Control
references.summary: Normative definition of `Cache-Control` request and response directives. Defines `max-age` (freshness for any cache), `s-maxage` (freshness only for shared caches, overrides `max-age` and `Expires` for those caches), `public`, `private`, `no-cache`, `no-store`, `must-revalidate`, and related directives.

references.title: RFC 9111: HTTP Caching - Expires
references.summary: Normative definition of the `Expires` response header as an absolute HTTP-date after which the response is considered stale. Specifies that if both `Cache-Control: max-age` (or `s-maxage`) and `Expires` are present in a response, the `Cache-Control` directive takes precedence.

references.title: Cache-Control header (MDN)
references.summary: Reference for `Cache-Control` directives in requests and responses, including `no-store`, `no-cache`, `private`, `public`, `max-age`, `s-maxage`, `must-revalidate`, `stale-while-revalidate`, and `immutable`. Documents how directives interact and how `Cache-Control` overrides `Expires`.

references.title: HTTP Cache Validation - Validation with the ETag Header
references.summary: Explains the ETag-based validation flow: the response carries an arbitrary `ETag` (often `md5($content)`), the cache stores it, and on the next request the cache sends `If-None-Match`. Symfony's `Response::isNotModified($request)` compares `If-None-Match` with the response `ETag` and, on match, sets the status to 304 with no body. Mentions weak ETags via `setEtag($value, true)`.

references.title: HTTP Cache Validation - Validation with the Last-Modified Header
references.summary: Documents `Last-Modified` validation: the application sets the `Last-Modified` header (typically the most recent update timestamp of the resource), the cache later sends `If-Modified-Since`, and `Response::isNotModified($request)` returns the response as 304 when the dates are equivalent. Notes that responses default to private; call `setPublic()` to allow shared cache reuse.

references.title: HTTP Cache Validation - Optimizing your Code with Validation
references.summary: Recommends computing only the minimum data needed for the `ETag` or `Last-Modified` value, calling `Response::isNotModified($request)` early, and returning the 304 response without rendering the full content. When not modified, `isNotModified()` sets the status to 304, removes the body, and strips headers that must not be present on a 304 response.

references.title: ETag header (MDN)
references.summary: Defines `ETag` as an opaque validator the server attaches to a representation. Distinguishes strong ETags (byte-for-byte equivalence) from weak ETags (prefixed `W/`, indicating semantic equivalence only). Used for conditional `GET` revalidation with `If-None-Match` and for optimistic concurrency control with `If-Match`.

references.title: Last-Modified header (MDN)
references.summary: Reference for the `Last-Modified` response header carrying an HTTP-date for the most recent modification of the resource. Used together with `If-Modified-Since` and `If-Unmodified-Since` for conditional requests; less precise than `ETag` and only ever a fallback validator when an `ETag` is also present.

references.title: If-None-Match header (MDN)
references.summary: Documents the conditional request header `If-None-Match`. For `GET`/`HEAD`, the server returns `304 Not Modified` when any listed `ETag` matches the current resource; for write methods, it is used with `*` to ensure the resource does not yet exist. Pairs with `ETag` validation.

references.title: If-Modified-Since header (MDN)
references.summary: Reference for the `If-Modified-Since` request header. The server returns `304 Not Modified` when the resource has not changed since the given HTTP-date; for `GET`/`HEAD` only. Ignored when `If-None-Match` is also present, in which case ETag validation is used.

references.title: HTTP Cache - HTTP Caching and User Sessions
references.summary: Explains that whenever a session is started during a request, Symfony turns the response into a private, non-cacheable response so that user-specific data is not exposed to other visitors. Describes the internal `AbstractSessionListener::NO_AUTO_CACHE_CONTROL_HEADER` opt-out flag for cases where a session-using response should remain cacheable.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Managing the HTTP Cache
references.summary: Describes how `Response::setPrivate()` marks a response so that only a private (browser) cache may store it, while `Response::setPublic()` allows shared caches to store it. Also documents the related `setMaxAge()`, `setExpires()`, and `setCache()` helpers used to set client-facing freshness.

references.title: RFC 9111: HTTP Caching - private
references.summary: Normative definition of `Cache-Control: private`: the response is intended for a single user and **must not** be stored by a shared cache. Private caches (typically the user agent's cache) may still store the response. Optionally takes a list of header field names to be omitted from a shared cache's storage when present.

references.title: RFC 9111: HTTP Caching - Storing Responses in Caches
references.summary: Lists the conditions a cache must check before storing a response: the request method must be cacheable, the response status code must be cacheable, the `Cache-Control` directives must allow storage (`no-store` forbids it), and shared caches must additionally honor `private`. Describes how stored responses become candidates for reuse.

references.title: HTTP caching (MDN) - Private caches
references.summary: Describes the browser's private cache, including how `Cache-Control: private` confines a response to that cache, how authenticated and personalized responses must avoid `public` caching, and how `no-cache` forces revalidation while still allowing storage. Explains why mixing `Authorization` headers with shared caches is dangerous without explicit opt-in.

references.title: Cache-Control header (MDN) - no-cache vs no-store
references.summary: Reference for the difference between `no-cache` (response may be stored but must be revalidated before reuse) and `no-store` (response must not be stored at all). Also covers `private`, `public`, `must-revalidate`, and `max-age` directives that primarily target browser-side behavior.

references.title: HTTP Cache - Symfony Reverse Proxy
references.summary: Documents enabling Symfony's PHP-based reverse proxy via `framework.http_cache: true` in the prod environment, which makes the kernel act as a gateway cache. Notes the `X-Symfony-Cache` trace header in debug mode and the `trace_level` (`none`, `short`, `full`) and `trace_header` configuration options.

references.title: HTTP Cache - Safe Methods: Only caching GET or HEAD requests
references.summary: Explains that HTTP caching is intended for safe methods such as `GET` and `HEAD`. Warns not to cache mutating methods such as `PUT` or `DELETE`, notes that `POST` caching requires explicit freshness information but is not widely implemented, and reinforces that `GET` and `HEAD` handlers should not change application state.

references.title: framework configuration reference - http_cache
references.summary: Reference for the `framework.http_cache` configuration tree: `enabled`, `debug`, `default_ttl`, `private_headers`, `skip_response_headers`, `allow_reload`, `allow_revalidate`, `stale_while_revalidate`, `stale_if_error`, `terminate_on_cache_hit`, `trace_level`, and `trace_header`. Used to fine-tune the built-in reverse proxy without code changes.

references.title: Using Varnish - Make Symfony Trust the Reverse Proxy
references.summary: Describes why Symfony must be configured with `Request::setTrustedProxies()` when sitting behind Varnish so that `X-Forwarded-For`, `X-Forwarded-Proto`, and `X-Forwarded-Port` are honored. Without it, all requests look like plain HTTP coming from the Varnish host, breaking URL generation and IP-based logic.

references.title: Using Varnish - Cookies and Caching
references.summary: Explains why most caching proxies refuse to cache responses for requests that include cookies or `Authorization`, since those typically vary the response. Recommends starting the session only when necessary, clearing it when no longer needed, and stripping non-essential cookies in VCL so that pages without an active session can be cached.

references.title: Cache Invalidation
references.summary: Covers manual invalidation of gateway caches via the `PURGE` HTTP method, including how to override `HttpCache::invalidate()` to handle `PURGE` and how to register the resulting kernel as a decorator of the `http_cache` service. Explains the difference between purging (drops a resource and all `Vary` variants), refreshing, banning by regex, and cache tagging.

references.title: Varying the Response for HTTP Cache
references.summary: Explains how the `Vary` response header extends the cache key beyond the URI by including selected request headers such as `Accept-Encoding` or `User-Agent`. Shows how to declare variants with the `#[Cache(vary: ...)]` attribute or `Response::setVary()` so caches store separate representations for the same URI.

references.title: Symfony source - HttpCache kernel
references.summary: Source of the `HttpCache` reverse-proxy kernel that wraps another `HttpKernelInterface`. Implements the RFC 9111 logic for lookup, validation, expiration, and storage on top of an `HttpCache\StoreInterface` (default: `Store` writing to `var/cache/<env>/http_cache/`).

references.title: Working with Edge Side Includes
references.summary: Introduces ESI (Akamai, 2001) as a way for a gateway cache to compose pages from fragments, each with its own caching strategy. Explains that Symfony only implements the `<esi:include src="..."/>` tag, which the gateway cache resolves by fetching each referenced URL independently and merging the result into the main response.

references.title: Working with Edge Side Includes - Using ESI in Symfony
references.summary: Shows how to enable ESI in `framework.esi: true` and use the Twig `render_esi()` function (and its `controller()` / `url()` arguments) to embed cacheable fragments. Documents the helper options `alt` (fallback `src`), `ignore_errors` (adds `onerror="continue"`), and `absolute_uri`, and explains that Symfony only emits an ESI tag when the gateway cache advertises `Surrogate-Capability: ESI/1.0`; otherwise the fragment is rendered inline.

references.title: framework configuration reference - esi
references.summary: Reference for the `framework.esi` configuration tree. Setting `framework.esi.enabled: true` enables the ESI fragment renderer service so that `render_esi()` works and the kernel emits the `Surrogate-Control` header that gateway caches use to detect ESI content.

references.title: framework configuration reference - fragments
references.summary: Reference for `framework.fragments`. Configures the path (default `/_fragment`) handled by `FragmentListener`, which is required to serve ESI controller references as standalone HTTP requests. The listener only accepts requests that carry Symfony's signed `_hash` parameter generated by the fragment renderer.

references.title: Using Varnish - Enable Edge Side Includes (ESI)
references.summary: Shows the VCL needed to make Varnish resolve Symfony's ESI tags: advertise `Surrogate-Capability = "abc=ESI/1.0"` in `vcl_recv`, then in `vcl_backend_response` detect the `Surrogate-Control: ESI/1.0` header Symfony adds and set `beresp.do_esi = true`. Notes that Varnish only supports the `src` attribute on ESI tags; `alt` and `onerror` are ignored.

references.title: Symfony source - Esi surrogate
references.summary: Source of Symfony's `Esi` surrogate used by `HttpCache`. It detects ESI capability through `Surrogate-Capability`, sets the `Surrogate-Control: content="ESI/1.0"` response header, and processes `<esi:include>` tags when the gateway is the built-in PHP reverse proxy.

references.title: Using Console Commands, Shortcuts and Built-in Commands - Built-in Commands
references.summary: Explains the Console component's built-in `list` and `help` commands in a standalone console application. This is relevant for recognizing the default command behavior and how command help is displayed.

references.title: Console Commands - Running Commands
references.summary: Shows how Symfony applications expose commands through `bin/console`, including the default `list` command and examples of framework commands such as `cache:clear`. This section is relevant for exam questions about discovering and invoking built-in application commands.

references.title: Console Commands - Console Completion
references.summary: Explains Symfony's shell completion support for Bash, Zsh, and Fish. It covers the built-in `completion` command, command and option completion, and value completion support.

references.title: Console Commands - Creating a Command
references.summary: Describes creating a Symfony command class by extending `Command` and implementing `execute()`. It covers the command name, the `InputInterface` and `OutputInterface` arguments, and the integer exit status constants.

references.title: Console Commands - Command Lifecycle
references.summary: Explains the optional `initialize()` and `interact()` lifecycle methods and the required `execute()` method. This section is relevant for understanding command execution order and when interactive prompting can occur.

references.title: Prevent Running the Same Console Command Multiple Times
references.summary: Describes using `LockableTrait` to prevent the same command from running multiple times on the same server. It covers the `lock()` and `release()` methods, waiting for lock release, and automatic lock release after command execution.

references.title: How to Call Other Commands
references.summary: Shows how a **command can run another command** from inside its own `execute()` (or `__invoke()`) — useful for "meta" commands that orchestrate a series of build/cache/migration commands. The recipe uses `$this->getApplication()->find()->run()` with an `ArrayInput`; output can be muted with a `NullOutput`.

references.title: How to Call a Command from a Controller
references.summary: Shows how to invoke a console command from an HTTP **controller** — by autowiring `KernelInterface`, building an `Application($kernel)`, calling `setAutoExit(false)`, and passing an `ArrayInput`. The captured output (e.g. via `BufferedOutput`) can then be inspected or returned in the response.

references.title: How to Make Commands Lazily Loaded
references.summary: In the framework setup, commands are **lazy by default** — registered through the `console.command` tag, they are instantiated only when invoked. The article also documents how to enable lazy loading manually by registering a `CommandLoaderInterface` (e.g. `ContainerCommandLoader`) on a stand-alone `Application`.

references.title: Console Commands - Configuring the Command
references.summary: Shows how `configure()` defines command descriptions, help text, and input arguments or options. It also explains constructor ordering when configuration depends on properties set by the constructor.

references.title: Console Commands - Registering the Command
references.summary: Explains registering commands with the `AsCommand` attribute and configuring command name, description, visibility, and aliases. It also covers the `console.command` service tag fallback when PHP attributes are not used.

references.title: How to Define Commands as Services
references.summary: Describes Symfony's recommended command-as-service setup with default service configuration and autowiring. This is relevant for configuring commands that depend on application services.

references.title: How to Define Commands as Services - Lazy Loading
references.summary: Explains how command lazy loading is enabled with the `AsCommand` attribute or the `command` attribute on the `console.command` tag. It also notes how lazy commands avoid instantiation until the command is actually called.

references.title: How to Hide Console Commands
references.summary: Explains how to configure commands that remain callable but are hidden from command listings. It covers the `hidden` property of the `AsCommand` attribute and notes that hidden commands remain available through JSON or XML descriptors.

references.title: Console Input (Arguments & Options) - Using Command Arguments
references.summary: Explains command arguments, including required, optional, and array arguments. It highlights that arguments are ordered and that an array argument must appear at the end of the argument list.

references.title: Console Input (Arguments & Options) - Using Command Options
references.summary: Describes command options, their unordered nature, shortcuts, value modes, and common syntax forms. It covers boolean flags, required values, optional values, negatable options, and array-valued options.

references.title: Console Input (Arguments & Options) - Options with optional arguments
references.summary: Explains how optional option values can distinguish between an option not being passed, being passed without a value, or being passed with a specific value. This is relevant for exam questions about `InputOption::VALUE_OPTIONAL` defaults.

references.title: Understanding how Console Arguments and Options Are Handled
references.summary: Covers Console's docopt-style parsing edge cases for short and long options, optional option values, and the `--` separator. This section is relevant for understanding ambiguous CLI input and how Symfony assigns values to options and arguments.

references.title: Console Input (Arguments & Options) - Adding Argument/Option Value Completion
references.summary: Shows how commands can provide shell completion suggestions for argument and option values. It covers passing a completion callback to `addArgument()` or `addOption()` and using `CompletionInput` to inspect the current value.

references.title: Console Commands - Console Output
references.summary: Shows how commands use the `OutputInterface` object to write messages with `writeln()` and `write()`. It explains the newline behavior of these output methods.

references.title: Console Commands - Output Sections
references.summary: Describes output sections created from `ConsoleOutputInterface` and manipulated through `ConsoleSectionOutput`. This is relevant for advanced console output that can be cleared or overwritten independently.

references.title: Console Commands - Console Input
references.summary: Shows how commands configure arguments and retrieve their values from the `InputInterface` object with `getArgument()`. This section grounds questions about reading command input inside `execute()`.

references.title: How to Style a Console Command - Basic Usage
references.summary: Explains creating a `SymfonyStyle` instance from the command's input and output objects. This is relevant for semantic console input/output helpers such as `title()`.

references.title: How to Style a Console Command - Helper Methods
references.summary: Describes `SymfonyStyle` helper methods for command titles, sections, text, lists, tables, definition lists, and blank lines. This is relevant for choosing semantic output methods rather than writing raw formatted text.

references.title: How to Color and Style the Console Output - Using Color Styles
references.summary: Explains how output formatter tags apply colors and styles to console text. It covers built-in tags such as `info`, custom `OutputFormatterStyle` definitions, hex colors, named colors, and style options.

references.title: The Console Helpers
references.summary: Introduces the helper system provided by the Console component for common command tasks. It points to helper classes used for formatting, processes, progress output, questions, tables, debugging, and cursor control.

references.title: Question Helper
references.summary: Explains `QuestionHelper`, which asks users for interactive input through `ask()`. It covers retrieving the helper from a command and using `Question`, `ConfirmationQuestion`, and `ChoiceQuestion` objects.

references.title: Progress Bar
references.summary: Describes `ProgressBar`, including starting, advancing, setting progress, and finishing a progress display. It also explains how progress bars can work with known or unknown maximum steps.

references.title: Table
references.summary: Shows how the `Table` helper renders tabular console output by setting headers and rows before rendering. It also covers table separators, titles, and column width controls.

references.title: Process Helper
references.summary: Explains how `ProcessHelper` displays process execution details and can run a command line string, an argument array, or a `Process` instance. It also covers custom failure messages and callbacks.

references.title: Formatter Helper
references.summary: Describes `FormatterHelper` methods for formatted sections, blocks, truncation, time, and memory output. It is relevant for recognizing when to use helper-generated formatted strings with console output.

references.title: Progress Indicator
references.summary: Explains `ProgressIndicator`, which is intended for commands with indeterminate duration or unquantifiable work. It covers starting, advancing, finishing, built-in formats, custom indicators, and placeholder customization.

references.title: Cursor Helper
references.summary: Describes the `Cursor` helper for moving the cursor, writing at specific terminal positions, and clearing output. It covers movement methods, `moveToPosition()`, current position access, and screen or line clearing.

references.title: Debug Formatter Helper
references.summary: Explains `DebugFormatterHelper` for formatting debug information from external programs such as processes or HTTP requests. It covers retrieving the helper, using program identifiers, and formatting start, progress, and stop messages.

references.title: Using Events
references.summary: Introduces Console application lifecycle events through the EventDispatcher component. It explains how to attach a dispatcher to an application and notes that only the main executed command triggers events.

references.title: Using Events - The `ConsoleEvents::COMMAND` Event
references.summary: Describes the event dispatched just before a command executes and the `ConsoleCommandEvent` object received by listeners. It also explains disabling a command from a listener and the disabled return code.

references.title: Using Events - The `ConsoleEvents::ERROR` Event
references.summary: Explains the event dispatched when an exception is thrown by a command or listener. It covers inspecting the output, command, current exit code, and replacing the error.

references.title: Using Events - The `ConsoleEvents::TERMINATE` Event
references.summary: Describes the event dispatched after command execution for cleanup and exit-code changes. It also notes that it is dispatched after `ConsoleEvents::ERROR` when an exception occurs.

references.title: Using Events - The `ConsoleEvents::SIGNAL` Event
references.summary: Explains handling interrupted command execution through `ConsoleSignalEvent` and signal listeners. It also covers `SignalableCommandInterface`, subscribed signals, and continuing execution by returning `false` or aborting exit.

references.title: Verbosity Levels
references.summary: Defines quiet, normal, verbose, very verbose, and debug output levels and their `-q`, `-v`, `-vv`, and `-vvv` switches. It also maps `SHELL_VERBOSITY` values to `OutputInterface` verbosity constants.

references.title: Console Input (Arguments & Options) - Command Global Options
