references.summary: Lists predefined global Console options available to commands, including `--verbose`, `--quiet`, and `--no-interaction`. It also notes FrameworkBundle-specific global options such as `--env` and `--no-debug`.

references.title: Testing - The PHPUnit Testing Framework
references.summary: How Symfony integrates with PHPUnit through the Symfony test pack and the generated testing configuration. This section is relevant for exam questions about installing test dependencies, running `bin/phpunit`, and the conventional test directory.

references.title: Testing - Unit Tests
references.summary: How unit tests fit into Symfony applications while remaining standard PHPUnit tests. This section explains where unit tests are conventionally placed and how `bin/phpunit` can run all tests, a directory, or a single test class.

references.title: Testing - Application Tests
references.summary: How Symfony application tests cover the complete application by making requests, interacting with pages, and checking responses. This section is relevant for exam questions about the functional-test workflow and how application tests differ from unit and integration tests.

references.title: Testing - Write Your First Application Test
references.summary: How controller-level application tests typically extend `WebTestCase`, create a test client, request a page, and assert the response. This section grounds questions about first functional-test structure and common response/content assertions.

references.title: How to Simulate HTTP Authentication in a Functional Test
references.summary: Symfony >= 5.1 exposes `WebTestCase::createClient()->loginUser($user)` to log in **without** going through the form login flow. The method writes a session token directly. For HTTP Basic firewalls use `client->setServerParameter('PHP_AUTH_USER', ...)` / `PHP_AUTH_PW` instead.

references.title: Testing - Making Requests
references.summary: How the Symfony test client simulates an HTTP client and uses `request()` to make application requests. This section details the request signature, the returned `Crawler`, and the availability of the `test.client` service.

references.title: Testing - Client State Across Requests
references.summary: How the test client isolates subsequent requests by rebooting the kernel and recreating the container. This section is relevant for questions about state reset, `disableReboot()`, and services tagged with `kernel.reset`.

references.title: The BrowserKit Component - History
references.summary: How the Symfony test client models browser navigation with methods such as `back()`, `forward()`, `reload()`, and `restart()`. This section grounds questions about client history, cookies, and redirect-skipping browser behavior.

references.title: How to Test the Interaction of several Clients
references.summary: When a functional test simulates **multiple clients** (e.g. testing a chat between two users), each client lives in **its own PHP process** by default to keep state isolated. Symfony exposes `KernelBrowser::insulate()` to opt in or out per client — useful when sharing fixtures across clients in the same request lifecycle.

references.title: The DOM Crawler - The DOM Crawler
references.summary: How a `Crawler` instance returned by the test client traverses HTML or XML documents. This section is relevant for questions about selecting nodes, finding links and forms, and reading node attributes or contents.

references.title: The DOM Crawler - Traversing
references.summary: How the crawler narrows node selections with CSS selectors, XPath expressions, position selectors, and traversal methods. This section supports exam questions about chaining crawler methods and the fact that traversal returns new `Crawler` instances.

references.title: The DOM Crawler - Extracting Information
references.summary: How to read data from crawler nodes using methods such as `attr()`, `text()`, `extract()`, and `each()`. This section is useful for questions about extracting node text, attributes, and arrays of values from matched elements.

references.title: Testing - Interacting with the Response
references.summary: How the test client and crawler interact with returned pages by clicking links and submitting forms. This section is relevant for questions about `clickLink()`, `selectLink()`, `submitForm()`, and `selectButton()`.

references.title: How to Use the Profiler in a Functional Test - Enabling the Profiler in Tests
references.summary: How Symfony disables profiler data collection by default in tests and how to enable it globally or per request. This section grounds questions about the `framework.profiler` test configuration and `$client->enableProfiler()`.

references.title: How to Use the Profiler in a Functional Test - Testing the Profiler Information
references.summary: How profiler data can be retrieved from the test client after a request and inspected through collectors. This section is relevant for questions about `$client->getProfile()`, collector access, and using profiler tokens in failed assertions.

references.title: Testing - Retrieving Services in the Test
references.summary: How tests that boot the Symfony kernel access the test service container through `static::getContainer()`. This section is relevant for exam questions about retrieving services, the special test container, and access to public and non-removed private services.

references.title: Testing - Integration Tests
references.summary: How Symfony integration tests use `KernelTestCase` to boot the kernel and work with the service container. This section is relevant for questions about `bootKernel()`, per-test kernel rebooting, and the `KERNEL_CLASS` environment variable.

references.title: Testing - Set-up your Test Environment
references.summary: How Symfony tests create a kernel in the `test` environment and can override kernel options. This section is relevant for exam questions about `bootKernel()` options such as `environment` and `debug`, and test-specific configuration under `config/packages/test/`.

references.title: The BrowserKit Component - Custom Header Handling
references.summary: How to provide default server parameters to `createClient()` and per-request headers to `request()`. This section grounds questions about FastCGI-style header names such as `HTTP_X_SESSION_TOKEN`.

references.title: Testing - Reporting Exceptions
references.summary: How the Symfony test client handles exceptions during application tests and how to disable exception catching. This section is relevant for questions about surfacing application exceptions directly in PHPUnit failures.

references.title: How to Customize the Bootstrap Process before Running Tests
references.summary: PHPUnit's `bootstrap.php` (default `tests/bootstrap.php`) sets up the Symfony environment before any test runs — it includes Composer's autoloader and may reset the test database. The `bootstrap` attribute of `phpunit.xml.dist` chooses the file. Custom bootstrap is the place to install fixtures, clear `var/cache/test`, or pre-warm caches.

references.title: Testing - Accessing Internal Objects
references.summary: How the Symfony test client exposes history, cookies, and the latest request, response, internal BrowserKit request and response, and crawler. This section is relevant for questions about distinguishing HttpKernel objects from BrowserKit internal objects.

references.title: Testing - Testing the Response (Assertions)
references.summary: How Symfony provides shortcut assertions for common response, request, browser, and crawler checks in application tests. This section supports exam questions about response status assertions, headers, cookies, routes, and selector assertions.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge - The PHPUnit Bridge
references.summary: The main PHPUnit Bridge capabilities, including deprecation reporting, consistent test locale, native-function mocks, and compatibility helpers. This section is relevant for questions about the bridge's role in Symfony-oriented test suites.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge - Usage
references.summary: How the bridge creates `vendor/bin/simple-phpunit` and wraps the original PHPUnit binary. This section grounds questions about running tests through the modified script and receiving bridge deprecation reports.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge - Running Tests in Parallel
references.summary: How `simple-phpunit` can run suites in parallel when a directory contains multiple `phpunit.xml.dist` files. This section is relevant for exam questions about suite discovery depth and `SYMFONY_PHPUNIT_MAX_DEPTH`.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge - Mark Tests as Legacy
references.summary: How tests that intentionally exercise deprecated behavior can be marked as legacy. This section is relevant for questions about `@group legacy`, legacy class names, legacy test method names, and legacy data provider prefixes.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge - Making Tests Fail
references.summary: How the PHPUnit Bridge decides when deprecation notices should fail a test suite and how `SYMFONY_DEPRECATIONS_HELPER` thresholds can be configured. This section grounds questions about total, self, direct, and indirect deprecation limits.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge - Baseline Deprecations
references.summary: How to create and use a deprecation baseline so known deprecations are ignored while new ones still get reported. This section is relevant for questions about `generateBaseline`, `baselineFile`, and keeping legacy deprecation debt controlled.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge - Write Assertions about Deprecations
references.summary: How to assert expected deprecation notices in tests with `ExpectDeprecationTrait` and `expectDeprecation()`. This section supports questions about testing code that deliberately triggers deprecations and matching expected messages.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge - Trigger Deprecation Notices
references.summary: How code can trigger deprecation notices through the `trigger_deprecation()` function from `symfony/deprecation-contracts`. This section is useful for questions about package/version arguments and printf-style deprecation messages.

references.title: Configuring Symfony - Configuration Files
references.summary: Explains the standard `config/` directory layout used by Symfony applications, including the roles of `routes.yaml`, `services.yaml`, `bundles.php`, and `config/packages/`. This is relevant for exam questions about where application, bundle, routing, and service configuration belongs.

references.title: Configuring Symfony - Configuring Environment Variables in .env Files
references.summary: Describes how Symfony loads `.env` files into `$_ENV` and `$_SERVER`, how local and environment-specific overrides are ordered, and why real environment variables take precedence. This supports questions about DotEnv behavior, local overrides, production handling, and environment-variable precedence.

references.title: The ExpressionLanguage Component - Usage
references.summary: Shows how the ExpressionLanguage component evaluates and compiles expressions with the `ExpressionLanguage` class. This is relevant for exam questions about dynamic expressions, evaluation, compilation, and explicit variable passing.

references.title: Environment Variable Processors - Built-In Environment Variable Processors
references.summary: Catalogs Symfony's built-in environment variable processors, including type casts, file loading, JSON decoding, parameter resolution, key extraction, and processor chaining. This supports questions about how `%env(...)%` values are transformed before they are injected into configuration.

references.title: How to Keep Sensitive Information Secret - Create or Update Secrets
references.summary: Explains how Symfony stores sensitive values with the secrets vault and how `secrets:set` can read values interactively, from files, from standard input, or generate random values. This is relevant for questions about secret creation, environment-specific secrets, and referencing secret names from configuration.

references.title: Debug Configuration Reference (DebugBundle)
references.summary: Reference for the **DebugBundle** configuration tree under `debug:` — wraps the `VarDumper` component and exposes options like `max_items`, `min_depth`, `max_string_length`, `dump_destination` (e.g. `tcp://localhost:9912` for the dump server), and `theme` (`dark` / `light`). The bundle is enabled in dev/test by default.

references.title: How to Customize Error Pages
references.summary: Explains Symfony's treatment of errors as exceptions and contrasts detailed development exception pages with generic production error pages. It also covers template overrides, non-HTML error output, custom error controllers, and the `kernel.exception` event.

references.title: How to Customize Error Pages - Working with the kernel.exception Event
references.summary: Explains how `HttpKernel` dispatches the `kernel.exception` event after catching an exception. This is relevant for questions about converting exceptions into responses, redirecting after failures, and centralizing exception handling logic.

references.title: The VarDumper Component - The dump() Function
references.summary: Describes the `dump()` debugging helper provided by VarDumper, including its richer output formats and behavior across CLI and web SAPIs. This is relevant for questions about inspecting runtime values without relying on PHP's native `var_dump()`.

references.title: The VarDumper Component - The Dump Server
references.summary: Explains how the dump server collects dump output outside normal HTML or console output. This supports questions about `server:dump`, `VAR_DUMPER_FORMAT=server`, and `debug.dump_destination` configuration.

references.title: The Web Debug Toolbar: Debugging Dream
references.summary: Introduces the **Web Debug Toolbar**, the black bar appended at the bottom of every page in dev/test that summarises the request, performance, query count, security state, cache hits, deprecations and more. Each icon links into the full Profiler. The toolbar is shipped via the `symfony/profiler-pack` Symfony pack and only renders when the response contains a `</body>` closing tag.

references.title: Profiler: Debugging Paradise
references.summary: Tutorial section showing how installing the **Profiler pack** (`composer require profiler --dev`) adds the full Symfony **Profiler** plus its Web Debug Toolbar. The recipe wires the bundle, ships its config, and registers the dev-only routes. Clicking the toolbar token opens the per-request Profiler with timeline, queries, security context, mailer, messenger, and more.

references.title: How to Deploy a Symfony Application - Symfony Deployment Basics
references.summary: Summarizes the common deployment tasks for Symfony applications, such as uploading code, installing vendors, running migrations, and clearing or warming the cache. This supports exam questions about production deployment workflow and typical post-deploy commands.

references.title: Setting up or Fixing File Permissions - Permissions Required by Symfony Applications
references.summary: Lists the writable directories Symfony requires at runtime and during cache operations. This supports deployment questions about the different write needs of the terminal user, web server user, cache directory, log directory, and filesystem-based cache.

references.title: How to Configure Symfony to Work behind a Load Balancer or a Reverse Proxy - Solution: setTrustedProxies()
references.summary: Explains how Symfony should be configured to trust reverse proxies and forwarded headers. This is relevant for exam questions about `trusted_proxies`, `trusted_headers`, `TRUSTED_PROXIES`, and correct client/request metadata behind load balancers.

references.title: Performance
references.summary: Production checklist for performance — enable the **OPcache** with `validate_timestamps=0` and `preload`, optimize Composer (`composer dump-autoload --classmap-authoritative`), use a **reverse proxy** for HTTP caching, warm the cache (`bin/console cache:warmup --env=prod`), trust proxies via `framework.trusted_proxies`/`trusted_headers`, and avoid `kernel.debug=true` in production.

references.title: Cache - Configuring Cache with FrameworkBundle
references.summary: Explains Symfony cache pools, adapters, providers, and the default `cache.app` and `cache.system` pools. This is relevant for exam questions about application cache configuration and the different roles of cache pools.

references.title: The Process Component - Usage
references.summary: Shows how the Process component executes subprocesses safely, captures standard and error output, and distinguishes array commands from shell command lines. This supports questions about command execution, portability, and process output handling.

references.title: The Serializer Component
references.summary: Introduces the Serializer component's workflow, including normalizers, encoders, serialization, and deserialization. This is relevant for questions about converting objects to arrays and arrays to specific exchange formats.

references.title: The Cache Component - Cache Contracts
references.summary: Explains Symfony Cache Contracts as a callback-oriented cache API centered on `CacheInterface::get()` and `delete()`. This supports questions about recomputation callbacks, cache keys, item expiration, and why there is no explicit `set()` method in the contracts API.

references.title: The Process Component - Process Timeout
references.summary: Documents default process timeouts, custom timeout configuration with `setTimeout()`, timeout exceptions, and timeout checks for long-running asynchronous processes. This is relevant for questions about preventing runaway subprocesses and handling long-running commands.

references.title: The Serializer Component - Attributes Groups
references.summary: Explains how serializer groups select subsets of object attributes during normalization and denormalization. This supports questions about `#[Groups]`, context configuration, selecting multiple groups, and using `*` to include all groups.

references.title: How to Use the Serializer
references.summary: The **Serializer** wires together encoders (JSON, XML, YAML, CSV) and normalizers (`ObjectNormalizer`, `GetSetMethodNormalizer`, `DateTimeNormalizer`, `ArrayDenormalizer`, `BackedEnumNormalizer`, …). `serialize()` walks the data through normalizers then encoders; `deserialize()` does the reverse. **Serialization groups** (via `#[Groups]` and the `groups` context) limit which properties are read or written.

references.title: Messenger: Sync & Queued Message Handling - Creating a Message & Handler
references.summary: Explains the message and handler classes at the center of Symfony Messenger, including the `#[AsMessageHandler]` attribute and `__invoke()` method pattern. This is relevant for questions about handler discovery and message dispatching.

references.title: Messenger: Sync & Queued Message Handling - Transports: Async/Queued Messages
references.summary: Describes Messenger transports, DSN configuration, and routing messages to asynchronous queues. This supports exam questions about when messages are handled immediately and how they are sent to workers.

references.title: Messenger: Sync & Queued Message Handling - Routing Messages to a Transport
references.summary: Explains how Messenger routing sends specific message classes, parent classes, interfaces, or wildcard matches to one or more transports. This is relevant for questions about asynchronous routing rules and when handlers are or are not called immediately.

references.title: Messenger: Sync & Queued Message Handling - Retries & Failures
references.summary: Describes Messenger retry behavior, retry strategy configuration, and how failed messages can be moved to a failure transport after retries are exhausted. This supports questions about `max_retries`, failed queues, and operational recovery commands.

references.title: The Messenger Component
references.summary: Component-level overview of **Messenger** (already documented under "Components" in 7.0/8.0). Provides a `MessageBusInterface` whose middlewares carry messages between the application and pluggable transports (in-memory, AMQP, Doctrine, Redis, Beanstalkd, etc.). Enables async / sync command/event/query buses, retries, and failure transports.

references.title: How to Create Your own Messenger Transport
references.summary: Custom transports register a **`TransportFactoryInterface::createTransport()`** that turns a DSN into a `TransportInterface` instance. The transport itself implements `SenderInterface::send()` and `ReceiverInterface::get()`/`ack()`/`reject()` and may also implement `MessageCountAwareInterface`, `ListableReceiverInterface`, etc., for richer integration.

references.title: Sending Emails with Mailer - Transport Setup
references.summary: Explains how Symfony Mailer delivers emails through transports configured with DSNs such as `MAILER_DSN`. This is relevant for questions about SMTP, sendmail, third-party providers, and mailer configuration.

references.title: The Mime Component - Usage
references.summary: Shows how the Mime component builds email messages using the high-level `Email` API and the lower-level `Message` API. This supports questions about composing message headers, bodies, recipients, priorities, and MIME structures.

references.title: Sending Emails with Mailer - Creating & Sending Messages
references.summary: Shows how application code creates `Email` messages and sends them through `MailerInterface`. This is relevant for questions about sender and recipient APIs, message bodies, `MailerInterface::send()`, and the relationship between Mailer and Mime.

references.title: Sending Emails with Mailer - Sending Messages Async
references.summary: Explains how Symfony Mailer integrates with Messenger to send `SendEmailMessage` instances through an async transport. This supports questions about routing mailer messages, serializability requirements, and when to render email bodies before dispatch.

references.title: The Filesystem Component - Filesystem Utilities
references.summary: Documents platform-independent filesystem operations provided by `Filesystem`, such as creating directories, checking existence, copying, touching, changing permissions, and removing files. This is relevant for questions about file and directory manipulation APIs.

references.title: The Finder Component - Searching for Files and Directories
references.summary: Explains Finder's fluent search criteria for locations, file or directory filtering, symbolic links, VCS files, names, sizes, and dates. This supports questions about composing file searches and understanding Finder state.

references.title: The Filesystem Component - dumpFile
references.summary: Documents `Filesystem::dumpFile()` for writing string content to a file atomically by first writing a temporary file and then moving it into place. This is relevant for questions about reliable file writes and avoiding partial files.

references.title: Dealing with Concurrency with Locks - Locking a Resource
references.summary: Explains how Symfony locks protect critical sections from concurrent access using `LockFactory` and lock instances. This is relevant for exam questions about race-condition prevention, lock acquisition, lock release, and separate lock instances.

references.title: Dealing with Concurrency with Locks - Locking a Dynamic Resource
references.summary: Shows how lock keys can include runtime data to protect a dynamically selected resource. This supports questions about creating separate locks for different resource identifiers, such as a generated file version.

concepts.concept: Web Profiler, Web Debug Toolbar and Data collectors

references.title: Profiler
references.summary: Introduces the Symfony Profiler and web debug toolbar as development tools for inspecting request execution. It emphasizes that the profiler must not be enabled in production because it exposes sensitive diagnostic information.

references.title: Profiler - Data Collectors
references.summary: Explains that profiler information is gathered by services tagged as data collectors and shown in the toolbar and profiler interface. This supports questions about discovering enabled collectors and creating custom collectors.

references.title: Profiler - Enabling the Profiler Conditionally
references.summary: Explains how the profiler can be enabled or disabled programmatically based on request conditions. This is relevant for questions about injecting `Profiler`, using `enable()` and `disable()`, and limiting profiler collection to selected requests.

references.title: Profiler - Creating a Data Collector
references.summary: Documents how custom profiler data collectors implement or extend Symfony data collector APIs. This supports questions about `AbstractDataCollector`, `collect()`, `reset()`, collector templates, and registering custom collectors.

references.title: Profiler Configuration Reference (WebProfilerBundle)
references.summary: Reference for the WebProfilerBundle configuration tree under `web_profiler:` — `toolbar` (enable the debug toolbar), `intercept_redirects` (capture intermediate redirect responses), and the dev-only nature of the bundle. The toolbar appears at the bottom of every HTML response; clicking the token opens the full profiler.

references.title: Translations
references.summary: Introduces Symfony internationalization, translation domains, translation resources, and locale handling. This is relevant for questions about `TranslatorInterface`, message translation, domains, and request locale selection.

references.title: The Intl Component - Accessing ICU Data
references.summary: Documents Symfony Intl access to ICU localization data such as language names, country names, locales, currencies, time zones, and emoji transliteration. This supports questions about locale-aware names, code conversion, and validation helpers.

references.title: Translations - Fallback Translation Locales
references.summary: Explains Symfony's fallback locale behavior when a translation is missing for the current locale. This is relevant for questions about locale fallbacks, parent locale lookup, and the `framework.translator.fallbacks` configuration.

references.title: The Runtime Component - Usage
references.summary: Explains how the Runtime component abstracts bootstrapping through `autoload_runtime.php`, runtimes, front-controller callables, and resolved arguments. This is relevant for questions about Symfony Runtime, Composer integration, front controllers, and supported application return types.

references.title: The Clock Component
references.summary: Introduces Symfony Clock as a PSR-20-compatible way to decouple applications from the system clock. It covers `NativeClock`, `MockClock`, `MonotonicClock`, the `now()` helper, and testable time-sensitive services.

references.title: The Clock Component - Monotonic Clock
references.summary: Explains `MonotonicClock` as a clock implementation intended for precise stopwatch-style measurements. This is relevant for questions about elapsed-time measurement that should not be affected by wall-clock changes.

concepts.concept: PHP API up to PHP 8.4 version

references.title: Language Reference
references.summary: This page is the Language Reference section of the PHP manual, providing an index of core language topics such as basic syntax, types, variables, constants, expressions, operators, control structures, functions, classes and objects, namespaces, enumerations, errors, exceptions, fibers, generators, and attributes. It also lists predefined variables, predefined exceptions, predefined interfaces and classes, predefined attributes, context options and parameters, and supported protocols and wrappers. The page serves as a navigational hub to the detailed subsections of PHP's language specification.

references.title: Basic syntax
references.summary: This Language Reference page covers PHP basic syntax topics such as PHP tags, escaping from HTML, instruction separation and comments. It serves as an overview entry in the PHP manual linking to related sections like types, variables, expressions, operators and control structures. The page is part of the PHP documentation’s Language Reference.

references.title: Types
references.summary: Documentation of PHP's type system within the Language Reference. The page lists and explains PHP types and related topics, including NULL, booleans, integers, floats, strings, arrays, objects, enumerations, resources, callables, mixed, void, never, relative class types, singleton types, iterables, type declarations, and type juggling.

references.title: Type declarations
references.summary: Explains PHP type declarations for function parameters, return values, class properties, class constants, and supported type forms. For the SFCE 8 PHP 8.4 cap, this page is relevant for scalar, nullable, iterable, object, union, intersection, DNF, mixed, never, true, standalone null and false, strict typing, typed class constants, and implicit-nullability deprecations.

references.title: Type Juggling
references.summary: Documents PHP's automatic type conversion behavior in numeric, string, logical, integral/string, comparative, and function contexts. This page is relevant for understanding when values are coerced, when the original value's type is unchanged, and when PHP raises type errors instead of converting.

references.title: Variables
references.summary: Documentation for PHP variables covering basic usage and concepts. The page includes sections on predefined variables, variable scope, variable variables, and variables from external sources.

references.title: Constants
references.summary: Constants are immutable, case-sensitive identifiers declared with `const` or `define()`, and `define()`'s case-insensitive option was removed in PHP 8.0. Constants live in global scope, magic constants are the exception to the case rule, and `constant()` retrieves a constant by its (possibly dynamic) name.

references.title: Expressions
references.summary: This page explains that expressions are PHP's fundamental building blocks — anything that has a value — and gives basic forms such as constants, variables and function calls. It documents expression-oriented features including that assignments evaluate to a value, pre- and post-increment semantics, comparison and strict equivalence operators, combined operator-assignment, the ternary conditional operator, expression statements, and automatic boolean conversion for truth-value testing.

references.title: Operators
references.summary: This page defines PHP operators, explaining that they take one or more values (expressions) and yield another value. It describes operator arity (unary, binary, ternary), and notes that a full list of PHP operators and an explanation of operator precedence and associativity are provided in the Operator Precedence section.

references.title: Assignment Operators
references.summary: Explains PHP assignment operators, including simple assignment, assignment by reference, compound assignments, and the null coalescing assignment operator ??=. It documents that assignment expressions evaluate to the assigned value and that ??= is equivalent to assigning the result of the null coalescing expression.

references.title: Control Structures
references.summary: Reference page listing PHP control structures and their variants. It covers conditional statements (if, else, elseif), looping constructs (while, do-while, for, foreach), flow control keywords (break, continue, return, goto), switch and match, declare, and inclusion directives (require, include, require_once, include_once), as well as alternative syntax for control structures.

references.title: Functions
references.summary: This manual page describes PHP functions and related language features. It covers user-defined functions, function parameters and arguments, return values, variable and internal (built-in) functions, anonymous functions, arrow functions, and first-class callable syntax. The page is part of the Language Reference and links to examples and contribution options.

references.title: Function parameters and arguments - Named Arguments
references.summary: Describes named arguments in PHP, where callers pass values by parameter name instead of position. The section covers order-independent named arguments, skipping optional parameters, the rule that named arguments must follow positional arguments, duplicate-parameter errors, and interaction with argument unpacking.

references.title: Generators
references.summary: Documentation page for PHP Generators within the Language Reference. It provides a generators overview, generator syntax, and a comparison of generators with Iterator objects.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 5.6.x to PHP 7.0.x
references.summary: Migration guide from PHP 5.6 to PHP 7.0 documenting backward-incompatible changes, new features, deprecated features, changed and new functions, new classes/interfaces, new global constants, SAPI changes, and removed extensions/SAPIs. The release focused on removing previously deprecated functionality and improving language consistency.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 7.0.x to PHP 7.1.x
references.summary: Migration guide from PHP 7.0 to PHP 7.1 documenting new features, new functions, new global constants, backward-incompatible changes, deprecated features, and changed functions. Notable additions include nullable type declarations, void return type, iterable pseudo-type, multi-catch via `|`, and visibility modifiers for class constants.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 7.1.x to PHP 7.2.x
references.summary: Migration guide from PHP 7.1 to PHP 7.2 listing new features, new functions and global constants, backward-incompatible changes, and deprecated features. Notable additions include the `object` parameter and return type, parameter type widening (covariance), `Argon2` password hashes, and the abstract-method-override rule.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 7.2.x to PHP 7.3.x
references.summary: Migration guide from PHP 7.2 to PHP 7.3 listing new features, new functions and global constants, backward-incompatible changes, and deprecated features. Notable additions include flexible heredoc/nowdoc syntax, `array_key_first()`/`array_key_last()`, and a trailing comma in function calls.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 7.3.x to PHP 7.4.x
references.summary: Migration guide from PHP 7.3 to PHP 7.4 covering new features, new classes/interfaces, new functions and global constants, backward-incompatible changes, deprecated features, and removed extensions. Notable additions include typed properties, arrow functions, the spread operator inside arrays, the null coalescing assignment `??=`, and weak references.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 7.4.x to PHP 8.0.x
references.summary: Migration guide from PHP 7.4 to PHP 8.0 covering new features, new classes/interfaces, backward-incompatible changes, and deprecated features. Major additions include union types, named arguments, attributes, constructor property promotion, the `match` expression, the nullsafe operator `?->`, and JIT compilation.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 8.0.x to PHP 8.1.x
references.summary: Migration guide from PHP 8.0 to PHP 8.1 covering new features, new classes/interfaces, new functions and global constants, backward-incompatible changes, and deprecated features. Major additions include enumerations, readonly properties, first-class callable syntax, the `never` return type, intersection types, final class constants, and fibers.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 8.1.x to PHP 8.2.x
references.summary: Migration guide that lists new features, new functions, new global constants, backward incompatible changes, deprecated features, other changes, and Windows support for PHP 8.2. It advises testing these incompatibilities and changes before switching PHP versions in production. The page also links to migration guides for other PHP versions.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 8.2.x to PHP 8.3.x
references.summary: This migration guide documents changes when upgrading from PHP 8.2.x to PHP 8.3.x, including new features, new classes and functions, new global constants, backward incompatible changes, deprecated features, and other changes as well as Windows-specific notes. It advises testing for incompatibilities before switching PHP versions in production and links to migration guides for other PHP versions.

references.title: Migrating from PHP 8.3.x to PHP 8.4.x
references.summary: Migration guide describing changes in PHP 8.4.x relative to 8.3.x. The page lists new features, new classes/enums/interfaces, new functions and global constants, backward incompatible changes, deprecated features, removed extensions, other changes and Windows support, and advises testing for incompatibilities before upgrading; it also links to other PHP migration guides.

references.title: Changelog
references.summary: Lists changes to functions of bundled PHP extensions across PHP versions. The page documents per-version modifications such as deprecated functions and parameters, return type changes, added or optional parameters, new exceptions or ValueError/TypeError behaviors, and other function- and API-level behavior changes.

references.title: Security
references.summary: This PHP manual page collects security guidance for PHP applications, covering general considerations and deployment modes (CGI and Apache module), session and filesystem security, database security and SQL injection, error reporting, and handling user-submitted data. It also lists security-related features and topics such as HTTP authentication, cookies, sessions, file uploads, using remote files, persistent connections, and includes a user-contributed notes section with practical advice (for example, protecting include files and using .htaccess).

references.title: Property Hooks
references.summary: Documents PHP 8.4 property hooks, which allow get and set behavior to be defined directly on non-static properties. The page explains backed versus virtual properties, shorthand hook syntax, set-parameter typing, restrictions such as incompatibility with readonly properties, inheritance behavior, parent hook access, references, and serialization interactions.

references.title: Introduction
references.summary: This page introduces PHP's object model and lists key OOP features such as visibility, abstract and final classes and methods, magic methods, interfaces, and cloning. It explains that PHP variables hold object references rather than copies and links to related topics like Objects and References and the Userland Naming Guide.

references.title: The Basics
references.summary: Covers PHP class declaration, instantiation with `new`, properties, methods, and the `$this` pseudo-variable. Documents inheritance with `extends`, the `::class` name resolution, and the nullsafe operator `?->` for safe member access on null.

references.title: Properties
references.summary: Properties are declared with at least one modifier (visibility, `static`, or `readonly`) and may include a type declaration (since PHP 7.4); typed properties must be initialized before access or an `Error` is thrown. Readonly properties (PHP 8.1) can be initialized only once from their declaring scope, cannot have default values, and are not supported on static properties.

references.title: Class Constants
references.summary: Describes how to declare and use class constants in PHP, noting their default public visibility and that they are allocated once per class rather than per instance. The page covers visibility modifiers for constants (PHP 7.1) and final constants preventing redefinition by child classes (PHP 8.1). It also shows examples of ::class, constant expressions, visibility behavior, late-static binding differences, and fetching constants via variables.

references.title: Autoloading Classes
references.summary: PHP autoloading uses `spl_autoload_register()` to register one or more callbacks that load classes, interfaces, traits, and enumerations on demand. The legacy `__autoload()` was removed in PHP 8.0, and throwing exceptions from an autoload function is discouraged because it prevents other registered autoloaders from running.

references.title: Constructors and Destructors
references.summary: `__construct` is invoked on instantiation and `__destruct` runs when the last reference is released or during shutdown; parent constructors and destructors are not called implicitly when a child redefines them. PHP 8.0+ supports constructor property promotion, and throwing exceptions from a destructor during shutdown is unsafe.

references.title: Visibility
references.summary: Defines member visibility in PHP using public, protected, and private for properties, methods, and (since PHP 7.1) class constants, with undeclared members defaulting to public. The page documents property and method access rules and notes that objects of the same class can access each other's private and protected members.

references.title: Object Inheritance
references.summary: Subclasses inherit public and protected methods, properties, and constants from a parent class, while private members are not visible and may be reimplemented in children. Overriding visibility may be relaxed but generally not restricted (constructors are the exception), readonly and read-write properties cannot be interchanged, and the `#[\ReturnTypeWillChange]` attribute suppresses internal-class return-type deprecation notices.

references.title: Scope Resolution Operator (::)
references.summary: The Scope Resolution Operator (::), also called Paamayim Nekudotayim or double-colon, is used to access a class constant, a static property, or a static method on a class or one of its parents. Static properties and methods can be overridden via late static binding, and when referencing from outside a class you may use the class name or a variable containing the class name (the variable value cannot be a keyword such as self, parent or static). Inside class definitions the special keywords self, parent and static are used to access properties or methods from the current or parent scope.

references.title: Static Keyword
references.summary: The `static` keyword declares static methods and properties that are reached without instantiating the class; static methods cannot use `$this`, and calling a non-static method statically throws an `Error` since PHP 8.0. Static properties must be accessed with `::` rather than `->`, and the class may be referenced via a class-name variable (but not via the keywords `self`, `parent`, or `static`).

references.title: Anonymous classes
references.summary: This page documents PHP anonymous classes: lightweight one-off class declarations that can be instantiated inline, accept constructor arguments, extend other classes, implement interfaces, and use traits like normal classes. It describes scoping rules for nested anonymous classes, notes that all objects from the same anonymous-class declaration share the same class identity, and explains that the engine assigns an implementation-detail name.

references.title: Overloading
references.summary: Overloading in PHP lets classes dynamically create and handle properties and methods via magic methods that are invoked for inaccessible or non-existing members. Property overloading uses __set, __get, __isset and __unset; method overloading uses __call and __callStatic. All overloading methods must be public, none of their arguments can be passed by reference, and property overloading only works in object (not static) context.

references.title: Object Iteration
references.summary: This page documents how PHP objects can be iterated (for example with foreach). By default foreach iterates over an object’s visible properties; iterating from within object scope can expose protected and private properties. The page includes a simple example demonstrating iteration and links to related interfaces and features such as Iterator, IteratorAggregate, SPL Iterators, and Generators.

references.title: Magic Methods
references.summary: This page documents PHP's magic methods (methods starting with __), listing their names and describing required visibility and signature rules. It explains specific behaviors and signatures for serialization (__serialize, __unserialize, __sleep, __wakeup), string conversion (__toString), callable objects (__invoke), export/import (__set_state), and debugging output (__debugInfo), including version notes, warnings about visibility and type declarations, and example usage for each method.

references.title: Final Keyword
references.summary: Documents the final keyword, which prevents child classes from overriding a method or constant when prefixed with final, and states that a final class cannot be extended. Includes examples for final methods, final classes, and final constants (since PHP 8.1.0). Notes that, as of PHP 8.0.0, private methods may not be declared final except for the constructor.

references.title: Object Cloning
references.summary: Describes how to create copies of objects in PHP using the clone keyword and that cloning performs a shallow copy of an object’s properties. After cloning, if a __clone() method is defined it is called to allow adjustment of the new object; properties that are references remain references unless explicitly cloned inside __clone(). The page includes examples showing forced cloning of contained objects and accessing members of a freshly cloned object in a single expression.

references.title: Comparing Objects
references.summary: Describes how PHP compares objects: the equality operator (==) considers two objects equal if they have the same attributes and values (values compared with ==) and are instances of the same class, while the identity operator (===) requires both variables to reference the exact same instance of the same class. The page shows example code illustrating differences between == and === and notes that equality comparison is recursive for object properties and that extensions can define their own rules for (==).

references.title: Function parameters and arguments - Named Arguments
references.summary: Describes named arguments in PHP, where callers pass values by parameter name instead of position. The section covers order-independent named arguments, skipping optional parameters, the rule that named arguments must follow positional arguments, duplicate-parameter errors, and interaction with argument unpacking.

references.title: Late Static Bindings
references.summary: Describes PHP's late static bindings feature, which lets code reference the class that was actually called at runtime using static:: and get_called_class(). Explains differences and limitations compared to self:: and __CLASS__, shows behavior in static and non-static contexts, and demonstrates forwarding versus non-forwarding calls with concrete examples and output. The page includes practical notes and user-contributed examples for using static:: with properties, methods, constructors, and inheritance.

references.title: Objects and references
references.summary: Explains that PHP object variables hold an object identifier rather than the object itself, so assigning, returning or passing an object copies that identifier (not an alias) and multiple variables can point to the same object. It distinguishes this from PHP references (aliases created with &), shows how mutating an object through a copied identifier affects all identifiers for that object while reassigning an identifier does not, and provides examples demonstrating these behaviors.

references.title: Object Serialization
references.summary: serialize() returns a string containing a byte-stream representation of a PHP value; unserialize() can recreate the original variable values. Serializing an object saves its properties and the class name but not its methods, so the class definition must be available (for example via include or spl_autoload_register()) when unserializing; otherwise PHP will create a __PHP_Incomplete_Class object that stores the original class name in __PHP_Incomplete_Class_Name. Objects can customize serialization and restoration with the __sleep() and __wakeup() magic methods, with __sleep() allowing a subset of properties to be serialized.

references.title: Covariance and Contravariance
references.summary: Describes PHP's support for covariance and contravariance: partial contravariance was introduced in PHP 7.2 and full covariance/contravariance in PHP 7.4. Covariance allows a child method to return a more specific type than its parent, while contravariance allows a child method to accept a less specific parameter type; the page illustrates both with Animal/Cat/Dog factories and an eat(Food) example.

references.title: OOP Changelog
references.summary: This page lists changes to PHP's object-oriented programming model organized by PHP version, documenting added features, changed behaviors, deprecations, and incompatibilities. For the PHP 8.1 cap, high-value entries include the nullsafe operator ?-> (8.0.0), final class constants and overridable interface constants (8.1.0), typed properties and limited variance (7.4.0), and many earlier additions and behavioral changes.

references.title: Attributes
references.summary: Documentation on PHP attributes, covering an overview of attributes, the attribute syntax, how to read attributes using the Reflection API, and how to declare attribute classes. The page serves as the Language Reference section for attributes in PHP and links to related subtopics for implementation and usage.

references.title: Attributes overview
references.summary: Introduced in PHP 8, attributes provide structured, machine-readable metadata that can be attached to classes, methods, functions, parameters, properties, and constants and inspected at runtime via the Reflection API. They offer a declarative way to annotate code without changing its structure, enabling decoupling of a feature's implementation from its usage (for example, marking optional setup methods with a SetUp attribute instead of enforcing an interface method).

references.title: Attributes syntax
references.summary: This page describes PHP attribute syntax: declarations begin with #[ and end with ], can list one or more attributes separated by commas, and use unqualified, qualified, or fully-qualified names. Arguments are optional, enclosed in parentheses, and must be literal values or constant expressions; both positional and named arguments are supported. Attribute names and arguments are resolved to an attribute class and passed to its constructor when an instance is created via the Reflection API, so a class is typically introduced for each attribute.

references.title: Reading Attributes with the Reflection API
references.summary: Describes using Reflection::getAttributes() to read attributes on classes, methods, functions, parameters, properties, and class constants. The method returns an array of ReflectionAttribute objects which expose the attribute name, arguments, and can create the actual attribute object via ReflectionAttribute::newInstance(). You can pass an attribute class name to getAttributes() to retrieve only attributes of that specific class, and separating the reflected representation from instantiation allows better control over error handling and argument validation.

references.title: Declaring Attribute classes
references.summary: This page describes how to define PHP attribute classes using the #[Attribute] declaration, recommending a separate class per attribute and that the Attribute attribute be imported from the global namespace. It explains restricting valid targets with a bitmask (e.g. Attribute::TARGET_METHOD, ::TARGET_FUNCTION, ::TARGET_PROPERTY, ::TARGET_ALL), making attributes repeatable with Attribute::IS_REPEATABLE, and notes that applying an attribute to an invalid target will throw an exception when ReflectionAttribute::newInstance() is called.

references.title: Predefined Attributes
references.summary: Lists the predefined attributes provided by PHP, including Attribute, AllowDynamicProperties, Deprecated, NoDiscard, Override, ReturnTypeWillChange, and SensitiveParameter. This page is a useful index for exam questions that distinguish user-defined attributes from engine-provided attributes.

references.title: The AllowDynamicProperties attribute
references.summary: Documents the #[AllowDynamicProperties] predefined attribute introduced with PHP 8.2 dynamic-property deprecations. It explains that the attribute marks classes that allow dynamic properties and that the effect is inherited by child classes.

references.title: The SensitiveParameter attribute
references.summary: Documents the #[SensitiveParameter] predefined attribute, which marks function or method parameters whose values should be redacted in stack traces. The page shows that reflected traces receive SensitiveParameterValue objects instead of exposing raw sensitive argument values.

references.title: The Override attribute
references.summary: Documents the #[Override] predefined attribute available in PHP 8.3 for methods. It indicates that a method is intended to override a parent method or implement an interface method, and PHP emits a compile-time error if no matching method exists.

references.title: Object Interfaces
references.summary: Interfaces are declared with the `interface` keyword, all their methods are implicitly public, and a class adopts them with `implements`; interfaces may extend other interfaces. Implementing methods must be signature-compatible with covariance and contravariance rules, and parameter names should match because of named arguments introduced in PHP 8.0.

references.title: Predefined Interfaces and Classes
references.summary: This page lists PHP's predefined interfaces and reserved classes, including Traversable, Iterator, IteratorAggregate, Throwable, Countable, ArrayAccess, Serializable, Closure, stdClass, Generator, Fiber, WeakReference, WeakMap, Stringable, UnitEnum, BackedEnum, SensitiveParameterValue and __PHP_Incomplete_Class. It references the SPL interfaces and reserved classes and includes a user note that extensions can also provide implementable interfaces such as JsonSerializable.

references.title: Type Operators
references.summary: This page documents PHP's instanceof operator and its behavior. It shows how instanceof checks whether a variable is an instantiated object of a class, including inherited classes and implemented interfaces, how negation works, and examples of using objects or string class names. It also notes that non-objects return false, constants on the left were allowed starting in PHP 7.3.0, arbitrary expressions wrapped in parentheses are supported as of PHP 8.0.0, and that a functional variant exists with is_a().

references.title: Function parameters and arguments - Variable-length argument lists
references.summary: Explains variadic parameter lists in PHP using the ... token. The section covers collecting extra arguments into an array, unpacking arrays or Traversable values into calls, type declarations before variadic parameters, and passing variadic arguments by reference.

references.title: Anonymous functions
references.summary: Anonymous functions (closures) are instances of the internal `Closure` class and may be used as callbacks or assigned to variables. The `use` clause imports parent-scope variables by value or by reference, `$this` is auto-bound when the closure is declared inside a class, and a `static` anonymous function opts out of that binding.

references.title: Closure
references.summary: Documentation for the Closure class, the object type used to represent anonymous functions in PHP. It lists the class synopsis and methods for manipulating closures, including bind, bindTo, call, fromCallable and a private constructor; notes that an __invoke method exists for consistency; and includes a changelog item about Closure::__debugInfo output.

references.title: Arrow functions
references.summary: Arrow functions (PHP 7.4+) are concise single-expression closures written as `fn(args) => expr` and are instances of `Closure`. They capture parent-scope variables automatically by value (so outer values cannot be modified from inside), and they support full function signatures including types, defaults, variadics, and by-reference parameters or returns.

references.title: Class Abstraction
references.summary: Describes PHP abstract classes and abstract methods within the PHP 8.1 cap: abstract classes cannot be instantiated, and any class with at least one abstract method must itself be declared abstract. Abstract methods declare only a signature and visibility and must be implemented by child classes following inheritance and signature compatibility rules. The page provides examples of valid abstract method declarations and concrete implementations.

references.title: Exceptions
references.summary: Covers PHP's exception model including throwing and catching objects that implement Throwable, try/catch/finally semantics, and exception propagation up the call stack. Notes language features: throw is an expression (PHP 8.0+), catch can specify multiple types with | (PHP 7.1+) and may omit the exception variable (PHP 8.0+). Describes finally interactions with return and exceptions, global handlers via set_exception_handler(), and converting errors to ErrorException, with numerous examples.

references.title: Errors
references.summary: This page introduces PHP error handling and explains that PHP reports errors, warnings, and notices for common coding and runtime problems. It emphasizes detecting and handling these errors to make debugging easier and includes sections such as Basics and Errors in PHP 7.

references.title: Errors - Basics
references.summary: Describes how PHP reports errors via internal error conditions, each carrying a type. Explains that without a custom handler, behavior is governed by the error_reporting, display_errors, log_errors, and error_log php.ini directives, with E_ALL recommended in development and display_errors disabled in production. Notes that user-defined error handlers can be registered with set_error_handler() to customize handling, though some error types cannot be handled this way.

references.title: Errors - Errors in PHP 7
references.summary: PHP 7 reports most fatal-class errors by throwing `Error` exceptions, which propagate like normal exceptions and fall back to `set_exception_handler()` if uncaught. The `Error` hierarchy does not extend `Exception`, so `catch (Exception $e)` does not catch `Error` instances; subclasses include `ArithmeticError`, `DivisionByZeroError`, `AssertionError`, `ParseError`, `TypeError`, `ArgumentCountError`, `ValueError`, `UnhandledMatchError`, and `FiberError`.

references.title: Class Exception
references.summary: Exception is the base class for all user exceptions in PHP (documented for PHP 5, 7, and 8). The page lists the class synopsis including properties (message, code, file, line, previous, trace, string) and methods such as __construct, getMessage, getPrevious, getCode, getFile, getLine, getTrace, getTraceAsString, __toString, and __clone, and describes that the exception properties reflect where the exception was created.

references.title: Class Error
references.summary: Error is the base class for all internal PHP errors (available in PHP 7 and PHP 8). The page documents the class synopsis, its properties (message, code, file, line, previous, string, trace) and methods including __construct, getters like getMessage/getFile/getTrace, getTraceAsString, __toString and the private __clone method.

references.title: Interface Throwable
references.summary: Throwable is the base interface for any object that can be thrown via a throw statement (including Error and Exception) and is available since PHP 7. PHP classes cannot implement Throwable directly and must instead extend Exception. The interface defines methods such as getMessage, getCode, getFile, getLine, getTrace, getTraceAsString, getPrevious and __toString, and as of PHP 8 Throwable implements Stringable.

references.title: Traits
references.summary: Traits provide a mechanism for code reuse in PHP's single-inheritance model, allowing sets of methods, properties, static members, and abstract members to be inserted into multiple independent classes; traits cannot be instantiated directly and enable horizontal composition of behavior. Method precedence is: methods in the current class override trait methods, which override inherited methods; naming conflicts between traits must be resolved explicitly with insteadof and can be aliased or have visibility changed with as. The documentation also covers traits composed from other traits, abstract trait members, property compatibility rules, and the PHP 8.1 deprecation of calling static trait members directly on a trait.

references.title: Enumerations
references.summary: Documentation for PHP enumerations covering syntax and language-level features. The page lists topics including basic and backed enumerations, instance and static methods, constants, traits, enum usage in constant expressions, differences from objects, value listing, serialization, and reasons enums cannot be extended, and includes examples.

references.title: Enumerations overview
references.summary: Introduced in PHP 8.1, enumerations (enums) let you define a custom type limited to a discrete set of possible values. In PHP, an Enum is a special kind of class whose cases are single-instance objects, so enum cases are valid objects and can be used anywhere an object is allowed, including type checks. The page notes that enums make invalid states unrepresentable and gives the built-in boolean type as the canonical example of an enumerated type.

references.title: Basic Enumerations
references.summary: This page explains PHP basic enums: they define a new type with a fixed set of possible values and are similar to classes (sharing namespaces and autoloading). By default cases are pure cases implemented as singleton objects (not scalar-backed), all cases have a read-only name property, zero-case enums are syntactically valid, enum values are instances of the enum type and cannot be meaningfully compared with < or >. The page also shows type-checking functions accepting enum types, and notes that defined() and constant() can be used to inspect cases dynamically (though backed enums are generally preferred).

references.title: Backed Enumerations
references.summary: Explains enums whose cases have scalar equivalents (Backed Cases) and enums composed entirely of such cases (Backed Enums). Backed Enums must be backed by either int or string (not both), all cases require unique explicit scalar values (constants or constant expressions are allowed as of PHP 8.2.0), and each backed case exposes a read-only value property. Backed enums implement BackedEnum with from() and tryFrom() to map scalars to cases, with described weak/strict typing coercions and error behaviors; manually defining from() or tryFrom() on a Backed Enum causes a fatal error.

references.title: Enumeration methods
references.summary: Enums (both Pure and Backed) may declare methods and implement interfaces; if an Enum implements an interface, type checks for that interface accept all cases and cases behave like object instances. Inside a method $this refers to the case instance; on Backed Enums the implements clause follows the backing type, and methods may be public, protected, or private and commonly use match on $this to return different results per case. Manually defining a cases() method on an Enum is disallowed.

references.title: Enumeration static methods
references.summary: This page explains that PHP enumerations can declare static methods, primarily used as alternative constructors, and provides an example (Size::fromLength) that returns enum cases via a match expression. It notes static methods may be public, protected, or private, and that private and protected behave equivalently because enums cannot be inherited.

references.title: Enumeration constants
references.summary: This page documents constants inside PHP enums, which may be declared public, protected or private (with private and protected effectively equivalent because enums cannot be inherited). An enum constant can refer to an enum case (for example public const Huge = self::Large), and constants may also hold values from other enums or other scalar values as shown in the user-contributed example. The excerpt includes an example of accessing nested constants across enums.

references.title: Enumerations using traits
references.summary: Traits may be used in PHP enums and behave the same as traits on classes. Traits used in an enum must not define properties; they may only include methods, static methods, and constants, and a trait with properties will cause a fatal error. The page gives a code example of an enum using a trait and implementing an interface.

references.title: Differences from objects
references.summary: Explains which object-oriented features PHP enums do and do not support. Enum cases cannot have state, constructors, destructors, inheritance, properties, cloning, or most magic methods, but enums may define methods, constants, implement interfaces, use selected magic methods, and receive attributes.

references.title: Value listing
references.summary: Documents UnitEnum::cases(), which is implemented by both pure and backed enums. The method returns a packed array of all enum cases in declaration order, and manually defining a cases() method on an enum is forbidden.

references.title: Serialization
references.summary: Describes how PHP serializes enum cases using a dedicated E serialization code and deserializes them back to singleton case instances. The page also explains JSON behavior: pure enums throw an error by default, while backed enums encode as their scalar backed value unless JsonSerializable customizes the result.

references.title: Overview of HTTP
references.summary: A practical overview of HTTP as a client-server, application-layer protocol used to exchange requests and responses for web resources. It explains the roles of clients, servers, proxies, messages, extensibility, statelessness, and connection handling at a level useful before reading RFC 9110.

references.title: HTTP messages
references.summary: A concise explanation of the structure of HTTP requests and responses, including start lines, status lines, headers, and optional bodies. This is useful for recognizing the message model that RFC 9110 describes abstractly.

references.title: HTTP resources and specifications
references.summary: An index of HTTP specifications and related resources, showing how modern HTTP is split across RFC 9110 for semantics, RFC 9111 for caching, RFC 9112 for HTTP/1.1, and later protocol mappings. This helps learners place RFC 9110 in the broader HTTP standards landscape.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Terminology and Core Concepts
references.summary: Defines core HTTP roles and concepts such as user agents, origin servers, resources, representations, connections, intermediaries, and caches. This section is the conceptual vocabulary needed to understand the rest of HTTP semantics.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - http and https URI Normalization and Comparison
references.summary: Specifies the `http` and `https` URI schemes, default ports, origin identity, normalization, fragments, and the deprecation of userinfo in HTTP(S) URIs. These rules matter when reasoning about target resources, authority, and whether two identifiers are equivalent.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Fields
references.summary: Defines HTTP fields, field names, field values, header and trailer sections, ordering rules, and the constraints around repeated field lines. This section is the basis for understanding request and response metadata without tying it to one wire format.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Message Abstraction
references.summary: Describes HTTP messages as requests or responses composed of control data, header fields, content, and optional trailer fields. It separates message semantics from protocol-specific framing details.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Representation Data and Metadata
references.summary: Explains representation data and metadata, including how fields such as `Content-Type`, `Content-Encoding`, `Content-Language`, and `Content-Location` describe the selected representation. This section is central to understanding what an HTTP response or request content means.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Methods Overview
references.summary: Introduces HTTP methods as tokens that define the primary request semantics and explains their extensible, case-sensitive nature. This is the general method model before studying individual methods in depth.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Common Method Properties
references.summary: Defines common method properties including safe, idempotent, and cacheable methods. These properties are fundamental for understanding how clients, servers, intermediaries, and caches may treat repeated or automated requests.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Status Codes
references.summary: Defines the status code model, including interim and final responses, status code classes, reason phrases, and registration of additional status codes. This gives the general response semantics needed before studying individual codes.

references.title: HTTP response status codes (MDN)
references.summary: Reference index of HTTP response status codes grouped into the five classes: 1xx informational, 2xx successful, 3xx redirection, 4xx client error, and 5xx server error. Useful for choosing the correct code for a response and understanding what each code communicates to the client.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Informational 1xx
references.summary: Defines 1xx status codes as interim responses used to communicate connection status or request progress before the final response. Covers general 1xx handling plus 100 Continue and 101 Switching Protocols semantics.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - 3xx Redirection
references.summary: Specifies the 3xx status code class for redirections, including 301 Moved Permanently, 302 Found, 303 See Other, 304 Not Modified, 307 Temporary Redirect, and 308 Permanent Redirect. Clarifies which codes preserve the request method and which allow the user agent to change it to GET.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - 4xx Client Error
references.summary: Specifies the 4xx client-error class, including 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 405 Method Not Allowed, 406 Not Acceptable, 409 Conflict, 410 Gone, 411 Length Required, 412 Precondition Failed, 415 Unsupported Media Type, and 422 Unprocessable Content. Distinguishes authentication (401) from authorization (403) and addresses Allow headers required with 405.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - 5xx Server Error
references.summary: Specifies the 5xx server-error class, including 500 Internal Server Error, 501 Not Implemented, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable, and 504 Gateway Timeout. Explains when a server should advertise transient unavailability with 503 and the use of Retry-After.

references.title: HTTP 304 Not Modified (MDN)
references.summary: Describes the 304 Not Modified response sent for conditional GET/HEAD requests when the cached representation is still fresh. Explains what headers must be present, what must not be sent (no message body), and how it integrates with ETag and If-None-Match validation.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Request
references.summary: Explains how to create a Symfony `Request` from PHP superglobals via `Request::createFromGlobals()` and the equivalent verbose constructor that accepts `$_GET`, `$_POST`, attributes, `$_COOKIE`, `$_FILES` and `$_SERVER`. Establishes the `Request` object as the OO wrapper around PHP request data.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Accessing Request Data
references.summary: Documents the public properties of the `Request` object (`request`, `query`, `cookies`, `attributes`, `files`, `server`, `headers`) and which `ParameterBag`/`InputBag`/`FileBag`/`ServerBag`/`HeaderBag` instance backs each. Covers `get()`/`all()`/`getContent()`/`toArray()`/`getPayload()` and the typed getters (`getInt`, `getBoolean`, `getEnum`, `getString`, `filter`).

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Processing HTTP Headers
references.summary: Documents the `HeaderUtils` helper for parsing, combining, formatting, quoting, unquoting, and parsing query-string-like values found in HTTP headers. Useful for understanding safe header handling instead of ad-hoc string parsing.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Identifying a Request
references.summary: Shows how to identify a request by its path info using `Request::getPathInfo()`, returning the part of the URL between the front controller and the query string. Useful to know how Symfony exposes routing-relevant URL parts.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Simulating a Request
references.summary: Demonstrates `Request::create()` to build a synthetic request from a URI, method and parameters, plus `overrideGlobals()`, `duplicate()`, and `initialize()` for tweaking an existing request. Useful for testing and sub-requests.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Overriding the Request
references.summary: Explains the rare legacy-integration case for customizing request instantiation with `Request::setFactory()`. Clarifies that `Request` is a data object representing an HTTP message and should generally not be overridden.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Routing of Requests
references.summary: Defines how an HTTP request is routed by clients and servers, including the request target forms (origin-form, absolute-form, authority-form, asterisk-form), the role of the `Host` header, and effective request URI reconstruction. Required reading for understanding what identifies an HTTP request.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Response
references.summary: Documents the `Response` class, whose constructor accepts content, status code, and headers. Shows `setContent()`, `setStatusCode()`, the `headers` `ResponseHeaderBag`, and `setCharset()`, and notes that Symfony assumes UTF-8 encoding by default.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Sending the Response
references.summary: Explains `Response::prepare($request)` to fix incompatibilities with the HTTP specification before sending and `Response::send()` to actually emit the response. Mentions the optional `flush` argument that controls whether `fastcgi_finish_request()`/`litespeed_finish_request()` are called.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Redirecting the User
references.summary: Shows `RedirectResponse` for redirecting the client to another URL. Establishes the standard way to issue an HTTP redirect from a Symfony controller without manually crafting `Location` headers.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Streaming a Response
references.summary: Documents `StreamedResponse`, which can be fed a string iterable via `setChunks()` or a PHP callable via `setCallback()`. Notes the interaction with `flush()`, `ob_flush()`, PHP output buffering, and the `X-Accel-Buffering` header to disable nginx FastCGI buffering for a single response.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Streaming a JSON Response
references.summary: Documents `StreamedJsonResponse` for streaming large JSON structures with PHP generators or other traversables. Shows how generators can keep memory usage low and how `flush()` can send accumulated chunks periodically.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Streaming Server-Sent Events
references.summary: Documents `EventStreamResponse` and `ServerEvent` for implementing Server-Sent Events over HTTP. Covers the automatically set SSE headers and the event fields for data, type, id, retry, and comments.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Serving Files
references.summary: Explains file responses with `Content-Disposition`, `HeaderUtils::makeDisposition()`, and `BinaryFileResponse`. Covers automatic Range and If-Range handling, X-Sendfile support, `deleteFileAfterSend()`, temporary files, streamed files, and unknown-size streams.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Creating a JSON Response
references.summary: Documents `JsonResponse`, which sets `Content-Type: application/json` and encodes the data argument. Covers `setData()`, `setEncodingOptions()`, `JsonResponse::fromJsonString()`, the recommendation to use associative arrays to avoid XSSI JSON Hijacking, and the non-standard `null` handling in the constructor.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - 2xx Successful
references.summary: Specifies the 2xx success status code class, including 200 OK, 201 Created (with Location), 202 Accepted, 203 Non-Authoritative Information, 204 No Content (no body), 205 Reset Content, and 206 Partial Content for range requests.

references.title: HTTP request methods (MDN)
references.summary: Reference index of the HTTP request methods (`GET`, `HEAD`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, `CONNECT`, `OPTIONS`, `TRACE`, `PATCH`) describing each method's intent, whether it is safe, idempotent, and cacheable, and whether it has a request body.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - GET
references.summary: Defines the `GET` method as transferring a representation of the target resource. Specifies that `GET` is safe, idempotent, cacheable, and the primary method used by HTTP retrieval.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - HEAD
references.summary: Defines `HEAD` as identical to `GET` except that the server must not send response content. Explains its use for obtaining representation metadata efficiently and notes that HEAD responses are cacheable.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - POST
references.summary: Defines `POST` for processing the enclosed representation according to the resource's own semantics. `POST` is neither safe nor idempotent and is the canonical method for creating subordinate resources or submitting data blocks.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - PUT
references.summary: Defines `PUT` as requesting that the state of the target resource be created or replaced with the state defined by the representation enclosed in the request. `PUT` is idempotent but not safe.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - DELETE
references.summary: Defines `DELETE` as requesting removal of the association between the target resource and its current functionality. `DELETE` is idempotent but not safe and is generally not cacheable.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - CONNECT
references.summary: Defines `CONNECT` as requesting that the recipient establish a tunnel to the destination server identified by the request target. Covers successful tunnel establishment and the special authority-form request target used for proxy tunneling.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - OPTIONS
