references.summary: Defines `OPTIONS` for requesting information about the communication options available for the target resource. Servers reply with an `Allow` header listing supported methods; commonly used in CORS preflight requests.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - TRACE
references.summary: Defines `TRACE` as a loop-back test for the request message along the path to the target resource. Explains that the final recipient reflects the received request message as `message/http` content in a 200 response.

references.title: RFC 5789: PATCH Method for HTTP
references.summary: Defines the `PATCH` method, which applies a partial modification described in the request body to a target resource. `PATCH` is neither safe nor idempotent in general (idempotency depends on the patch document) and uses media types like `application/json-patch+json`.

references.title: Using HTTP cookies (MDN)
references.summary: Overview of HTTP cookies: how the server sets them via `Set-Cookie`, how the browser stores and returns them in the `Cookie` header, and the role of attributes like `Domain`, `Path`, `Expires`, `Max-Age`, `Secure`, `HttpOnly`, `SameSite`, and `Partitioned`. Covers session vs persistent cookies and basic security considerations.

references.title: Set-Cookie header (MDN)
references.summary: Reference for the `Set-Cookie` response header, listing supported directives such as `Expires`, `Max-Age`, `Domain`, `Path`, `Secure`, `HttpOnly`, `SameSite=Strict|Lax|None`, and `Partitioned`. Explains semantics, defaults, and interactions (e.g. `SameSite=None` requires `Secure`).

references.title: RFC 6265: HTTP State Management Mechanism
references.summary: Defines the Cookie and Set-Cookie HTTP headers and the cookie storage model. Specifies how user agents parse, store, and send cookies, attribute semantics (`Domain`, `Path`, `Expires`, `Max-Age`, `Secure`, `HttpOnly`), and cookie matching rules.

references.title: RFC 6265: HTTP State Management - Set-Cookie syntax
references.summary: Specifies the formal syntax of the `Set-Cookie` header, including the cookie-pair, attributes, and the rules for `Expires`, `Max-Age`, `Domain`, `Path`, `Secure`, and `HttpOnly`. Also describes how `Max-Age` takes precedence over `Expires` when both are present.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Setting Cookies
references.summary: Explains how to set, build, and clear response cookies using `ResponseHeaderBag::setCookie()`, `ResponseHeaderBag::clearCookie()`, the `Cookie::create()` factory, `Cookie::fromString()`, and the immutable `with*()` builders such as `withValue()`, `withExpires()`, `withDomain()`, `withSecure()`, and `withPartitioned()` for CHIPS partitioned cookies.

references.title: RFC 9111: HTTP Caching
references.summary: Normative specification of HTTP caching, replacing RFC 7234. Defines storing, freshness, validation, expiration, the cache role classification (private vs shared), `Cache-Control` directives, `Age`, conditional revalidation with `If-None-Match`/`If-Modified-Since`, and how `Vary` affects cache key matching.

references.title: RFC 9111: HTTP Caching - Cache-Control
references.summary: Defines the `Cache-Control` request and response directives, including `max-age`, `s-maxage`, `public`, `private`, `no-cache`, `no-store`, `must-revalidate`, `proxy-revalidate`, `immutable`, and `stale-while-revalidate`/`stale-if-error`. Specifies how shared caches (CDNs, reverse proxies) interpret `s-maxage` versus `max-age`.

references.title: HTTP Cache
references.summary: Top-level overview of Symfony's HTTP cache support: gateway caches, the built-in Symfony reverse proxy enabled via `framework.http_cache`, and the four cache headers `Cache-Control`, `Expires`, `ETag`, `Last-Modified`. Introduces expiration vs validation models and notes that only safe methods (`GET`/`HEAD`) are cached.

references.title: HTTP Cache Expiration
references.summary: Explains the **expiration model** using either `Cache-Control` or `Expires`. Shows `Response::setMaxAge()`, `Response::setSharedMaxAge()`, `Response::setExpires()` and the `#[Cache]` attribute. Notes that `setSharedMaxAge()` (s-maxage) prohibits stale-if-error and that `Expires` is ignored if `max-age`/`s-maxage` is set.

references.title: HTTP Cache Validation
references.summary: Explains the **validation model** using `ETag` and `Last-Modified`. Documents `Response::setEtag()` (with weak-etag flag), `Response::setLastModified()`, and `Response::isNotModified($request)` which compares `If-None-Match`/`If-Modified-Since` and automatically returns 304 when not modified.

references.title: HTTP Cache Vary
references.summary: Documents the `Vary` HTTP header which tells caches which request headers form part of the cache key. Shows how to set `Vary` via `Response::setVary()` so that, for example, content negotiated by `Accept-Language` or `Accept-Encoding` is cached separately per variant.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Managing the HTTP Cache
references.summary: Lists the rich `Response` API for cache headers: `setPublic()`, `setPrivate()`, `setMaxAge()`, `setSharedMaxAge()`, `setExpires()`, `setEtag()`, `setLastModified()`, `setVary()`, `setNotModified()`, `setCache()`, and `expire()`. The single `setCache()` method accepts the keys `public`, `private`, `max_age`, `s_maxage`, `must_revalidate`, `no_cache`, `no_store`, `no_transform`, `proxy_revalidate`, `immutable`, `last_modified`, and `etag`.

references.title: HTTP Cache Invalidation
references.summary: Discusses **cache invalidation** with HTTP reverse proxies. Symfony's built-in `HttpCache` does not implement explicit invalidation; production setups use Varnish or similar with `BAN`/`PURGE` requests, or rely on validation (ETag/Last-Modified) plus short TTLs. The article warns that "invalidating a cache" is one of computer science's two hard problems.

references.title: Working with Edge Side Includes
references.summary: **Edge Side Includes (ESI)** lets a reverse proxy assemble a final response from independently cacheable fragments — each fragment can have its own TTL. Symfony renders fragments with `{{ render_esi(...) }}` (Twig) or `Esi` listener / `EsiFragmentRenderer`. The full page can be public, while user-specific blocks remain uncached.

references.title: Working with Server Side Includes
references.summary: **Server Side Includes (SSI)** are similar to ESI but processed by the **web server** (e.g. Apache `mod_include`) rather than a reverse proxy. Symfony exposes `framework.ssi: { enabled: true }` and the `{{ render_ssi(...) }}` Twig function; output is `<!--#include virtual="..."-->` directives.

references.title: How to Use Varnish to Speed up my Website
references.summary: Walks through pairing Symfony with **Varnish** as the gateway cache: VCL configuration to honour `Cache-Control: s-maxage`, ESI processing setup, and how to forward `X-Reverse-Proxy-TTL`. Recommends removing cookies from cacheable responses or splitting auth pages from anonymous content.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Content Negotiation
references.summary: Defines proactive (server-driven) and reactive (agent-driven) content negotiation. Specifies the role of `Accept`, `Accept-Charset`, `Accept-Encoding`, `Accept-Language`, the q-value preference syntax, the relationship to the `Vary` response header, and how 406 Not Acceptable is returned when no representation matches.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Accept
references.summary: Defines the `Accept` request header used to indicate preferred media types. Specifies the `media-range` syntax, parameters, the optional `q` quality value, and how more specific ranges are preferred over wildcards.

references.title: Content negotiation (MDN)
references.summary: Practical overview of HTTP content negotiation, focusing on server-driven negotiation using `Accept`, `Accept-Encoding`, `Accept-Language`, and `Accept-Charset`. Explains q-values, why responses should advertise variants with `Vary`, and the role of 406 Not Acceptable.

references.title: Accept header (MDN)
references.summary: Reference for the `Accept` request header listing preferred media types and their q-values. Useful when reasoning about how clients express format preferences and how servers select a representation.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Accessing Accept-* Headers Data
references.summary: Documents the convenience getters `Request::getAcceptableContentTypes()`, `getLanguages()`, `getCharsets()`, and `getEncodings()` (all sorted by descending quality), plus the `AcceptHeader` utility class with `AcceptHeader::fromString()`, `has()`, `get()`, `getQuality()`, `getAttribute()`, and `all()` to fully parse `Accept`, `Accept-Language`, `Accept-Charset`, or `Accept-Encoding`.

references.title: RFC 9110: HTTP Semantics - Accept-Language
references.summary: Defines the `Accept-Language` request header used to indicate the natural languages preferred by the client. Specifies the q-value preference syntax, the use of language ranges (per RFC 4647), and how a server selects the best representation.

references.title: RFC 4647: Matching of Language Tags
references.summary: Defines the algorithms used to match language ranges (such as those in `Accept-Language`) against language tags: basic filtering, extended filtering, and lookup. Provides the formal basis for falling back from `fr-CH` to `fr`, `en-US` to `en`, etc.

references.title: Accept-Language header (MDN)
references.summary: Reference for the `Accept-Language` request header listing preferred language tags and their q-values. Useful when reasoning about how clients communicate locale preferences and how servers should respond, possibly with `Vary: Accept-Language`.

references.title: Translations - Selecting the Language Preferred by the User
references.summary: Documents `Request::getPreferredLanguage()`, which takes an array of supported locales and returns the best match against the `Accept-Language` header. Specifies that on no perfect match it falls back to a partial match by language and finally returns the first locale in the supported list.

references.title: Symfony Framework Configuration - set_locale_from_accept_language
references.summary: Documents the `framework.set_locale_from_accept_language` option (default `false`). When enabled, the request locale is automatically set from the `Accept-Language` header; if the `_locale` request attribute is also present, `Accept-Language` is ignored.

references.title: HTTP Client - Basic Usage
references.summary: Introduces `HttpClient::create()` and the framework `http_client` service autowired for `HttpClientInterface`. Shows `request($method, $url)`, `Response::getStatusCode()`, `Response::getHeaders()`, `Response::getContent()`, and `Response::toArray()` for JSON decoding.

references.title: HTTP Client - Making Requests
references.summary: Documents the single `request()` method used for all HTTP methods and the third argument for per-request options. Explains that Symfony's HttpClient starts requests immediately and only blocks when response data is needed.

references.title: HTTP Client - Scoping Client
references.summary: Documents `ScopingHttpClient`, configured via `framework.http_client.scoped_clients`, which auto-applies options based on a regex `scope` or a `base_uri`. Covers `ScopingHttpClient::forBaseUri()`, named autowiring aliases (e.g. `HttpClientInterface $githubClient` injects the `github.client` service), and the merge rule that per-request options override scoped defaults.

references.title: HTTP Client - Authentication
references.summary: Documents the built-in authentication options: `auth_basic` (HTTP Basic), `auth_bearer` (HTTP Bearer/token), and `auth_ntlm` (Microsoft NTLM, requiring the cURL transport). Shows global, scoped, and per-request configuration and notes that credentials can also be embedded in the URL for Basic auth.

references.title: HTTP Client - Query String Parameters
references.summary: Shows the `query` request option for adding query-string parameters as an associative array. Symfony encodes these values and merges them with any query string already present in the URL.

references.title: HTTP Client - Headers
references.summary: Documents the `headers` option for configuring default request headers globally or per client and overriding them per request. Covers the common case of setting headers such as `User-Agent` or `Content-Type`.

references.title: HTTP Client - Redirects
references.summary: Explains that Symfony's HttpClient follows redirects by default up to a limit of 20. Documents the `max_redirects` option, including `0` to disable following redirects and the `RedirectionException` raised when the limit is exceeded.

references.title: HTTP Client - Retry Failed Requests
references.summary: Documents automatic retry behavior through the `retry_failed` option and the `RetryableHttpClient` decorator. Covers default retry counts, exponential delays, default retried status codes, idempotent-method handling, and retry strategies.

references.title: HTTP Client - SSRF Handling
references.summary: Explains Server-Side Request Forgery risks when user-provided URIs are fetched by backend code. Documents `NoPrivateNetworkHttpClient`, which decorates another client and blocks requests to private networks by default or to configured CIDR ranges.

references.title: HTTP Client - Streaming Responses
references.summary: Documents `HttpClientInterface::stream()` for iterating response chunks (`ChunkInterface`) without buffering the full body. Notes the default buffering of `text/*`, JSON, and XML response bodies into a `php://temp` stream, controllable via the `buffer` option (boolean or closure).

references.title: HTTP Client - Handling Exceptions
references.summary: Describes the three exception interfaces: `HttpExceptionInterface` (for status 300-599), `TransportExceptionInterface` (lower-level transport issues), and `DecodingExceptionInterface` (decoding failures), all implementing `ExceptionInterface`. Explains that `getHeaders()`, `getContent()`, `toArray()` throw on 3xx-5xx unless `false` is passed, and that calling `getStatusCode()` opts out of the destructor-time exception.

references.title: HTTP Client - Concurrent Requests
references.summary: Explains that the HttpClient is asynchronous by default: dispatching multiple `request()` calls and only consuming responses later allows them to complete in parallel. Highlights that lazy responses do not block until the response body is read.

references.title: HTTP Client - Multiplexing Responses
references.summary: Explains how to process multiple lazy responses in the order chunks arrive by passing them to `HttpClientInterface::stream()`. Documents first, last, timeout, and content chunks plus `user_data` for identifying responses during streaming.

references.title: HTTP Client - Dealing with Network Timeouts
references.summary: Documents the `timeout` request option for idle request/response periods and the stream timeout argument for monitoring several responses. Explains timeout chunks, non-blocking monitoring with timeout `0`, and `max_duration` for limiting total transaction duration.

references.title: HTTP Client - Caching Requests and Responses
references.summary: Documents the `CachingHttpClient` decorator for caching HTTP responses according to RFC 9111. Explains its dependency on a tag-aware cache and the requirement that responses be fully consumed before they can be stored.

references.title: HTTP Client - Testing
references.summary: Documents `MockHttpClient` and `MockResponse` for unit testing without real HTTP. Covers passing a list of `MockResponse`, using a callback factory, `MockResponse::fromFile()`, the `http_code` option for non-200 responses, simulating chunked and timeout responses, and the `framework.http_client.mock_response_factory` option.

references.title: HTTP Client - PSR-18 and PSR-17
references.summary: Explains interoperability with PSR-18 (HTTP Client) and PSR-17 (HTTP Factories) via Symfony's `Psr18Client` adapter and the `psr/http-client` / `psr/http-factory` packages. Useful when integrating libraries that depend on PSR interfaces while keeping Symfony's HttpClient as the underlying transport.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component
references.summary: Overview of the HttpFoundation component, which defines an object-oriented layer over the HTTP specification. It replaces PHP superglobals such as $_GET, $_POST, $_FILES, $_COOKIE, $_SESSION and helper functions like header() and setcookie() with Request and Response objects. This page documents installation and the role of the component as Symfony's HTTP layer.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Accessing the Session
references.summary: Explains that a session can be retrieved from a Request or RequestStack via getSession() and how to detect with hasPreviousSession() whether a session was started in a previous request. The session is provided by HttpFoundation and is not automatically started until first use.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Matching a Request Against a Set of Rules
references.summary: Documents the RequestMatcher utility shipped with HttpFoundation, used to determine whether a Request matches a configured set of constraints (path, method, IP, host, attributes, scheme). Such matchers power firewall rules, access controls, and other request-based decisions in Symfony applications.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Anonymizing IP Addresses
references.summary: Documents the IpUtils::anonymize() helper, which removes the host portion of an IPv4 or IPv6 address so that it can be stored without identifying a single user. This is one of several IP utilities the HttpFoundation component ships for trusted-proxy and access-control use cases.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Check If an IP Belongs to a CIDR Subnet
references.summary: Describes IpUtils::checkIp() and IpUtils::checkIp4()/checkIp6(), which test whether an IP address belongs to a given CIDR-notated subnet. These helpers underpin trusted-proxy, trusted-host, and IP-based access-control configuration in Symfony.

references.title: Symfony and HTTP Fundamentals
references.summary: Recaps the **HTTP request/response lifecycle**: the client sends a `Request`, the server hands it to the `HttpKernel`, which routes it to a controller that returns a `Response`. Symfony's **HttpFoundation** component models these primitives in object form — replacing PHP's superglobals (`$_GET`, `$_POST`, `$_COOKIE`, `$_FILES`, `$_SERVER`) with first-class objects.

references.title: Upgrading Existing Applications to Symfony Flex
references.summary: Explains what Symfony Flex is, the directory structure it expects (assets/, bin/, config/, public/, src/, templates/, var/, vendor/, ...), and the manual steps to migrate a legacy symfony/symfony application to Flex. Flex is optional but strongly recommended for productivity.

references.title: Upgrading Existing Applications to Symfony Flex - Customizing Flex Paths
references.summary: Lists the directories that recipes assume by default — bin-dir, config-dir, src-dir, var-dir, public-dir — and shows how to override each one through the extra section of composer.json. Customizing these paths may require manual fixes to recipe-generated files that hardcode the original locations.

references.title: How to Create Symfony Flex Private Recipes
references.summary: Documents how Symfony Flex resolves recipes from the official symfony/recipes and symfony/recipes-contrib repositories, and how to publish private recipes through a private repository. Recipes describe how a package should be installed (files copied, env variables, configuration) when composer require is run.

references.title: Symfony Documentation
references.summary: The **Symfony documentation index** — entry point that organizes the docs into Getting Started, Configuration, Forms, Doctrine, Security, Cache, Console, HTTP Cache, Logging, Mailer, Notifier, Performance, Profiler, Sessions, Testing, Validation, plus the bundles and components reference. The page also links the Quick Tour and the Symfony Flex tutorials that drive the recipe-based workflow.

references.title: Flex: Compose your Application
references.summary: **Symfony Flex** is the Composer plugin that turns `composer require <alias>` into a fully-wired feature: it expands aliases (e.g. `mailer`, `orm`, `messenger`) to the right packages and applies a **recipe** that registers the bundle in `bundles.php`, copies default config under `config/packages/`, registers env vars in `.env`, and adds default routes. Recipes are tracked in `symfony.lock`.

references.title: Symfony Code License
references.summary: States that Symfony code is released under the MIT license, copyright Fabien Potencier. The MIT license grants permission to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, and sell copies of the software, subject to inclusion of the copyright and permission notice in all copies.

references.title: Coding Standards - License
references.summary: Notes that because Symfony is released under the MIT license, the license header block must be present at the top of every PHP file in the codebase, before the namespace declaration.

references.title: Symfony 8.0 LICENSE file (GitHub)
references.summary: Canonical LICENSE file shipped with the Symfony 8.0 source tree, containing the verbatim text of the MIT license that applies to all Symfony components, bridges and bundles. Copyright Fabien Potencier; permission to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense and sell copies is granted provided the copyright and permission notice is included.

references.title: The Components
references.summary: Index page of the Symfony Components documentation. Each Symfony component is a decoupled, reusable PHP library that can be installed via Composer (for example symfony/http-foundation, symfony/console) and used standalone or together as part of the Symfony framework.

references.title: The Contracts Component
references.summary: Describes Symfony Contracts as a set of small, domain-split PHP interfaces and traits extracted from Symfony components. Packages implementing a given contract advertise it via the symfony/*-implementation entry of their composer.json provide section, allowing loose coupling and interoperability across the PHP ecosystem.

references.title: Conventions
references.summary: Documents naming, CHANGELOG and deprecation conventions that apply uniformly to Symfony Components, Bridges and Bundles. Each Component, Bridge or Bundle keeps its own CHANGELOG.md at the root of its package describing changes per minor version.

references.title: Symfony Bridges (8.0 source)
references.summary: Source tree of the Symfony Bridge packages on the 8.0 branch. A Bridge integrates a Symfony component with a third-party library (for example Doctrine, Twig, Monolog, PsrHttpMessage), so each Bridge is shipped as its own Composer package such as symfony/doctrine-bridge or symfony/twig-bridge.

references.title: The PSR-7 Bridge
references.summary: Documents the Symfony PSR-7 Bridge, which converts HttpFoundation Request and Response objects to and from PSR-7 HTTP message objects. The bridge is installed as symfony/psr-http-message-bridge and requires a PSR-7/PSR-17 implementation such as nyholm/psr7 to create the interoperable message objects.

references.title: The Bundle System
references.summary: A **bundle** is Symfony's plugin-equivalent: every framework feature (`FrameworkBundle`, `SecurityBundle`, `DebugBundle`, …) ships as a bundle, and reusable third-party code is distributed the same way. Since Symfony 4 the recommendation is to **not** organize your own application as bundles — they exist to share code between projects. Bundles are enabled per environment via `config/bundles.php`.

references.title: Best Practices for Reusable Bundles
references.summary: Guidelines for shipping a **reusable bundle** — separate from application bundles. Covers naming, the recommended directory layout, semantic versioning, dependency management, configuration class, services and tests. Also defines what makes a bundle "reusable" (shareable across many applications) versus an internal application bundle.

references.title: How to Load Service Configuration inside a Bundle
references.summary: Explains how a bundle defines its own services rather than using the application's `config/services.yaml`. The bundle ships an `Extension` class (e.g. `AcmeHelloExtension`) whose `load()` method reads the bundle's configuration files and registers services into the container.

references.title: How to Simplify Configuration of Multiple Bundles
references.summary: Shows how a bundle's `Extension` can pre-configure **other bundles** by implementing `PrependExtensionInterface::prepend()`. This avoids forcing each user of a meta-bundle to configure its dependencies manually — the meta-bundle injects sensible defaults into other bundles' configuration namespaces.

references.title: The Asset Component
references.summary: The **Asset** component manages URL generation and **versioning** of web assets such as CSS, JavaScript and images. Versioning strategies (e.g. `StaticVersionStrategy`, `JsonManifestVersionStrategy`) append a fingerprint or read manifest entries; named "packages" let you mix several strategies (e.g. local files vs. CDN).

references.title: The Cache Component
references.summary: The **Cache** component implements both **PSR-6** (`CacheItemPoolInterface`) and the Symfony **Cache Contracts** (`CacheInterface`). It ships ready-to-use adapters for filesystem, Redis, APCu, Memcached, and PDO; supports tag-based invalidation, and provides cache-stampede protection via locking and early expiration.

references.title: The Config Component
references.summary: The **Config** component provides utilities to **define and validate configuration** trees. It supplies a `ConfigurationInterface`, a `TreeBuilder` DSL for declaring nodes, multiple loaders (YAML, XML, PHP, INI, directory) and resource trackers used by the kernel's container cache to invalidate when config files change.

references.title: The Console Component
references.summary: The **Console** component eases the creation of **command-line interfaces**. Around `Application` and `Command`, it provides input/output abstractions, helpers (progress bar, table, question, formatter), styling (`SymfonyStyle`), event hooks (`ConsoleEvents::COMMAND`, `TERMINATE`, `ERROR`, `SIGNAL`), and easily testable commands via `CommandTester`.

references.title: The CssSelector Component
references.summary: The **CssSelector** component converts CSS selectors (e.g. `div.menu > a:first-child`) into **XPath** expressions, so they can be used to query an XML/HTML document with PHP's native XPath engine (e.g. via `DomCrawler`).

references.title: The ExpressionLanguage Component
references.summary: The **ExpressionLanguage** component compiles and evaluates one-line **expressions** that return a value (booleans, numbers, strings, objects). It is used by Symfony for routing conditions, security expressions, validation expressions, and DI expression services. Custom functions can be registered through `ExpressionFunctionProvider` instances.

references.title: The Filesystem Component
references.summary: The **Filesystem** component provides cross-platform helpers for filesystem operations (`copy`, `mkdir`, `remove`, `chmod`, `mirror`, `tempnam`, `dumpFile`) and for **path manipulation** (`isAbsolutePath`, `makePathRelative`, `Path::normalize`). Methods raise `IOException` on failure for consistent error handling.

references.title: The Finder Component
references.summary: The **Finder** component locates files and directories using a **fluent interface** based on criteria such as name (`->name('*.php')`), depth (`->depth('< 3')`), file size (`->size('> 1K')`), modification time (`->date('since yesterday')`), and location. The result of `Finder::create()->in(...)` is an iterator yielding `SplFileInfo` objects (Symfony's extended subclass).

references.title: The Intl Component
references.summary: The **Intl** component exposes the **ICU** library's localization data (countries, currencies, languages, locales, scripts, timezones) through PHP classes. It serves as a fallback when the PHP `intl` extension is unavailable and powers form types like `CountryType`, `CurrencyType`, `LanguageType`, `LocaleType`, `TimezoneType`.

references.title: The Ldap Component
references.summary: The **Ldap** component connects to an **LDAP server** (OpenLDAP, Active Directory). It exposes a typed `Ldap` client wrapping `bind()`, `query()`, `add()`, `update()` and `delete()` operations and integrates with the Security component for LDAP authentication and user provisioning.

references.title: The Lock Component
references.summary: The **Lock** component provides advisory **locks** to grant exclusive access to a shared resource. Stores include `FlockStore` (filesystem flock), `RedisStore`, `MongoDbStore`, `PdoStore`, `SemaphoreStore`. Locks can be **blocking** or **non-blocking**, support **TTL with auto-release**, and may be **shared** (read) or **exclusive** (write).

references.title: The Mime Component
references.summary: The **Mime** component manipulates **MIME messages** (email envelopes, attachments, parts, embedded resources) and exposes utilities to detect, normalize and convert MIME types. It is the foundation that the Mailer component's `Email` and `RawMessage` classes are built on.

references.title: The PHPUnit Bridge
references.summary: The **PHPUnit Bridge** reports usage of **deprecated** code during the test run, fails the suite when a deprecation count threshold is exceeded (`SYMFONY_DEPRECATIONS_HELPER`), and provides helpers for **mocking time, DNS** and **`class_exists`** calls. It is published as `symfony/phpunit-bridge` and ships its own `simple-phpunit` runner.

references.title: The Process Component
references.summary: The **Process** component executes external commands in **sub-processes**. `Process` (synchronous), `Process::start()` (asynchronous with iteration over output streams) and `Process::fromShellCommandline()` (only when shell expansion is required) are the main entry points. STDIN, output buffering, environment variables and timeouts are configurable; `getOutput()`/`getErrorOutput()` expose the captured streams.

references.title: The PropertyAccess Component
references.summary: The **PropertyAccess** component reads from and writes to objects/arrays using a **string property path** (e.g. `address.zipCode`, `[items][0].name`). It looks up public properties, getters/setters/issers/hassers, magic accessors (`__get`/`__set`), and array offsets, and powers form data binding and the `Expression` validation constraint.

references.title: The PropertyInfo Component
references.summary: The **PropertyInfo** component returns metadata about an object's properties — list, type, description, read/write visibility — drawn from multiple sources: PHP attributes, PHPDoc, Doctrine, Reflection, and Serializer metadata. It powers the Form and Serializer components when they need to know property types automatically.

references.title: The Runtime Component
references.summary: The **Runtime** component decouples application **bootstrapping** from any global state, so the same `public/index.php` can run under PHP-FPM, Swoole, FrankenPHP, ReactPHP, RoadRunner, etc. Custom runtimes implement `RunnerInterface`; the `APP_RUNTIME` env var or `extra.runtime.class` in `composer.json` selects which one boots the application.

references.title: The Semaphore Component
references.summary: The **Semaphore** component manages **counting semaphores**, a generalisation of locks that allows up to *N* concurrent holders rather than just one. Stores include `RedisStore`, `MongoDbStore`, etc.; like the Lock component it supports TTL and automatic release, and is useful for capping concurrency to an external resource.

references.title: The UID Component
references.summary: The **UID** component provides PHP value objects for unique identifiers, namely **UUID** (`Uuid`, `UuidV1`/`V3`/`V4`/`V5`/`V6`/`V7`/`V8`) and **ULID** (`Ulid`). It includes encoders to base32/58/RFC 4122 string representations and custom Doctrine types so UIDs can be persisted as binary or string columns.

references.title: How to Install and Use the Symfony Components
references.summary: Walks through using a single Symfony component **outside** the full framework — Composer install (`composer require symfony/finder`), manual autoloading via `vendor/autoload.php`, and direct instantiation (`Finder::create()`). Useful when integrating one component into a non-Symfony codebase.

references.title: Loading Resources
references.summary: Explains how the **Validator** component loads constraint metadata from external **resources** — annotations/attributes (`AttributeLoader`), XML files (`XmlFileLoader`), YAML files (`YamlFileLoader`) — through `ValidatorBuilder`. Multiple loaders can be chained via `LoaderChain`.

references.title: The VarExporter Component
references.summary: The **VarExporter** component exports any **serializable** PHP value to plain PHP source code via `VarExporter::export()`. Unlike `serialize()`, the output is human-readable and instantiable without a `unserialize()` call. It also instantiates and populates objects **without invoking their constructor** (`Instantiator::instantiate()`) and supports **lazy ghost** and **lazy proxy** objects since 6.2/7.0.

references.title: The Workflow Component
references.summary: The **Workflow** component provides finite state machines and workflows for any PHP object. It distinguishes **state machines** (a single state at a time) from **workflows** (multiple "places" in parallel via Petri nets). Transitions can be guarded by events (`workflow.<name>.guard.<transition>`), audited via the marking store, and visualised through `bin/console workflow:dump`.

references.title: The Yaml Component
references.summary: The **Yaml** component parses YAML strings into PHP arrays (`Yaml::parse()`, `Yaml::parseFile()`) and dumps PHP arrays back into YAML (`Yaml::dump()`). Parser flags `Yaml::PARSE_OBJECT_FOR_MAP`, `PARSE_DATETIME`, `PARSE_CUSTOM_TAGS`, `PARSE_CONSTANT` opt-in to richer PHP types; dumper flags such as `DUMP_OBJECT_AS_MAP` mirror them.

references.title: Pushing Data to Clients Using the Mercure Protocol
references.summary: **Mercure** is an open protocol — built on Server-Sent Events — for pushing real-time updates from server to client. The MercureBundle integrates a `HubInterface`, a JWT-based publisher and a Twig helper. Clients subscribe via an `EventSource` to a topic URL; the server publishes `Update` messages through `$hub->publish(...)`.

references.title: Creating and Sending Notifications
references.summary: The **Notifier** component is an abstraction over many delivery channels — SMS, chat (Slack, Discord, Teams, …), email (via the Mailer bridge), push and browser notifications. A `Notification` is dispatched to one or more `Recipient`s through registered transports identified by DSNs (`twilio://`, `slack://`, etc.).

references.title: Rate Limiter
references.summary: A **rate limiter** controls how often an event (HTTP request, login attempt, expensive job) may happen. Symfony ships three policies — **fixed window**, **sliding window** and **token bucket** — each with different burst/precision trade-offs. Limiters are configured under `framework.rate_limiter` and consumed via `RateLimiterFactory::create($key)`.

references.title: Asset Preloading and Resource Hints with WebLink
references.summary: The **WebLink** component manages **`Link` HTTP headers** (RFC 5988) used by browsers and HTTP/2 servers for **preload**, **preconnect**, **dns-prefetch**, **prefetch**, and **prerender** resource hints. It exposes Twig helpers (`{{ preload(...) }}`, `{{ preconnect(...) }}`, …) so templates can declare hints inline; the bundle's response listener writes the matching `Link` header.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Use the Default Directory Structure
references.summary: Recommends following Symfony's default flat directory structure (assets/, bin/, config/, migrations/, public/, src/, templates/, tests/, translations/, var/, vendor/) unless a development practice imposes another layout. The structure is self-explanatory and not coupled to Symfony.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Don't Create any Bundle to Organize your Application Logic
references.summary: Recommends not creating bundles to divide application code (UserBundle, ProductBundle, ...). A bundle is meant to be a reusable stand-alone piece of software; for application code use PHP namespaces under the App\ namespace inside src/.

references.title: How to Override the Default Directory Structure
references.summary: Explains how to change the default location of the cache, log, configuration and public directories of a Symfony application by overriding methods on the Kernel (for example getCacheDir(), getLogDir()) or via the extra.public-dir composer.json setting. Useful when deployment constraints conflict with Symfony defaults.

references.title: The Architecture
references.summary: Quick-tour article that explains the **canonical Symfony directory layout** — `bin/`, `config/`, `public/`, `src/`, `templates/`, `tests/`, `translations/`, `var/`, `vendor/` — and how the kernel boots from `public/index.php` through `Kernel`, `Container`, then to controllers via the routing/firewall/argument-resolver pipeline.

references.title: Installing & Setting up the Symfony Framework
references.summary: How to bootstrap a new Symfony project — install the **Symfony CLI** (`symfony new`), check requirements (`symfony check:requirements`), run the local server (`symfony server:start`), and set up Docker, TLS, and IDE integration. The page also documents the project structure that `symfony new` produces and the difference between the *web app* skeleton and the minimalist version.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component
references.summary: Describes the HttpKernel component as a structured process that converts a Request into a Response through a chain of events (kernel.request, kernel.controller, kernel.controller_arguments, kernel.view, kernel.response, kernel.terminate, kernel.exception). It is flexible enough to power both the Symfony framework and third-party systems such as Drupal.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component - The Request-Response Lifecycle
references.summary: Explains the simplified request-response lifecycle: the user asks for a resource, the browser sends a Request to the server, Symfony hands the application a Request object, the application returns a Response, and the browser displays the result. The HttpKernelInterface::handle() method formalizes this process.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component - Sub Requests
references.summary: Documents sub-requests issued via HttpKernel::handle($request, HttpKernelInterface::SUB_REQUEST). Sub-requests run a separate Request through the lifecycle (typically to render a fragment); listeners can call KernelEvent::isMainRequest() to skip work that should only run for the main request.

references.title: Understanding how the Front Controller, Kernel and Environments Work together
references.summary: Explains how every web request passes through the public/index.php front controller, which creates the Kernel (typically extending Symfony\Component\HttpKernel\Kernel and using MicroKernelTrait) and calls handle(). Documents the role of the environment name and debug flag passed to the Kernel constructor.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component - Handling Exceptions: the kernel.exception Event
references.summary: Explains that the body of HttpKernel::handle() is wrapped in a try/catch and that any thrown exception triggers the kernel.exception event. Listeners receive an ExceptionEvent, can call getThrowable() to inspect the exception and setResponse() to convert it into a proper Response (including 4xx/5xx error pages).

references.title: Built-in Symfony Events - kernel.exception
references.summary: Reference for the kernel.exception event (ExceptionEvent class). Documents the rules Symfony uses to derive the HTTP status code: the response's own client-error/server-error/redirect status, HttpExceptionInterface::getStatusCode() and headers, and otherwise 500. Also documents allowCustomResponseCode() to override the derived status.

references.title: Events and Event Listeners
references.summary: Demonstrates how to write a kernel.exception listener (or subscriber) that builds a custom Response. If the exception implements HttpExceptionInterface, the listener should reuse getStatusCode() and getHeaders(); otherwise it must default to a 500 status code. Listeners are registered with the kernel.event_listener tag.

references.title: The EventDispatcher Component
references.summary: Describes EventDispatcher as an implementation of the Mediator and Observer patterns: listeners (PHP callables) register with a central dispatcher to be notified when an event is dispatched. addListener() takes the event name, the callable, and an optional priority (higher runs earlier).

references.title: The EventDispatcher Component - Using Event Subscribers
references.summary: Documents EventSubscriberInterface, whose static getSubscribedEvents() method returns an array indexed by event name pointing to the method (or [method, priority]) that should handle each event. A subscriber registers itself for several events at once via addSubscriber().

references.title: The EventDispatcher Component - Stopping Event Flow/Propagation
references.summary: Explains how a listener can call Event::stopPropagation() to prevent any remaining listeners from being called for the current event, and how Event::isPropagationStopped() can be checked after a dispatch to detect that situation.

references.title: Events and Event Listeners
references.summary: Explains Symfony-specific event listener registration via the kernel.event_listener tag, and the rules Symfony uses to pick the listener method: explicit method attribute, then __invoke(), otherwise an exception. Also documents the priority attribute used to order listeners.

references.title: Built-in Symfony Events
references.summary: Reference for the built-in HttpKernel events: kernel.request, kernel.controller, kernel.controller_arguments, kernel.view, kernel.response, kernel.finish_request, kernel.terminate, kernel.exception. Each event ships its own KernelEvent subclass and can be inspected with `php bin/console debug:event-dispatcher <event>`.

references.title: The Generic Event Object
references.summary: The base `Event` class deliberately exposes no payload, encouraging applications to write **typed event subclasses**. As a quick alternative, `GenericEvent` carries a `subject`, an associative `arguments` map, and array-access to those arguments, making it convenient for one-off events or third-party libraries that don't ship dedicated event classes.

references.title: The Immutable Event Dispatcher
references.summary: The **`ImmutableEventDispatcher`** wraps another dispatcher to forbid registering or removing listeners and subscribers at runtime. It is useful in long-running workers / library APIs where the event-listener graph must be **frozen** to prevent late mutations.

references.title: The Traceable Event Dispatcher
references.summary: The **`TraceableEventDispatcher`** wraps another dispatcher and records, for each dispatched event, which listeners were called, in what order, and how long they took (via `Stopwatch`). It powers the WebProfiler's **Events** panel.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices
references.summary: Describes the official Symfony best practices for developing web applications, covering project creation, configuration, business logic, controllers, templates, forms, internationalization, security, web assets and tests. The article notes the recommendations are a starting point and Symfony is flexible enough to accommodate other approaches.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Use Environment Variables for Infrastructure Configuration
references.summary: Recommends using env vars (with multiple .env files per environment) for values that change between machines without altering application behavior, and using the secrets system for sensitive infrastructure credentials. Application-behavior options should be parameters instead of env vars.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Make your Controller Extend the AbstractController Base Controller
references.summary: Recommends extending Symfony\Bundle\FrameworkBundle\Controller\AbstractController to gain shortcut methods for templates, security, etc. The coupling is acceptable because controllers should only contain glue code, not business logic. Services must be obtained via dependency injection rather than $this->container->get().

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Use Attributes to Configure Routing, Caching, and Security
references.summary: Recommends configuring routing, HTTP caching and security via PHP attributes on controller actions to keep configuration close to the code it affects and avoid juggling YAML/PHP config files. Single-format configuration also simplifies discoverability.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Define a Single Firewall
references.summary: Recommends defining only one firewall under security.firewalls unless an application legitimately exposes two distinct authentication systems (for example form login for the main site and a token system for an API). A single firewall keeps the security configuration simple.

references.title: Our Backward Compatibility Promise
references.summary: States that Symfony follows Semantic Versioning: only major releases (5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0) are allowed to break backward compatibility, while minor releases (5.1, 5.2, ...) may add features but not break the public API. Experimental features and code marked @internal are excluded from the BC promise, and BC breaks are tolerated to fix security issues.

references.title: Our Backward Compatibility Promise - Using our Interfaces
references.summary: Documents which uses of Symfony interfaces are covered by BC: type-hinting, calling declared methods, implementing the interface, adding arguments or default values to implemented methods, and adding return types. Interfaces tagged @internal are excluded.

references.title: Our Backward Compatibility Promise - Using our Classes
references.summary: Documents which uses of Symfony classes are covered by BC: type-hinting, instantiating, extending, accessing public properties, calling public methods, and overriding public/protected members. Classes/properties/methods tagged @internal and code under *\Tests\ namespaces are excluded.

references.title: Our Backward Compatibility Promise - Making Code Changes in a Backward Compatible Way
references.summary: Documents the multi-step procedure for adding a new argument to a public method without breaking BC: declare the argument as a comment in the signature, fetch it via func_num_args()/func_get_arg(), trigger a deprecation through trigger_deprecation(), then enable the argument for real in the next major version.

references.title: Experimental Features
references.summary: Defines experimental features as features that are explicitly excluded from Symfony's BC promise so the team can iterate on their design across minor versions. Experimental features are clearly marked in the documentation; relying on them means accepting potential breaks in any subsequent minor release.

references.title: Conventions - Deprecating Code
references.summary: Documents the rules for deprecating Symfony APIs: a deprecation must be introduced on the next minor version of the impacted package, marked with a @deprecated PHPDoc tag stating the version and replacement, and trigger a runtime notice via trigger_deprecation('symfony/<package>', 'X.Y', 'message'). New classes/methods cannot be introduced as already deprecated.

references.title: Conventions - Removing Deprecated Code
references.summary: States that deprecated code may only be removed on the next major version of the impacted component (every two years). The removal must be documented in the component's CHANGELOG.md inside the major version section, in the same pull request that performs the removal.

references.title: symfony/deprecation-contracts (GitHub)
references.summary: Source repository of the symfony/deprecation-contracts package, a tiny library that provides the trigger_deprecation() function. Symfony components depend on this package to emit deprecation notices in a single, consistent way that bridges to PHP's E_USER_DEPRECATED error.

references.title: How to Override any Part of a Bundle
references.summary: Documents how to customize third-party bundles: override templates from <project>/templates/bundles/<BundleName>/ (using the @! prefix to extend the original), override controllers via service decoration, modify services through compiler passes, override entity mappings via mapped superclasses, override translations from the project translations/ directory, and bypass routing by importing your own copy.

references.title: How to Load Service Configuration inside a Bundle
references.summary: Explains the two mechanisms a bundle uses to load its services and process its configuration: implementing loadExtension() on a class extending AbstractBundle (recommended for new bundles) or providing a separate Extension class in the bundle's DependencyInjection namespace (legacy). Bundle DI aliases default to the underscored bundle name without the Extension suffix.

references.title: How to Simplify Configuration of Multiple Bundles
references.summary: Documents PrependExtensionInterface (and the prependExtensionConfig() / prepend() helpers on AbstractBundle), which let one bundle inject configuration into another bundle before that bundle's own load()/loadExtension() runs. Used to share or default configuration values across multiple bundles.

references.title: How to Override the Default Directory Structure
references.summary: Documents how a Symfony application can override the default cache, log, build, public and configuration directories by implementing methods on the Kernel class such as getCacheDir(), getLogDir() and getBuildDir(), or via composer.json extra entries like public-dir.

references.title: The Release Process
references.summary: Documents Symfony's time-based release schedule: a new patch version every month, a new minor version every six months (May and November) without breaking changes, and a new major version every two years in November of odd years. Each minor version's six-month cycle is split into four months of development and two months of stabilization.

references.title: The Release Process - Maintenance
references.summary: Explains that each branch is limited to five minor versions (X.0 through X.4); the last minor (e.g. 5.4, 6.4) is a long-term-support (LTS) version maintained for 3 years of bug fixes and 4 years of security fixes, while standard versions receive 8 months of bug fixes and 8 months of security fixes.

references.title: The Release Process - Backward Compatibility
references.summary: Explains that the BC promise drives a parallel-development process for major versions: while preparing a new major (e.g. 6.0), Symfony also continues to develop the previous LTS (5.4) with the same features but keeping the deprecated APIs, so applications can fix deprecations on the LTS and upgrade to the major without effort.

references.title: Maintenance
references.summary: Defines what is and is not allowed in a patch release: bug fixes, support for newer PHP/OS versions, translations, ICU updates and tests are accepted; new features, new classes, new public/protected methods, new configuration options, new deprecations and BC breaks are rejected (BC breaks are only tolerated to fix security issues).

references.title: The Contracts Component
references.summary: Documents Symfony Contracts as a set of small abstractions extracted from Symfony components (cache, event-dispatcher, http-client, service, translation, deprecation). Contracts focus on interoperability and may be implemented by any package; when applicable they build on top of PHP-FIG's PSRs while serving Symfony-specific use cases.

references.title: The DependencyInjection Component
references.summary: Describes the Symfony service container as a PSR-11 (Container Interface) compatible implementation. Any code that depends only on Psr\Container\ContainerInterface can use Symfony's container or any other PSR-11 implementation interchangeably.

references.title: Coding Standards
references.summary: States that Symfony's coding standards are based on PSR-1, PSR-2, PSR-4 and PSR-12 from PHP-FIG. The recommended way to apply them is the PHP CS Fixer tool, which automatically fixes any violations detected in contributed code.

references.title: HTTP Client - PSR-18 and PSR-17
references.summary: Documents how Symfony's HttpClient bridges to PSR-18 (HTTP Client) and PSR-17 (HTTP Factories) via the Symfony\Component\HttpClient\Psr18Client class. This adapter exposes a Symfony HttpClientInterface implementation as a PSR-18 ClientInterface and uses PSR-17 factories to create PSR-7 messages.

references.title: PSRs - PHP-FIG
references.summary: PHP-FIG (the PHP Framework Interop Group) publishes PHP Standards Recommendations (PSRs) that allow PHP frameworks and libraries to interoperate. Relevant PSRs for Symfony include PSR-1/PSR-12 coding style, PSR-3 logger interface, PSR-4 autoloading, PSR-6/PSR-16 caching, PSR-7/PSR-15/PSR-17/PSR-18 HTTP messages and clients, and PSR-11 container interface.

references.title: Adapters For Interoperability between PSR-6 and PSR-16 Cache
references.summary: The **Cache** component bridges **PSR-6** (`CacheItemPoolInterface`) and **PSR-16** (`SimpleCacheInterface`) in both directions. `Psr16Cache` wraps a PSR-6 pool to expose the simpler PSR-16 API; `Psr6Cache` (a.k.a. `SimpleCacheAdapter`) wraps a PSR-16 cache to expose the richer PSR-6 API.

references.title: The PSR-7 Bridge
references.summary: The **PSR-7 Bridge** (`symfony/psr-http-message-bridge`) converts between Symfony `Request`/`Response` and **PSR-7** message objects (`RequestInterface`, `ResponseInterface`, `ServerRequestInterface`, `StreamInterface`). It depends on a PSR-17 factory implementation to produce concrete PSR-7 instances.

references.title: Coding Standards - Naming Conventions
references.summary: Documents Symfony's identifier naming rules: camelCase for variables/functions/methods, snake_case for configuration parameters/route names/Twig variables, SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE for constants, UpperCamelCase for class/interface/trait/enum names and enum cases, Interface and Trait suffixes, the Abstract prefix for abstract classes, and the Exception suffix for exception classes.

references.title: Coding Standards - Service Naming Conventions
references.summary: States that a service id must be the FQCN of the service class (e.g. App\EventSubscriber\UserSubscriber). When several services share a class, the FQCN identifies the main one and the rest use lowercase, underscored names optionally grouped with dots (something.service_name). Public services should also receive an FQCN-to-id alias.

references.title: Conventions - Naming a Method
references.summary: Documents the canonical method names used when an object has a single "main" relation with related items: get(), set(), has(), all(), replace(), remove(), clear(), isEmpty(), add(), register(), count(), keys(). Methods for non-main relations append the related thing's name (getXXX(), addXXX(), removeXXX()).

references.title: Conventions - Naming Commands and Options
references.summary: States that console commands and their options must be named and described using the English imperative mood (run, list, create) rather than the third-person indicative (runs, lists, creates), to remain concise and consistent with traditional Unix command-line interfaces.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component
references.summary: Overview of the HttpKernel component, which provides a structured process for converting a `Request` into a `Response` using the EventDispatcher. Explains that this component is the heart of the Symfony Framework and other PHP applications/CMSs.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component - The Request-Response Lifecycle
references.summary: Documents the `HttpKernelInterface::handle()` method that turns a `Request` into a `Response`, including its three arguments (the request, the request type with `MAIN_REQUEST`/`SUB_REQUEST` constants, and the `$catch` flag) and its overall flow.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component - HttpKernel: Driven by Events
references.summary: Describes the eight kernel events dispatched by `HttpKernel::handle()`: `kernel.request`, `kernel.controller`, `kernel.controller_arguments`, `kernel.view`, `kernel.response`, `kernel.finish_request`, `kernel.terminate`, and `kernel.exception`. Each event is mapped to its `KernelEvents` constant and the dedicated event class passed to listeners.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component - Sub Requests
references.summary: Explains how to dispatch sub-requests through `HttpKernel::handle()` with `HttpKernelInterface::SUB_REQUEST` and how listeners can use `KernelEvent::isMainRequest()` to act only on the main request. Also notes the `_format` request attribute defaults to `html`.

references.title: Controller (Symfony Framework)
references.summary: Introduces controllers in Symfony as PHP callables that read information from the `Request` object and return a `Response`. This page is the canonical FrameworkBundle entry point for everything controllers can do (rendering, redirects, sessions, flash, JSON, files, SSE, etc.).

references.title: How to Define Controllers as Services
references.summary: Explains how FrameworkBundle registers controllers as services. Three opt-in mechanisms (`#[Route]`, `#[AsController]`, the `controller.service_arguments` tag) all apply the same tag, marking the service public and non-lazy and enabling action-argument injection.

references.title: Controller - A Basic Controller
references.summary: Shows the conventional layout of a Symfony controller: classes live under `App\Controller`, the file matches the class name (`LuckyController.php`), and the class name is suffixed with `Controller` by convention. Action methods are public methods that return a `Response`.

references.title: The Symfony Framework Best Practices - Controllers
references.summary: Official best practices for controllers. Recommends extending `AbstractController`, configuring routing/caching/security with attributes, using dependency injection rather than service locators for non-helper services, and keeping controllers thin so they only contain glue code.

references.title: Best Practices - Make your Controller Extend the AbstractController Base Controller
references.summary: Explains why the recommended Symfony naming/structure rule for controllers is to extend `Symfony\Bundle\FrameworkBundle\Controller\AbstractController`. The coupling is acceptable because controllers should not contain business logic.

references.title: How to Define Controllers as Services - Using the #[Route] Attribute
references.summary: Documents that placing `#[Route]` on any PHP class automatically tags it as a controller service. The recommended controller service id is the controller's fully-qualified class name (FQCN), referenced in routes as `App\Controller\HelloController::index`.

references.title: How to Define Controllers as Services - Invokable Controllers
references.summary: Describes the single-action controller convention: a class that exposes a single `__invoke()` method, often used with the ADR (Action-Domain-Responder) pattern. When the class is registered as a service, `__invoke()` benefits from action-argument injection like any other method.

references.title: Controller - The Base Controller Class & Services
references.summary: Introduces `Symfony\Bundle\FrameworkBundle\Controller\AbstractController` as the optional base class that grants helper methods such as `render()`, `redirectToRoute()`, `generateUrl()`, `json()`, `file()`, `addFlash()`, `createNotFoundException()`, and `getParameter()`.

references.title: Controller - Decoupling Controllers from Symfony
references.summary: Documents the `Symfony\Bundle\FrameworkBundle\Controller\ControllerHelper` class, which exposes every `AbstractController` helper as a public method. Combined with `#[AutowireMethodOf]`, it lets you use those helpers without extending `AbstractController`.

references.title: How to Define Controllers as Services - Alternatives to base Controller Methods
references.summary: Explains how to replace `AbstractController` shortcuts with direct service injection (e.g., injecting `Twig\Environment` instead of using `$this->render()`). The page points to `getSubscribedServices()` in `AbstractController` to know which services back each helper.

references.title: Best Practices - Make your Controller Extend the AbstractController Base Controller
references.summary: Best practice recommendation: most application controllers should extend `AbstractController` to use its shortcuts. The slight coupling is acceptable because controllers should remain thin glue code.

references.title: Controller - Rendering Templates
references.summary: Documents the `AbstractController::render()` helper for HTML responses. The helper renders a Twig template and wraps the rendered content in a `Response` object.

references.title: Controller - Accessing Configuration Values
references.summary: Explains how controllers that extend `AbstractController` can read container parameters through `getParameter()`. The example uses `kernel.project_dir` to build an application path.

references.title: Controller - The Request object as a Controller Argument
references.summary: Explains how Symfony injects the `Symfony\Component\HttpFoundation\Request` into a controller when the action argument is type-hinted with `Request`. Once injected, you can call methods like `$request->query->get()` to read query parameters.

references.title: Controller - The Request and Response Object
references.summary: Lists common operations on the injected `Request`: `query`, `request`/`getPayload()`, `server`, `files`, `cookies`, `headers` parameter bags, plus helpers like `isXmlHttpRequest()` and `getPreferredLanguage()`. Header names are normalized (case- and `-`/`_`-insensitive).

references.title: Controller - Automatic Mapping Of The Request
references.summary: Introduces the four mapping attributes shipped by HttpKernel: `#[MapQueryParameter]`, `#[MapQueryString]`, `#[MapRequestPayload]`, and `#[MapUploadedFile]`. They populate controller arguments directly from the request so that controllers do not need to read the `Request` object manually.

references.title: Controller - Mapping Query Parameters Individually
references.summary: Describes `#[MapQueryParameter]` which fills one controller argument from a single query parameter. Supports `\BackedEnum`, `array`, `bool`, `float`, `int`, `string`, and `AbstractUid` subclasses, and accepts an optional `filter` (PHP `FILTER_VALIDATE_*`) plus `options`.

references.title: Controller - Mapping The Whole Query String
references.summary: Documents `#[MapQueryString]` which maps the entire query string into a DTO and validates it. Default failure status code is 404; this can be customized with `validationGroups` and `validationFailedStatusCode`. The `key` option targets a nested query subarray.

references.title: Controller - Mapping Request Payload
references.summary: Documents `#[MapRequestPayload]` which deserializes the request body (JSON, XML, etc.) into a DTO. The default validation-failed status code is 422 (`HTTP_UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY`); options include `acceptFormat`, `serializationContext`, custom resolver, and `type` for nested DTO arrays.

references.title: Controller - The Request and Response Object
references.summary: States that a Symfony controller is required to return a `Response` object. The `Response` exposes a public `headers` property of type `ResponseHeaderBag` for normalized header access. Constants such as `Response::HTTP_OK` should be preferred over hard-coded numeric status codes.

references.title: Controller - Returning JSON Response
references.summary: Documents `AbstractController::json()` which returns a `JsonResponse`. Signature: `json($data, $status = 200, $headers = [], $context = [])`. If the Serializer is enabled it is used to encode the data, otherwise `json_encode()` is used.

references.title: Controller - Streaming File Responses
references.summary: Documents `AbstractController::file()` which returns a `BinaryFileResponse`. Optional arguments allow renaming the downloaded file and selecting `ResponseHeaderBag::DISPOSITION_INLINE` to display the file in the browser instead of downloading it.

references.title: Controller - Sending Early Hints
references.summary: Briefly explains that controllers can send HTTP `103 Early Hints` responses to ask the browser to start downloading assets before the full response is ready. This is part of Symfony's WebLink integration.

references.title: Controller - Streaming Server-Sent Events
references.summary: Documents `EventStreamResponse` and `ServerEvent`. The response automatically sets `Content-Type: text/event-stream`, `Cache-Control: no-cache`, and `Connection: keep-alive`. `ServerEvent` accepts `data`, `type`, `id`, `retry`, and `comment` per the WHATWG SSE specification.

references.title: Controller - The Request and Response Object
references.summary: Shows that incoming cookies are read in a controller through the injected `Request` via `$request->cookies->get('PHPSESSID')`. The `cookies` ParameterBag exposes the request cookies just like other parameter bags.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Setting Cookies
references.summary: Explains how a controller attaches or removes cookies on the outgoing response via `$response->headers->setCookie(Cookie::create(...))` and `$response->headers->clearCookie('cookie_name')`. Covers `Cookie::create()`, the immutable `with*()` builders, and `Cookie::fromString()`.

references.title: Set-Cookie header (MDN)
references.summary: Reference for the `Set-Cookie` response header used when controllers attach cookies. Lists the supported attributes (`Expires`, `Max-Age`, `Domain`, `Path`, `Secure`, `HttpOnly`, `SameSite`, `Partitioned`) and their interactions (e.g. `SameSite=None` requires `Secure`).

references.title: Controller - Managing the Session
references.summary: Explains how to access the session inside a controller. The controller obtains the session through the injected `Request` (`$request->getSession()`) and reads/writes attributes via `$session->set()` and `$session->get()`. In services use the `RequestStack` instead.

references.title: Session - Basic Usage
references.summary: Documents the recommended way to obtain the session in a controller: type-hint a `SessionInterface` argument or call `$request->getSession()`. Sessions auto-start on first read/write and expose the `set()`, `get()`, `has()`, `remove()`, and `clear()` API.

references.title: Session - Session Attributes
references.summary: Describes how session attributes are read and written. Symfony's session uses an `AttributeBag` accessible through methods like `set()`/`get()`/`all()` directly on the session, providing a key-value namespace per user.

references.title: Controller - Flash Messages
references.summary: Explains flash messages: special session messages meant to be used exactly once that disappear from the session as soon as they are retrieved. The `AbstractController::addFlash($type, $message)` shortcut is equivalent to `$request->getSession()->getFlashBag()->add($type, $message)`.

references.title: Session - Flash Messages
references.summary: Documents the FlashBag API: `add()` to push a flash, `get($type)` to consume and remove flashes of a type, and `peek()` to read without removing. Flashes are typically rendered after a redirect (Post/Redirect/Get pattern) and consumed in the next response.

references.title: Controller - Redirecting
references.summary: Documents the two redirect helpers on `AbstractController`: `redirectToRoute($route, $params = [], $status = 302)` returns a `RedirectResponse` to a named route and `redirect($url, $status = 302)` redirects to an arbitrary URL. Permanent redirects pass `301` or `Response::HTTP_MOVED_PERMANENTLY`. The page warns that `redirect()` does not validate its destination, so user-supplied URLs may cause unvalidated-redirect vulnerabilities.

references.title: The HttpFoundation Component - Redirecting the User
references.summary: Documents `Symfony\Component\HttpFoundation\RedirectResponse`. Constructing `new RedirectResponse($url)` produces a 302 response with a `Location` header. The status code can be customized with the second argument.

references.title: How to Forward Requests to another Controller
references.summary: Explains `AbstractController::forward($controller, $attributes = [], $query = [])` which performs an internal sub-request to another controller instead of redirecting the browser. The helper returns the `Response` produced by the target controller; argument matching is done by name.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component - Sub Requests
references.summary: Describes how internal forwarding works under the hood: `HttpKernel::handle()` is called with `HttpKernelInterface::SUB_REQUEST` to run another full request lifecycle. Listeners can detect sub-requests with `KernelEvent::isMainRequest()`.

references.title: Controller - Managing Errors and 404 Pages
references.summary: Explains how to trigger a 404 from a controller. `AbstractController::createNotFoundException($message)` is a shortcut that returns a `NotFoundHttpException`, which must be thrown. Throwing any `HttpException` lets Symfony pick the matching status code; other exceptions yield a 500.

references.title: How to Customize Error Pages
references.summary: Lists the four ways to customize production error pages in Symfony: override default Twig error templates, create a serializer normalizer for non-HTML output, override the default `ErrorController` via `framework.error_controller`, or write a `kernel.exception` listener for full control.

references.title: How to Customize Error Pages - Overriding the Default Error Templates
references.summary: Documents the template lookup performed by `TwigErrorRenderer`: it looks for a status-specific template like `error404.html.twig` and falls back to a generic `error.html.twig`. Templates live under `templates/bundles/TwigBundle/Exception/` and receive `status_code`, `status_text`, and `exception`.

references.title: How to Customize Error Pages - Overriding the Default ErrorController
references.summary: Explains how to point `framework.error_controller` to a custom controller. The `ErrorListener` (subscribed to `kernel.exception`) builds the sub-request and passes `exception` (a `Throwable`) and `logger` (a `DebugLoggerInterface`) parameters.

references.title: How to Customize Error Pages - Testing Error Pages during Development
references.summary: Shows how to preview production-style error pages in the development environment. FrameworkBundle's special `_errors` routes can be loaded under the `/_error` prefix and then visited as `/_error/{statusCode}` or `/_error/{statusCode}.{format}`.

references.title: How to Customize Error Pages - Overriding Error output for non-HTML formats
references.summary: Explains how to customize JSON, XML, CSV, YAML or other non-HTML error output. The Serializer component provides a `FlattenException` normalizer, and applications can add their own normalizer for that input.

references.title: How to Upload Files
references.summary: Step-by-step controller-side recipe for handling form-uploaded files. Shows reading an `UploadedFile` from the form (`$form->get('brochure')->getData()`), generating a safe filename with `SluggerInterface::slug()` plus `uniqid()` and `UploadedFile::guessExtension()`, and moving the file with `UploadedFile::move()` inside a try/catch on `FileException`.

references.title: Controller - Mapping Uploaded Files
references.summary: Documents the `#[MapUploadedFile]` attribute. By default it injects an `UploadedFile` matched by argument name and throws an `HttpException` if no file is sent (use a nullable type to opt out). Constraints such as `Assert\File` and `Assert\Image` are validated before injection; the `name` option renames the field, and array/variadic types accept multiple files.

references.title: How to Upload Files - Uploading Multiple Files
references.summary: Explains how to upload several files at once: set the `multiple` option of `FileType` to `true` and wrap the `File` constraint in `Assert\All` to validate every uploaded file. Inside the controller the field returns an array of `UploadedFile` instances.

references.title: How to Upload Files - Creating an Uploader Service
references.summary: Recommends extracting the upload logic into a dedicated `FileUploader` service that depends on `SluggerInterface` and a target directory, so controllers stay thin. Lists `FileException` subclasses (`IniSizeFileException`, `FormSizeFileException`, `PartialFileException`, `NoFileException`, etc.) that signal specific upload failures.

references.title: Routing - Redirecting to URLs and Routes Directly from a Route
references.summary: Documents `Symfony\Bundle\FrameworkBundle\Controller\RedirectController`, a built-in invokable-style controller that emits a redirect response straight from a route definition. Useful to redirect from a path or to a named route without writing a controller class.

references.title: Templates - Rendering a Template Directly from a Route
references.summary: Documents `Symfony\Bundle\FrameworkBundle\Controller\TemplateController`, a built-in controller that renders a Twig template referenced directly from a route. It is automatically allowed by the controller allowlist.

references.title: How to Define Controllers as Services - Controller Allowlist
references.summary: Describes the controller allowlist used when Symfony needs to verify a controller's legitimacy (e.g., ESI fragments). Automatically allowed controllers are: classes using `#[AsController]`, classes extending `AbstractController`, the built-in `TemplateController`, and any service tagged with `controller.service_arguments`.

references.title: Extending Action Argument Resolving
references.summary: Explains that controller arguments are resolved by `Symfony\Component\HttpKernel\Controller\ArgumentResolver`, which delegates to services implementing `ValueResolverInterface`. You can extend resolution by adding your own resolvers.

references.title: Extending Action Argument Resolving - Built-In Value Resolvers
references.summary: Lists the built-in value resolvers shipped by HttpKernel: `BackedEnumValueResolver`, `RequestPayloadValueResolver`, `RequestAttributeValueResolver`, `DateTimeValueResolver`, `RequestValueResolver`, `ServiceValueResolver`, `SessionValueResolver`, `DefaultValueResolver`, `UidValueResolver`, and `VariadicValueResolver`. Security adds `UserValueResolver` and `SecurityTokenValueResolver`; Doctrine adds `EntityValueResolver`; PSR-7 Bridge adds the PSR-7 resolver.

references.title: Extending Action Argument Resolving - Managing Value Resolvers
references.summary: Explains resolver ordering by priority and the `#[ValueResolver]` attribute that targets a specific resolver and can disable it via `disabled: true`. `RequestAttributeValueResolver` runs at priority 100, so custom resolvers acting on request attributes should set their priority accordingly.

references.title: Extending Action Argument Resolving - Adding a Custom Value Resolver
references.summary: Shows how to implement `ValueResolverInterface::resolve(Request $request, ArgumentMetadata $argument): iterable`. The method must return an empty array when it cannot resolve and an array of values otherwise (always an array, even for a single value, because variadics may produce many).

references.title: Extending Action Argument Resolving - controller.argument_value_resolver
references.summary: Documents the standard `controller.argument_value_resolver` service tag. It is automatically added to services implementing `ValueResolverInterface`, but can be configured manually to set resolver `priority` or `name` attributes.

references.title: Extending Action Argument Resolving - controller.targeted_value_resolver
references.summary: Documents the `controller.targeted_value_resolver` tag and the `#[AsTargetedValueResolver('name')]` attribute. Resolvers tagged this way only run when a controller argument explicitly targets them through `#[ValueResolver('name')]`.

references.title: Routing
references.summary: Symfony's routing maps every incoming request to a controller action and is configurable in YAML, PHP, or attributes. The article gives the high-level model: routes match URLs, expose parameters to controllers, and double as URL generators.

references.title: Routing - Creating Routes
references.summary: All three configuration formats (attributes, YAML, PHP) are equivalent in features and performance. Attributes are recommended because the route lives next to the controller it triggers.

references.title: Routing - Route Parameters
references.summary: Route placeholders such as {slug} define variable URL segments that Symfony stores as request attributes and passes to matching controller arguments. The section explains how parameter names map to PHP variables and that each route parameter name can appear only once in a route path.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component - The kernel.request Event
references.summary: Symfony's RouterListener subscribes to kernel.request to match the URL and place the resolved controller and route attributes on the Request. This event is the integration point between FrameworkBundle's Router and the HttpKernel request lifecycle.

references.title: How to Create a custom Route Loader
references.summary: FrameworkBundle can import routes from files, bundle resources, PSR-4 roots, attribute directories, individual attribute classes and directories of routing files. Custom loaders can provide routes from non-standard sources by returning a RouteCollection and registering services with the routing.loader tag.

references.title: Routing - Parameter Conversion
references.summary: Symfony can convert route parameter values into typed controller arguments, for example mapping {slug:post} to a BlogPost $post argument. If no matching object is found, Symfony returns a 404 response, and more complex mappings can be customized with #[MapEntity].

references.title: Routing - Backed Enum Parameters
references.summary: Backed enumerations can be used as route parameters because Symfony converts them automatically to their scalar values. This supports controller arguments and defaults that use PHP backed enum cases.

references.title: Routing - Stateless Routes
