references.summary: The stateless route option declares that a matched route should not use the session. When session usage happens on a stateless route, Symfony throws an UnexpectedSessionUsageException in debug mode or logs a warning when debug is disabled.

references.title: Routing - Creating Routes as Attributes
references.summary: PHP attributes use the #[Route] attribute from Symfony\Component\Routing\Attribute\Route on a controller method. With Symfony Flex, attribute routing is enabled by default; otherwise a routes.yaml entry must declare a routing.controllers resource.

references.title: Routing - Creating Routes in YAML or PHP Files
references.summary: Routes can also be declared in YAML or PHP configuration files separate from controllers. The controller value uses the format controller_class::method_name and may be omitted when the controller is invokable.

references.title: Routing - Route Groups and Prefixes
references.summary: Common options for a set of routes can be shared via a class-level #[Route] when using attributes, or via prefix, name_prefix, requirements, host and trailing_slash_on_root when importing them in YAML or PHP.

references.title: Routing - Route Aliasing
references.summary: Aliases provide multiple names for the same route, useful when renaming routes or referring to third-party routes. YAML and PHP can alias routes you do not own; PHP attributes can declare aliases (and DeprecatedAlias) on the route owner.

references.title: Routing - Deprecating Route Aliases
references.summary: Deprecated route aliases let applications rename routes while warning callers that still use the old name. The configuration includes package, version and an optional message that must contain the %alias_id% placeholder.

references.title: Routing - Priority Parameter
references.summary: Symfony evaluates routes in definition order, which can make greedy patterns hide more specific routes. Attribute routes can use the priority option to sort higher-priority routes before lower-priority ones; the default priority is 0.

references.title: Routing - Parameters Validation
references.summary: The requirements option attaches PCRE patterns to route parameters, ensuring the entire route only matches when each parameter satisfies its expression. Inline syntax {name<regex>} and the Requirement enum (DIGITS, UUID, etc.) make common patterns concise.

references.title: Routing - Slash Characters in Route Parameters
references.summary: Route parameters do not match the / character by default because slashes separate URL segments. A permissive requirement such as .+ allows slashes in a parameter; with {_format} it must be tightened (e.g. [^.]+) so dots remain available for the format suffix.

concepts.concept: Set default values to URL parameters

references.title: Routing - Optional Parameters
references.summary: A parameter becomes optional by giving it a default value: with attributes via the controller argument default, in YAML/PHP via the defaults option. Default values are allowed to violate requirements, all parameters after an optional one must also be optional, and {!name} forces inclusion when generating URLs.

references.title: Routing - Extra Parameters
references.summary: The defaults option can also carry parameters that are not part of the path. These extra values are available as route parameters and can be passed to controller arguments even though they are not URL placeholders.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs
references.summary: The router is bidirectional: it both matches URLs to controllers and generates URLs from a route name and its parameters. Each route therefore needs a unique internal name, which can be set explicitly with the name option or derived from the controller and action when omitted.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs in Controllers
references.summary: Controllers extending AbstractController call generateUrl($name, $params, $referenceType). The third argument accepts UrlGeneratorInterface constants such as ABSOLUTE_URL; extra parameters not in the route definition are appended as a query string.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs in Services
references.summary: In services, inject Symfony\Component\Routing\Generator\UrlGeneratorInterface and call its generate() method. Generated URLs are absolute paths by default; pass UrlGeneratorInterface::ABSOLUTE_URL, NETWORK_PATH, or RELATIVE_PATH to change the reference type.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs in Templates
references.summary: Twig templates use the path() function to generate relative URLs and the url() function to generate absolute URLs for route names. Both functions accept route parameters in the same way as controller and service URL generation.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs in JavaScript
references.summary: JavaScript embedded in Twig templates can receive generated URLs from the path() or url() Twig functions, with escape('js') used for JavaScript-safe output. For dynamic or pure JavaScript URL generation, Symfony points to FOSJsRoutingBundle.

references.title: Routing - Generating URLs in Commands
references.summary: Commands run outside an HTTP request, so absolute URLs default to http://localhost/. Configure framework.router.default_uri to provide the request context (host, scheme, base path) used when generating URLs from console code.

references.title: Routing - Forcing HTTPS on Generated URLs
references.summary: By default, generated URLs reuse the current request scheme (or http in CLI). The schemes route option, the router.request_context.scheme parameter, and an importer-level schemes option force HTTPS for individual routes or whole groups.

references.title: Routing - Checking if a Route Exists
references.summary: Avoid Router::getRouteCollection() because it rebuilds the routing cache. Instead, attempt to generate the URL and catch Symfony\Component\Routing\Exception\RouteNotFoundException to detect missing routes.

references.title: Routing - Signing URIs
references.summary: Symfony\Component\HttpFoundation\UriSigner signs URIs with a hash query parameter (_hash) and optional expiration via _expiration. The verify() method differentiates UnsignedUriException, UnverifiedSignedUriException and ExpiredSignedUriException; #[IsSignatureValid] enforces signature checks at the controller level.

references.title: Routing - Redirecting to URLs and Routes Directly from a Route
references.summary: Symfony\Bundle\FrameworkBundle\Controller\RedirectController handles redirect-only routes through defaults. Use the route default to point to a route name, the path default to redirect to a URL, and flags such as permanent, keepQueryParams, keepRequestMethod and ignoreAttributes to tune status codes and forwarded data; the section also documents automatic trailing-slash redirects for GET and HEAD requests.

references.title: Routing - Special Parameters
references.summary: Symfony recognises _controller, _format, _fragment, _locale and _query as special routing parameters. Same-name attribute options without leading underscore (controller, format, locale, query) provide cleaner declarations of these values.

references.title: Routing - Getting the Route Name and Parameters
references.summary: After matching, the request stores routing data in the attributes bag: $request->attributes->get('_route') returns the route name and _route_params returns the matched parameters. In Twig the same data is exposed as app.current_route and app.current_route_parameters.

references.title: Routing - Sub-Domain Routing
references.summary: The host route option restricts a route to requests whose Host header matches the given pattern, which can include parameters and requirements. Functional tests must set HTTP_HOST so the matcher can recognise the requested host.

references.title: Routing - Matching Expressions
references.summary: The condition route option uses the ExpressionLanguage to add arbitrary matching logic, with context (RequestContext), request and params variables and env() and service() functions available. Conditions are compiled to PHP and are intentionally ignored by URL generation.

references.title: Routing - Matching Environments
references.summary: The env option (a string or array) registers a route only when the current Symfony environment matches. In YAML, the equivalent is a when@<env> block grouping environment-specific route definitions.

references.title: Routing - Matching HTTP Methods
references.summary: Routes match any verb by default; the methods option restricts them to a list (e.g. ['GET', 'HEAD']) or a YAML pipe form (GET|HEAD). HTML forms only support GET and POST, so a hidden _method field plus framework.http_method_override (and allowed_http_method_override) is required for verb spoofing.

references.title: Routing - Localized Routes (i18n)
references.summary: Each route can define a different path per locale by passing an array to the path option. When such a route matches, Symfony sets the request locale automatically; locale-specific prefixes and hosts can also be configured when importing routes.

references.title: Routing - Debugging Routes
references.summary: debug:router lists application routes in matching order and accepts options like --show-aliases, --show-controllers and --method. router:match <url> shows which route the matcher resolves for a given URL and is useful when debugging unexpected controller resolution.

references.title: Routing - Troubleshooting
references.summary: Lists common routing errors: missing controller arguments because the path lacks a {placeholder}, and 'Some mandatory parameters are missing' when a required parameter is omitted from generateUrl() or path().

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Installation
references.summary: Explains that TwigBundle integrates the Twig library with Symfony and is installed via the symfony/twig-bundle package using Symfony Flex. Without this bundle, Symfony cannot render Twig templates from controllers, services, or routes.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Twig Templating Language
references.summary: Introduces the Twig syntax used in Symfony applications, explaining the three core constructs: {{ ... }} for printing values, {% ... %} for executing logic, and {# ... #} for comments. It also notes that Twig templates are compiled into PHP and cached in prod.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Twig Configuration
references.summary: Pointer section noting that TwigBundle exposes configuration for date/number formatting, caching and other options, all documented in the Twig configuration reference.

references.title: Twig Configuration Reference (TwigBundle)
references.summary: Lists every TwigBundle configuration option under the twig: key: auto_reload, autoescape_service, cache, charset, date, debug, default_path, file_name_pattern, form_themes, globals, mailer, number_format, optimizations, paths and strict_variables. Includes default values and behavioral notes for each option.

references.title: Twig Configuration Reference - default_path
references.summary: Documents the twig.default_path option which defines the directory where Symfony looks for application templates. Defaults to %kernel.project_dir%/templates and can be combined with the paths option for additional template directories.

references.title: Twig Configuration Reference - paths
references.summary: Documents the twig.paths option used to register additional template directories. Each entry maps a directory to an optional namespace; entries without a namespace are searched before the default templates/ directory.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Template Namespaces
references.summary: Explains how Twig namespaces group template directories under logical names. Shows that un-namespaced twig.paths directories are searched before the default templates/ directory, while namespaced templates are referenced with the @namespace/template.html.twig syntax.

references.title: Twig Configuration Reference - cache
references.summary: Documents the twig.cache option which controls where compiled templates are stored. Defaults to true (uses %kernel.cache_dir%/twig) and can be a custom path or false to disable compilation, which is not recommended even in dev because auto_reload handles template changes.

references.title: Twig Configuration Reference - auto_reload
references.summary: Documents the twig.auto_reload option which defaults to %kernel.debug%. When enabled, Symfony recompiles templates whose source code changed since their last compilation, ensuring dev changes are picked up automatically.

references.title: Twig Configuration Reference - strict_variables
references.summary: Documents the twig.strict_variables option which defaults to %kernel.debug%. When true, Symfony throws an exception on access to undefined variables, attributes or methods; when false such accesses silently return null.

references.title: Twig for Template Designers
references.summary: Comprehensive Twig 3.x reference covering the three syntax delimiters, variables, filters, functions, tags, control structures, expressions, literals, operators, named arguments, whitespace control, comments, escaping, macros, template inheritance and string interpolation.

references.title: Twig for Template Designers - Variables
references.summary: Describes how Twig accesses variables. The dot notation (foo.bar) and subscript notation (foo['bar']) are both supported, with Twig trying array access, public property, method, getter, isser, hasser in turn before failing or returning null based on strict_variables.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Template Variables
references.summary: Documents how Symfony Twig templates access variables passed from controllers or services. Explains the foo.bar lookup order across arrays, public properties, methods, getters, issers and hassers, and how strict_variables changes missing-value behavior.

references.title: Twig for Template Designers - Literals
references.summary: Documents Twig literals: strings (single or double quoted), numbers, arrays ([...]), hashes ({...}), booleans (true/false) and null. Explains that strings in single quotes do not interpret escape sequences or interpolation, while double quotes do.

references.title: Twig for Template Designers - Operators
references.summary: Lists Twig operators including arithmetic (+, -, *, /, %, //, **), comparison (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=, ===, starts with, ends with, matches), logical (and, or, not), string concatenation (~), the ternary (?:), null-coalescing (??), and the membership (in) operator.

references.title: Twig for Template Designers - Named Arguments
references.summary: Explains that Twig supports passing function and filter arguments by name using name=value or name: value syntax. Named arguments improve readability and let callers skip optional positional arguments; positional arguments must come before named arguments.

references.title: Twig for Template Designers - Whitespace Control
references.summary: Documents Twig whitespace control modifiers on tag/expression delimiters. The - modifier removes all whitespace including newlines, while the ~ modifier removes whitespace except newlines; both can be used on either side of a tag.

references.title: Twig for Template Designers - Comments
references.summary: Describes Twig's {# ... #} comment syntax. Unlike HTML comments, Twig comments are removed at compile time and never sent to the rendered output.

references.title: Twig set Tag
references.summary: Documents the set tag for assigning values to variables inside templates. Supports both single-line {% set foo = 'bar' %} and block form using {% set foo %}...{% endset %} where the captured content becomes the variable's string value.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Output Escaping and XSS Attacks
references.summary: Explains that Symfony Twig templates apply automatic output escaping to defend against XSS attacks. Variables printed with {{ ... }} are escaped according to the template's filename (HTML for *.html.twig, JS for *.js.twig, etc.), and the raw filter disables escaping for trusted content.

references.title: Twig for Template Designers - HTML Escaping
references.summary: Describes Twig's HTML escaping mechanism. Variables are escaped automatically when output, and the escape (alias e) filter explicitly applies escaping with strategies such as html, html_attr, js, css and url.

references.title: Twig autoescape Tag
references.summary: Documents the autoescape tag used to enable or disable automatic escaping for a section of template, optionally with a strategy (html, js, css, url, html_attr) or false to turn it off entirely.

references.title: Twig escape Filter
references.summary: Documents the escape filter (alias e) which converts characters to their HTML/JS/CSS/URL/HTML-attribute safe form. Strategies html, js, css, url, html_attr are built in and the default strategy is html.

references.title: Twig raw Filter
references.summary: Documents the raw filter which marks the value as safe so it is not auto-escaped on output. Important: raw must be the very last filter applied because Twig's escaper looks at the final node to decide whether to escape.

references.title: Twig Configuration Reference - autoescape_service
references.summary: Documents the autoescape_service option which lets the application override the default file-extension-based escaping strategy. Combined with autoescape_service_method, a custom service decides which strategy applies to each template.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Template Inheritance and Layouts
references.summary: Recommends a three-level template hierarchy in Symfony (base.html.twig, layout.html.twig, page templates) using extends and block. Notes that with extends a child template cannot define content outside of a block, otherwise a SyntaxError is thrown.

references.title: Twig for Template Designers - Template Inheritance
references.summary: Explains how a child template uses extends to inherit from a parent and overrides parent blocks by name. Multiple inheritance is supported by chaining extends, and parent() lets a block reuse parent content.

references.title: Twig extends Tag
references.summary: Documents the extends tag for declaring template inheritance. Must be the first tag in the child template, can take a list/expression to choose dynamically, and forbids any content outside of blocks in the child.

references.title: Twig block Tag
references.summary: Documents the block tag used to declare named regions that child templates can override. Supports a short {% block name 'value' %} form and pairs with the parent() function and block() function for advanced inheritance.

references.title: Twig use Tag
references.summary: Documents the use tag which provides 'horizontal reuse' of blocks from another template without inheritance. Imported blocks can be aliased with as to avoid name collisions and overridden in the importing template.

references.title: Twig embed Tag
references.summary: Documents the embed tag which combines include and extends: it includes another template while letting the embedding template override its blocks. Useful for reusable components whose internal blocks need customization at the call site.

references.title: Twig parent Function
references.summary: Documents the parent() function used inside a block to render the parent template's version of that block. Lets a child append to or wrap parent content rather than fully replace it.

references.title: Twig block Function
references.summary: Documents the block() function which renders the content of a named block; an optional second argument selects the template to read the block from. Useful for re-rendering a block elsewhere on the page or pulling a block from another template.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - The App Global Variable
references.summary: Documents the app variable that Symfony injects into every Twig template (an instance of Symfony\Bridge\Twig\AppVariable). Lists its members: app.user, app.request, app.session, app.flashes, app.environment, app.debug, app.token, app.current_route, app.current_route_parameters, app.locale and app.enabled_locales.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Global Variables
references.summary: Shows how to register custom Twig globals through twig.globals. Static values become available in every template; service references prefixed with @ inject services, but those services are not loaded lazily, so they are instantiated whenever Twig boots.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - app
references.summary: Reference entry for the app global Twig variable. Lists every property exposed by AppVariable and notes that some properties may be unavailable depending on the request lifecycle (for example, app.session may be null if the session was not started).

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - Global Variables
references.summary: Reference index of the global Twig variables provided by Symfony, currently the app variable. Points back to the dedicated app variable documentation for details.

references.title: Twig Configuration Reference - globals
references.summary: Documents the twig.globals configuration option which defines variables auto-injected into every template. Values can be scalars, arrays, or service references prefixed with @.

references.title: Twig for Template Designers - Filters
references.summary: Explains how filters transform values using the pipe (|) syntax. Filters can be chained, accept arguments, and may take a block of content (apply tag) to filter multiple lines at once.

references.title: Twig for Template Designers - Functions
references.summary: Describes how functions return values and are called like name(args). Twig ships with built-in functions (range, cycle, max, min, parent, block, attribute, dump, etc.) and applications can register their own.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - Functions
references.summary: Catalogues the Twig functions added by Symfony, including render, render_esi, fragment_uri, controller, asset, asset_version, csrf_token, is_granted, is_granted_for_user, logout_path/url, path, url, absolute_url, relative_path, expression, impersonation_*, t and importmap.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - Filters
references.summary: Catalogues the Twig filters added by Symfony, including humanize, trans, sanitize_html, yaml_encode, yaml_dump, abbr_class, abbr_method, format_args, format_args_as_text, file_excerpt, format_file, format_file_from_text, file_link, file_relative, serialize and emojify.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - Tests
references.summary: Index of the Twig tests added by Symfony. Tests are used with the is operator, as in {% if foo is something %}; Symfony adds form-related tests such as selectedchoice() and rootform().

references.title: Twig for Template Designers - Macros
references.summary: Describes Twig macros: small reusable template snippets that behave like functions. Macros are isolated from the calling template's context (no access to caller variables) and must be imported with import or from to be used.

references.title: Twig macro Tag
references.summary: Documents the macro tag which defines a reusable template fragment with named arguments. Macros are not aware of the calling template's variables but can use the special _self reference to call sibling macros.

references.title: Twig import Tag
references.summary: Documents the import tag for loading macros from another template under an alias, e.g. {% import 'forms.html' as forms %}. After import, macros are accessed via forms.input(...), forms.textarea(...) and so on.

references.title: Twig from Tag
references.summary: Documents the from tag for importing specific macros directly into the namespace, e.g. {% from 'forms.html' import input as input_field, textarea %}. The macros become callable by their (aliased) names without the import alias prefix.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Including Templates
references.summary: Explains how to share template fragments across pages by extracting them into separate files (conventionally prefixed with _) and reusing them with the include() Twig function. Variables of the parent template are inherited unless with_context is set to false, and additional variables can be passed.

references.title: Twig include Tag
references.summary: Documents the include tag for embedding another template's rendered output. Supports passing variables with with, isolating context with only, and gracefully ignoring a missing template via ignore missing.

references.title: Twig include Function
references.summary: Documents the include() function — the expression form of the include tag — usable inside expressions and filters. Like the tag it accepts a list of templates (first found wins), variables, with_context flag and ignore_missing flag.

references.title: Twig embed Tag
references.summary: Documents the embed tag which both includes another template and lets the calling template override the included template's blocks. Combines include's contextual access with extends-style block overrides.

references.title: Twig for Tag
references.summary: Documents the for tag which iterates over arrays/iterators. Provides the loop variable (loop.index, loop.index0, loop.first, loop.last, loop.length, loop.parent, loop.revindex), supports an else branch for empty sequences, and allows iteration over keys, values, or both.

references.title: Twig if Tag
references.summary: Documents the if/elseif/else/endif conditional construct. Twig considers empty strings, the literal 0, empty arrays and null as false; whitespace-only strings are truthy. Tests like is defined, is empty, is iterable can be used in the condition.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Linking to Pages
references.summary: Recommends generating links from routes with the path() Twig function instead of hard-coded URLs. Pass the route name as the first argument and parameters as the second. Use url() instead of path() when an absolute URL is required (for example in emails or RSS feeds).

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - path
references.summary: Documents the path() Twig function which returns a relative URL for a Symfony route. Arguments: name (string, required), parameters (array, optional) and relative (boolean, optional, defaults to false).

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - url
references.summary: Documents the url() Twig function which returns an absolute URL for a Symfony route. Arguments: name (string, required), parameters (array, optional) and schemeRelative (boolean, optional). Useful for emails and external APIs that require absolute links.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - absolute_url
references.summary: Documents the absolute_url() Twig function which converts a path (such as one produced by asset()) into an absolute URL using the current request's scheme and host.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - relative_path
references.summary: Documents the relative_path() Twig function which converts an absolute path or URL into a path that is relative to the current request URI. Useful for templates that may be reused at different URL depths.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Embedding Controllers
references.summary: Explains how to embed the result of a controller in a template using the render() Twig function combined with path(), url() or controller(). The controller() function targets a controller via its FQCN::method, in which case the framework.fragments path must be configured.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - hinclude.js
references.summary: Documents the render_hinclude() function for asynchronously embedding controller output via the hinclude.js JavaScript library. Supports a default fallback template/string, attributes such as data-with-credentials and evaljs, and requires the framework.fragments configuration when used with controller().

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - render
references.summary: Documents the render() Twig function used to embed the response of another controller. Accepts a URI string or a ControllerReference (returned by controller()) and an options array with strategy (inline, esi, hinclude) and other transport-specific values.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - render_esi
references.summary: Documents the render_esi() Twig function — equivalent to render() with the esi strategy. Generates an ESI tag when ESI is enabled, otherwise falls back to inline rendering of the controller.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - fragment_uri
references.summary: Documents the fragment_uri() Twig function which builds the URI of a controller fragment from a ControllerReference. Useful for advanced cases where the URI is needed before the actual rendering.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - controller
references.summary: Documents the controller() Twig function which returns a ControllerReference object suitable for render(). Arguments: controller (FQCN::method, required), attributes (array, optional) and query (array, optional).

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - trans (filter)
references.summary: Documents the trans Twig filter which translates a string or Translatable object in templates. Arguments include placeholder values, an optional translation domain and an optional locale; complex plural-sensitive messages are handled by Symfony's translation message format support.

references.title: Translations - Message Format
references.summary: Explains translation placeholders and plural-sensitive messages in Symfony. Basic placeholders are replaced by matching parameter keys, while complex count-, gender- or locale-dependent messages use ICU MessageFormat syntax in +intl-icu translation files.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - t
references.summary: Documents the t() Twig function which creates a TranslatableMessage instance from a message id, parameters and domain. The returned object can be passed around and translated later by the trans filter or the translator service.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - trans_default_domain
references.summary: Documents the trans_default_domain Twig tag which sets the default translation domain for the current template. Only affects the trans filter and t() function calls in the template that omit an explicit domain; does not affect included templates.

references.title: Twig for Template Designers - String Interpolation
references.summary: Describes string interpolation in Twig: only double-quoted strings interpolate #{...} expressions. The braces can be omitted for simple variable names, and single-quoted strings keep the #{...} sequence as a literal.

references.title: Twig for Template Designers - Literals
references.summary: Covers Twig string literals. Double-quoted strings support escape sequences like \n and #{...} interpolation; single-quoted strings are literal. The ~ operator concatenates strings, casting non-strings to string when needed.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Linking to CSS, JavaScript and Image Assets
references.summary: Recommends the asset() Twig function for linking to CSS, JavaScript and image files. Benefits include automatic version hashes for cache busting and correct base path handling whether the application lives at the domain root or under a subdirectory. absolute_url(asset(...)) returns an absolute URL.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - asset
references.summary: Documents the asset() Twig function which returns the public path of an application asset. Arguments: path (string, required) and packageName (string, optional) to select a specific asset package configured under framework.assets.

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - asset_version
references.summary: Documents the asset_version() Twig function which returns just the version string of an asset (the hash or version appended by asset()). Arguments: path (string, required) and packageName (string, optional).

references.title: Twig Extensions Defined by Symfony - importmap
references.summary: Documents the importmap() Twig function which renders the <script type="importmap"> tag and any preload tags for AssetMapper's importmap-managed entrypoints. Argument: entryPoint (string or array of strings, optional, defaults to 'app').

references.title: Front-end Tools: Handling CSS & JavaScript
references.summary: Overview of the **front-end stack** Symfony recommends: **AssetMapper** (no Node.js required, importmaps + JSDelivr), **Webpack Encore** (Node.js / Webpack), **StimulusBundle**, **UX components**, and the legacy **Asset** component. Compares trade-offs and links the dedicated guides for each tool.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Debugging Templates
references.summary: Lists the Twig debugging tools provided by Symfony: the lint:twig CLI command, the debug:twig CLI command, and the dump() Twig function and {% dump %} tag from the VarDumper component.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Linting Twig Templates
references.summary: Documents the lint:twig CLI command for finding syntax errors in Twig templates. Supports linting directories and single files, --show-deprecations for triggered deprecations, --excludes for ignoring directories, and --format=github for GitHub Actions output.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - Inspecting Twig Information
references.summary: Documents the debug:twig CLI command which lists all available Twig functions, filters, tests and global variables. Supports --filter=keyword to narrow the output and accepts a template path (e.g. @Twig/Exception/error.html.twig) to show the physical file resolved by the loader.

references.title: Creating and Using Templates - The Dump Twig Utilities
references.summary: Documents the {% dump %} tag and the dump() Twig function. The tag sends data to the Web Debug Toolbar; the function dumps inline within the rendered page. Both are only available in the dev and test environments and require symfony/debug-bundle.

references.title: Twig dump Function
references.summary: Documents Twig's dump() function for inspecting variables. Without arguments it dumps the current template's context; with arguments it dumps those values. Output requires Twig's debug option to be enabled, otherwise dump() returns nothing.

references.title: Twig Configuration Reference - debug
references.summary: Documents the twig.debug option (default %kernel.debug%). Controls whether compiled templates expose a __toString() method for inspection and whether dump() produces output; when false dump() outputs nothing.

references.title: The Form Component
references.summary: Introduces the standalone Symfony Form component as a tool to create, process and reuse forms, focusing on processing data to and from the client whether the source is an HTML form post or an API. Lists the optional integrations (Request handling, CSRF protection, Twig templating, Translation, Validation) that the component layers on top of its core form factory.

references.title: The Form Component - Configuration
references.summary: Explains that forms in Symfony are represented by objects built by a form factory created via Forms::createFormFactory(), and lists the optional features (request handling, CSRF, Twig templating, translation, validation) that can be added by registering extensions on the factory builder. Notes that those features rely on additional Symfony components such as HttpFoundation, Translation, and Validator that can each be replaced by another library.

references.title: The Form Component - Accessing the Form Factory
references.summary: Explains that an application only needs one FormFactory and that this single factory should be used to build every form in the application. Recommends instantiating it in a central bootstrap location (typically a service container) so it can be retrieved wherever forms are created.

references.title: The Form Component - Creating a simple Form
references.summary: Shows how to build a form from a FormBuilder obtained from the form factory by chaining add() calls and finishing with getForm(). Notes that in the Symfony framework the form factory is available as the form.factory service (FormFactoryInterface) and that AbstractController exposes the createFormBuilder() shortcut.

references.title: The Form Component - Accessing Form Errors
references.summary: Explains that FormInterface::getErrors() returns a FormErrorIterator. By default it returns only errors attached to the current form level; passing true returns child errors flattened, and passing (true, false) preserves the nested structure so getOrigin() can identify the originating field.

references.title: The Form Component - Clearing Form Errors
references.summary: Documents ClearableErrorsInterface::clearErrors() to manually clear errors from a form, useful for partial AJAX submissions or dynamic form modification scenarios. Warns that clearing errors makes the form valid, so the method should only be called after first checking that the form is valid.

references.title: Forms
references.summary: Top-level guide to Symfony forms describing the install (composer require symfony/form) and the recommended workflow: build the form, render it, then process the submitted data. Establishes the Task example used throughout the chapter and lists the main sections covering creation, rendering, and processing.

references.title: Forms - Understanding How Forms Work
references.summary: Explains the mental model behind Symfony forms: a form is a bidirectional mapping layer between PHP objects (or arrays) and HTML forms. Covers the two directions of object-to-HTML rendering and HTML-to-object submission, both backed by the same Form component pattern.

references.title: Forms - Building Forms
references.summary: Introduces the form builder, a fluent API used to describe the fields of a form before producing the actual form object that will render and process the data. The builder pattern is the foundation for both controller-based forms and reusable form classes.

references.title: Forms - Creating Forms in Controllers
references.summary: Shows how to build a form directly in a controller via AbstractController::createFormBuilder(), chaining add() calls for each field and finishing with getForm(). For controllers that do not extend AbstractController, the same can be achieved by injecting the form.factory service and calling its createBuilder() method.

references.title: Forms - Creating Form Classes
references.summary: Explains why complex forms should be moved out of controllers into dedicated form classes that extend AbstractType (which already implements FormTypeInterface). Inside such a class, buildForm(FormBuilderInterface, array) defines the fields, configureOptions(OptionsResolver) declares the data_class option, and the controller instantiates it with createForm(TaskType::class, $task).

references.title: Forms - Mapping Fields to Object Properties
references.summary: Describes how form fields map to object properties through the PropertyAccess component, working with public properties or get*/is*/has*/set* accessors. The property_path option overrides the default mapping and supports dot notation for nested properties, while unmapped fields are used when a field should not be written back to the underlying data.

references.title: Forms - Using a Form without a Data Class
references.summary: Documents that a form is bound to an object only when an object is passed as the first argument of createFormBuilder()/second argument of createForm(), or when the data_class option is set. Without either, the form returns the data as an array from $form->getData(), making this the standard approach for plain contact forms or arbitrary payloads.

references.title: How to Embed Forms
references.summary: Explains how to compose a parent form from form types that represent related objects, such as adding CategoryType as a child field inside TaskType. Covers the role of the data_class option on embedded types, cascading validation with the Valid constraint, rendering nested fields in Twig, and how submitted child data is written back to the parent object.

references.title: Forms - Processing Forms
references.summary: Documents the recommended single-action pattern that both renders and processes a form using $form->handleRequest($request) followed by an isSubmitted() && isValid() check. Notes that passing $form (instead of $form->createView()) to render() automatically returns HTTP 422 Unprocessable Content for invalid submissions, which is required by tools such as Symfony UX Turbo.

references.title: Forms - Accessing Form Data
references.summary: Lists the three accessors of a form's data layers: getData() returns the model data (object or array), getNormData() returns the normalized intermediate representation, and getViewData() returns the view data used for rendering and submission. getExtraData() is the equivalent for unexpected submitted fields, and isSynchronized() reveals when a transformer has failed.

references.title: Forms - Using the submit() Method
references.summary: Explains how FormInterface::submit() can replace handleRequest() when you need exact control over which data is fed to the form, typically for JSON payloads from React/Angular/Vue clients. The optional second boolean ($clearMissing) controls PATCH-like partial submissions: passing false leaves missing fields untouched and only validates the submitted ones, while passing true (default) sets missing fields to null.

references.title: Forms - Handling Multiple Submit Buttons
references.summary: Documents how to detect which submit button triggered the form: ClickableInterface::isClicked() on a specific button, or Form::getClickedButton() to get the clicked button object/name. For nested forms with duplicate names the documentation recommends comparing the button objects rather than names.

references.title: Forms - Validating Forms
references.summary: Explains that $form->isValid() does not validate the form itself but the underlying object after the submitted data has been applied. Validation is enabled by adding constraints either on the entity (PHP attributes, YAML, XML, PHP) or via the form-type constraints option, both approaches being usable together.

references.title: Forms - Disabling Validation
references.summary: Describes how to suppress validation for a form by setting the validation_groups option to false, either on the form type (configureOptions()) or on a specific submit button (typical for a "Previous" button in multi-step forms). Even when validation is disabled, basic integrity checks such as upload size and unexpected fields are still applied.

references.title: Forms - Unmapped Fields
references.summary: Documents the mapped option used to declare a form field that is not stored on the underlying object (for example an "agree to terms" checkbox). Its data is read and written through $form->get('agreeTerms')->getData()/setData() and unsubmitted form fields are explicitly set to null on submission.

references.title: Forms - Extra fields
references.summary: Explains that any submitted field not declared in the form is treated as an "extra field". Such values are accessible via FormInterface::getExtraData() but make the form invalid unless the allow_extra_fields option is set to true.

references.title: Configuring Validation Groups in Forms
references.summary: Documents how the validation_groups form option controls which constraint groups are applied during form validation. Shows static groups in form types or controllers, using false on a submit button to skip validation, callbacks based on submitted data, and invokable services for more complex group-resolution logic.

references.title: Forms - Form Types
references.summary: Defines the Symfony notion of a "form type": a single text input, a group of fields, or an entire form are all form types built around the same FormTypeInterface contract. Every form type has a parent type that determines its base behavior, options, and rendering, with FormType as the root parent for all built-in types and types like EmailType inheriting from TextType.

references.title: Forms - Form Type Guessing
references.summary: Explains how Symfony can guess a field's type by introspecting the underlying object's validation/Doctrine metadata. Pass null (or omit it) as the second argument of add() to enable guessing; this also guesses the required and maxlength options based on NotBlank/NotNull, Length/Range or nullable column metadata.

references.title: How to Create a Custom Form Field Type
references.summary: Introduces custom form types as PHP classes that should extend AbstractType, conventionally stored in src/Form/Type/. Distinguishes between types built on top of an existing type (via getParent()) and types built from scratch, and previews the methods used to define and render them.

references.title: How to Create a Custom Form Field Type - Creating Form Types Based on Symfony Built-in Types
references.summary: Shows how to extend an existing type by overriding getParent() to return the parent type's FQCN and configureOptions() to override defaults. The example builds ShippingType on top of ChoiceType with predefined choices, so consumers do not have to repeat the choices option.

references.title: How to Create a Custom Form Field Type - Creating Form Types Created From Scratch
references.summary: Documents the four most common methods of an AbstractType subclass: getParent() (omit when starting from scratch — AbstractType already returns FormType), configureOptions(), buildForm() to add child fields, and buildView()/finishView() to expose extra variables to the template. getParent() is preferred over PHP inheritance to compose types.

references.title: How to Create a Custom Form Field Type - Adding Configuration Options for the Form Type
references.summary: Custom options live in configureOptions() and rely on the OptionsResolver component for defaults, allowed types, allowed values and normalizers. Once declared, callers can pass these options when instantiating the type and the form type can react to them in buildForm().

references.title: How to Create a Custom Form Field Type - Creating the Form Type Template
references.summary: Custom form types use the application's form themes by default, but a custom template can be added by registering a Twig template under the twig.form_themes option. The block prefix derives from the type's class name (PostalAddressType -> postal_address) and can be overridden via getBlockPrefix(); a unique block prefix is required when the type's name collides with a built-in type.

references.title: How to Create a Custom Form Field Type - Passing Variables to the Form Type Template
references.summary: buildView(FormView $view, FormInterface $form, array $options) and finishView() expose extra variables to the form template via $view->vars. With the default services configuration, form classes are autowired and tagged form.type automatically; otherwise the service must be tagged with form.type manually.

references.title: Forms - Rendering Forms
references.summary: Shows the recommended pattern for rendering a form in a Twig template by passing $form (instead of $form->createView()) to render() and using {{ form(form) }} to output the entire form. Internally render() calls $form->createView(); the form() function generates field rows together with the surrounding <form> tags.

references.title: How to Customize Form Rendering - Form Rendering Functions
references.summary: Walks through the layered Twig rendering API: form() renders the whole form; form_start()/form_end() render the wrapping tags; form_row() renders a label, widget, help and errors triple; and form_label(), form_widget(), form_help(), form_errors() render individual parts of a field. Reminds developers to render the _token field (or rely on form_rest()) when manually rendering each field.

references.title: How to Customize Form Rendering - Form Field Helpers
references.summary: Documents the field_*() helpers (field_name, field_id, field_value, field_label, field_help, field_errors, field_choices) that return raw values without surrounding HTML so the developer fully controls the markup. Useful when the application's HTML/CSS is too custom to be expressed in a form theme.

references.title: How to Customize Form Rendering - Form Rendering Variables
references.summary: Explains how the second argument of the rendering helpers passes per-call variables (e.g. attr to override HTML attributes, label to override the displayed label). Notes that variables are not applied recursively to child fields when rendering an entire form, and that individual values are also accessible through the FormView's vars property such as form.task.vars.id.

references.title: How to Customize Form Rendering - Functions
references.summary: Reference for the core form Twig functions: form_start() automatically adds enctype="multipart/form-data" when the form contains upload fields; form_end() also calls form_rest() unless render_rest is false; form_rest() renders any remaining fields including hidden ones; form_parent() returns the parent FormView (preferred over form.parent which collides with a child named "parent").

references.title: How to Customize Form Rendering - Tests
references.summary: Documents the Twig tests usable on form views: selectedchoice(value) tests whether a choice is selected (matching a scalar or array of selected values), and rootform tests whether a form is the root form, which is reliable even if the form defines a child field named "parent".

references.title: How to Customize Form Rendering - Form Variables Reference
references.summary: Lists every variable exposed by FormView::vars including action, attr, block_prefixes, compound, data, disabled, errors, full_name, help, id, label, label_attr, method, multipart, name, required, submitted, translation_domain, valid, and value. Notes that form.errors only contains errors attached to that node — to know if the whole form is valid, use the valid variable.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes
references.summary: Top-level guide to Symfony form themes, explaining that a theme is a Twig template containing one or more form fragment blocks (label, widget, errors, help, row) that override the default markup. Covers how to use built-in themes and create custom ones.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes - Symfony Built-In Form Themes
references.summary: Lists the built-in form themes shipped with Symfony: form_div_layout.html.twig (default), form_table_layout.html.twig, the bootstrap_3/4/5 layouts (with horizontal variants), foundation_5/6 layouts and tailwind_2_layout.html.twig. Each theme is enabled via the twig.form_themes option.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes - Applying Themes to all Forms
references.summary: Shows how to register themes globally through the twig.form_themes option, which accepts an ordered array of themes. The order matters because each theme overrides previous ones, so the most specific themes go at the end of the list.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes - Applying Themes to Single Forms
references.summary: Documents the {% form_theme %} Twig tag for applying a theme to one form rendered in a template. Multiple themes can be supplied via the with keyword (their order is significant), and you can apply different themes to specific child forms by targeting form.a_child_form.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes - Disabling Global Themes for Single Forms
references.summary: Adding the only keyword to {% form_theme form with [...] only %} disables every globally-configured theme for that form (typical for reusable bundles). Because none of the built-in themes are loaded, the custom theme must either be fully featured or extend a built-in theme via Twig's use tag (not extends).

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes - Form Fragment Naming
references.summary: Explains the two naming patterns used to override form fragments: type_part (e.g. textarea_widget) overrides every field of a given type, while _id_part (e.g. _product_description_widget) overrides one specific field by its HTML id. Field types inherit fragments from their parent type, so Symfony falls back to text_errors and form_errors when textarea_errors does not exist.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes - Creating a Form Theme in the same Template as the Form
references.summary: Documents the {% form_theme form _self %} tag, which tells Twig to look in the current template for overridden form blocks. This works only when the template uses {% extends %}; the customized blocks cannot be reused in other templates and will not apply outside the current one.

references.title: How to Work with Form Themes - Reusing Parts of a Built-In Form Theme
references.summary: To extend a built-in theme without rewriting every block, use the Twig {% use 'form_div_layout.html.twig' %} tag (not extends) so the theme inherits all its blocks. The Twig parent() function lets a custom block reuse the original built-in block content; in same-template themes the use tag's "with ... as ..." form imports a renamed alias accessible via block('alias').

references.title: Bootstrap 4 Form Theme
references.summary: Built-in Twig theme that renders forms with **Bootstrap 4** markup. Activate it project-wide by setting `twig.form_themes: ["bootstrap_4_layout.html.twig"]`, or per-template with `{% form_theme form 'bootstrap_4_layout.html.twig' %}`. A horizontal variant (`bootstrap_4_horizontal_layout.html.twig`) is also shipped.

references.title: How to Implement CSRF Protection
references.summary: Introduces Cross-Site Request Forgery and the anti-CSRF token strategy: hidden tokens added to forms and validated server-side. Details installation via composer require symfony/security-csrf and the framework.csrf_protection toggle, and notes that stateful tokens are stored in the session, which causes a session to start as soon as a CSRF-protected form is rendered.

references.title: How to Implement CSRF Protection - CSRF Protection in Symfony Forms
references.summary: Symfony Forms include a CSRF token by default in a hidden field named _token, validated automatically. The field name (csrf_field_name), an arbitrary token id (csrf_token_id), and the protection itself (csrf_protection) can be configured globally under framework.form.csrf_protection or per-form via configureOptions(). Per OWASP, CSRF is only required for state-changing operations (not GET).

references.title: How to Implement CSRF Protection - Generating and Checking CSRF Tokens Manually
references.summary: For raw HTML forms outside Symfony Forms, csrf_token('id') generates a token in Twig and AbstractController::isCsrfTokenValid('id', $submittedToken) validates it. Alternatively, the #[IsCsrfTokenValid('id', tokenKey: 'token')] attribute can be applied to a controller action or class; it accepts a static id, an Expression object, a methods restriction, and a tokenSource bitfield (SOURCE_PAYLOAD default, SOURCE_QUERY, SOURCE_HEADER).

references.title: How to Implement CSRF Protection - CSRF Tokens and Compression Side-Channel Attacks
references.summary: Explains BREACH and CRIME attacks against HTTPS combined with HTTP compression. To mitigate these and prevent token guessing, Symfony prepends a random mask to the CSRF token and uses it to scramble the token before transmission.

references.title: How to Implement CSRF Protection - Stateless CSRF Tokens
references.summary: Documents the stateless_token_ids option (enabled by default in Symfony Flex applications for ['submit', 'authenticate', 'logout']) that allows full-page caching by avoiding session storage. Stateless validation checks the request's Origin and Referer headers against the application's target origin and therefore requires correctly configured reverse proxies.

references.title: How to Implement CSRF Protection - Using a Default Token ID
references.summary: Stateful CSRF tokens are scoped per form/action, while stateless ones use a small set of identifiers (authenticate and logout are used by Security; submit is the default for autoconfigured form types). Forms whose token id appears in stateless_token_ids automatically use stateless CSRF protection.

references.title: How to Implement CSRF Protection - Generating CSRF Token Using Javascript
references.summary: Stateless CSRF can be hardened with an opt-in JavaScript double-submit pattern in which a freshly generated token is sent through both a cookie and a header (default name csrf-token). The cookie uses samesite=strict and the __Host- prefix; once a session has successfully validated double-submit, it becomes required for that session to prevent downgrade attacks.

references.title: The Form Component - CSRF Protection
references.summary: Standalone Form component recipe: install symfony/security-csrf and register the CsrfExtension on the form factory builder with a CsrfTokenManager. The extension automatically adds a hidden _token field whose value is generated and validated by the CSRF manager. The csrf_protection option turns it off per form, and applications without HttpFoundation can use NativeSessionTokenStorage instead of SessionTokenStorage.

references.title: FileType Field
references.summary: Reference page for FileType: renders an <input type="file"> with FormType as its parent and Symfony\Component\HttpFoundation\File\File as its data_class. Submitted data becomes an UploadedFile (or array thereof) and the field's invalid message defaults to "Please select a valid file.".

references.title: FileType Field - Basic Usage
references.summary: On submission the field's data is a Symfony\Component\HttpFoundation\File\UploadedFile that exposes move($directory, $name) to persist the file. The doc warns that getClientOriginalName()/getClientOriginalPath() can be tampered with by the user; safer choices are to compute a unique name and use guessExtension() (falling back to a default like 'bin') to derive the extension from the actual MIME type.

references.title: FileType Field - multiple
references.summary: Documents the multiple option (boolean, default false). When set to true, the user can upload several files at once, the rendered <input> gains the multiple HTML attribute, and the form field's data becomes an array of UploadedFile instances.

references.title: How to Upload Files
references.summary: End-to-end recipe: store only the filename in the entity, declare the FileType field as 'mapped' => false (and usually 'required' => false) so it is not bound to the entity, and validate it with the Assert\File constraint (e.g. maxSize, extensions). In the controller, get the UploadedFile through $form->get('brochure')->getData(), build a safe filename with a slugger and uniqid()/guessExtension(), then move() it to the target directory wrapped in a try/catch for FileException.

references.title: How to Upload Files - Uploading Multiple Files
references.summary: Setting the multiple option to true on FileType makes the field's data an array of UploadedFile instances and adds the multiple HTML attribute automatically (no template change required). Validation must wrap the File constraint in an All constraint so each uploaded file is validated, and the controller iterates over the array to process every file.

references.title: How to Upload Files - Creating an Uploader Service
references.summary: Refactors upload logic out of the controller into a FileUploader service that takes a target directory and a SluggerInterface. Lists the specialised exception classes thrown on upload failure (CannotWriteFileException, ExtensionFileException, FormSizeFileException, IniSizeFileException, NoFileException, NoTmpDirFileException, PartialFileException), all extending FileException.

references.title: Form Types Reference
references.summary: Overview of all natively available form field types in Symfony, organised into categories such as Text Fields, Choice Fields, Date and Time Fields, Other Fields, UID Fields, Field Groups, Hidden Fields, Buttons and the Base Field (FormType). Lists the canonical types like TextType, ChoiceType, EntityType, EnumType, CollectionType, RepeatedType, FileType, HiddenType, ButtonType, SubmitType, ResetType.

references.title: ChoiceType Field (select drop-downs, radio buttons & checkboxes)
references.summary: Multi-purpose field for selecting one or more options, rendered as a <select>, radio buttons or checkboxes via the expanded and multiple options. Requires either a choices array (label => value) or a choice_loader; supports grouping (multi-dimensional choices array or group_by callback), choice_label/choice_value/choice_attr callbacks, and a placeholder for optional choices.

references.title: EntityType Field
references.summary: Doctrine-aware extension of ChoiceType (provided by the DoctrineBridge as Symfony\Bridge\Doctrine\Form\Type\EntityType) that loads its choices from a Doctrine entity. Requires the class option (the entity FQCN); the visible label can be set with choice_label, and the loaded set can be customised through choices or a query_builder option that accepts a QueryBuilder, a closure returning one, or null.

references.title: EnumType Field
references.summary: Choice field whose options come from a backed PHP enum. Extends ChoiceType and only requires the class option pointing to the enum FQCN; by default the case name is used as the option label, while implementing TranslatableInterface lets the enum return translated labels. expanded and multiple still control whether it renders as <select>, radio buttons or checkboxes.

references.title: CollectionType Field
references.summary: Renders a collection of similar fields or entire embedded forms (e.g. emails, photos). The entry_type option chooses the inner type and entry_options is forwarded to each entry. allow_add and allow_delete enable adding/removing items dynamically; the prototype option provides a template entry to clone in JavaScript. When backing Doctrine collections, watch the by_reference option.

references.title: RepeatedType Field
references.summary: Field group that renders two identical sub-fields (default names first and second, configurable with first_name/second_name) and validates that they match (default invalid message "The values do not match."). The type option (default TextType, often PasswordType) selects the underlying type; options is forwarded to both fields, while first_options and second_options are merged into one specific child. mapped is always true on both sub-fields.

references.title: SubmitType Field
references.summary: Renders a <button type="submit">. Inherits from ButtonType and uniquely exposes ClickableInterface::isClicked() to detect which submit button triggered the form. Provides validate (false to disable client-side HTML validation), validation_groups (per-button validation groups), and a clicked Twig variable on the form view.

references.title: HiddenType Field
references.summary: Renders an <input type="hidden">. error_bubbling defaults to true so errors propagate to the root form (otherwise they would not be visible), and required is forced to false because hidden inputs cannot be marked required. The default empty_data is the empty string.

references.title: How to Embed a Collection of Forms
references.summary: Shows how CollectionType embeds many child form instances, usually with entry_type pointing to another form type and entry_options forwarded to each entry. Covers dynamic addition with allow_add and the prototype variable, removal with allow_delete, and the by_reference=false pattern needed to call adder/remover methods on Doctrine-style collections.

references.title: BirthdayType Field
references.summary: A specialization of `DateType` for **birth dates**. The defaults narrow the year range to the **last 120 years**, which is the only behavioural difference from `DateType`. Like its parent, it can be rendered as three select boxes or one/three text boxes depending on the `widget` option.

references.title: ButtonType Field
references.summary: A simple, non-responsive `<button>` element. It is the parent of the `SubmitType` and `ResetType` button fields and is **not mapped** to a property of the underlying data object — buttons exist only in the form, not in the model.

references.title: CheckboxType Field
references.summary: Renders a single `<input type="checkbox">`. The submitted value becomes `true` when the box is checked and `false` otherwise. The `false_values` option lists the strings that should be interpreted as the unchecked state (default `[null]`).

references.title: ColorType Field
references.summary: Text field rendered as `<input type="color">`. The `html5` option (default `false`) toggles a strict format check: when `true`, only valid HTML5 simple colors (lowercase 7-character hex like `#abcdef`) are accepted, otherwise any string passes through.

references.title: CountryType Field
references.summary: A specialized `ChoiceType` listing every world country. The choice keys are localized country names in the user's locale and the values are **ISO 3166-1 alpha-2** codes by default. Setting the `alpha3` option to `true` switches the values to the three-letter alpha-3 codes.

references.title: CurrencyType Field
references.summary: A specialized `ChoiceType` listing currencies as **3-letter ISO 4217** codes (e.g. `EUR`, `USD`). Choice labels are localized currency names rendered in the user's locale; the choices come from the **Intl** component, not a hard-coded list.

references.title: DateType Field
references.summary: Field for date input. The `widget` option chooses between `single_text` (one `<input type="date">`), `text` (three text inputs), or `choice` (three `<select>` boxes). The `input` option chooses the underlying PHP type — `datetime` (default), `datetime_immutable`, `string`, `timestamp`, or `array` — and the form converts between this representation and the rendered widget automatically.

references.title: DateIntervalType Field
references.summary: Field for capturing a span of time as a `DateInterval` (years, months, days, hours, minutes, seconds). The `widget` option (`single_text`, `text`, `choice`) controls rendering, and `with_*` flags (e.g. `with_years`, `with_hours`, `with_invert`) toggle which interval components are exposed. The `input` option chooses the model type (`dateinterval` default, `string`, `array`).

references.title: DateTimeType Field
references.summary: Field that combines a date and time into one form. It can render as one `<input type="datetime-local">`, a single text box, or five select fields, controlled by `date_widget` and `time_widget`. The `format` option uses **`IntlDateFormatter`** constants (e.g. `IntlDateFormatter::SHORT`) when `widget` is `single_text`; the `input` option chooses the PHP model type (`datetime` default, `datetime_immutable`, `string`, `timestamp`, `array`).

references.title: EmailType Field
references.summary: Text field rendered as **`<input type="email">`**. It does **not** validate the submitted address by itself — pair it with the `Email` validation constraint when validation is required. It inherits options from its parent `TextType`.

references.title: FormType Field
references.summary: Root form type that **every other form type** inherits from. Its options (e.g. `data_class`, `empty_data`, `mapped`, `required`, `label`, `attr`, `constraints`, `error_bubbling`, `inherit_data`, `trim`, `validation_groups`) are therefore available on all form types.

references.title: IntegerType Field
references.summary: Renders an HTML5 `<input type="number">` and parses the submitted string into a PHP `int`. The `rounding_mode` option chooses between modes such as `IntegerType::ROUND_DOWN` (the default truncation), `ROUND_FLOOR`, `ROUND_HALF_UP`, etc.; `grouping` controls thousands separators in display.

references.title: LanguageType Field
references.summary: A specialized `ChoiceType` listing **Unicode language identifiers** (e.g. `fr`, `zh-Hant`). With `alpha3: true` it instead lists ISO 639-2 alpha-3 codes (`fra`); `choice_self_translation` (default `false`) renders each label in its own language instead of the user's locale.

references.title: LocaleType Field
references.summary: A specialized `ChoiceType` listing all **locales** (language+region pairs such as `en_GB`, `fr_FR`). The labels are localized into the user's locale and the choices come from the Intl component.

references.title: MoneyType Field
references.summary: Renders an HTML5 `<input type="number">` (or text input when `html5: false`) for monetary input. The `currency` option (default `EUR`) decides which currency symbol is rendered next to the field; `scale` (default `2`) sets the number of fraction digits, and `divisor` (default `1`) lets you store an integer cents value while displaying the correct decimal amount.

references.title: NumberType Field
references.summary: Renders a text input for numeric data. Options include `scale` (number of fraction digits, default `null` = locale dependent), `rounding_mode` (`NumberType::ROUND_HALF_UP`, `ROUND_DOWN`, `ROUND_FLOOR`, …), `grouping` (thousands separators), and `html5` (when `true`, renders `<input type="number">` instead of a text input).

references.title: PasswordType Field
references.summary: Renders an `<input type="password">`. By default the submitted value is **not re-rendered** when validation fails; the `always_empty` option (default `true`) controls this behaviour. The field inherits all `TextType` options.

references.title: PercentType Field
references.summary: Renders a text input for percentage values. The `type` option chooses between `fractional` (default — `0.95` is rendered as `95%`), `integer` (`95` is rendered as `95%`), or `decimal`. The `symbol` option (default `%`) controls the suffix and `scale` sets the number of fraction digits.

references.title: RadioType Field
references.summary: Renders a single `<input type="radio">`. It inherits from `CheckboxType` and is rarely used directly — `ChoiceType` with `expanded: true` and `multiple: false` builds groups of radio buttons automatically.

references.title: RangeType Field
references.summary: Field rendered as an HTML5 `<input type="range">` slider. The `attr` option is typically used to pass the `min`, `max`, and `step` HTML attributes that define the slider's allowed range and increment.

references.title: ResetType Field
references.summary: A button rendered as `<button type="reset">` that resets all fields of the surrounding form to their original values. It inherits from `ButtonType` and is **not mapped** to the underlying data.

references.title: SearchType Field
references.summary: Text field rendered as **`<input type="search">`** (the search variant of a text input — some browsers add a clear "x" icon and dedicated search styling). It otherwise behaves like `TextType`.

references.title: TelType Field
references.summary: Text field rendered as **`<input type="tel">`**. The submitted value is **not validated** — phone-number formats vary too widely between countries — so pair the field with a custom validator if validation is required.

references.title: TextType Field
references.summary: The most basic input field, rendered as `<input type="text">`. It is the parent of many specialized text-based types (`EmailType`, `PasswordType`, `UrlType`, `SearchType`, `TelType`, `ColorType`, `RangeType`, `TextareaType`).

references.title: TextareaType Field
references.summary: Renders an HTML `<textarea>`. It inherits from `TextType` and is therefore a multi-line variant of the basic text field.

references.title: TimeType Field
references.summary: Field for time input. The `widget` option chooses between `single_text` (a `<input type="time">` or text), `text` (two/three text inputs), or `choice` (two/three select boxes). `with_seconds` (default `false`) toggles the seconds component, and `input` chooses the PHP model type (`datetime` default, `datetime_immutable`, `string`, `timestamp`, `array`).

references.title: TimezoneType Field
references.summary: A specialized `ChoiceType` listing every IANA timezone identifier (e.g. `Europe/Paris`). With `intl: true` (default `false`), the choices come from the **Intl** component; with `intl: false`, they come from `DateTimeZone::listIdentifiers()`.

references.title: UlidType Field
references.summary: Renders a text input that holds a **ULID** as a string and transforms the value back to a `Symfony\Component\Uid\Ulid` instance on submit. Inherits from `FormType` (not `TextType`), so it uses `FormType` defaults but renders as `<input type="text">`.

references.title: UrlType Field
references.summary: Text field rendered as `<input type="url">`. The `default_protocol` option (default `"http"`) is **prepended automatically** to submitted values that lack a protocol; setting it to `null` disables that behaviour.

references.title: UuidType Field
references.summary: Renders a text input that holds a **UUID** as a string and transforms the value back to a `Symfony\Component\Uid\Uuid` instance on submit. Inherits from `FormType` and renders as `<input type="text">`.

references.title: WeekType Field
references.summary: Field for **ISO 8601 week numbers** such as `1984-W05`. The `widget` option chooses between `single_text` (one `<input type="week">`), `text` (two text boxes), or `choice` (two select boxes). `input` chooses the model type (`array` default, `string`, plus the years range supplied via the `years` option for the choice widget).

references.title: How to Use Data Transformers
references.summary: Introduces data transformers as the mechanism that translates a field's data between the format used by the application and the format used in the rendered HTML form. Transformers are used internally by many built-in types (e.g. DateType converts a DateTime to a yyyy-MM-dd string and back) and are skipped on fields whose inherit_data option is set to true.

references.title: How to Use Data Transformers - Example #1: Transforming Strings Form Data Tags from User Input to an Array
references.summary: Demonstrates Symfony\Component\Form\CallbackTransformer, the simplest way to attach a transformer with two callables. The first callable converts the original value to the rendered format, the second reverses the submitted value back to the model format; the transformer is registered via $builder->get('field')->addModelTransformer(...).

references.title: How to Use Data Transformers - Creating the Transformer
references.summary: A custom transformer implements DataTransformerInterface with transform() (model -> view-friendly value) and reverseTransform() (submitted value -> model). When reverseTransform() cannot convert the value it must throw TransformationFailedException; the exception's plain message is private (the user sees the field's invalid_message option). When null is passed to transform(), the implementation should return an equivalent empty value of the target type.

references.title: How to Use Data Transformers - Using the Transformer
references.summary: Transformers are attached to a specific child via $builder->get('field')->addModelTransformer($transformer); attaching them to $builder directly applies them to the entire form, which is documented as wrong. The end-user error message can be customised inside reverseTransform() by calling TransformationFailedException::setInvalidMessage($publicMessage, ['{{ value }}' => $submitted]).

references.title: How to Use Data Transformers - Creating a Reusable issue_selector Field
references.summary: When the same transformation is reused, encapsulate it inside a custom form type extending TextType (via getParent()) and call addModelTransformer() inside buildForm(). configureOptions() can also set a sensible default invalid_message; with autowire/autoconfigure the type is registered automatically as a form.type service.

references.title: How to Use Data Transformers - About Model and View Transformers
references.summary: There are three data layers (model, norm, view) and two kinds of transformers: model transformers (transform: model -> norm; reverseTransform: norm -> model) attached via addModelTransformer(), and view transformers (transform: norm -> view; reverseTransform: view -> norm) attached via addViewTransformer(). With CollectionType the underlying ResizeFormListener creates children at PRE_SET_DATA, so a model transformer cannot reduce the number of items in the collection.

references.title: How to Use Data Transformers - So why Use the Model Transformer?
references.summary: Picking the right transformer requires reasoning about what the "norm" data should be: when the field is rendered as a simple text input, the norm and view data are both scalar strings, so a transformer that converts between the model object and that scalar is naturally a model transformer.

references.title: When and How to Use Data Mappers
references.summary: Explains that data mappers read data from parent compound forms into child fields and write submitted child values back to the parent data. Contrasts mappers with data transformers, shows custom DataMapperInterface implementations for immutable value objects, and notes the getter/setter field options for special per-field mapping.

references.title: How to Configure empty Data for a Form Class
references.summary: The **`empty_data`** option supplies a value when the form is submitted without `setData()` having been called and no data was passed in. For class-bound forms, set `empty_data` to a closure (`function (FormInterface $form): YourClass`) so the constructor receives validated submitted children — handy when the data class has required constructor arguments.

references.title: Form Events
references.summary: The Form component dispatches events through Symfony's EventDispatcher at well-defined points so listeners can modify data or fields. The five constants on FormEvents are PRE_SET_DATA, POST_SET_DATA (during pre-population via Form::setData()) and PRE_SUBMIT, SUBMIT, POST_SUBMIT (during Form::handleRequest()/submit()). Listeners receive a FormEvent instance giving access to the form and the data being processed.

references.title: Form Events - The Form Workflow
references.summary: PRE_SET_DATA fires at the start of setData() and lets a listener modify the model data (the form is locked, so calling setData() again throws). POST_SET_DATA fires at the end of setData() and is the place for adding/removing fields based on the populated data. PRE_SUBMIT exposes the raw request data (and is where the CSRF and trim listeners run); SUBMIT exposes the normalized data and forbids adding/removing fields; POST_SUBMIT runs after denormalization (validation listener fires here) and likewise forbids adding fields.

references.title: Form Events - Registering Event Listeners or Event Subscribers
references.summary: Listeners and subscribers are attached to the form builder via addEventListener(FormEvents::CONST, $callable) and addEventSubscriber(EventSubscriberInterface).
