The events table maps each constant to its event data: pre_set_data and post_set_data carry model data, pre_submit carries request data, submit carries normalized data and post_submit carries view data. Subscribers list the events they handle in getSubscribedEvents().

references.title: How to Dynamically Modify Forms Using Form Events
references.summary: Top-level recipe for changing form fields based on either the bound data or the submitted data, using FormEvents listeners. Distinguishes the two scenarios ("based on the underlying data" vs. "based on user data") and links to the dedicated sections.

references.title: How to Dynamically Modify Forms - Customizing your Form Based on the Underlying Data
references.summary: When the form fields depend on the object passed to createForm(), use a PRE_SET_DATA listener (or subscriber) to inspect $event->getData() and add/remove children with $event->getForm()->add(...) or remove(). The same pattern is used to register the listener inside a form-type class via $builder->addEventListener(FormEvents::PRE_SET_DATA, [$this, 'onPreSetData']).

references.title: How to Dynamically Modify Forms - Dynamic Generation for Submitted Forms
references.summary: When the field set must depend on submitted values (e.g. cascading selects), use a PRE_SUBMIT listener that reads the raw request payload through $event->getData() and calls $event->getForm()->add(...) accordingly. SUBMIT and POST_SUBMIT cannot add or remove fields, so adjustments tied to the submission must happen on PRE_SUBMIT.

references.title: How to Create a Form Type Extension
references.summary: Form type extensions modify existing form types across the entire application without subclassing them. The two canonical use cases are: adding a specific feature to one type (e.g. extending FileType to display a thumbnail) and adding a generic feature to many types (e.g. adding help text to every text-like input).

references.title: How to Create a Form Type Extension - Defining the Form Type Extension
references.summary: An extension typically extends Symfony\Component\Form\AbstractTypeExtension (or implements FormTypeExtensionInterface). The only required method is the static getExtendedTypes() returning an iterable of the type FQCNs to extend; optional methods are buildForm(), buildView(), configureOptions() and finishView(), which behave the same as in custom form types.

references.title: How to Create a Form Type Extension - Registering your Form Type Extension as a Service
references.summary: Form type extensions must be registered as services and tagged form.type_extension; with the default services.yaml configuration this is automatic via autoconfiguration. The optional priority tag attribute (default 0) controls the load order — higher priorities load earlier — and using it requires explicit service configuration.

references.title: How to Create a Form Type Extension - Adding the extension Business Logic
references.summary: configureOptions() declares new options the extended type accepts (typically via $resolver->setDefined(['my_option'])), and buildView() adds extra Twig variables to $view->vars. The example uses PropertyAccess to read a value from the parent form's data when the consumer passes the new option.

references.title: How to Create a Form Type Extension - Using the Form Type Extension
references.summary: Once the extension is registered, every field of the extended type can use the new options (e.g. a FileType field can pass image_property => 'webPath'). No additional configuration on the consumer side is required because the extension is applied globally to all matching types.

references.title: How to Create a Form Type Extension - Generic Form Type Extensions
references.summary: Targeting a parent type extends every type that inherits from it (e.g. extending TextType also affects EmailType, SearchType, UrlType...; extending FormType affects most types except buttons such as ButtonType). Alternatively, getExtendedTypes() can return multiple type FQCNs to extend several specific types at once.

references.title: Creating a custom Type Guesser
references.summary: A **type guesser** infers the form type, required flag, max length and pattern from class metadata — Symfony ships a guesser based on Validator constraints, but you can register your own by implementing `FormTypeGuesserInterface` and tagging it `form.type_guesser`. Each guess returns a `TypeGuess`/`ValueGuess` carrying a `Guess::HIGH_CONFIDENCE` / `MEDIUM_CONFIDENCE` / `LOW_CONFIDENCE` score; the guess with the highest confidence wins.

references.title: The OptionsResolver Component
references.summary: Improved replacement for array_replace() that lets you build a full options system: defaults, required options, type validation, value validation, normalization, and more. It is the same component Symfony Forms use to declare and validate form-type options inside configureOptions(OptionsResolver $resolver).

references.title: The OptionsResolver Component - Usage
references.summary: Common pattern: build an OptionsResolver, register defaults via setDefaults() (or split into a configureOptions() method), then call resolve($options) to merge user input with defaults. Passing an unknown option throws UndefinedOptionsException, listing all defined options.

references.title: The OptionsResolver Component - Required Options
references.summary: setRequired() (string or array of strings) marks options that the caller must supply; resolving without them throws MissingOptionsException. Helpers include isRequired(), getRequiredOptions(), isMissing() (returns false once a required option has been given a default) and getMissingOptions().

references.title: The OptionsResolver Component - Type Validation
references.summary: setAllowedTypes() validates each option's type via PHP's is_<type>() functions or instanceof for class names. Multiple types can be supplied as an array or as the int|null union form, and array element types can be expressed with [] suffix (e.g. DateTime[], (int|string)[]). Sub-classes use addAllowedTypes() to extend the parent set.

references.title: The OptionsResolver Component - Value Validation
references.summary: setAllowedValues() restricts an option to a fixed list of acceptable values; an invalid value triggers InvalidOptionsException. A closure returning bool is accepted for arbitrary checks, and the Validator component can plug in via Validation::createIsValidCallable(). addAllowedValues() extends the parent set in sub-classes.

references.title: The OptionsResolver Component - Option Normalization
references.summary: setNormalizer($name, fn(Options $options, $value) => $normalized) runs after validation and may rewrite the option's value. The closure can read other options through its Options argument; addNormalizer() chains another normalizer onto an existing one (with an optional third argument to prepend instead of append).

references.title: The OptionsResolver Component - Default Values that Depend on another Option
references.summary: Passing a closure as a default makes that default lazy: it receives an Options instance (must be type-hinted, otherwise the closure is treated as the value itself) and is only evaluated if the option was not set explicitly. A second closure argument exposes the previously-set default value, which is useful when overriding parents.

references.title: The OptionsResolver Component - Options without Default Values
references.summary: setDefined() declares an option as legal without giving it a default; its key only appears in the resolved array if the user actually supplied it. This is the right tool when the code needs to distinguish "not set" from "set to the default value". Multiple options can be defined at once by passing an array; isDefined()/getDefinedOptions() inspect the configuration.

references.title: The OptionsResolver Component - Nested Options
references.summary: setOptions($name, fn(OptionsResolver $nested) => ...) defines a nested set of sub-options under a single key; required, type, value validation and normalization all work inside the inner resolver. The closure can take a second Options $parent argument to read parent options, and parent normalizers/defaults can read the nested values back as plain arrays.

references.title: The OptionsResolver Component - Prototype Options
references.summary: Inside a nested option, setPrototype(true) declares that the option expects an array of arrays — each inner array is resolved against the same set of sub-options (e.g. a connections option holding multiple {host, database, user, password} entries). Outer keys (default, test...) are arbitrary and not validated.

references.title: The OptionsResolver Component - Deprecating the Option
references.summary: setDeprecated($name, $package, $version, $message?) marks an option as deprecated. The %name% placeholder can be used in custom messages, and a closure form can deprecate only specific values or types. Deprecation messages are triggered only when the option is used (provided by user or evaluated in a lazy default/normalizer) and are visible in the profiler's Deprecations tab.

references.title: The OptionsResolver Component - Chaining Option Configurations
references.summary: The fluent define($name) method exposes a per-option chain (->required(), ->default(), ->allowedTypes(), ->allowedValues(), ->info()) so multiple configurations for the same option can be expressed without repeating its name.

references.title: FormType Field
references.summary: Reference for options defined by the root FormType and inherited by most form fields, including allow_extra_fields, by_reference, data_class, empty_data, error_mapping, getter, setter, mapped, method, property_path, required and validation_groups. Documents how default empty_data depends on data_class, required and compound, and points to the callback mapping options used when property_path is not enough.

references.title: The Validator Component
references.summary: Introduces the standalone Validator component, installed via `composer require symfony/validator`. It is built around two concepts: constraints (rules) and validators (classes containing the validation logic). The component is based on the JSR-303 Bean Validation specification.

references.title: The Validator Component - Retrieving a Validator Instance
references.summary: Shows how `Validation::createValidator()` returns a validator implementing `ValidatorInterface`. The basic instance can validate scalars and arrays against constraints but not objects, which require additional configuration via `ValidatorBuilder`.

references.title: Validation - Using the Validator Service
references.summary: Explains how the `validator` service (`ValidatorInterface`) returns a `ConstraintViolationList` from `validate()`. The list implements `Countable`, supports iteration, and casting to string yields a human-readable error summary used in templates.

references.title: The Validator Component - Metadata
references.summary: Describes `ClassMetadata`, which represents and manages all configured constraints on a class. Constraints can be added at the property, getter, or class level via `addPropertyConstraint()`, `addGetterConstraint()`, and `addConstraint()`.

references.title: The Validator Component - Loading Resources
references.summary: Documents the loaders that collect validation metadata: `StaticMethodLoader`, `XmlFileLoader`, `YamlFileLoader`, and `AttributeLoader`. The `ValidatorBuilder` chains loaders, supports a PSR-6 mapping cache and a custom `MetadataFactoryInterface` implementation.

references.title: Validation
references.summary: Top-level page introducing the Validator component, installation via Symfony Flex, the JSR-303 inspiration, and the workflow of mapping constraints onto objects and validating them through the `validator` service.

references.title: Validation - Validation Callables
references.summary: Documents `Validation::createCallable()` which returns a closure throwing `ValidationFailedException` when constraints fail, and `Validation::createIsValidCallable()` which returns a closure returning `false` on failure. Useful for Console question validators and OptionsResolver value normalization.

references.title: How to Validate Raw Values (Scalar Values and Arrays)
references.summary: Shows how to call `$validator->validate($value, $constraint)` to validate scalars (e.g. an `Email` instance against a string) and arrays via the `Collection` constraint. The result is a `ConstraintViolationList` exposing each `ConstraintViolation`'s `getMessage()`.

references.title: Validation - Validating Object With Inheritance
references.summary: Explains that constraints defined on a parent class are automatically merged into a subclass, even when the child redefines its own constraints on the same property. Validation groups are the recommended way to override that merge.

references.title: Validation - Extending Validation for a Class
references.summary: Covers the `#[ExtendsValidationFor(Target::class)]` attribute that lets you declare constraints on a class you cannot modify (e.g. a third-party model). Constraints can only target properties that exist on the target class, otherwise a `MappingException` is thrown.

references.title: Validation - Supported Constraints
references.summary: Provides the index of constraints bundled with Symfony, grouped into Basic Constraints (`NotBlank`, `Type`, etc.), String, Comparison, Number, Date, Choice, File, Financial, Other, and Collection constraints. Custom constraints are introduced from this page.

references.title: Validation - Constraint Configuration
references.summary: Demonstrates how to configure constraints with options such as `Choice(choices: [...], message: '...')` using PHP attributes, YAML, XML, or PHP loadValidatorMetadata. Options control accepted values and customize the violation message.

references.title: NotBlank
references.summary: Validates that a value is not blank (empty string, empty array, `false` or `null` when `allowNull` is `false`). To accept `null`, set `allowNull: true`. For null-only checks use `NotNull` instead.

references.title: Length
references.summary: Validates that a string length is between `min` and `max`, equals `exactly`, or matches a `charset`. The `countUnit` option chooses between `COUNT_BYTES`, `COUNT_CODEPOINTS` (default), and `COUNT_GRAPHEMES`. `null` is always considered valid.

references.title: Choice
references.summary: Validates that a value is in a list given via `choices` or by a `callback` function. With `multiple: true` the value must be an array; `min` and `max` then limit the number of selections. The `match` option inverts the constraint.

references.title: Range
references.summary: Validates that a number or `DateTime` falls between `min` and `max`. Both bounds may be omitted to create open-ended ranges, and date strings accepted by the `DateTime` constructor can be used to compare against time ranges.

references.title: Type
references.summary: Validates that a value is of a specific PHP type or class instance using functions like `is_string`, `is_int`, `ctype_alpha`, or `instanceof`. It is best used on untyped variables since typed properties already enforce their types at the language level.

references.title: Email
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid email address. The `mode` option selects between `html5` (default), `html5-allow-no-tld`, and `strict` (RFC 5322 via egulias/email-validator). Empty values pass; combine with `NotBlank` to forbid them.

references.title: Valid
references.summary: Cascades validation into nested objects/collections referenced by a property. Unlike most constraints it does not belong to the `Default` group, so it is always applied regardless of the requested groups unless `groups` is explicitly set.

references.title: Collection
references.summary: Validates array-like or traversable data where different keys need different constraints. It also controls whether missing or extra keys are allowed and supports `Required` and `Optional` wrappers for field-level presence rules.

references.title: All
references.summary: Applies the same set of constraints to every element of an array or `Traversable` object. This is useful when each member of a collection must satisfy identical rules such as `NotBlank` and `Length`.

references.title: Count
references.summary: Validates the number of elements in an array or `Countable` object using `min`, `max`, `divisibleBy`, and exact-count behavior. Its messages expose placeholders such as `{{ count }}` and `{{ limit }}` for the current size and expected bounds.

references.title: Regex
references.summary: Validates that a value matches a regular expression, or does not match it when `match: false` is configured. Empty values are considered valid, and `htmlPattern` can customize the generated HTML5 `pattern` attribute.

references.title: Url
references.summary: Validates URL syntax without checking whether the host actually exists in DNS. Options such as `protocols` and `relativeProtocol` control which schemes are accepted and whether protocol-relative URLs are valid.

references.title: File
references.summary: Validates existing file paths, stringable file paths, and HttpFoundation `File` or `UploadedFile` objects. It can enforce size limits and should generally use `extensions` instead of raw `mimeTypes` checks for safer file validation.

references.title: AtLeastOneOf
references.summary: Checks that a value satisfies at least one constraint from a configured list and stops once one succeeds. Its failure message can include internal constraint messages, controlled by `includeInternalMessages`.

references.title: When
references.summary: Applies constraints conditionally based on an expression and can apply alternative constraints through `otherwise` when the expression is false. Expressions can access variables such as `this`, `value`, and `context`.

references.title: Cascade
references.summary: Applies at class scope to validate the whole object graph, including objects stored in properties, without adding `Valid` to each child property. The `groups` option is not available, while `exclude` can omit selected properties from cascade validation.

references.title: Unique
references.summary: Validates that elements in an array or `Traversable` collection are unique, using strict comparison by default. The `fields`, `normalizer`, `errorPath`, and `stopOnFirstError` options customize how uniqueness is computed and reported.

references.title: Expression
references.summary: Uses Symfony's ExpressionLanguage for dynamic class-level or property-level validation. The expression must evaluate successfully for validation to pass, and the `value` variable refers to the validated property when applied at property scope.

references.title: Compound
references.summary: Provides a base constraint for creating reusable named bundles of other constraints by extending `Compound`. It cannot be used on its own; subclasses return the composed constraints from `getConstraints()` and may expose configuration as public properties.

references.title: Bic
references.summary: Validates that a value matches the **Business Identifier Code (BIC)** format. The `iban` and `ibanPropertyPath` options additionally cross-check that the BIC's country code matches the country code of a paired IBAN. The `mode` option accepts `Bic::VALIDATION_MODE_STRICT` (default) or `Bic::VALIDATION_MODE_CASE_INSENSITIVE`, which uppercases the value before validating.

references.title: Blank
references.summary: Validates that a value is blank, meaning equal to an empty string `""` or `null`. It is the inverse use case of `NotBlank`, but the two are not strictly opposites because `Blank` accepts only those two values. To require strictly `null`, use `IsNull` instead.

references.title: Callback
references.summary: Defines a custom validation method that runs during validation and adds violations directly through the `ExecutionContextInterface`. The `callback` option accepts a method name on the validated class, an `[Class, method]` array, or a `Closure`; using a closure with PHP attributes disables attribute caching for that target. It applies to a class, property, or method, including static methods that receive the validated object as the first argument.

references.title: CardScheme
references.summary: Validates that a value is a credit-card number that matches one of the configured card networks. The `schemes` option is **required** and accepts a string or array of constants like `VISA`, `MASTERCARD`, `AMEX`, `DISCOVER`, `DINERS`, `JCB`, `MAESTRO`, `MIR`, `UATP`, `CHINA_UNIONPAY`, `INSTAPAYMENT`, `LASER`. Empty values are valid; combine with `NotBlank` if needed.

references.title: Charset
references.summary: Validates that a string (or `Stringable` object) is encoded in one of the configured character sets. The `encodings` option accepts a single encoding name or an array — if any of them matches according to `mb_detect_encoding()`, validation passes. The default error message exposes `{{ detected }}` and `{{ encodings }}` placeholders.

references.title: Cidr
references.summary: Validates that a string is a valid **CIDR** (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) notation. By default both IPv4 and IPv6 are accepted; the `version` option restricts which IP version is allowed and supports the same range tokens as the `Ip` constraint (e.g. `4`, `6`, `all`, `4_no_priv`). The `netmaskMin` and `netmaskMax` options bound the netmask value, with `netmaskMax` defaulting to `32` for IPv4 or `128` for IPv6.

references.title: Country
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid country code. By default it expects an **ISO 3166-1 alpha-2** two-letter code (e.g. `FR`); enabling the `alpha3` option switches to the **ISO 3166-1 alpha-3** three-letter code (e.g. `FRA`). Empty values are valid; combine with `NotBlank` to forbid them.

references.title: CssColor
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid CSS color. The `formats` option restricts which CSS color notations are accepted; constants such as `Assert\CssColor::HEX_LONG`, `HEX_SHORT`, `BASIC_NAMED_COLORS`, `EXTENDED_NAMED_COLORS`, `SYSTEM_COLORS`, `KEYWORDS`, `RGB`, `RGBA`, `HSL`, and `HSLA` enumerate the allowed formats. Without `formats`, any of those formats is accepted.

references.title: Currency
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **3-letter ISO 4217** currency code (e.g. `EUR`, `USD`). It applies to a property or method and rejects values that are not registered ISO 4217 codes. Empty values are accepted; pair with `NotBlank` to forbid them.

references.title: Date
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid date string in the **`Y-m-d`** format (e.g. `2023-10-18`). It accepts strings or `Stringable` objects; if a custom format is required, use the `DateTime` constraint. Empty values are valid.

references.title: DateTime
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid datetime string. The `format` option (default `Y-m-d H:i:s`) accepts any format string supported by `DateTime::createFromFormat()`. The default message exposes `{{ value }}` and `{{ format }}` placeholders.

references.title: DisableAutoMapping
references.summary: Disables Doctrine's validation auto-mapping for a class or property when auto-mapping is **enabled globally**. It tells the validator not to derive validation rules from Doctrine ORM mapping attributes on the targeted scope. The `groups` option is unavailable for this constraint.

references.title: DivisibleBy
references.summary: Validates that a number is divisible by another value. The `value` option (or constructor argument) sets the divisor and may be an integer or float (e.g. `0.25` for quarter-step weights). It supports `propertyPath` to compare against another property of the same object.

references.title: EnableAutoMapping
references.summary: Enables Doctrine's validation auto-mapping for a class or property when auto-mapping is **disabled globally**. It tells the validator to derive validation rules from Doctrine ORM mapping attributes on the targeted scope. The `groups` option is not available for this constraint.

references.title: EqualTo
references.summary: Validates that a value is equal to another value using **loose comparison (`==`)**. The reference value is set through the `value` option or constructor argument, or via `propertyPath` to point at another property. Use `IdenticalTo` instead when type-strict comparison is required.

references.title: ExpressionSyntax
references.summary: Validates that a string contains a syntactically valid Symfony **ExpressionLanguage** expression. The `allowedVariables` option restricts which variables the expression may reference; setting it to `null` (the default) allows any variables. Useful when end-users write expressions stored in configuration.

references.title: GreaterThan
references.summary: Validates that a value is **strictly greater than** another value. The compared value can be a literal in the `value` option/constructor argument or another property via `propertyPath`. It also compares `DateTime` objects with any string accepted by the `DateTime` constructor (e.g. `"today"`, `"+5 hours"`, `"today UTC"`).

references.title: GreaterThanOrEqual
references.summary: Validates that a value is **greater than or equal to** another value. Like `GreaterThan`, the comparison value may be supplied via `value` or `propertyPath`, and `DateTime` properties accept any string accepted by the `DateTime` constructor (e.g. `today`, `+5 hours`).

references.title: Hostname
references.summary: Validates that a string is a valid hostname. Internally it uses `filter_var()` with `FILTER_VALIDATE_DOMAIN`. The `requireTld` option (default `true`) requires a top-level domain such as `example.com`; when set to `false`, single-label hostnames such as `example` are accepted. RFC 2606 reserved TLDs (`.example`, `.invalid`, `.localhost`, `.test`) are rejected.

references.title: IBAN
references.summary: Validates that a value is a syntactically valid **International Bank Account Number (IBAN)**. The validator transparently strips various whitespace characters (regular, non-breaking, narrow non-breaking) before validating, so the same input may be accepted while still failing strict downstream APIs. Empty values are considered valid.

references.title: IdenticalTo
references.summary: Validates that a value is identical to another value using **strict comparison (`===`)** — both type and value must match. The compared value comes from the `value` option/constructor argument or `propertyPath`. Use `EqualTo` instead when loose `==` comparison is desired.

references.title: Image
references.summary: Extends the `File` constraint with image-specific defaults — `mimeTypes` defaults to `image/*` — and adds dimension/orientation rules. Options like `minWidth`/`maxWidth`/`minHeight`/`maxHeight` cap pixel dimensions, `minRatio`/`maxRatio` cap the aspect ratio, `minPixels`/`maxPixels` cap the total pixel count, and `allowLandscape`/`allowPortrait`/`allowSquare` reject specific orientations. Setting `detectCorrupted` to `true` runs `imagecreatefromstring()` to detect broken image bytes (requires the GD extension).

references.title: Ip
references.summary: Validates that a string is a valid IP address. The `version` option chooses the accepted address family and range — for example `4`, `6`, `all`, `4_no_priv` (no RFC 1918 ranges), `4_public`, `6_reserved`, `all_public`. The default is `4`, so IPv6 addresses fail unless `version` is changed.

references.title: IsFalse
references.summary: Validates that a value is **`false`**, exactly the integer `0`, or exactly the string `"0"`. Often used on boolean getter methods (e.g. `isStateInvalid()`) so that the surrounding object is valid only when the getter returns one of those falsy values. `null` is treated as valid.

references.title: IsNull
references.summary: Validates that a value is exactly `null`. To accept the empty string in addition to `null`, use `Blank`; to accept any non-`null` value, use `NotNull`.

references.title: IsTrue
references.summary: Validates that a value is **`true`**, exactly the integer `1`, or exactly the string `"1"`. Commonly applied to a boolean getter method to enforce that some derived state is true. `null` is treated as valid.

references.title: Isbn
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **International Standard Book Number (ISBN)**. The `type` option selects between `ISBN_10`, `ISBN_13`, or no value (which accepts either). Different message options (`isbn10Message`, `isbn13Message`, `bothIsbnMessage`) tailor the error per mode.

references.title: Isin
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **International Securities Identification Number (ISIN)** — a 12-character alphanumeric code identifying a security. It applies to a property or method.

references.title: Issn
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **International Standard Serial Number (ISSN)**. The `caseSensitive` option (default `false`) controls whether a final `x` checksum digit must be uppercase. The `requireHyphen` option (default `false`) requires the canonical `XXXX-XXXX` form.

references.title: Json
references.summary: Validates that a string contains syntactically valid **JSON**. It does not validate schema or shape — only that `json_decode()` parses the input without errors.

references.title: Language
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **Unicode language identifier** (e.g. `fr` or `zh-Hant`). With `alpha3` set to `true` it instead expects ISO 639-2 alpha-3 codes (e.g. `fra`).

references.title: LessThan
references.summary: Validates that a value is **strictly less than** another value. As with the other comparison constraints, the comparison target is given by `value` (or constructor argument) or `propertyPath`, and `DateTime` properties may be compared against any string accepted by `new DateTime(...)`.

references.title: LessThanOrEqual
references.summary: Validates that a value is **less than or equal to** another value. Use `LessThan` for a strict bound, or `LessThanOrEqual` when the bound itself must be accepted. Like the other comparison constraints, it accepts both literal values and `propertyPath` references and supports `DateTime` comparisons via `DateTime`-constructor strings.

references.title: Locale
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **locale** identifier (a language code optionally combined with a region/script, e.g. `en`, `fr_FR`, `zh_Hant`). It applies to a property or method.

references.title: Luhn
references.summary: Validates that a numeric string passes the **Luhn algorithm** checksum, used as a quick sanity check on credit-card-like numbers before sending them to a payment gateway. It does not check that the number belongs to a real account or matches a specific scheme — combine it with `CardScheme` for that.

references.title: MacAddress
references.summary: Validates that a string is a valid **MAC address**. Internally it uses `filter_var()` with `FILTER_VALIDATE_MAC`, so any of the formats accepted by that PHP filter (colon-, hyphen-, or dot-separated) are valid.

references.title: Negative
references.summary: Validates that a value is a **negative number**, i.e. strictly less than zero. Zero itself is **not** accepted; use `NegativeOrZero` if zero must be allowed.

references.title: NegativeOrZero
references.summary: Validates that a value is a **negative number or zero**. Use `Negative` instead if zero must be rejected.

references.title: NoSuspiciousCharacters
references.summary: Validates that a string does not contain characters used in **spoofing attacks** — invisible characters (zero-width spaces) or visually confusable characters from mixed scripts. The `restrictionLevel` option controls Unicode script restriction (e.g. ASCII-only, single-script, highly restrictive), `locales` lists the allowed locales, and `checks` enables specific suspicious-character checks.

references.title: NotCompromisedPassword
references.summary: Validates that a password has not appeared in any **haveibeenpwned.com** public data breach. The `threshold` option sets how many leak occurrences are tolerated (default `1`, so any leaked password is rejected). When `skipOnError` is `true`, network failures while contacting the service are silently ignored instead of failing validation.

references.title: NotEqualTo
references.summary: Validates that a value is **not equal** to another value, using PHP loose comparison (`!=`). The reference is supplied via `value` or `propertyPath`. Use `NotIdenticalTo` for strict (`!==`) comparison.

references.title: NotIdenticalTo
references.summary: Validates that a value is **not identical** to another value, using PHP strict comparison (`!==`). It rejects values that match the reference both in type and value, and is paired with `IdenticalTo` for the inverse use case.

references.title: NotNull
references.summary: Validates that a value is **not strictly equal to `null`**. Empty strings, empty arrays, `0`, and `false` are accepted. Use `NotBlank` if those should also be rejected.

references.title: PasswordStrength
references.summary: Validates that a password reaches a minimum strength score. Strength is measured by **password entropy**, not by hard rules like "must contain a digit". The `minScore` option (default `STRENGTH_MEDIUM`) accepts the constants `STRENGTH_VERY_WEAK`, `STRENGTH_WEAK`, `STRENGTH_MEDIUM`, `STRENGTH_STRONG`, `STRENGTH_VERY_STRONG`.

references.title: Positive
references.summary: Validates that a value is a **positive number**, i.e. strictly greater than zero. Zero is **not** accepted; use `PositiveOrZero` to allow it.

references.title: PositiveOrZero
references.summary: Validates that a value is a **positive number or zero**. Use `Positive` instead when zero must be rejected.

references.title: Sequentially
references.summary: Applies a list of constraints **step by step** and stops at the first violation, so later constraints only run if earlier ones passed. This avoids redundant errors and is useful when the next check would itself fail or be expensive when the input is already invalid (e.g. `NotBlank` then `Length` then `Regex`).

references.title: Time
references.summary: Validates that a string is a valid time in the **`H:i:s`** format (e.g. `16:27:36`). The `withSeconds` option (default `true`) controls whether the seconds component is required; setting it to `false` accepts the `H:i` form.

references.title: Timezone
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid timezone identifier (e.g. `Europe/Paris`). The `zone` option restricts the timezone group via `DateTimeZone` constants such as `DateTimeZone::EUROPE`. The `countryCode` option further restricts to identifiers of a specific country (paired with `zone: DateTimeZone::PER_COUNTRY`). The `intlCompatible` option requires the value to also be valid under the Intl extension.

references.title: Traverse
references.summary: Class-level constraint that asks the validator to traverse the object as a `Traversable` and validate every yielded element. Useful when an object is iterable but its inner data is not exposed through public properties or accessors. It only applies to a class.

references.title: Twig Constraint
references.summary: Validates that a string contains syntactically valid **Twig** template source. Useful when template fragments come from configuration or user input and you want to fail fast before they ever reach the renderer.

references.title: ULID
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **Universally Unique Lexicographically Sortable Identifier (ULID)**. The `format` option accepts representation constants such as `Ulid::FORMAT_BASE_32` (the default 26-char base32 form), `Ulid::FORMAT_BASE_58`, `Ulid::FORMAT_RFC_4122`, and `Ulid::FORMAT_ALL` for any of them.

references.title: UniqueEntity
references.summary: Class-level constraint that ensures one or several **Doctrine** entity fields are unique in the database. The `fields` option (required) lists the fields to check together; `entityClass` overrides the queried entity, `repositoryMethod` swaps in a custom finder, `errorPath` chooses on which property the violation is reported, and `ignoreNull` toggles whether `null` values participate in uniqueness checks.

references.title: UserPassword
references.summary: Validates that a string equals the **currently authenticated user's password**. Typically used in a "change password" form where the user must re-enter their old password. It only applies when there is an authenticated user and a configured password hasher.

references.title: UUID
references.summary: Validates that a value is a valid **UUID** per RFC 4122 by default. The `versions` option restricts which UUID versions are accepted (e.g. `[Uuid::V4_RANDOM, Uuid::V7_UNIX_TIME]`). Setting `strict` to `false` accepts non-RFC formats used by other systems (e.g. PostgreSQL UUIDs without dashes).

references.title: Video
references.summary: Extends `File` with video-specific validation. It accepts pixel-dimension and aspect-ratio bounds (`minWidth`/`maxWidth`, `minHeight`/`maxHeight`, `minPixels`/`maxPixels`, `minRatio`/`maxRatio`), orientation toggles (`allowLandscape`, `allowPortrait`, `allowSquare`), `mimeTypes`, and the `allowedCodecs` and `allowedContainers` options that restrict which encoded streams and container formats pass.

references.title: Week
references.summary: Validates that a string is a valid **ISO-8601** week number such as `2025-W01`. The `min` and `max` options bound the accepted week (also expressed as `YYYY-Www` strings) and produce dedicated `tooLowMessage`/`tooHighMessage` violations.

references.title: WordCount
references.summary: Validates that a string contains a configured number of **words**. The `min` and `max` options bound the count, while the `locale` option drives `IntlBreakIterator`-based word segmentation so that languages with non-space-separated words (e.g. Japanese) are counted correctly.

references.title: Yaml
references.summary: Validates that a string is syntactically valid **YAML**. The `flags` option accepts a bitmask of `Symfony\Component\Yaml\Yaml::PARSE_*` flags (e.g. `PARSE_OBJECT_FOR_MAP`) so the validator runs the parser with the same options the application will use at runtime.

references.title: Validation - Constraint Targets
references.summary: Defines the three constraint targets supported by Symfony: properties, getter methods, and the entire class. Each target represents a different scope the constraint is applied to during validation.

references.title: Validation - Properties
references.summary: Property-level constraints can validate public, protected, or private properties. The validator uses reflection and treats uninitialized typed properties as `null`, which can trigger unexpected violations.

references.title: Validation - Getters
references.summary: Constraints may target methods whose name starts with `get`, `is`, or `has`. In YAML, XML, and PHP mappings the prefix is omitted from the property name so a constraint can be moved between a getter and a property without changing the configuration.

references.title: Validation - Classes
references.summary: Some constraints (like `Callback`) apply to the whole class. When a class-level constraint is validated, the validator passes the entire object so that custom logic can inspect multiple properties at once.

references.title: The Validator Component - Metadata - Getters
references.summary: Shows that `ClassMetadata::addGetterConstraint($propertyName, $constraint)` registers a constraint on a public method whose name starts with `get`, `has`, or `is`. The mapping uses the property name without the prefix.

references.title: How to Apply only a Subset of all Your Validation Constraints (Validation Groups)
references.summary: Documents validation groups: a constraint without a `groups` option belongs to the `Default` group, and the class-name group is an alias of `Default` except when the class is embedded in another via `Valid`. The third argument of `validate()` selects the groups to run.

references.title: How to Sequentially Apply Validation Groups
references.summary: Describes `#[Assert\GroupSequence([...])]` and `ClassMetadata::setGroupSequence()`, which validate groups in order and stop on the first failing group. With a sequence the `Default` group references the sequence itself, so you must use the class-name group inside it.

references.title: How to Sequentially Apply Validation Groups - Group Sequence Providers
references.summary: Lets a class implement `GroupSequenceProviderInterface::getGroupSequence()` to return a dynamic group list at validation time. The class is then marked with `#[Assert\GroupSequenceProvider]` (or `setGroupSequenceProvider(true)`); a nested array enables parallel groups.

references.title: How to Sequentially Apply Validation Groups - Advanced Validation Group Provider
references.summary: Shows how to externalize the sequence logic into a `GroupProviderInterface` service referenced via `#[Assert\GroupSequenceProvider(provider: ProviderClass::class)]`. The service is autowired or tagged with `validator.group_provider`.

references.title: Sequentially
references.summary: Wraps multiple constraints so they are validated step-by-step on a single property and stops at the first violation. Useful to avoid `UnexpectedValueException`, redundant errors, or expensive checks; complements `GroupSequence` for property-scoped sequencing.

references.title: Callback
references.summary: Defines the `Callback` constraint, applied to a class or property/method, whose configured method receives an `ExecutionContextInterface` (and the validated value when the callback is static) and adds violations directly. The `callback` option accepts a method name, an array `[Class, method]` callable, or a closure (closures disable attribute caching).

references.title: How to Create a Custom Validation Constraint
references.summary: Walks through extending `Symfony\Component\Validator\Constraint` and declaring `#[\Attribute]` plus options as public properties. Symfony resolves the validator class via `validatedBy()` which defaults to the constraint class name suffixed with `Validator`.

references.title: How to Create a Custom Validation Constraint - Creating the Validator itself
references.summary: Shows how to extend `ConstraintValidator` and implement `validate(mixed $value, Constraint $constraint): void`. Validators should ignore `null`/empty values, throw `UnexpectedTypeException` when the constraint type is wrong and `UnexpectedValueException` when the value type is wrong.

references.title: How to Create a Custom Validation Constraint - Class Constraint Validator
references.summary: To validate the entire object, override `getTargets()` to return `self::CLASS_CONSTRAINT`. The validator then receives the object as the first argument; use `$this->context->buildViolation(...)->atPath('user.email')->addViolation()` to attribute the violation to a sub-property.

references.title: How to Handle Different Error Levels
references.summary: Describes how to attach an arbitrary error level (e.g. `severity` => `error`/`warning`) through the `payload` option of any constraint, and how to read it back from `ConstraintViolation::getConstraint()->payload` to render errors differently.

references.title: How to Create a Custom Validation Constraint - Creating the Validator itself
references.summary: Documents the violation builder API: `$this->context->buildViolation($message)` returns a `ConstraintViolationBuilderInterface`, which exposes `setParameter()`, `atPath()`, and `addViolation()` to push the violation into the execution context.

references.title: How to Translate Validation Constraint Messages
references.summary: Shows that constraint messages are translated through the `validators` translation domain. Builders can use `setTranslationDomain()` and `TranslatableMessage` to customize translation, or be disabled with `disableTranslation()` on the violation builder.

references.title: How to Translate Validation Constraint Messages - Custom Translation Domain
references.summary: Explains how `framework.validation.translation_domain` changes the default translation domain globally, while individual violations can override it from a validator with `$this->context->buildViolation($message)->setTranslationDomain('validation_errors')`.

references.title: The DependencyInjection Component
references.summary: Overview of the standalone DependencyInjection component and how to register services and parameters with the ContainerBuilder. Explains that the component is PSR-11 compatible and shows constructor argument injection, setter injection, and parameter substitution.

references.title: The DependencyInjection Component - Getting Services That Don't Exist
references.summary: Documents the invalid reference behaviors used when fetching missing services, including EXCEPTION_ON_INVALID_REFERENCE (default), RUNTIME_EXCEPTION_ON_INVALID_REFERENCE, NULL_ON_INVALID_REFERENCE, IGNORE_ON_INVALID_REFERENCE, and IGNORE_ON_UNINITIALIZED_REFERENCE.

references.title: Container Building Workflow
references.summary: Describes how the framework builds the service container: cache check, application-level configuration loading, bundle extension processing, compiler passes for cross-bundle interaction, and final compilation with caching.

references.title: How to work with Service Definition Objects
references.summary: Explains the Definition object that describes how the container should build a service. Covers hasDefinition, getDefinition, findDefinition, register, setArgument/addArgument/replaceArgument, addMethodCall, and setFile to manipulate definitions during compilation.

references.title: Service Container
references.summary: Main reference for using Symfony's service container: type-hinting services in controllers and services, default services.yaml with autowire/autoconfigure and the App\ resource loader, and the basic dependency injection workflow.

references.title: Types of Injection
references.summary: Compares constructor injection, immutable-setter injection, setter injection, and property injection, listing the advantages and disadvantages of each style for required vs optional dependencies.

references.title: Service Method Calls and Setter Injection
references.summary: Explains the calls option for setter injection in YAML and PHP, including the !returns_clone tag to support immutable wither methods that return a new instance.

references.title: How to Create Service Aliases and Mark Services as Public
references.summary: Covers public versus private services, the default visibility, the AsAlias attribute and YAML alias syntax, anonymous (inlined) services, and how to deprecate services and aliases.

references.title: Lazy Services
references.summary: Documents how to mark a service as lazy so the container injects a proxy that only instantiates the underlying object on first use. Covers the lazy YAML option, Autoconfigure(lazy: true), the Lazy attribute, and interface proxifying for final classes.

references.title: How to Manage Common Dependencies with Parent Services
references.summary: Shows how an abstract parent service definition shares arguments and method calls with child services through the parent option. Notes that shared, abstract, and tags are not inherited and explains how index_N overrides specific arguments.

references.title: How to Define Non Shared Services
references.summary: Explains that container services are shared by default so each fetch returns the same instance, and shows how setting shared: false (or Autoconfigure(shared: false)) makes the container return a new instance on every retrieval.

references.title: How to Inject Instances into the Container
references.summary: Explains synthetic services, which are declared at compile time but injected at runtime via Container::set(). Used for objects like the kernel that the container cannot construct itself.

references.title: How to Configure a Service with a Configurator
references.summary: Describes the configurator option that runs a callable after a service is instantiated to apply complex or shared post-instantiation setup. Shows method-and-service form and invokable-configurator form.

references.title: How to Make Service Arguments/References Optional
references.summary: Explains how to handle missing optional services using the null strategy (nullOnInvalid) and the ignore strategy (@? or ignoreOnInvalid), including how the latter removes the surrounding method call when used in calls.

references.title: Container Building Workflow
references.summary: Walks through how the container is **built** in the framework: extensions load YAML/XML/PHP into a `ContainerBuilder`, compiler passes run in their priority/types (`PassConfig::TYPE_BEFORE_OPTIMIZATION`, `TYPE_OPTIMIZE`, `TYPE_BEFORE_REMOVING`, `TYPE_REMOVE`, `TYPE_AFTER_REMOVING`), and the result is dumped to PHP for production use.

references.title: How to Debug the Service Container & List Services
references.summary: Documents the **`debug:container`** and **`debug:autowiring`** console commands. `debug:container <id>` prints definition details; `--show-arguments` includes constructor arguments; `--tag=<tag>` lists every service tagged with the given tag; `--parameters` shows container parameters; `debug:autowiring [type]` shows autowireable types/aliases.

references.title: Service Container - Fetching and using Services
references.summary: Shows that the container is preloaded with many services such as logger, request_stack, and router.default that can be injected by type-hinting their interface or class. Documents the debug:autowiring command that lists every autowireable type.

references.title: How to Debug the Service Container & List Services
references.summary: Documents the debug:container console command (with --show-hidden and --tag filters) and debug:autowiring for inspecting registered services, hidden services, and tagged service collections.

references.title: How to Retrieve the Request from the Service Container
references.summary: Explains why services should depend on the request_stack service (RequestStack::getCurrentRequest()) instead of the Request object directly, since the Request is not a regular service in the container.

references.title: Built-in Symfony Service Tags
references.summary: Reference catalogue of every **service tag** Symfony recognises — `console.command`, `kernel.event_listener`, `kernel.event_subscriber`, `controller.argument_value_resolver`, `twig.extension`, `serializer.normalizer`, `validator.constraint_validator`, `messenger.message_handler`, `monolog.processor`, `routing.loader`, etc. Lists the attributes each tag accepts (priority, event, method, alias) and what processing the corresponding compiler pass applies.

references.title: Service Container - Service Parameters
references.summary: Documents container parameters: the @ prefix in YAML to inject services, the parameter accessor methods (hasParameter, getParameter, setParameter), and the rule that parameters can only be set before the container is compiled, not at runtime.

references.title: Service Container - Binding Arguments by Name or Type
references.summary: Describes the bind keyword in _defaults to set values for any argument matching a name (e.g. $adminEmail), a type (e.g. Psr\Log\LoggerInterface), or both, including the special string $name and tagged_iterator forms.

references.title: Service Container - Abstract Service Arguments
references.summary: Explains the !abstract argument type used when an argument cannot be defined in configuration but is filled in by a compiler pass or extension. A RuntimeException is thrown if the abstract argument is not replaced before runtime.

references.title: Using Parameters within a Dependency Injection Class
references.summary: Explains how parameters such as %kernel.debug% behave when used in bundle Configuration nodes. Default values are passed as raw strings, so the parameter must be injected through the Extension's getConfiguration() method to be evaluated correctly.

references.title: How to Inject Values Based on Complex Expressions
references.summary: Documents the @= prefix for injecting expression-language values into arguments and properties. Provides the service(), parameter(), and env() functions plus the container variable, and lists the contexts where expressions are accepted.

references.title: Compiling the Container - Resolving Environment Variable At Compile Time
references.summary: Explains how to force env placeholder resolution during container compilation with resolveEnvPlaceholders(..., true). Warns that this is discouraged because changing the environment variable value then requires clearing the container cache.

references.title: Configuring Symfony - Configuration Parameters
references.summary: Explains reusable configuration parameters in Symfony applications, including the parameters key, %parameter_name% references, supported value types, escaping literal percent signs, dot-prefixed compile-time parameters, and parameterCannotBeEmpty().

references.title: Configuring Symfony - Accessing Configuration Parameters
references.summary: Shows how to access configuration parameters from services and controllers by injecting them as constructor arguments. Covers parameter autowiring through bind and the Autowire attribute.

references.title: Environment Variable Processors - Built-In Environment Variable Processors
references.summary: Lists Symfony's built-in env var processors for converting string environment variables into values such as bools, integers, floats, constants, base64 data, JSON arrays, and resolved parameter-containing strings.

references.title: Environment Variable Processors - Custom Environment Variable Processors
references.summary: Explains how to create custom environment variable processors by implementing EnvVarProcessorInterface and tagging the service with container.env_var_processor. Shows how getEnv() receives the prefix, variable name, getter closure, and expected type map.

references.title: How to work with Service Definition Objects
references.summary: A **`Definition`** describes how the container should build a service: class, arguments, calls, configurator, public/private, lazy, decorator, factory, parent, etc. It is the in-memory representation manipulated by compiler passes; the container reads `Definition` objects when generating the optimised dumped container.

references.title: Service Container - Creating/Configuring Services in the Container
references.summary: Shows the default services.yaml _defaults block (autowire, autoconfigure) and the App\ resource loader that registers every class in src/ as a private service. Explains the exclude option and the Exclude attribute.

references.title: Service Container - Manually Wiring Arguments
references.summary: Explains how to override individual constructor arguments in YAML/PHP configuration using the arguments key with the $name syntax for non-autowireable scalars, while leaving the remaining arguments to autowiring.

references.title: Service Container - Limiting Services to a specific Symfony Environment
references.summary: Documents the When and WhenNot attributes and the when@<env> YAML block that restricts (or excludes) a service from registration in given Symfony environments. Notes that _defaults must be redeclared inside each when@<env> block.

references.title: Service Container - Importing Many Services at once with resource
references.summary: Explains how the resource and exclude options bulk-register classes by namespace, the use of glob patterns, and the Exclude attribute. Imported services are registered with their FQCN as service id and stay private unless explicitly marked public.

references.title: Service Container - Explicitly Configuring Services and Arguments
references.summary: Shows how to register multiple services for the same class under different ids with explicit arguments, and how to add an alias from the class to a chosen id so type-hinting still works deterministically.

references.title: How to Import Configuration Files/Resources
references.summary: Documents the imports YAML directive and PHP equivalent for splitting service configuration across files and directories. Explains the rule that later definitions override earlier ones and how to control auto-discovery vs. explicit definitions.

references.title: Service Container - Remove Services
references.summary: Shows how to remove a service from the container with the PHP configurator remove() method, commonly for environment-specific service registration such as disabling a service in test.

references.title: Service Container - Multiple Service Definitions Using the Same Namespace
references.summary: Explains how YAML service definitions that use the same PHP namespace need unique service keys plus the namespace option. This allows different resource globs under the same namespace to receive different tags or options.

references.title: Service Aliases and Public/Private Services
references.summary: Documents **service aliases** (`Alias` definitions, declared with `alias:` or `#[Autowire(service: ...)]`) and the **public vs private** flag. Symfony 4+ services are **private by default**, meaning `$container->get()` cannot retrieve them at runtime — only DI injection works. Make a service public when external code or templates need to fetch it.

references.title: Service Method Calls and Setter Injection
references.summary: Defines how to call methods after instantiation (**setter injection**): YAML's `calls:` key, PHP's `->call('method', [args])`, the `#[Required]` attribute (autowiring calls all `#[Required]` methods automatically), and `returns_clone` to chain a method that returns `$this` and have its return value discarded.

references.title: Types of Injection
references.summary: Compares the three injection styles: **constructor injection** (recommended default), **setter / method injection** (for optional dependencies), and **property injection** (rare, for legacy code). Constructor injection is preferred because it makes mandatory dependencies explicit and the resulting object is fully usable from the moment it is constructed.

references.title: Lazy Services
references.summary: Marking a service `lazy: true` makes the container inject a **virtual proxy** (since 7.x via PHP 8.4 `\ReflectionClass::newLazyProxy()` natively, otherwise via `symfony/proxy-manager-bridge`). The real instance is built only on first method call, which is useful for breaking circular dependencies and avoiding the cost of building heavy services that may never be used.

references.title: How to Make Service Arguments/References Optional
references.summary: When a referenced service may be absent, use the **`?`** prefix in YAML/XML (`@?logger`) or the `IGNORE_ON_INVALID_REFERENCE` / `NULL_ON_INVALID_REFERENCE` constants in PHP definitions. The container then injects `null` (or skips the call) instead of throwing if the dependency is missing.

references.title: How to Manage Common Dependencies with Parent Services
references.summary: A **parent service** is an abstract template definition (`abstract: true`) whose arguments, tags, and method calls are inherited by child definitions through the `parent:` key. Children may override individual arguments by index; parent services were widely used before autowiring became the norm.

references.title: How to Define Non-Shared Services
references.summary: By default services are **shared** (the same instance is returned every time). Set `shared: false` to make the container build a **new instance on each retrieval** — useful for stateful workers or message handlers that must not leak state between dispatches.

references.title: How to Decorate Services
references.summary: Explains how to wrap an existing service with a decorator using the decorates option or the AsDecorator/AutowireDecorated attributes. The original service is exposed as .inner (configurable via decoration_inner_name) and replaces the original behind the original id.

references.title: How to Decorate Services - Decoration Priority
references.summary: Documents the decoration_priority option (and AsDecorator priority argument) that orders multiple decorators for the same service. Higher priorities are applied earlier so they end up wrapping the lower-priority decorators.

references.title: How to Decorate Services - Stacking Decorators
references.summary: Describes the YAML stack option to compose an ordered chain of decorators with each frame referencing .inner. Stacks support inlined services, references and child definitions, and only allow public and deprecated attributes like aliases.

references.title: How to Decorate Services - Control the Behavior When the Decorated Service Does Not Exist
references.summary: Documents the decoration_on_invalid option with three values: exception (default, throws ServiceNotFoundException), ignore (removes the decorator), and null (keeps the decorator and injects null for the decorated dependency).

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags
references.summary: Explains service tags as markers that integrate a service into Symfony or third-party subsystems. Shows YAML and PHP forms, autoconfiguration via _instanceof and AutoconfigureTag, and how to introduce custom tags processed by compiler passes.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - Reference Tagged Services
references.summary: Documents the !tagged_iterator YAML tag and the AutowireIterator attribute used to inject all services tagged with a given tag as an iterable. Covers exclude, exclude_self, and the rule that a service is excluded from its own tagged collection by default.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - Tagged Services with Priority
references.summary: Explains the priority tag attribute and the static getDefaultPriority() method that order tagged services in a collection. Higher priorities are placed earlier; defaultPriorityMethod customizes the method used to resolve the priority.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - Tagged Services with Index
references.summary: Documents the index_by/indexAttribute and default_index_method options that change the keys used in a tagged iterator/locator from service ids to a custom value, with fallback to getDefault<Name>Name() and finally to the service id.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - The #[AsTaggedItem] attribute
references.summary: Explains the AsTaggedItem attribute that sets both the priority and the index for a tagged service directly on its class. The attribute can be applied multiple times to register the same class under different indexes.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - Creating custom Tags
references.summary: Explains that custom tags are inert metadata until a compiler pass reads them. Shows a compiler pass using findTaggedServiceIds() and addMethodCall() with Reference objects to wire tagged services into another service.

references.title: How to Work with Service Tags - Adding Additional Attributes on Tags
references.summary: Documents tag attributes such as alias and the YAML syntax needed when a tag itself needs an attribute named name. Shows that the compiler pass receives every tag occurrence and can read each attribute set.

references.title: How to Load Service Configuration inside a Bundle
references.summary: Documents the two ways to load bundle service configuration: loadExtension() in an AbstractBundle subclass (recommended) and the traditional Extension class in the bundle's DependencyInjection namespace. Covers naming conventions and how to override getContainerExtension and getAlias.

references.title: How to Create Friendly Configuration for a Bundle
references.summary: Explains how bundles expose user-friendly configuration via a Configuration class and TreeBuilder. Shows AbstractBundle::configure(), processConfiguration() in the Extension::load(), and the ConfigurableExtension::loadInternal() shortcut.

references.title: How to Create Friendly Configuration for a Bundle - Dump the Configuration
references.summary: Documents the config:dump-reference console command that prints the default YAML configuration for a bundle. Works automatically when the Configuration class lives in src/DependencyInjection/Configuration and has no constructor arguments.

references.title: How to Simplify Configuration of Multiple Bundles
references.summary: Explains the PrependExtensionInterface and AbstractBundle::prependExtension() method used to inject default configuration into other bundle extensions before their load() runs. When several bundles prepend the same key, the first registered bundle wins.

references.title: Compiling the Container - Prepending Configuration Passed to the Extension
references.summary: Component-level reference for PrependExtensionInterface::prepend() and the prependExtensionConfig() method on the ContainerBuilder. Used to inject configuration into other extensions before they are loaded.

references.title: Using a Factory to Create Services
references.summary: Documents the factory option that delegates service creation to a static or non-static method, an invokable service, or a first-class callable. Notes that the configured class is informational because the actual class is the factory's return value.

references.title: Using a Factory to Create Services - Using the Class as Factory Itself
references.summary: Explains how to declare a factory whose static method lives on the same class as the produced service by passing null as the factory class, or by using the constructor option to name the static method.

references.title: Using a Factory to Create Services - Non-Static Factories
references.summary: Shows that non-static factories must themselves be registered as services and referenced via @factory_service_id with the method name as second factory argument. Arguments to the factory method use the regular arguments option.

references.title: Using a Factory to Create Services - Invokable Factories
references.summary: Documents how to use an __invoke()-based factory by referencing the factory service alone (no method name). Symfony will invoke the factory service to obtain the produced service instance.

references.title: Using a Factory to Create Services - Using Expressions in Service Factories
references.summary: Shows how to use an expression-language string as a factory to dynamically choose a service based on a parameter or another service. Provides examples using the parameter(), service(), and arg() expression functions.

references.title: How to Work with Compiler Passes
references.summary: Explains how compiler passes manipulate service definitions during compilation. Shows kernel-level usage by implementing CompilerPassInterface, registering separate pass classes via build()/addCompilerPass, and bundle-level passes through AbstractBundle::build().

references.title: Compiling the Container
references.summary: Component-level reference for container compilation. Covers built-in optimization and removal passes, the compile() entry point, ExtensionInterface::load() and CompilerPassInterface::process() ordering, and the warning to operate on definitions only (no get()/set()).

references.title: Compiling the Container - Creating Separate Compiler Passes
references.summary: Documents the optional pass-type and priority arguments of addCompilerPass and lists the PassConfig types: TYPE_BEFORE_OPTIMIZATION (default), TYPE_OPTIMIZE, TYPE_BEFORE_REMOVING, TYPE_REMOVE, TYPE_AFTER_REMOVING.

references.title: Compiling the Container - Dumping the Configuration for Performance
references.summary: Explains how PhpDumper writes a compiled container subclass to disk so subsequent requests skip parsing/compilation. Covers the class option, ConfigCache freshness checks in debug mode, and resource tracking via $container->getResources().

references.title: Defining Services Dependencies Automatically (Autowiring)
references.summary: Explains how autowiring matches a service whose id exactly equals a constructor argument's type-hint. Notes the absence of runtime overhead thanks to the compiled container and the actionable exception thrown when the type cannot be resolved.

references.title: Defining Services Dependencies Automatically (Autowiring) - Using Aliases to Enable Autowiring
references.summary: Shows how to add a service alias whose id matches a class or interface so type-hints can be autowired. Core bundles use aliases to expose services like Psr\Log\LoggerInterface that point at the logger service.

references.title: Defining Services Dependencies Automatically (Autowiring) - Dealing with Multiple Implementations of the Same Type
references.summary: Explains how to disambiguate multiple implementations of the same interface using named autowiring aliases (Interface $argName) and the Target attribute, including registerAliasForArgument() for compiler-pass and extension-based registration.

references.title: Defining Services Dependencies Automatically (Autowiring) - Fixing Non-Autowireable Arguments
references.summary: Documents the Autowire attribute that injects scalars, parameters (param: 'kernel.debug'), expressions, and environment variables (with processors like bool:) into arguments that cannot be resolved purely from a type-hint.

references.title: Defining Services Dependencies Automatically (Autowiring) - Autowiring other Methods (Setters and Public Typed Properties)
references.summary: Shows how the Required attribute makes the container autowire setter methods and public typed properties at instantiation time, complementing constructor autowiring with optional setter-based dependencies.

references.title: Defining Services Dependencies Automatically (Autowiring) - Generate Closures With Autowiring
references.summary: Documents AutowireServiceClosure (returns the service), AutowireCallable (closure with the same signature as a method), and AutowireMethodOf (uses the property name as method) for injecting closures that wrap services.

references.title: Service Container - The autoconfigure Option
references.summary: Explains how autoconfigure: true automatically applies tags based on the service's class (e.g. ExtensionInterface gets twig.extension) and integrates with attributes like AsMessageHandler, AsEventListener, and AsCommand.

references.title: Service Container - Generating Adapters for Functional Interfaces
references.summary: Documents functional interfaces and how Symfony can generate an adapter that implements a single-method interface. Shows using AutowireCallable or from_callable so calls to the interface method are forwarded to a method on an underlying service.

references.title: Defining Services Dependencies Automatically (Autowiring) - Autowiring Anonymous Services Inline
references.summary: Documents the AutowireInline attribute for declaring anonymous service definitions directly on constructor arguments. Shows how to provide a class or factory callable plus arguments, while recommending configuration files for complex definitions.

references.title: Service Subscribers & Locators
references.summary: Explains the service locator pattern as a PSR-11 container that exposes a curated subset of services and only instantiates them on first access. Recommends locators over injecting the entire container.

references.title: Service Subscribers & Locators - Defining a Service Subscriber
references.summary: Documents the ServiceSubscriberInterface::getSubscribedServices() method and how Symfony injects a matching ServiceLocator. Shows how typing the locator as ServiceCollectionInterface enables iteration and counting of provided services.

references.title: Service Subscribers & Locators - Including Services
references.summary: Explains the array shape returned by getSubscribedServices(), key/type pairs for aliases, the ? prefix for optional services that should not raise when missing, and the rule that array_merge(parent::getSubscribedServices(), ...) must be used when extending AbstractController.

references.title: Service Subscribers & Locators - The AutowireLocator Attribute
references.summary: Describes the AutowireLocator attribute that creates a service locator inline on a constructor argument. Supports keyed entries for aliases, the ? prefix for optional services, and nesting SubscribedService for advanced configuration.

references.title: Service Subscribers & Locators - The AutowireIterator Attribute
references.summary: Explains the AutowireIterator attribute, a variant of AutowireLocator that injects an iterable of services tagged with a specific tag. This is useful when a service needs to loop over all tagged handlers instead of fetching them individually.

references.title: Service Subscribers & Locators - Defining a Service Locator
references.summary: Shows the !service_locator YAML tag and PHP service_locator() helper to inject a custom locator without ServiceSubscriberInterface. Covers reusing a stand-alone ServiceLocator service and the container.service_locator tag.

references.title: Service Subscribers & Locators - Using Service Locators in Compiler Passes
references.summary: Documents the recommended ServiceLocatorTagPass::register() helper for creating service locators inside compiler passes. The helper reduces boilerplate and shares identical locators among services that reference them.

references.title: Service Closures
references.summary: Documents service closures injected with !service_closure (or @>service shortcut) and the service_closure() helper. Calling the closure returns the wrapped service so heavy dependencies are only instantiated when actually needed.

concepts.concept: Security Core, CSRF and PasswordHasher components

references.title: Security
references.summary: Symfony's security guide describes the SecurityBundle, which bundles together user providers, firewalls, authentication, authorization, and access control on top of the underlying security-core, security-http, and password-hasher packages. It is the central installable package (`composer require symfony/security-bundle`) that wires those components into a full-stack application.

references.title: How to Implement CSRF Protection
references.summary: Describes the security-csrf package, which generates and validates anti-CSRF tokens used by Symfony Forms, login forms, and the logout action. It explains how the `symfony/security-csrf` component is installed, how stateful (session-based) tokens differ from stateless tokens, and how the framework integrates token validation transparently with the Form component.

references.title: How to Implement CSRF Protection - Generating and Checking CSRF Tokens Manually
references.summary: Documents the lower-level CSRF API exposed by the security-csrf component for non-Form workflows. It covers the `csrf_token()` Twig function, the `AbstractController::isCsrfTokenValid()` helper, and the `IsCsrfTokenValid` controller attribute (with `tokenKey`, `methods`, and `tokenSource` options for SOURCE_PAYLOAD/SOURCE_QUERY/SOURCE_HEADER).

references.title: How to Implement CSRF Protection - Stateless CSRF Tokens
references.summary: Describes stateless CSRF tokens introduced for Symfony Flex applications, configured via `framework.csrf_protection.stateless_token_ids`. Validation does not rely on the session: Symfony checks the request's `Origin` and `Referer` headers against the application's target origin, with optional double-submit cookie/header validation as a defense-in-depth layer.

references.title: Password Hashing and Verification
references.summary: Introduces the PasswordHasher component (`symfony/password-hasher`), which provides utilities to hash and verify user passwords independently from the rest of the security stack. It is configured under `security.password_hashers` and exposes services such as `UserPasswordHasherInterface` and the lower-level `PasswordHasherFactory` for stand-alone usage.

references.title: Security - Authenticating Users
references.summary: Lists Symfony's built-in authenticators activated under each firewall: Form Login, JSON Login, HTTP Basic, Login Link, Access Tokens, X.509 client certificates, Remote Users, and Custom Authenticators. The firewall configuration enables one or more of these authenticators to identify the visitor.

references.title: Security - Form Login
references.summary: The `form_login` firewall option activates the FormLoginAuthenticator. It uses `login_path` (where the login form is rendered) and `check_path` (where credentials are submitted). By default the form must POST `_username` and `_password` parameters and the `AuthenticationUtils` service exposes the last error and last entered username.

references.title: Security - JSON Login
references.summary: The `json_login` firewall option enables the JSON login authenticator. Clients POST JSON with `username` and `password` keys to the configured `check_path`; on success the controller is invoked and can use `#[CurrentUser]` to retrieve the authenticated user, and on failure Symfony returns HTTP 401 Unauthorized.

references.title: Security - HTTP Basic
references.summary: The `http_basic` firewall option enables HTTP Basic authentication. Symfony asks the browser for credentials with a `WWW-Authenticate` response header and the `realm` option. Note that you cannot truly log out from HTTP Basic since the browser keeps re-sending the credentials on every request.

references.title: How to use Passwordless Login Link Authentication
references.summary: Login links are a passwordless authenticator that emails users a short-lived signed URL. The `login_link` firewall option configures the link's signature, lifetime (default 600 seconds), and maximum number of uses, and `LoginLinkHandlerInterface::createLoginLink()` produces the link from a user object.

references.title: How to use Access Token Authentication
references.summary: Configures the `access_token` firewall option for API authentication based on opaque or OIDC tokens. A token extractor reads the token (default: `Authorization: Bearer ...`) and a `token_handler` resolves it to a user, with built-in support for OIDC user-info and signed JWT validation.

references.title: How to Add "Remember Me" Login Functionality
references.summary: The `remember_me` firewall option creates a long-lived signed cookie so that returning users stay authenticated across sessions. It documents `secret`, `lifetime`, `path`, `secure`, `samesite`, and `always_remember_me` options and explains that remember-me users get the `IS_AUTHENTICATED_REMEMBERED` attribute but not `IS_AUTHENTICATED_FULLY`.

references.title: Security - Limiting Login Attempts
references.summary: The `login_throttling` firewall option uses the Rate Limiter component to limit failed login attempts. By default 5 attempts per minute are allowed per IP+username pair, and `5 * max_attempts` per IP, with `max_attempts`, `interval`, and `limiter` options to customize the behavior.

references.title: Security - Login Programmatically
references.summary: The `Symfony\Bundle\SecurityBundle\Security` helper exposes a `login($user, $authenticatorName = null, $firewallName = null, array $badges = [], array $attributes = [])` method to authenticate a user from PHP code. The authenticator name is required when the firewall has more than one authenticator, and the call returns a `RedirectResponse` reproducing the regular login redirect logic.
