references.title: Security - Logging Out
references.summary: The `logout` firewall option registers an internal route that clears the security token. It accepts `path` (URL or route name), `target` (redirect target after logout), and is paired with the `_logout_<firewallname>` internal route name. The `LogoutEvent` is dispatched and listeners can attach a custom response, while the `Security::logout()` helper performs a programmatic logout.

references.title: Customizing the Form Login Authenticator Responses
references.summary: Documents how to control the redirect after success and failure for the FormLoginAuthenticator: `default_target_path`, `always_use_default_target_path`, `target_path_parameter`, `failure_path`, and `failure_path_parameter`. It also covers `use_referer` and `use_forward` for using the referring URL or forwarding to the failure controller.

references.title: Security - X.509 Client Certificates
references.summary: Explains how Symfony's `x509` authenticator integrates with web-server client certificate authentication. Symfony reads the certificate distinguished name exposed by the server, extracts an email address as the user identifier, and loads the user through the configured provider.

references.title: Security - Remote Users
references.summary: Documents the `remote_user` authenticator for environments where the web server or another module pre-authenticates the user. Symfony uses the `REMOTE_USER` server variable by default as the user identifier and delegates loading to the configured provider.

references.title: Security - Security Events
references.summary: Lists the Security events dispatched during authentication and logout and explains the firewall-specific event dispatcher names. It covers events such as `CheckPassportEvent`, `AuthenticationTokenCreatedEvent`, `AuthenticationSuccessEvent`, `LoginSuccessEvent`, `LoginFailureEvent`, `InteractiveLoginEvent`, and `LogoutEvent`.

references.title: Security - Access Control (Authorization)
references.summary: Symfony's authorization layer determines whether the authenticated user can access a resource (URL, controller, model, ...). Authorization works by assigning roles to the user via `getRoles()` and protecting resources with `access_control` rules in `security.yaml` or controller-level checks like `denyAccessUnlessGranted` or `#[IsGranted]`.

references.title: Security - Add Code to Deny Access
references.summary: Documents controller-level authorization. The `AbstractController::denyAccessUnlessGranted($attribute, $subject = null, $message = '')` shortcut throws an `AccessDeniedException` when the user lacks the attribute, and the `#[IsGranted]` attribute supports `subject`, `message`, `statusCode`, `exceptionCode`, and `methods` arguments. Outside controllers, the `Security` service exposes `isGranted()` and `isGrantedForUser()`.

references.title: Security - Access Control in Templates
references.summary: Twig integration for authorization. The `is_granted()` and `is_granted_for_user()` functions check authorization for the current or another user, while `access_decision()` and `access_decision_for_user()` return the full vote object including the message returned by custom voters.

references.title: Security - Checking to see if a User is Logged In
references.summary: Documents the special authorization attributes that are not real roles: `IS_AUTHENTICATED`, `IS_AUTHENTICATED_FULLY`, `IS_AUTHENTICATED_REMEMBERED`, `IS_REMEMBERED`, and `IS_IMPERSONATOR`. They can be used wherever roles are accepted (access_control, isGranted, Twig).

references.title: Security - Allowing Unsecured Access (i.e. Anonymous Users)
references.summary: The `PUBLIC_ACCESS` security attribute allows unauthenticated users through `access_control` rules. It is typically used to expose the login form or other public routes when the rest of the firewall requires authentication.

references.title: Using Expressions in Security Access Controls
references.summary: The `isGranted()` shortcut and `#[IsGranted]` attribute accept an `Expression` object using the Expression Language. Available variables include `user`, `role_names`, `object`/`subject`, `token`, `trust_resolver`, and helper functions `is_authenticated()`, `is_remember_me()`, `is_fully_authenticated()`, and `is_granted()`.

references.title: How to Impersonate a User
references.summary: The `switch_user` firewall option lets administrators authenticate as another user without logging out. By default, only users with the `ROLE_ALLOWED_TO_SWITCH` role can impersonate, the `_switch_user` query parameter triggers the switch, and `_switch_user=_exit` returns to the original user. The session token becomes a `SwitchUserToken` and `IS_IMPERSONATOR` is granted to the impersonator.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference (SecurityBundle)
references.summary: Master reference of every option exposed under the `security` configuration root by SecurityBundle. Lists top-level options (`access_denied_url`, `expose_security_errors`, `session_fixation_strategy`, `access_decision_manager`, `access_control`, `firewalls`, `password_hashers`, `providers`, `role_hierarchy`) and the `config:dump-reference security` and `debug:config security` console commands.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference - access_denied_url
references.summary: Defines the URL the user is redirected to after a 403 HTTP error when no custom access denial handler is configured. Default value is `null` and the option accepts a URL string such as `/no-permission`.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference - expose_security_errors
references.summary: Controls how much detail Symfony reveals about authentication failures to mitigate user enumeration. Accepts `'none'` (default, all errors are converted to a generic `BadCredentialsException`), `'account_status'` (account-related errors visible only when the password is correct), or `'all'` (all security exceptions are visible).

references.title: Security Configuration Reference - session_fixation_strategy
references.summary: Selects the session fixation strategy applied at login. Accepts `NONE` (no change, not recommended), `MIGRATE` (default; new session ID, attributes preserved) and `INVALIDATE` (full session regeneration, all attributes lost). Constants are defined on `SessionAuthenticationStrategy`.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference - access_decision_manager
references.summary: Configures the access decision manager. The `strategy` option (default `affirmative`) chooses between `affirmative`, `consensus`, `unanimous`, and `priority`. `allow_if_all_abstain` (default `false`) controls behavior when every voter abstains. `allow_if_equal_granted_denied` (default `true`) only applies to the `consensus` strategy.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference - firewalls
references.summary: Documents the `firewalls` map: each named firewall declares its `pattern`, optional authenticators (`form_login`, `json_login`, `http_basic`, `x509`, `remote_user`, `access_token`, `login_link`, `login_throttling`, `remember_me`), and shared firewall options like `context`, `stateless`, `lazy`, `user_checker`, `required_badges`, and `provider`.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference - password_hashers
references.summary: Maps user classes (or interfaces) to a hashing configuration. Each entry accepts `algorithm`, `cost` (bcrypt), `memory_cost` and `time_cost` (argon), `hash_algorithm`, `key_length`, `iterations`, `encode_as_base64`, `migrate_from`, and `id` to reference a custom hasher service.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference - providers
references.summary: Defines the user providers used by firewalls. Built-in providers are `entity` (Doctrine), `ldap`, `memory`, and `chain`. Each named provider can also reference a custom `id` (service ID) of a class implementing `UserProviderInterface`.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference - role_hierarchy
references.summary: Maps a parent role to one or more child roles that are automatically granted to users holding the parent role. Hierarchy is static and processed by the access decision manager so `isGranted('ROLE_PARENT')` recognizes inherited roles.

references.title: User Providers
references.summary: Overview of user providers, the security building block responsible for (re)loading a user object from storage based on a user identifier. Symfony ships four built-in providers: entity (Doctrine), LDAP, memory, and chain. Each firewall is bound to exactly one user provider used during login and on every stateful request to refresh the user.

references.title: User Providers - Entity User Provider
references.summary: The `entity` provider loads users from the database via Doctrine. Required option `class` selects the Doctrine entity class, `property` declares the column queried by user identifier, and the optional `manager_name` selects which entity manager to use when several are defined.

references.title: User Providers - Using a Custom Query to Load the User
references.summary: When loading a user requires more than a single field comparison, the Doctrine repository class can implement `Symfony\Bridge\Doctrine\Security\User\UserLoaderInterface::loadUserByIdentifier()`. With this in place, the `property` option must be omitted from the entity provider and Symfony delegates user loading to the repository method.

references.title: User Providers - Memory User Provider
references.summary: The `memory` provider stores user identifiers, hashed passwords, and roles directly in `security.yaml`. It is intended for prototypes and limited apps; passwords must already be hashed using a salt-less algorithm such as bcrypt unless `auto` is configured.

references.title: User Providers - Chain User Provider
references.summary: The `chain` provider combines multiple providers in a declared order. Symfony queries each provider in sequence and stops at the first one that returns a user. This is the only built-in way to expose multiple sources to a single firewall.

references.title: User Providers - Creating a Custom User Provider
references.summary: A custom user provider implements `UserProviderInterface::loadUserByIdentifier()`, `refreshUser()`, and `supportsClass()`. It is registered under `security.providers.<name>.id` with the service ID. `refreshUser()` is invoked on every request unless the firewall is `stateless: true`, and `loadUserByIdentifier()` is invoked during login, `switch_user`, and Remember Me.

references.title: How to Load Security Users from LDAP
references.summary: The `ldap` user provider queries an LDAP directory for users. It is paired with `Symfony\Component\Ldap\Ldap` and configured by `service` (LDAP client), `base_dn`, `search_dn`, `search_password`, `default_roles`, `uid_key`, and `filter`. LDAP can also be used as the authenticator via `form_login_ldap` and `http_basic_ldap`.

references.title: Security - The Firewall
references.summary: Firewalls define how Symfony authenticates users and which parts of the application are secured. Only one firewall is active per request — Symfony selects the first whose `pattern` (and optional matchers) matches. Firewalls can declare multiple authenticators, a `provider`, `lazy: true` to defer session start, and `stateless: true` for APIs.

references.title: Security - Fetching the Firewall Configuration for a Request
references.summary: The `Symfony\Bundle\SecurityBundle\Security` service exposes `getFirewallConfig(Request $request)` which returns the `FirewallConfig` matched for that request, allowing services to inspect the current firewall name and options at runtime.

references.title: How to Restrict Firewalls to a Request
references.summary: Firewall matchers determine when a firewall is initialized. Built-in restrictions include `pattern` (request path regex), `host` (Host regex), `methods` (HTTP method list), and the catch-all `request_matcher` option that takes the service ID of any class implementing `RequestMatcherInterface`. The first firewall that matches handles the request.

references.title: How to Customize Access Denied Responses
references.summary: When `AccessDeniedException` is thrown, Symfony either calls the firewall `entry_point` (for unauthenticated users) or returns a 403 (for authenticated users). The firewall option `entry_point` accepts a service ID implementing `AuthenticationEntryPointInterface::start()`, while `access_denied_handler` accepts a service ID implementing `AccessDeniedHandlerInterface::handle()`.

references.title: The Entry Point: Helping Users Start Authentication
references.summary: The `entry_point` firewall option is mandatory when a firewall declares more than one authenticator that could start authentication. Its value is the name of a built-in authenticator (e.g. `form_login`) or the service ID of a class implementing `AuthenticationEntryPointInterface`. Different parts of the application that need different entry points must use separate firewalls.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference - stateless
references.summary: When `stateless: true` is set on a firewall, Symfony does not load the user from the session and does not call `UserProviderInterface::refreshUser()` on subsequent requests. This is the recommended setting for APIs authenticated on every request via tokens or HTTP Basic.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference - lazy
references.summary: When `lazy: true` is set on a firewall, the user object is only loaded when explicitly accessed (e.g. by `isGranted()` or `getUser()`). This avoids starting the session on every request and improves cacheability for endpoints that do not need authorization checks.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference - context
references.summary: Two firewalls share the same authentication if they declare the same `context` value. Without it, being authenticated on firewall A does not imply being authenticated on firewall B, even when both use the same user provider.

references.title: How to Impersonate a User
references.summary: Enables admin user impersonation under a firewall via `switch_user: { provider: ..., role: ROLE_ALLOWED_TO_SWITCH }`. Append `?_switch_user=username` to any URL to assume the target user, `?_switch_user=_exit` to switch back. The `IS_IMPERSONATOR` and `ROLE_PREVIOUS_ADMIN` security attributes mark the impersonation state.

references.title: How to Add "Remember Me" Login Functionality
references.summary: The **`remember_me`** firewall option keeps a user authenticated across sessions through a signed cookie. Two strategies are available: the **signature-based** default (HMAC over user properties) and the **token-based** strategy that stores opaque tokens in a `RememberMeHandler` (e.g. Doctrine).

references.title: Security - The User
references.summary: Symfony's security system requires a user class implementing `Symfony\Component\Security\Core\User\UserInterface`. The interface mandates `getRoles()` and `getUserIdentifier()`, while password-aware users additionally implement `PasswordAuthenticatedUserInterface`. The `make:user` MakerBundle command scaffolds a Doctrine entity that satisfies these contracts.

references.title: Security - Fetching the User from a Controller
references.summary: Inside a controller, the authenticated user is obtained via the `#[CurrentUser]` controller-argument attribute or the `getUser()` shortcut on `AbstractController`. `#[CurrentUser]` is preferred because it provides type-hinting, works in non-AbstractController controllers, and a non-nullable type causes a 403 when no user is authenticated.

references.title: Security - Fetching the User from a Service
references.summary: Inside a service, the authenticated user is obtained from the `Symfony\Bundle\SecurityBundle\Security` helper via `$security->getUser()`. The Security service should be injected (not called from the constructor) because authentication may not be complete at construction time.

references.title: Security - Fetch the User in a Template
references.summary: In Twig templates the authenticated user is exposed as `app.user` via the global `app` variable. Combined with `is_granted()`, this allows access checks like `{% if is_granted('IS_AUTHENTICATED_FULLY') %}{{ app.user.email }}{% endif %}` directly in the view.

references.title: How to Create and Enable Custom User Checkers
references.summary: User checkers implement `UserCheckerInterface::checkPreAuth()` and `checkPostAuth()` to enforce additional account rules around login (e.g. account disabled, expired). They are wired per firewall via `user_checker` and may throw any subclass of `AccountStatusException`, including `CustomUserMessageAccountStatusException` for end-user messages.

references.title: How to Create and Enable Custom User Checkers - Using Multiple User Checkers
references.summary: Multiple checkers can be combined per firewall using `ChainUserChecker`. Tag services with `security.user_checker.<firewall>` (with optional `priority`) and configure the firewall option `user_checker: security.user_checker.chain.<firewall>` to call them in sequence.

references.title: Security - Understanding how Users are Refreshed from the Session
references.summary: Unless a firewall is `stateless`, the user is serialized to the session and reloaded on each request through the user provider. Symfony then compares the session user with the freshly loaded one using `getPassword()`, `getSalt()`, and `getUserIdentifier()`; if any differ, the user is logged out. Custom comparison logic is enabled by implementing `EquatableInterface::isEqualTo()`.

references.title: Security - Comparing Users Manually with EquatableInterface
references.summary: When the default refresh comparison (password, salt, identifier) is not appropriate, the user class can implement `Symfony\Component\Security\Core\User\EquatableInterface`. Symfony will then call `isEqualTo(UserInterface $user)` to decide whether to keep the session token or de-authenticate the user.

references.title: Password Hashing and Verification - Configuring a Password Hasher
references.summary: Hashers are configured under `security.password_hashers` keyed by user class (or interface). Each entry maps to either an algorithm name (e.g. `'auto'`) or an array with `algorithm`, `cost`, `time_cost`, `memory_cost`, and `migrate_from` options. Test environments can use lower costs (e.g. bcrypt cost `4`) for faster runs.

references.title: Password Hashing and Verification - Hashing the Password
references.summary: The `UserPasswordHasherInterface` service exposes `hashPassword(UserInterface $user, string $plainPassword)` to produce a hash and `isPasswordValid(UserInterface $user, string $plainPassword)` to verify a password. The hashing algorithm is resolved automatically from the user class via the `password_hashers` configuration.

references.title: Password Hashing and Verification - Injecting a Specific Password Hasher
references.summary: Named hashers (defined under `password_hashers` without a user class) can be injected into services using the `#[Target('hasher_name')]` attribute on a `PasswordHasherInterface` argument. Such hashers expose `hash()` and `verify()` (rather than `hashPassword()`) because they are not tied to a `UserInterface` instance.

references.title: Password Hashing and Verification - Password Migration
references.summary: The `migrate_from` option lists legacy hashers that should still be accepted at login. After a successful login with an old hash, Symfony rehashes the password with the new algorithm and persists it via `PasswordUpgraderInterface::upgradePassword()`. The `auto`, `native`, `bcrypt`, and `argon` hashers automatically migrate from `pbkdf2` and message-digest hashes.

references.title: Password Hashing and Verification - Dynamic Password Hashers
references.summary: Named hashers can be selected dynamically per user. The user class implements `PasswordHasherAwareInterface::getPasswordHasherName()`, returning the name of the configured hasher (or `null` to use the default). This is typically used to apply a stronger algorithm to admin users.

references.title: Password Hashing and Verification - Hashing a Stand-Alone String
references.summary: `PasswordHasherFactory` allows hashing arbitrary strings without a `UserInterface` instance. It is constructed with a map of named hashers and exposes `getPasswordHasher(string $name)` returning a `PasswordHasherInterface` whose `hash()` and `verify()` methods operate on plain strings.

references.title: Password Hashing and Verification - Supported Algorithms
references.summary: Built-in algorithms shipped with the PasswordHasher component are `auto`, `bcrypt`, `sodium` (Argon2), and `pbkdf2` (legacy). PBKDF2 is no longer recommended; applications should use `auto` (which currently selects bcrypt) or the explicit modern algorithms.

references.title: Password Hashing and Verification - The "auto" Hasher
references.summary: The `auto` hasher selects the best available hashing algorithm at runtime (currently bcrypt). The hash length may grow as PHP introduces new algorithms, so columns persisting hashed passwords should be at least `varchar(255)`.

references.title: Password Hashing and Verification - The Bcrypt Password Hasher
references.summary: The `bcrypt` hasher uses the bcrypt password hashing function. Hashes are exactly 60 characters long, the salt is embedded in the hash, and the only configuration option is `cost` (range `4`-`31`, default `13`). Each cost increment doubles the hashing time; existing passwords keep verifying with their original cost.

references.title: Password Hashing and Verification - The Sodium Password Hasher
references.summary: The `sodium` hasher uses Argon2 via PHP's bundled libsodium extension. Hashes are about 96 characters long (subject to change), and the salt is embedded in the hash.

references.title: Security - Roles
references.summary: Roles are arbitrary strings returned from `User::getRoles()` that drive Symfony's role-based authorization. Every role must start with the `ROLE_` prefix; otherwise role checks do not behave as expected. The `User` skeleton always grants at least `ROLE_USER` so authenticated users can be distinguished from anonymous visitors.

references.title: Security - Hierarchical Roles
references.summary: The `security.role_hierarchy` configuration maps a parent role to one or more child roles that are automatically granted to users holding the parent role. Hierarchy resolution happens through the access decision manager, which is why role checks must go through `isGranted()` / `denyAccessUnlessGranted()` rather than `in_array($role, $user->getRoles())`.

references.title: Security Configuration Reference - role_hierarchy
references.summary: Documents the `role_hierarchy` configuration node: each key is a role and its value is either a single child role string or an array of child roles. The hierarchy is static and processed by Symfony's `RoleHierarchyVoter`, so it cannot be sourced from a database without a custom voter.

references.title: How Does the Security access_control Work?
references.summary: For each request Symfony evaluates `access_control` rules from top to bottom and applies the **first** rule whose matching options fit the request. Each rule has matching options (`path`, `ip`/`ips`, `port`, `host`, `methods`, `route`, `attributes`, `request_matcher`) and enforcement options (`roles`, `allow_if`, `requires_channel`).

references.title: How Does the Security access_control Work? - Matching Options
references.summary: Each `access_control` rule uses `ChainRequestMatcher` with `path` (regex without delimiters), `ip`/`ips` (with netmask support), `port` (integer), `host` (regex), `methods` (HTTP method list), `route` (route name shortcut for `_route` attribute), `attributes` (request-attribute matchers), or `request_matcher` (service ID). Omitted matchers are not constrained.

references.title: How Does the Security access_control Work? - Access Enforcement
references.summary: Once a rule matches, Symfony enforces access using `roles` (required roles or attributes), `allow_if` (Expression Language string), and `requires_channel` (`http` / `https`). With the default affirmative strategy, defining both `roles` and `allow_if` behaves like an OR — access is granted if either matches.

references.title: How Does the Security access_control Work? - Matching access_control By IP
references.summary: The `ips` option supports plain IPs (IPv4 and IPv6) and CIDR netmasks. When the option is set on a rule, that rule only matches when the client IP is in the list, otherwise Symfony falls through to the next rule. A common pattern is to use a follow-up rule requiring an unassigned role like `ROLE_NO_ACCESS` to deny everyone else.

references.title: How Does the Security access_control Work? - Securing by an Expression
references.summary: The `allow_if` option accepts an Expression Language string evaluated by `ExpressionVoter`. Available variables include `request`, `user`, `roles`, `token`, and the standard security functions (`is_granted`, `is_authenticated`, `is_remember_me`, `is_fully_authenticated`).

references.title: How Does the Security access_control Work? - Forcing a Channel (http, https)
references.summary: The `requires_channel` option requires a request to use a specific channel; if the channel doesn't match, Symfony issues a redirect (e.g. http to https). Common values are `http` and `https`. Behind a reverse proxy, trusted proxies must be configured for the redirect to behave correctly.

references.title: How Does the Security access_control Work? - Restrict to a port
references.summary: The `port` option restricts an `access_control` rule to a specific TCP port (e.g. `8080`). It is most useful when an application exposes different functionality on different ports, such as an admin port behind a reverse proxy.

references.title: How to Force HTTPS or HTTP for different URLs
references.summary: Recommends forcing HTTPS through `access_control` rules with `requires_channel: https`, optionally driven by an environment variable so dev environments can stay on HTTP. An alternative is to use the `schemes` option directly on routes.

references.title: How to Customize Access Denied Responses
references.summary: Throwing `AccessDeniedException` in a controller or voter triggers Symfony's access-denied flow. The response depends on whether a user is authenticated: **anonymous** users are redirected to the firewall's `entry_point`; **authenticated** users get a `403`. Override the response with the `access_denied_handler` firewall option (a service implementing `AccessDeniedHandlerInterface`).

references.title: How to Write a Custom Authenticator
references.summary: Custom authenticators implement `AuthenticatorInterface` (or extend `AbstractAuthenticator` / `AbstractLoginFormAuthenticator`). Their key methods are `supports()`, `authenticate()` (returning a Passport), `onAuthenticationSuccess()`, and `onAuthenticationFailure()`. They are wired by listing the service ID under `firewalls.<name>.custom_authenticators`.

references.title: How to Write a Custom Authenticator - Security Passports
references.summary: A `Passport` is the object returned by `authenticate()` that carries the user-to-be-authenticated together with credentials and additional badges. The base `Passport` class requires a `UserBadge` and a credentials object such as `PasswordCredentials` or `CustomCredentials`.

references.title: How to Write a Custom Authenticator - User Identifier
references.summary: `UserBadge` carries the unique user identifier. Its constructor accepts `(string $userIdentifier, ?callable $userLoader = null, ?callable $identifierNormalizer = null)`. Identifier strings are limited to 4096 characters to avoid session-storage flooding attacks.

references.title: How to Write a Custom Authenticator - User Credential
references.summary: Built-in credential classes are `PasswordCredentials` (validated against the configured password hasher) and `CustomCredentials` (which takes a closure receiving the credentials and the user, returning a boolean). Credentials are passed as the second constructor argument to `Passport`.

references.title: How to Write a Custom Authenticator - Self Validating Passport
references.summary: `SelfValidatingPassport` is used when no separate credentials need to be checked (e.g. opaque API tokens already validated in `authenticate()`). It only requires a `UserBadge` and an optional list of badges.

references.title: How to Write a Custom Authenticator - Passport Badges
references.summary: Badges attach extra behavior to a passport. Built-in badges include `RememberMeBadge` (enable remember-me cookie), `PasswordUpgradeBadge` (auto-rehash password; added automatically with `PasswordCredentials`), `CsrfTokenBadge` (validate CSRF token), and `PreAuthenticatedUserBadge` (skip the pre-auth user checker).

references.title: How to Write a Custom Authenticator - Passport Attributes
references.summary: Passports also expose arbitrary `setAttribute()` / `getAttribute()` storage so `authenticate()` can pass data to other authenticator methods such as `createToken()` (e.g. an OAuth scope or custom token-claims map).

references.title: Security Configuration Reference - required_badges
references.summary: Lists badge class names that **must** be present on every passport produced by authenticators of the firewall, otherwise authentication is rejected. A common use case is requiring `CsrfTokenBadge` to ensure CSRF protection is never accidentally skipped.

references.title: How to use Access Token Authentication
references.summary: Documents the **`access_token` firewall authenticator**, used for API tokens. It pulls the token from a configurable extractor (`header`, `query_string`, `request_body`), then resolves it to a user through a `token_handler` service implementing `AccessTokenHandlerInterface::getUserBadgeFrom(string $accessToken)`.

references.title: Customizing the Form Login Authenticator Responses
references.summary: Built-in **form_login authenticator** handles standard username/password login forms. The article shows how to control the success/failure responses by implementing `AuthenticationSuccessHandlerInterface` / `AuthenticationFailureHandlerInterface` (or providing a `default_target_path`, `success_handler`, `failure_handler` firewall option).

references.title: How to use Passwordless Login Link Authentication
references.summary: The **login link authenticator** implements "magic link" passwordless login. Configure `login_link: { check_route, signature_properties, lifetime }` under a firewall; `LoginLinkHandlerInterface::createLoginLink($user)` produces the signed URL, while clicking it authenticates the user via a `LoginLinkAuthenticator`.

references.title: How to Use Voters to Check User Permissions
references.summary: Voters centralize permission logic. Symfony invokes every registered voter when `isGranted()`, `denyAccessUnlessGranted()`, `#[IsGranted]`, or `access_control` rules are evaluated, and reaches a final decision through the configured decision strategy (`affirmative`, `consensus`, `unanimous`, `priority`).

references.title: How to Use Voters to Check User Permissions - The Voter Interface
references.summary: Custom voters either implement `VoterInterface` directly or extend the abstract `Voter` class, which exposes the `supports(string $attribute, mixed $subject)` and `voteOnAttribute(string $attribute, mixed $subject, TokenInterface $token, ?Vote $vote)` template methods. The base `Voter` class also implements `CacheableVoterInterface` for performance optimizations.

references.title: How to Use Voters to Check User Permissions - Creating the Custom Voter
references.summary: `supports()` must return `true` only when the voter cares about the attribute/subject combination; otherwise the voter abstains. `voteOnAttribute()` returns a boolean indicating grant or deny and may call `$vote->addReason(...)` to record explanations consumed by exception pages and logs.

references.title: How to Use Voters to Check User Permissions - Configuring the Voter
references.summary: Voters become active once registered as services tagged with `security.voter`. With the default `services.yaml` configuration this happens automatically through autoconfiguration, so no manual tag is needed for application-defined voters.

references.title: How to Use Voters to Check User Permissions - Checking for Roles inside a Voter
references.summary: To check role-based access from inside a voter, inject `AccessDecisionManagerInterface` and call `decide($token, ['ROLE_SUPER_ADMIN'])`. Calling `Security::isGranted()` from a voter is discouraged because it does not guarantee that the same `TokenInterface` is used.

references.title: How to Use Voters to Check User Permissions - Improving Voter Performance
references.summary: By implementing `CacheableVoterInterface` (already inherited by the abstract `Voter` class), a voter can override `supportsAttribute(string $attribute)` and `supportsType(string $subjectType)` so Symfony skips the voter entirely for unsupported attributes or subject classes.

references.title: How to Use Voters to Check User Permissions - Changing the Access Decision Strategy
references.summary: The four built-in strategies are `affirmative` (default; one grant is enough), `consensus` (majority wins; ties are decided by `allow_if_equal_granted_denied`), `unanimous` (no voter may deny), and `priority` (the first non-abstaining voter, ranked by service priority, decides). When all voters abstain the result is governed by `allow_if_all_abstain` (default `false`).

references.title: How to Use Voters to Check User Permissions - Custom Access Decision Strategy
references.summary: When the four built-in strategies are not enough, set `security.access_decision_manager.strategy_service` to the service ID of a class implementing `AccessDecisionStrategyInterface::decide()`. Voters can also store data in `$vote->extraData[...]` to be aggregated by the custom strategy (e.g. weighted voting).

references.title: Security - Granting Anonymous Users Access in a Custom Voter
references.summary: Shows how a custom voter can handle unauthenticated visitors explicitly. The voter checks whether `$token->getUser()` is an instance of `UserInterface`; if not, it can apply anonymous-access business rules such as allowing public posts.

references.title: The Messenger Component
references.summary: Introduces the Messenger component as a standalone library that helps applications send and receive messages locally or via brokers. The component is heavily inspired by command-bus patterns and is the foundation underneath the FrameworkBundle integration.

references.title: The Messenger Component - Concepts
references.summary: Defines the core building blocks: Sender, Receiver, Handler, Middleware, Envelope, and Envelope Stamps. Senders serialize and emit messages; Receivers pull and forward them to handlers via the `HandleMessageMiddleware`; Stamps attach metadata that travels with the Envelope across middleware and transports.

references.title: The Messenger Component - Bus
references.summary: Explains the message bus and its ordered middleware stack. With FrameworkBundle the bus is configured with `SendMessageMiddleware` and `HandleMessageMiddleware`; every middleware must implement `Symfony\Component\Messenger\Middleware\MiddlewareInterface`.

references.title: Messenger - Getting Results from your Handlers
references.summary: After dispatching, the returned `Envelope` carries one `HandledStamp` per handler that ran. `Envelope::last(HandledStamp::class)` returns the last handler's value, while `Envelope::all(HandledStamp::class)` lists every handler invocation with its result.

references.title: Messenger - Getting Results when Working with Command & Query Buses
references.summary: `HandleTrait` exposes a `handle()` method that requires exactly one registered handler and returns the handler's result. It is the recommended way to build query-bus classes that return values synchronously; the trait expects a `$messageBus` property.

references.title: Messenger - Multiple Buses, Command & Event Buses
references.summary: Shows how to declare command, query, and event buses, each with its own middleware stack. The `default_bus` decides which bus is autowired with `MessageBusInterface`; the other buses are autowired by parameter name (e.g., `MessageBusInterface $queryBus`).

references.title: Messenger - Restrict Handlers per Bus
references.summary: By default a handler runs for any bus. The `bus` option of the `messenger.message_handler` tag (or `#[AsMessageHandler(bus: ...)]`) restricts it to a single bus. The `_instanceof` service configuration assigns buses based on implemented interfaces.

references.title: Messenger - Redispatching a Message
references.summary: `RedispatchMessage` paired with the built-in `RedispatchMessageHandler` re-sends a message through the same bus and envelope. A second constructor argument can override the transports that should receive the redispatch.

references.title: Messenger - Debugging the Buses
references.summary: The `debug:messenger` console command lists each bus and the messages each handler can dispatch, optionally restricted to a single bus by name. The output includes PHPDoc descriptions of message and handler classes.

references.title: Messenger - Transports: Async/Queued Messages
references.summary: By default messages are handled synchronously. A transport, registered with a DSN under `framework.messenger.transports`, sends messages elsewhere so they can be received later by a worker. Each transport entry can be defined as a single DSN string or expanded with `dsn` and `options` keys.

references.title: Messenger - Routing Messages to a Transport
references.summary: Routing is configured under `framework.messenger.routing` (YAML/PHP) or with the `#[AsMessage]` attribute on the message class. A trailing `*` in a namespace matches all messages within it; `*` alone is a default route. YAML/PHP configuration takes precedence over `#[AsMessage]`, and routing for both child and parent classes applies cumulatively.

references.title: Messenger - Transport Configuration
references.summary: Transport options are accepted through a DSN string and through the `options` key in YAML/PHP. When the same option is set in both places, values defined under `options` take precedence over the DSN.

references.title: Messenger - In Memory Transport
references.summary: The `in-memory://` transport keeps messages in process memory instead of delivering them, which is ideal for tests. Within `KernelTestCase`/`WebTestCase` the transport is reset automatically between tests, and `getSent()` exposes the recorded envelopes for assertions.

references.title: Messenger - Serializing Messages
references.summary: By default Messenger serializes messages with PHP's native `serialize()`/`unserialize()`. The `messenger.transport.symfony_serializer` service uses the Serializer component (default JSON) and can be set globally as `default_serializer` or per transport. `SerializerStamp` configures per-message serialization context.

references.title: Messenger - Rate Limited Transport
references.summary: Setting `rate_limiter` on a transport applies a configured rate limiter (RateLimiter component) to its worker. When the limit is hit the entire worker blocks, so a rate-limited transport should run on a dedicated worker.

references.title: Messenger - Message Serializer For Custom Data Formats
references.summary: Implementing `Symfony\Component\Messenger\Transport\Serialization\SerializerInterface` produces a custom serializer that decodes/encodes messages from another application's format. The serializer is selected per transport via the `serializer` option on the transport configuration.

references.title: How to Create Your own Messenger Transport
references.summary: Custom transports are implemented by writing a `TransportFactoryInterface` that returns a transport when its DSN is selected. The factory is registered as a service tagged with `messenger.transport_factory` and consumed automatically by Messenger.

references.title: Messenger - Creating a Message & Handler
references.summary: A message is any serializable object; a handler is a PHP callable. The recommended handler is a class annotated with `#[AsMessageHandler]` exposing an `__invoke()` typed with the message class; autoconfiguration registers it. The attribute can also be applied to individual methods so a single class handles multiple message types.

references.title: Messenger - Dispatching the Message
references.summary: Messages are sent by calling `dispatch()` on `MessageBusInterface`, which is autowired to the `messenger.default_bus` service. The bus then runs its middleware chain to send to a transport or invoke handlers.

references.title: Messenger - Handling Messages Synchronously
references.summary: Messages without a routing rule are handled immediately. Defining a `sync://` transport and routing messages to it handles them synchronously through the same code paths used for asynchronous messages, which helps when binding handlers to specific transports.

references.title: Messenger - Manually Configuring Handlers
references.summary: Handlers accept options through the `messenger.message_handler` tag or the `#[AsMessageHandler]` attribute: `bus`, `from_transport`, `handles`, `method`, `priority`, and `sign`. Higher `priority` runs first when multiple handlers are registered for the same message, and each handler runs only after the previous one finishes.

references.title: Messenger - Handling Multiple Messages
references.summary: A single class can handle several message types by attaching `#[AsMessageHandler]` to multiple methods. Each method handles the message type matched by its parameter type-hint.

references.title: Messenger - Binding Handlers to Different Transports
references.summary: The `from_transport` option in YAML, or `#[AsMessageHandler(fromTransport: '...')]`, restricts a handler to messages received from a specific transport. Without `from_transport`, a handler runs whenever the message is received, regardless of which transport delivered it.

references.title: Messenger - Process Messages by Batches
references.summary: Implementing `BatchHandlerInterface` together with `BatchHandlerTrait` lets a handler accumulate messages until a batch is full and process them via `process(array $jobs)`. The default batch size is 10 (override `getBatchSize()`); pending batches flush when the worker is idle or stops.

references.title: Messenger - Doctrine Entities in Messages
references.summary: Pass primary keys (or other minimal identifying data) inside messages instead of full Doctrine entities, then re-fetch the entity in the handler. This avoids issues with the Entity Manager and ensures the handler sees fresh data.

references.title: Messenger - Versioning Message Classes
references.summary: Messages already in queues must remain deserializable across deploys. Backwards-compatible changes use new optional constructor arguments with defaults; removing a property risks PHP 8.2+ dynamic-property warnings, so options include keeping the property temporarily, marking the class with `#[\AllowDynamicProperties]`, or shipping a versioned class such as `SendInvoiceV2` until the queue drains.

references.title: Messenger - Transactional Messages: Handle New Messages After Handling is Done
references.summary: Adding a `DispatchAfterCurrentBusStamp` defers a dispatched message until the current handler finishes successfully, so dependent messages are not handled if the parent throws. The `dispatch_after_current_bus` middleware is enabled by default and must precede `doctrine_transaction` in the middleware chain.

references.title: Messenger - Trigger a Command
references.summary: Dispatching `RunCommandMessage` lets Messenger execute a Symfony console command through its built-in handler. `throwOnFailure` and `catchExceptions` configure error behavior, and a handled command returns a `RunCommandContext` containing details such as the exit code and output.

references.title: Messenger - Trigger An External Process
references.summary: Dispatching `RunProcessMessage` lets Messenger execute an external process using the Process component. For shell-specific features such as redirections or pipes, `RunProcessMessage::fromShellCommandline()` creates the message, and a handled process returns a `RunProcessContext` with execution details.

references.title: Messenger - Consuming Messages (Running the Worker)
references.summary: The `messenger:consume` command runs a worker that pulls messages from a transport and invokes handlers. `--all` consumes from every receiver and `--exclude-receivers` skips specific ones. To stop a worker cleanly from inside a handler, throw `Symfony\Component\Messenger\Exception\StopWorkerException`.

references.title: Messenger - Deploying to Production
references.summary: Workers should run under a process manager such as Supervisor or systemd and should not run forever. Use `--limit`, `--memory-limit`, `--time-limit`, and `--failure-limit` to bound their lifetime, and run `messenger:stop-workers` after each deploy so each worker finishes its current message and restarts against the new code.

references.title: Messenger - Prioritized Transports
references.summary: Assign different message types to dedicated transports so a slow handler does not delay other flows. A worker can list multiple transports in priority order: it always pulls from the first non-empty transport before falling back to lower-priority ones.

references.title: Messenger - Checking the Number of Queued Messages Per Transport
references.summary: The `messenger:stats` command reports how many messages each transport currently holds, optionally filtered by transport name and with `--format=json`. The transport's receiver must implement `MessageCountAwareInterface` for stats to be available.

references.title: Messenger - Supervisor Configuration
references.summary: Provides a sample `[program:messenger-consume]` Supervisor configuration that keeps multiple worker processes running. With the PCNTL extension installed, workers handle `SIGTERM`/`SIGINT` by finishing the current message before exiting, and `framework.messenger.stop_worker_on_signals` overrides the default signal list.

references.title: Messenger - Systemd Configuration
references.summary: Documents running workers as systemd user services with template units like `messenger-worker@.service`. Lingering must be enabled with `loginctl enable-linger` so consumers start at boot, and logs are inspected through `journalctl --user-unit`.

references.title: Messenger - Stateless Worker
references.summary: Workers are long-running CLI processes, so service state can leak between messages. Symfony resets the container between messages by calling `reset()` on services that implement `Symfony\Contracts\Service\ResetInterface`. The `--no-reset` option of `messenger:consume` disables this behavior.

references.title: Messenger - Retries & Failures
references.summary: Failed messages are retried up to `max_retries` (default 3) before being discarded or sent to the failure transport. The retry strategy is configured per transport via `delay`, `multiplier`, `max_delay`, and `jitter`, or by providing a custom service implementing `RetryStrategyInterface`. `SerializedMessageStamp` preserves the original serialized form across retries.

references.title: Messenger - Avoiding Retrying
references.summary: Throwing `UnrecoverableMessageHandlingException` from a handler stops retry attempts immediately. The message is still sent to the configured failure transport unless the handler decides to swallow the error itself.

references.title: Messenger - Forcing Retrying
references.summary: Throwing `RecoverableMessageHandlingException` forces the message to be retried, bypassing the `max_retries` setting. The constructor's `retryDelay` argument lets the handler set a custom delay in milliseconds (for example to honor an HTTP `Retry-After` header).

references.title: Messenger - Saving & Retrying Failed Messages
references.summary: Setting `failure_transport` routes messages that exhausted their retries to a separate transport. The `messenger:failed:show`, `messenger:failed:retry`, and `messenger:failed:remove` commands inspect, retry, or delete failed messages, with options like `--class-filter`, `--stats`, and `--all`.

references.title: Messenger - Writing Idempotent Handlers
references.summary: Messages may be delivered more than once because a transport can redeliver a message a worker crashed before acknowledging. Handlers should be idempotent, and non-idempotent operations should rely on a stable idempotency key derived from the business event (not generated at dispatch time) plus a database uniqueness constraint.

references.title: Messenger - Multiple Failed Transports
references.summary: Each transport can override the global `failure_transport` by setting its own `failure_transport`. The `messenger:failed:*` commands accept a `--transport` option to operate on a specific failure transport, and without any `failure_transport` exhausted messages are simply discarded.

references.title: Messenger - Middleware
references.summary: Each bus runs an ordered middleware chain. The default chain is `add_bus_name_stamp_middleware`, `dispatch_after_current_bus`, `failed_message_processing_middleware`, user middleware, `send_message`, and `handle_message`. Setting `default_middleware: false` disables the defaults, and the chain runs both on dispatch and again when the worker receives the message.

references.title: Messenger - Message Deduplication
references.summary: Adding a `DeduplicateStamp` to the envelope makes the deduplication middleware skip duplicate dispatches based on the stamp's business-level key. The middleware is enabled automatically when the Lock component is installed; the third constructor argument toggles whether the lock is released as soon as the message is received instead of after handling.

references.title: Messenger - Middleware for Doctrine
references.summary: Optional Doctrine middlewares for buses include `doctrine_ping_connection` (pings/reconnects between messages), `doctrine_close_connection` (closes the connection after handling), `doctrine_open_transaction_logger` (warns about leaked transactions), and `doctrine_transaction` (wraps handlers in a single transaction with automatic rollback on errors).

references.title: Messenger - Other Middlewares
references.summary: The `router_context` middleware preserves the current request context so absolute URLs can be generated inside handlers. The `validation` middleware validates the message via the Validator component before handling and throws `ValidationFailedException` on failure; `ValidationStamp` can choose validation groups.

references.title: Messenger - Securing Messages with Signatures
references.summary: When the queue is shared with untrusted producers, attackers could inject forged payloads. Symfony adds an extra layer through HMAC message signing, which is enabled by default for `RunProcessHandler` because it executes external processes.

references.title: Messenger - Enabling Message Signing
references.summary: Setting `sign: true` on a handler tag (or `#[AsMessageHandler(sign: true)]`) signs outgoing messages with HMAC using `kernel.secret`. The signature is stored in the `Body-Sign` and `Sign-Algo` headers and verified on receive; an invalid or missing signature raises `InvalidMessageSignatureException` and the message is not handled.

references.title: Messenger - Envelopes & Stamps
references.summary: Every message is wrapped in an `Envelope` that holds the message and an ordered set of stamps; built-in stamps include `DelayStamp`, `HandledStamp`, `ReceivedStamp`, `SentStamp`, `SerializerStamp`, `ValidationStamp`, `ErrorDetailsStamp`, and `TransportNamesStamp`. Stamps that travel through transports must be serializable when the default Messenger serializer is used.

references.title: Messenger - Default Stamps on Messages
references.summary: A message can declare its own default stamps by implementing `DefaultStampsProviderInterface::getDefaultStamps()`. On dispatch each default stamp is added only if no stamp of the same class is already present on the envelope, so explicit stamps override the defaults.

references.title: Messenger - Additional Handler Arguments
references.summary: Adding a `HandlerArgumentsStamp` to the envelope (typically inside a custom middleware) injects extra positional arguments into the handler's `__invoke()` after the message itself. The stamp's value is an array of additional arguments to pass to the handler.

references.title: Messenger - Messenger Events
references.summary: Messenger dispatches several events on top of its middleware chain so that listeners can hook into the lifecycle: `SendMessageToTransportsEvent`, `MessageSentToTransportsEvent`, `WorkerMessageReceivedEvent`, `WorkerMessageHandledEvent`, `WorkerMessageFailedEvent`, `WorkerMessageRetriedEvent`, `WorkerRateLimitedEvent`, `WorkerStartedEvent`, `WorkerRunningEvent`, and `WorkerStoppedEvent`. `MessageSentToTransportsEvent` fires only once even if the message was sent to multiple transports.

references.title: The Console Component
references.summary: The Console component lets you build command-line interfaces by creating an Application and registering Command instances. The standalone bootstrap requires composer require symfony/console, instantiates Application, registers commands via addCommand() (or inline via register()->setCode()), and calls run() from a PHP script.

references.title: Console Commands
references.summary: Symfony's bin/console script exposes all framework and user-defined commands; commands are PHP classes annotated with #[AsCommand] and discovered via autoconfiguration. Each command must return an integer exit status using Command::SUCCESS, Command::FAILURE or Command::INVALID.

references.title: How to Call Other Commands
references.summary: A command can call another command in the same process by building an ArrayInput and invoking Application::doRun(). doRun() (unlike run()) does not auto-exit and dispatches console events for the inner command, while NullOutput suppresses its output.

references.title: How to Call a Command from a Controller
references.summary: FrameworkBundle's Application can be instantiated from the kernel inside a controller; calling setAutoExit(false) and run() with an ArrayInput and BufferedOutput captures the command result as a Response. The doc notes a small performance overhead from the request stack and recommends extracting reusable logic into services.

references.title: Console Commands - Running Commands
references.summary: Built-in commands include list (also the default when no command is given), help (displays a command's docs and global options), about and completion. The --help/-h global option is provided for every command, including custom ones.

references.title: Using Console Commands, Shortcuts and Built-in Commands
references.summary: The component ships with the list and help built-in commands plus global options (--help, --quiet, --silent, --verbose, --version, --ansi/--no-ansi, --no-interaction). It also documents shortcut syntax: any unambiguous prefix of a command name (or namespaced segment) selects that command.

references.title: Console Commands - Console Completion
references.summary: The completion command dumps shell completion scripts for Bash, Zsh and Fish; once installed, it auto-completes command names, options and even argument values. Many third-party CLIs built on the Console component (Composer, PHPStan, Behat) expose the same completion command.

references.title: How to Hide Console Commands
references.summary: Setting hidden: true on the #[AsCommand] attribute (or prefixing the name with a pipe like '|app:legacy') excludes a command from the list output, but it remains executable and visible in the JSON/XML descriptor.

references.title: Console Commands - Creating a Command
references.summary: Symfony 8 commands are defined as PHP classes auto-registered by the #[AsCommand] attribute (with name, description, help, usages parameters). Invokable commands implement __invoke() and are recommended; classes that extend Command also gain initialize(), interact() and execute() lifecycle hooks.

references.title: Console Commands - Command Aliases
references.summary: Aliases are declared inside the #[AsCommand] name parameter using the pipe (|) separator (e.g. 'app:create-user|app:add-user|app:new-user'); the first segment becomes the canonical name and subsequent ones are aliases.

references.title: Console Commands - Command Lifecycle
references.summary: When extending Command, three lifecycle methods run in order: initialize() to set up shared state, interact() to ask the user for missing required values (skipped with --no-interaction), and __invoke()/execute() (the only required method) which contains the logic and returns the integer exit status.

references.title: How to Define Commands as Services
references.summary: With the default services.yaml configuration, command classes are autowired and tagged with console.command via autoconfiguration. To register manually, tag the service with console.command and a 'command' attribute equal to the command name (one tag per alias for non-#[AsCommand] commands).

references.title: How to Make Commands Lazily Loaded
references.summary: Application::setCommandLoader() accepts any CommandLoaderInterface; FactoryCommandLoader registers callables that build Command instances on demand, while ContainerCommandLoader resolves commands from a PSR-11 container by service id. LazyCommand wraps a factory so the underlying command body is not built until the command is actually executed.

references.title: Prevent Running the Same Console Command Multiple Times
references.summary: The LockableTrait adds lock() and release() methods to a command; lock() returns false if another invocation already holds the lock, and the lock is auto-released when the command ends. The trait uses SemaphoreStore when available and falls back to FlockStore, or a custom LockFactory injected into the $lockFactory property.

references.title: How to Call Other Commands
references.summary: Shows how a **command can run another command** from inside its own `execute()` (or `__invoke()`) — useful for "meta" commands that orchestrate a series of build/cache/migration commands. The recipe uses `$this->getApplication()->find()->run()` with an `ArrayInput`; output can be muted with a `NullOutput`.

references.title: How to Call a Command from a Controller
references.summary: Shows how to invoke a console command from an HTTP **controller** — by autowiring `KernelInterface`, building an `Application($kernel)`, calling `setAutoExit(false)`, and passing an `ArrayInput`. The captured output (e.g. via `BufferedOutput`) can then be inspected or returned in the response.

references.title: How to Hide Console Commands
references.summary: Explains how to keep a command **hidden** from the `list` output: set the `hidden` attribute on `#[AsCommand(name: ..., hidden: true)]`, or call `$this->setHidden(true)` in `configure()`. Hidden commands are still executable when invoked by name.

references.title: Console Commands - APP_ENV & APP_DEBUG
references.summary: Console commands run in the environment defined by the APP_ENV variable (default dev) and use APP_DEBUG to toggle debug mode (default 1). Both can be overridden inline (e.g. APP_ENV=prod php bin/console cache:clear) and the FrameworkBundle adds the matching --env/-e and --no-debug global options.

references.title: Building a single Command Application
references.summary: SingleCommandApplication lets a CLI tool expose a single default command without typing its name on each call. It accepts setName(), setVersion() and setCode() with parameters annotated by #[Argument]/#[Option], and is equivalent to calling Application::setDefaultCommand($name, true).

references.title: Changing the Default Command
references.summary: Application::setDefaultCommand($name) replaces the built-in ListCommand with the given command when the application is invoked without a command name. The default-command mode documented here cannot receive arguments or options for that default command because they are ignored.

references.title: Console Commands - Console Completion
references.summary: Configuring shell completion is a one-time setup: install the script printed by bin/console completion --help for Bash, Zsh or Fish; on Bash, the bash-completion package is required. After restart, Tab triggers command, option and value completion.

references.title: Console Input - Using Arguments in Invokable Commands
references.summary: The #[Argument] attribute declares a console argument directly on an __invoke() parameter. The mode is inferred from the parameter type: required when there is no default and the type is not nullable, optional when a default value is provided, and an array when the parameter is typed as array. BackedEnum types are resolved with from() and gain autocompletion.

references.title: Console Input - Using Options in Invokable Commands
references.summary: The #[Option] attribute declares a console option on an __invoke() parameter. The mode is inferred from the type/default pair: bool false yields VALUE_NONE, bool true (or ?bool null) yields VALUE_NEGATABLE, scalar types with a default yield VALUE_REQUIRED, array yields VALUE_IS_ARRAY, and string|bool/int|bool/float|bool unions with default false yield VALUE_OPTIONAL.

references.title: Understanding Console Arguments and Options - Option Attribute Constraints
references.summary: The #[Option] attribute enforces strict rules in invokable commands: options must always have a default value, nullable bool options must default to null (not true/false), nullable non-bool options must default to null, and only string|bool, int|bool and float|bool unions with default false are allowed.

references.title: Console Input - Mapping Input to Objects
references.summary: #[MapInput] groups #[Argument]/#[Option] attributes into a DTO class, which is instantiated without invoking its constructor (values are assigned directly to public properties); private/protected/static properties are ignored. DTOs can be nested via #[MapInput] on a property to compose reusable input definitions.

references.title: Console Input - Interactive Input
references.summary: The #[Ask] attribute prompts for missing required arguments without a custom interact() method (with options like hidden, timeout, maxAttempts, multiline) and #[Interact] marks a custom interactive callback. The execution order during interactive mode is #[Ask] on parameters, then #[Ask] on DTO properties, then #[Interact] on the DTO, then #[Interact] on the command.

references.title: Console Input - Adding Argument/Option Value Completion
references.summary: The suggestedValues parameter on #[Argument] and #[Option] supplies completion data: a static array of values, or a callable referencing a method that receives a CompletionInput and returns the list. Removing static from the suggestion method allows it to use injected services to compute dynamic suggestions.

references.title: Console Input - Using the Classic configure() Method
references.summary: Inside Command::configure(), addArgument() declares arguments with InputArgument modes: REQUIRED, OPTIONAL or IS_ARRAY (which only makes sense as the last argument). Argument values are read with $input->getArgument(name); IS_ARRAY can be combined with REQUIRED to mandate at least one value.

references.title: Console Input - Using the Classic addOption() Method
references.summary: addOption() supports five InputOption modes: VALUE_NONE (default, boolean flag), VALUE_REQUIRED, VALUE_OPTIONAL, VALUE_IS_ARRAY (must be combined with REQUIRED or OPTIONAL) and VALUE_NEGATABLE (--flag/--no-flag). Long options accept a space or = before the value, while short options use a space or no separator.

references.title: Console Input - Fetching The Raw Command Input
references.summary: ArgvInput::getRawTokens() returns the array of raw tokens passed to the command (including the command name); passing true skips the command name. This is useful to forward the original input to a subprocess without re-parsing arguments and options.

references.title: Console Commands - Console Output
references.summary: OutputInterface::writeln() writes one or more strings followed by a newline (an array of strings produces one line per element), while write() writes without appending a newline. Both accept the verbosity level as the second argument to filter messages.

references.title: Console Commands - Output Sections
references.summary: ConsoleOutput::section() returns a ConsoleSectionOutput that can be independently overwritten via overwrite() or cleared via clear() (optionally limiting how many trailing lines to remove). setMaxHeight() caps the number of visible lines so new lines replace the oldest, enabling features like multiple progress bars and dynamic tables.

references.title: Understanding how Console Arguments and Options Are Handled
references.summary: Symfony follows the docopt standard for parsing arguments and options: short options accept their value with a space or no separator (e.g. -b Hello, -bHello), long options accept either a space or = (e.g. --bar Hello or --bar=Hello), and the special -- separator forces the rest of the command line to be parsed as arguments.

references.title: Progress Bar
references.summary: ProgressBar shows iteration progress; pass a max number of steps and call advance() (or pass a negative number to step backwards), iterate() to wrap an iterable, or omit the max to render a throbber. setFormat() switches between built-in formats (normal/verbose/very_verbose/debug and their _nomax variants), and ProgressBar::setFormatDefinition() defines named formats.

references.title: Question Helper
references.summary: QuestionHelper::ask() reads input via Question, ConfirmationQuestion or ChoiceQuestion. Confirmation questions match anything starting with 'y' by default (configurable regex), choice questions accept the value or numeric index, and ChoiceQuestion::setMultiselect(true) accepts comma-separated answers.

references.title: Table Helper
references.summary: Table renders data as ASCII tables with setHeaders() and setRows() before render(); insert TableSeparator instances inside rows to draw separators. Compared to SymfonyStyle::table(), Table allows full styling customization (column padding, borders, cell colspans/rowspans).

references.title: How to Style a Console Command
references.summary: SymfonyStyle wraps an OutputInterface to provide semantic helpers like title(), section(), text(), listing(), table(), note(), caution(), success(), info(), warning(), error(), as well as ask(), askHidden(), confirm(), choice() and progressStart()/progressAdvance()/progressFinish()/progressIterate(). getErrorStyle() returns a sibling style that writes to stderr.

references.title: How to Style a Console Command - Output Coloring
references.summary: Output coloring uses XML-like tags such as <info>, <comment>, <question>, <error> (auto-closing with </>); custom styles are registered via OutputFormatterStyle and OutputFormatter::setStyle(). Tags also accept inline attributes (fg, bg, options) with named colors, hex colors, and options like bold, underscore, blink, reverse, conceal.

references.title: The Console Helpers
references.summary: Index page that catalogues Symfony's built-in **Console helpers**: `formatter`, `process`, `progress bar`, `progress indicator`, `question`, `table`, `tree`, `cursor`, `debug formatter`. Helpers are reusable pieces of console UI that ship inside the Console component.

references.title: Cursor Helper
references.summary: The **`Cursor`** helper moves the terminal cursor to arbitrary positions and clears parts of the screen. Methods include `moveUp()`, `moveDown()`, `moveLeft()`, `moveRight()`, `moveToColumn()`, `moveToPosition()`, `savePosition()`, `restorePosition()`, `clearLine()`, `clearOutput()`, `clearScreen()`, `hide()`, `show()`.

references.title: Debug Formatter Helper
references.summary: The **`DebugFormatterHelper`** prints structured debug output for an external program (e.g. a `Process` or HTTP request) using a coloured, indented stream that distinguishes start/output/error/finish events. It is typically used together with the `Process` component to live-stream a sub-process's stdout/stderr.

references.title: Formatter Helper
references.summary: The **`FormatterHelper`** wraps text with predefined or custom **`OutputFormatterStyle`** styles. Methods include `formatSection()` (label + message), `formatBlock()` (multi-line block, optional `large` mode), and `truncate()` (cut to a maximum length with a configurable suffix).

references.title: Process Helper
references.summary: The **`ProcessHelper`** runs an external command via the `Process` component while reporting its progress through the `DebugFormatterHelper`. The `run()` method accepts a `Process` instance or a command line, an output, an optional error message, an optional callback for streaming and a `verbosity` threshold below which output is hidden.

references.title: Progress Indicator
references.summary: The **`ProgressIndicator`** shows an animated spinner for tasks **without a known size** (when a regular progress bar can't be used). The lifecycle is `start("...")`, repeated `advance()`, then `finish("...")`; the spinner format and frame set are configurable via `setFormat()` and `setFormatDefinition()`.

references.title: Tree Helper
references.summary: The **`TreeHelper`** (and `TreeNode` value object) renders **tree-shaped data** in the console — directory hierarchies, category trees, organisational charts. Style is configurable through `TreeStyle` constants such as `BOX`, `BOX_DOUBLE`, `LIGHT`, `MAKEFILE`, `ROUNDED`.

references.title: Using Events
references.summary: The Console Application can be wired to a Symfony EventDispatcher via setDispatcher(). Console events are dispatched only by the main command being executed; commands invoked indirectly without going through the Application do not trigger events.

references.title: Using Events - The ConsoleEvents::COMMAND Event
references.summary: ConsoleEvents::COMMAND fires before any command runs and dispatches a ConsoleCommandEvent exposing getInput(), getOutput() and getCommand(). A listener may call disableCommand() to skip execution (the application then returns the reserved code 113), and enableCommand() in a later listener undoes that decision.

references.title: Using Events - The ConsoleEvents::ERROR Event
references.summary: ConsoleEvents::ERROR is dispatched whenever an exception is thrown by a command (or one of its listeners). The ConsoleErrorEvent provides getOutput(), getCommand(), getError() and getExitCode(), and setError() allows a listener to swap the exception that will eventually be thrown by the application.

references.title: Using Events - The ConsoleEvents::TERMINATE Event
references.summary: ConsoleEvents::TERMINATE fires after every command run, including failed ones (after ConsoleEvents::ERROR) and on signal-driven termination. Listeners receive a ConsoleTerminateEvent and may call setExitCode() to change the application's final exit status.

references.title: Using Events - The ConsoleEvents::SIGNAL Event
references.summary: ConsoleEvents::SIGNAL is dispatched when a command receives a POSIX signal (e.g. SIGINT from Ctrl+C). The ConsoleSignalEvent exposes getHandlingSignal() and setExitCode(); abortExit() lets the command keep running after the listener completes. Signal handling depends on the PCNTL extension.

references.title: Using Events - The ConsoleAlarmEvent
references.summary: Calling Application::setAlarmInterval($seconds) registers a recurring SIGALRM and dispatches ConsoleAlarmEvent on every tick, useful for keepalives and TTL extensions during long-running commands. The feature requires the pcntl PHP extension and is unavailable on Windows.

references.title: Console Commands - Logging Command Errors
references.summary: When a command throws, Symfony logs the failing invocation including the full command line. It also registers an event subscriber on ConsoleEvents::TERMINATE that adds a log message whenever a command exits with a status other than 0.

references.title: Using the Logger
references.summary: The Console component ships **`ConsoleLogger`** — a stand-alone `Psr\Log\LoggerInterface` implementation that writes to an `OutputInterface`. The verbosity threshold of each PSR-3 level (`emergency`, `alert`, …, `debug`) is configurable, defaulting to a sensible mapping (`error` → `VERBOSITY_NORMAL`, `info` → `VERBOSITY_VERBOSE`, `debug` → `VERBOSITY_VERY_VERBOSE`).

references.title: Verbosity Levels
references.summary: Symfony defines six verbosity levels mapped to OutputInterface constants: VERBOSITY_SILENT (-2, --silent), VERBOSITY_QUIET (-1, -q/--quiet), VERBOSITY_NORMAL (0), VERBOSITY_VERBOSE (1, -v), VERBOSITY_VERY_VERBOSE (2, -vv) and VERBOSITY_DEBUG (3, -vvv). The SHELL_VERBOSITY env variable supplies a global default but is overridden by explicit -q/-v options. OutputInterface exposes ->isSilent()/->isQuiet()/->isVerbose()/->isVeryVerbose()/->isDebug(), and write()/writeln() accept a verbosity level as their second argument.

references.title: Using Console Commands - Global Options
references.summary: Every command supports verbosity-related global options: --silent suppresses everything including errors, --quiet/-q suppresses output but still prints errors, and --verbose/-v, -vv, -vvv increase verbosity from VERBOSE to DEBUG. They pair with --ansi/--no-ansi for color control and --no-interaction/-n to disable prompts.

references.title: Testing - Types of Tests
references.summary: Defines the three categories Symfony recognizes: unit tests cover individual classes, integration tests combine classes and use the service container, and application tests exercise the complete application via HTTP. This vocabulary is exam-relevant for telling these test types apart.

references.title: Testing - Installation
references.summary: Installs PHPUnit via the symfony/test-pack metapackage and runs tests with php bin/phpunit. Test classes end with Test, live in tests/, and PHPUnit is configured by phpunit.dist.xml (named phpunit.xml.dist on PHPUnit older than 10) generated by Symfony Flex.

references.title: Testing - Unit Tests
references.summary: Explains that unit tests in Symfony are written with plain PHPUnit and the tests/ directory mirrors the application source tree (a class in src/Form/ is tested in tests/Form/). Autoloading is enabled via vendor/autoload.php as configured by Symfony Flex.

references.title: How to Unit Test your Forms
references.summary: Shows how to unit test custom form types by extending Symfony\Component\Form\Test\TypeTestCase. The test creates the form via the factory, submits data, and asserts that the form is synchronized and that the underlying model is populated as expected.

references.title: How to Unit Test your Forms - Testing Types Registered as Services
references.summary: Shows how to test form types that depend on injected services. The dependencies are mocked, the type is instantiated manually, and Symfony\Component\Form\PreloadedExtension is returned from getExtensions() so the FormRegistry uses the prepared instance.

references.title: How to Unit Test your Forms - Adding Custom Extensions
references.summary: Explains that TypeTestCase only loads the CoreExtension by default. Override getExtensions() to register additional form extensions such as ValidatorExtension, and use FormIntegrationTestCase::getTypes/getTypeExtensions/getTypeGuessers when testing types, type extensions, and type guessers.

references.title: How to Test a Doctrine Repository - Mocking a Doctrine Repository in Unit Tests
references.summary: Shows the PHPUnit pattern for mocking the EntityManager and EntityRepository to unit test classes that depend on Doctrine. Symfony explicitly notes that mocking Doctrine repositories is not recommended in general and they should be tested against a real database.

references.title: Testing - Integration Tests
references.summary: Integration tests use Symfony\Bundle\FrameworkBundle\Test\KernelTestCase to boot the kernel via self::bootKernel() and reboot it for every test so tests stay isolated. The kernel class is read from the KERNEL_CLASS env var defined in .env.test, and getKernelClass() or createKernel() can be overridden.

references.title: Testing - Application Tests
references.summary: Application tests (also called functional tests) extend Symfony\Bundle\FrameworkBundle\Test\WebTestCase, which adds web-client logic on top of KernelTestCase. The workflow is to make a request, interact with the page, assert against the response, and repeat.

references.title: Testing - Write Your First Application Test
references.summary: A WebTestCase test creates a client with static::createClient(), calls $client->request() to issue an HTTP request and obtain a Crawler, and uses Symfony assertions such as assertResponseIsSuccessful() and assertSelectorTextContains(). The make:test command from MakerBundle scaffolds these classes.

references.title: Testing - Configuring a Database for Tests
references.summary: Tests should use a separate database configured via DATABASE_URL in .env.test or .env.test.local. The test schema is created with bin/console --env=test doctrine:database:create and doctrine:schema:create, and DAMADoctrineTestBundle can wrap each test in a transaction that is rolled back.

references.title: Testing - End to End Tests (E2E)
references.summary: End-to-end tests exercise the whole application including JavaScript using a real browser. Symfony provides the Panther component for this, which runs a real browser via WebDriver instead of using the in-process simulated client.

references.title: End-to-End Testing
references.summary: Documents Symfony Panther: install symfony/panther, register the PHPUnit extension, extend Symfony\Component\Panther\PantherTestCase, and use createPantherClient() to drive a real Chromium or Firefox browser. Browser-Kit clients can also be created in the same TestCase.

references.title: How to Customize the Bootstrap Process before Running Tests
references.summary: Symfony Flex creates tests/bootstrap.php which loads Composer's autoloader and the Dotenv environment. Custom logic such as clearing the cache or creating the test schema can be added there, and the file is referenced via the bootstrap attribute of phpunit.dist.xml.

references.title: How to Test a Doctrine Repository - Functional Testing of a Doctrine Repository
references.summary: In functional tests, Doctrine repositories are exercised against a real database by extending KernelTestCase, retrieving the doctrine service via the test container, and calling the repository's methods. The entity manager should be closed in tearDown() to avoid memory leaks.

references.title: How to Test the Interaction of several Clients
references.summary: When several clients are needed in one test (e.g. a chat scenario), call self::ensureKernelShutdown() before creating the next client because creating a client while a kernel is still running triggers a LogicException. Calling $client->insulate() runs each request in a separate PHP process.

references.title: Testing - Making Requests
