references.summary: The test client simulates a browser. $client->request($method, $uri, $parameters = [], $files = [], $server = [], $content = null, $changeHistory = true) returns a Crawler. The kernel is rebooted between requests for isolation; $client->disableReboot() resets services tagged kernel.reset instead. Helpers include back(), forward(), reload(), restart(), followRedirect()/followRedirects(), and xmlHttpRequest(), which sets HTTP_X_REQUESTED_WITH automatically.

references.title: Testing - Interacting with the Response
references.summary: After a response is returned, $client->clickLink($text) clicks the first matching link and $client->submitForm($buttonText, $values) submits the form that contains the named button. Lower-level access uses $crawler->selectLink()->link() with $client->click($link), and $crawler->selectButton()->form() with $client->submit($form).

references.title: The BrowserKit Component
references.summary: BrowserKit simulates a web browser at the PHP level and is the basis of the Symfony test client. It provides AbstractBrowser with request(), click(), submit(), getHistory(), and getCookieJar(), and HttpBrowser implements the same API on top of HttpClient for real HTTP calls.

references.title: How to Test the Interaction of several Clients
references.summary: When a functional test simulates **multiple clients** (e.g. testing a chat between two users), each client lives in **its own PHP process** by default to keep state isolated. Symfony exposes `KernelBrowser::insulate()` to opt in or out per client — useful when sharing fixtures across clients in the same request lifecycle.

references.title: The DOM Crawler
references.summary: The Crawler returned by every $client->request() lets you traverse HTML or XML documents to select nodes, follow links, and submit forms. Each traversal method returns a new Crawler instance so calls can be chained, and count($crawler) returns the number of stored nodes.

references.title: The DOM Crawler - Traversing
references.summary: Lists Crawler traversal methods: filter() for CSS selectors, filterXPath() for XPath, eq() / first() / last() for index-based selection, siblings(), nextAll(), previousAll(), ancestors(), children(), and reduce() for callback-based filtering.

references.title: The DOM Crawler - Extracting Information
references.summary: $crawler->attr() returns the first node's attribute, $crawler->text() returns its text content (with an optional default and a whitespace-normalization flag), $crawler->extract([...]) returns an array of attributes for each node (with _text giving the node value), and $crawler->each() runs a callback per node.

references.title: The DomCrawler Component - Node Filtering
references.summary: Crawler::filter() requires the CssSelector component to translate CSS selectors into XPath; without it only filterXPath() is available. Selection methods include filter(), filterXPath(), and selectLink/selectImage/selectButton for clickable elements.

references.title: The DomCrawler Component - Forms
references.summary: $crawler->selectButton()->form() returns a Symfony\Component\DomCrawler\Form with helpers such as getUri(), getMethod(), getValues(), getFiles(), getPhpValues(), and getPhpFiles(). Field objects expose select(), tick(), and upload() to set values before submission.

references.title: The CssSelector Component
references.summary: The CssSelector component converts CSS selectors to XPath expressions so that the DomCrawler can use CSS-style queries. Methods such as Crawler::filter() throw a RuntimeException when CssSelector is not installed; some advanced CSS pseudo-classes are not supported.

references.title: How to Use the Profiler in a Functional Test
references.summary: Explains how to access the Symfony Profiler from a functional test to assert profiling metrics such as the number of database queries or the time spent in the framework. Profiler usage in tests is reserved for production-monitoring scenarios; regular tests should target the response.

references.title: How to Use the Profiler in a Functional Test - Enabling the Profiler in Tests
references.summary: In the test environment Symfony keeps the profiler enabled but disables data collection (framework.profiler.enabled: true, collect: false). Setting collect to true enables collection globally; calling $client->enableProfiler() turns it on for the next request only and requires the profiler to be enabled in configuration.

references.title: How to Use the Profiler in a Functional Test - Testing the Profiler Information
references.summary: $client->getProfile() returns the Profile collected for the previous request, exposing collectors via $profile->getCollector('db'), $profile->getCollector('time'), and so on. $profile->getToken() can be embedded in assertion messages so the corresponding profile can be inspected in the Web Profiler after a failure.

references.title: Testing - Integration Tests
references.summary: KernelTestCase boots the kernel via self::bootKernel() so tests can access framework objects. The kernel class is taken from the KERNEL_CLASS environment variable provided by Symfony Flex; getKernelClass() or createKernel() can be overridden, and the kernel is rebooted for every test.

references.title: Testing - Retrieving Services in the Test
references.summary: After bootKernel(), static::getContainer() returns a special test container that exposes both public services and non-removed private services. To access private services that have been removed because nothing else uses them, redeclare them as public in config/services_test.yaml.

references.title: Testing - Mocking Dependencies
references.summary: Inside a KernelTestCase, $this->createMock() builds a mock and $container->set($id, $mock) replaces the corresponding service or alias in the test container. Subsequent $container->get() calls receive the mock without any extra configuration because the test container exposes private aliases too.

references.title: Testing - Set-up your Test Environment
references.summary: Tests run in the test environment, which can be configured under config/packages/test/ or via when@test. self::bootKernel(['environment' => 'my_test_env', 'debug' => false]) overrides the environment and debug mode of the booted kernel; running with debug = false speeds up tests on CI but disables cache clearing.

references.title: Testing - Customizing Environment Variables
references.summary: Test env vars can be overridden in .env.test (committed) or .env.test.local (per machine). The test environment loads .env, then .env.test, then .env.test.local; .env.local is intentionally ignored to keep test setups consistent across machines.

references.title: Testing - Making Requests (client configuration)
references.summary: Documents how the WebTestCase client is configured before issuing requests: createClient([], $server) seeds default server parameters, $client->loginUser($user) stores a TestBrowserToken in the session (and accepts a firewall name and tokenAttributes), $client->getSession() lets you preload session data, custom HTTP headers must use the RFC 3875 HTTP_ uppercase prefix, and $client->catchExceptions(false) lets exceptions reach PHPUnit.

references.title: How to Customize the Bootstrap Process before Running Tests
references.summary: PHPUnit's `bootstrap.php` (default `tests/bootstrap.php`) sets up the Symfony environment before any test runs — it includes Composer's autoloader and may reset the test database. The `bootstrap` attribute of `phpunit.xml.dist` chooses the file. Custom bootstrap is the place to install fixtures, clear `var/cache/test`, or pre-warm caches.

references.title: Testing - Making Requests (accessing internal objects)
references.summary: Shows how the Symfony functional test client exposes its internal objects after a request. The client provides access to the latest HttpKernel request, BrowserKit request, HttpKernel response, BrowserKit response, Crawler, history, and cookie jar.

references.title: The BrowserKit Component - History
references.summary: $client->getHistory() returns a History object whose isFirstPage() and isLastPage() methods indicate whether the client is on the first or last entry of the navigation history. Symfony's assertBrowserHistoryIsOnFirstPage() and assertBrowserHistoryIsOnLastPage() asserts wrap these checks.

references.title: The BrowserKit Component - Cookies
references.summary: $client->getCookieJar() returns the CookieJar that holds all cookies received by the client, allowing inspection with get(), iteration with all(), and direct seeding with set(). Symfony's assertBrowserHasCookie() and assertBrowserCookieValueSame() ride on top of this jar.

references.title: Testing - Response Assertions
references.summary: Lists Symfony's response assertions including assertResponseIsSuccessful(), assertResponseStatusCodeSame(), assertResponseRedirects(), assertResponseHasHeader()/assertResponseHeaderSame(), assertResponseHasCookie()/assertResponseCookieValueSame(), assertResponseFormatSame(), and assertResponseIsUnprocessable().

references.title: Testing - Request Assertions
references.summary: assertRequestAttributeValueSame() asserts a request attribute has the expected value, and assertRouteSame($expectedRoute, $parameters = []) asserts that the request matched the given route name and optional parameters.

references.title: Testing - Browser Assertions
references.summary: Browser assertions inspect the test Client itself: assertBrowserHasCookie()/assertBrowserCookieValueSame() check cookies set by any response, assertBrowserHistoryIsOnFirstPage()/assertBrowserHistoryIsOnLastPage() verify history position, and assertThatForClient() runs a custom Constraint against the client.

references.title: Testing - Crawler Assertions
references.summary: Crawler assertions cover the response's DOM: assertSelectorExists()/assertSelectorCount(), assertSelectorTextContains()/assertSelectorTextSame(), assertAnySelectorTextContains(), assertPageTitleSame()/assertPageTitleContains(), assertInputValueSame(), assertCheckboxChecked(), and assertFormValue().

references.title: Testing - Mailer Assertions
references.summary: Mailer assertions include assertEmailCount() and assertQueuedEmailCount() (for emails queued through Messenger), helpers like getMailerEvent() and getMailerMessage(), and per-message asserts such as assertEmailTextBodyContains(), assertEmailHtmlBodyContains(), assertEmailHasHeader(), assertEmailAddressContains(), and assertEmailSubjectContains().

references.title: Testing - HttpClient Assertions
references.summary: HttpClient assertions require $client->enableProfiler() before the code that issues HTTP calls. assertHttpClientRequest() checks that an outgoing URL was called with an optional method, body, and headers, assertNotHttpClientRequest() asserts the absence of a call, and assertHttpClientRequestCount() checks the total number of requests.

references.title: The Contracts Component
references.summary: The Contracts component publishes small, semantic packages such as symfony/deprecation-contracts. The deprecation-contracts package is the canonical way for libraries built on Symfony to mark methods, arguments, or options as deprecated without depending on a specific framework version.

references.title: Our Backward Compatibility Promise - Making Code Changes in a Backward Compatible Way
references.summary: Documents how Symfony marks legacy behavior as deprecated when introducing new arguments or default values: comment the new argument in the signature, document it in PHPDoc, read it with func_num_args()/func_get_arg(), and emit trigger_deprecation('symfony/COMPONENT', 'X.Y', '...') to warn callers before the next major version.

references.title: Upgrading a Third-Party Bundle for a Major Symfony Version - Look for Deprecations and Fix Them
references.summary: Explains how to detect Symfony deprecations while preparing a bundle for a new major Symfony version. It covers running PHPUnit with deprecation reporting, fixing the reported usages, and rerunning the test suite until no deprecations remain.

references.title: Configuring Symfony
references.summary: Overview of how Symfony applications are configured: YAML, XML, and PHP formats, the config/ directory layout, configuration files importing, and the relationship between configuration parameters and bundle configuration. Sets the foundation for environment-based configuration.

references.title: Configuring Symfony - Configuration Environments
references.summary: Explains Symfony's three default environments (dev, test, prod), how config/packages/<env>/ files override defaults, and how to select the active environment via the APP_ENV variable. Covers creating new environments and overriding bundle configuration per environment.

references.title: Configuring Symfony - Configuration Parameters
references.summary: Defines container parameters in the parameters key, demonstrates the %parameter% syntax to reference them, and explains how parameters are validated at runtime. Covers parameter naming conventions and access via ParameterBagInterface.

references.title: Configuring Symfony - Configuring Environment Variables in .env Files
references.summary: Documents the cascade of .env, .env.local, .env.<env>, and .env.<env>.local files, which are committed and which are git-ignored, and the precedence rules between system env vars and .env files. Includes .env file syntax such as variable interpolation and shell-style defaults.

references.title: Configuring Symfony - Configuring Environment Variables in Production
references.summary: Describes the composer dump-env (and bin/console dotenv:dump) commands that compile every .env file into a single .env.local.php to skip parsing on each request in production. Useful for deployment workflows and performance tuning.

references.title: Environment Variable Processors
references.summary: Lists the built-in env var processors used inside %env(...)% syntax: string, bool, not, int, float, const, base64, json, resolve, csv, shuffle, file, require, trim, key, default, url, query_string, enum, urlencode, and others. Explains how to chain processors and how to register custom ones.

references.title: How to Keep Sensitive Information Secret
references.summary: Covers Symfony's secrets management vault: generating cryptographic keys, secrets:set/secrets:list/secrets:remove commands, referencing secrets in configuration with %env(SECRET)%, separating prod and dev vaults, and rotating keys. Explains why secrets must not be committed in plaintext.

references.title: Understanding how the Front Controller, Kernel and Environments Work together
references.summary: Explains the Kernel class, debug mode, and how the front controller (public/index.php) chooses an environment using APP_ENV and APP_DEBUG. Describes per-environment cache and log directories under var/.

references.title: The ExpressionLanguage Component
references.summary: Introduces the ExpressionLanguage component used by Symfony for security expressions, validation, and routing conditions. Covers ExpressionLanguage::evaluate() vs compile(), passing variables, parsing/linting expressions, and the optional cache pool.

references.title: The Expression Syntax
references.summary: Reference for the ExpressionLanguage syntax: literals, accessing object properties and methods with the . operator, the ?. null-safe operator, the ?? null-coalescing operator, default functions (constant, enum, min, max), and all supported operators (arithmetic, bitwise, comparison, logical, string, array, range, ternary).

references.title: Debug Configuration Reference (DebugBundle)
references.summary: Reference for the **DebugBundle** configuration tree under `debug:` — wraps the `VarDumper` component and exposes options like `max_items`, `min_depth`, `max_string_length`, `dump_destination` (e.g. `tcp://localhost:9912` for the dump server), and `theme` (`dark` / `light`). The bundle is enabled in dev/test by default.

references.title: How to Customize Error Pages
references.summary: Explains how Symfony treats every error (including 404s and PHP errors) as exceptions. In dev, the kernel renders a detailed exception page with stack trace; in prod, it renders a generic error page. Lists the four customization paths: override templates, change non-HTML output, override the ErrorController, or listen to kernel.exception.

references.title: How to Customize Error Pages - Overriding the Default Error Templates
references.summary: Documents the TwigErrorRenderer template lookup: error<code>.html.twig (for example error404.html.twig) is checked first, then a generic error.html.twig fallback. Templates are overridden under templates/bundles/TwigBundle/Exception/ and receive status_code, status_text, and exception variables.

references.title: How to Customize Error Pages - Overriding Error output for non-HTML formats
references.summary: Describes the FlattenException Serializer-based pipeline used for non-HTML error responses (JSON, XML, CSV, YAML). Adding a custom Normalizer that supports FlattenException lets you control the structure of API error payloads.

references.title: How to Customize Error Pages - Working with the kernel.exception Event
references.summary: Explains the kernel.exception event dispatched by HttpKernel when an exception escapes a controller. Listeners receive an ExceptionEvent and may call setResponse() to produce a Response (which stops propagation) or simply log/wrap the exception.

references.title: The HttpKernel Component - Handling Exceptions: the kernel.exception Event
references.summary: Component-level documentation for the kernel.exception event: when it fires inside HttpKernel::handle(), the ExceptionEvent::setResponse() contract that converts an exception into a Response, and the HttpExceptionInterface used by HttpKernel to derive the HTTP status code.

references.title: The Controller - Managing Errors and 404 Pages
references.summary: Documents how to trigger a 404 response by throwing NotFoundHttpException (or its shortcut $this->createNotFoundException()). Notes that exceptions implementing HttpException carry a status code; any other Throwable produces a 500 response.

references.title: How to Customize Error Pages - Dumping Error Pages as Static HTML Files
references.summary: Documents the bin/console error:dump command that pre-generates static HTML for 4xx/5xx error pages so the web server can serve them when the PHP application is unreachable. Includes example Nginx configuration using error_page directives.

references.title: Logging
references.summary: Symfony ships two minimal **PSR-3** loggers — `HttpKernel\Log\Logger` for the HTTP context and `Console\Logger\ConsoleLogger` for the CLI context — both writing `WARNING` and above to **stderr** (per the twelve-factor app methodology). Real applications add **MonologBundle** for handlers, channels, processors, and richer formatters.

references.title: How to Log Messages to different Files
references.summary: **Monolog channels** group log records by source (`app`, `doctrine`, `event`, `security`, `request`, …); each channel can be routed to a different **handler** (file, stream, console, deduplication, fingers-crossed). Use `monolog.handlers.<name>.channels: ["!event"]` to **exclude** a channel and `["security"]` to **include** only it.

references.title: The VarDumper Component
references.summary: Introduces the VarDumper component which adds a global dump() function (and dd() for dump-and-die) for inspecting any PHP variable. Covers SAPI-based output selection, the VAR_DUMPER_FORMAT env var, integration with DebugBundle and Twig {% dump %}, and how dumps render in the Web Debug Toolbar.

references.title: The VarDumper Component - The dump() Function
references.summary: Documents the dump() helper as a structured replacement for var_dump(), with HTML or CLI output selected by SAPI. Notes that dump() returns its argument so it can be chained, and that dd() dumps then exits the script.

references.title: The VarDumper Component - The Dump Server
references.summary: Explains the bin/console server:dump command (and var-dump-server binary) which collects dumps over TCP at 127.0.0.1:9912 so they don't pollute the application output. Configured via debug.dump_destination or the VAR_DUMPER_FORMAT=server env var.

references.title: Debug Configuration Reference (DebugBundle)
references.summary: Reference for the debug.* configuration provided by DebugBundle: dump_destination, max_items (default 2500), max_string_length (default -1, no truncation), min_depth (default 1), and theme (dark or light).

references.title: How to Debug the Service Container & List Services
references.summary: Shows debug:container (with --show-hidden, --tag=, --env-vars, --parameters) and debug:autowiring console commands used to inspect registered services, tagged services, and types available for autowiring.

references.title: Event Dispatcher - Debugging Event Listeners
references.summary: Describes the debug:event-dispatcher command: list every event and its listeners, filter by exact event name, partial match across event names, and the --dispatcher option used to inspect per-firewall security event dispatchers.

references.title: Page Creation - The bin/console Command
references.summary: Introduces bin/console as the entry point for Symfony's debugging and maintenance commands. Lists examples such as debug:router (show all routes), debug:autowiring (show services available for autowiring), and the convention that all introspection commands are prefixed with debug:.

references.title: Profiler: Debugging Paradise
references.summary: Tutorial section showing how installing the **Profiler pack** (`composer require profiler --dev`) adds the full Symfony **Profiler** plus its Web Debug Toolbar. The recipe wires the bundle, ships its config, and registers the dev-only routes. Clicking the toolbar token opens the per-request Profiler with timeline, queries, security context, mailer, messenger, and more.

references.title: How to Deploy a Symfony Application
references.summary: Outlines the standard Symfony deployment workflow: upload code, install vendors, run database migrations, clear and warm up the cache. Lists deployment strategies (FTP/SCP, source control with tags, PaaS such as Upsun, build scripts like Deployer, Capistrano, Ansistrano).

references.title: How to Deploy a Symfony Application - Common Deployment Tasks
references.summary: Lists the common per-deploy steps: check requirements (symfony/requirements-checker), configure env vars (real env vars or .env.prod.local), composer install --no-dev --optimize-autoloader, APP_ENV=prod APP_DEBUG=0 cache:clear, and ancillary steps such as DB migrations, asset compilation, restarting workers, and dumping static error pages.

references.title: How to Configure Symfony to Work behind a Load Balancer or a Reverse Proxy
references.summary: Documents how to declare trusted proxies and trusted headers via framework.trusted_proxies / trusted_headers (or the SYMFONY_TRUSTED_PROXIES env var). Covers private_ranges, REMOTE_ADDR, PRIVATE_SUBNETS placeholders, the X-Forwarded-Prefix header for subpath proxies, and HTTP Host header attack risks.

references.title: Performance
references.summary: Production performance checklist: dump the service container into a single file (.container.dumper.inline_factories), use OPcache, configure OPcache settings (memory_consumption, max_accelerated_files, interned_strings_buffer), disable opcache.validate_timestamps in production, tune realpath_cache, and run composer dump-autoload --no-dev --classmap-authoritative.

references.title: Configuring Symfony - Configuring Environment Variables in Production
references.summary: Explains how composer dump-env prod (or bin/console dotenv:dump) compiles every .env file into a single .env.local.php loaded directly by Symfony to skip per-request parsing in production. Documents APP_ENV=prod APP_DEBUG=0 as the canonical production settings.

references.title: Configuring a Web Server
references.summary: Shows production web server configuration for FrankenPHP/Caddy, PHP-FPM, Nginx, and Apache. Covers using public/ as the document root, routing requests to the front controller, denying direct access to other PHP files, and passing environment variables to PHP.

references.title: Setting up or Fixing File Permissions - Permissions Required by Symfony Applications
references.summary: Lists the production filesystem permissions Symfony requires for var/log/ and var/cache/. Explains which directories must be writable by the web server user and which must be writable by the terminal user that runs cache warmup or clearing commands.

concepts.concept: Web Profiler, Web Debug Toolbar and Data collectors

references.title: Profiler
references.summary: Introduces the Symfony Profiler as a development-only tool that records detailed information about every request, accessible from the web debug toolbar or the /_profiler URL. Highlights the security warning that the profiler must never be enabled in production and the X-Debug-Token-Link response header used to find the profile of non-HTML responses.

references.title: Profiler - Accessing Profiling Data Programmatically
references.summary: Documents the profiler service's API: loadProfileFromResponse(), loadProfile($token) using the X-Debug-Token header, and find() to query past tokens by IP, URL pattern, limit, HTTP method, and date window. Shows how to type-hint Profiler for autowiring.

references.title: Profiler - Data Collectors
references.summary: Explains that profiler panels are populated by services tagged data_collector. Lists how to discover enabled collectors via debug:container --tag=data_collector and notes the built-in collectors for request, logger, routing, cache, security, twig, doctrine, etc.

references.title: Profiler - Enabling the Profiler Conditionally
references.summary: Documents framework.profiler.collect: false combined with collect_parameter so the profiler only records requests that include a configured query parameter, form field, or request attribute. Useful in staging-like environments to reduce overhead.

references.title: Profiler - Creating a Data Collector
references.summary: Shows how to implement DataCollectorInterface or extend AbstractDataCollector with collect(), reset(), and getName(). Explains LateDataCollectorInterface::lateCollect() for data only available during kernel.terminate, and that data must be serializable because the profiler stores collector instances.

references.title: Profiler Configuration Reference (WebProfilerBundle)
references.summary: Reference for the web_profiler.* options: excluded_ajax_paths regex, intercept_redirects (default false) which pauses redirects so you can inspect the original profile, toolbar.enabled (default false) and toolbar.ajax_replace. Notes that the toolbar does not support StreamedResponse.

references.title: Translations
references.summary: Symfony's translation system overview: install symfony/translation, configure framework.default_locale and translator.default_path, and call $translator->trans() to translate messages identified by an id and an optional domain (defaults to messages). Recommends ICU's lang_REGION locale convention such as fr_FR.

references.title: Translations - Translation Resource/File Names and Locations
references.summary: Documents the <domain>.<locale>.<format> filename convention (for example messages.fr.yaml, validators.de.xlf, security.es.php) and the default load path translations/. Lists the supported formats: YAML, XLIFF (.xlf or .xliff), PHP, JSON, INI, CSV, and others provided by Translation loaders.

references.title: Translations - Translatable Objects
references.summary: Introduces the Translatable value object and t() helper that defer translation until rendering, letting you pass a message + parameters + domain through controllers, validators, or services without requiring the translator at construction time. Useful for form labels, exceptions, and notifications.

references.title: Translations - Extracting Translation Contents and Updating Catalogs Automatically
references.summary: Documents the bin/console translation:extract command that scans templates and PHP code for trans()/|trans/{% trans %} calls, plus debug:translation to find missing or unused messages. Notes which formats cannot be auto-extracted (dynamic strings, controllers without translatable objects).

references.title: Translations - Handling the User's Locale
references.summary: Explains the special _locale routing parameter, why setLocale() must be called before LocaleListener (via a kernel.request listener with higher priority, the URL, or the translator service), and the recommended pattern of including the locale in the URL rather than using session-only locales.

references.title: Translations - Fallback Translation Locales
references.summary: Describes Symfony's fallback chain when a key is missing: the requested locale (es_AR), then automatically derived parent locales (es_419, es), then translator.fallbacks, defaulting to default_locale. Missing translations can be logged via reference-framework-translator-logging.

references.title: Translations - Switch Locale Programmatically
references.summary: Documents the LocaleSwitcher service: getLocale(), setLocale(), reset(), and runWithLocale($locale, $callable) which temporarily switches the locale and restores it afterwards. The switcher updates kernel.locale_aware services, \Locale::setDefault(), and the RequestContext _locale used by URL generation.

references.title: Framework Configuration - default_locale
references.summary: Reference for framework.default_locale (default 'en'), used when no _locale routing parameter or LocaleListener-detected locale is available. Also serves as the final translator fallback when translator.fallbacks is not configured.

references.title: Framework Configuration - enabled_locales
references.summary: Reference for framework.enabled_locales: an explicit allow-list of locales the application supports. Restricting this list speeds up translation cache warming and is the recommended performance tweak when only a few locales are used.

references.title: How to Translate Messages using the ICU MessageFormat
references.summary: Reference for ICU MessageFormat support in Symfony: prefix the domain with +intl-icu (for example messages+intl-icu.en.xlf), use {placeholder} interpolation, and write select/plural blocks. Describes pluralization (=0/=1/one/other) and additional placeholder functions for ordinal, date, and number formatting.

references.title: The XLIFF format (reference)
references.summary: The XLIFF format reference page documents the structure of `<file>`, `<unit>`, `<segment>`, `<source>`, and `<target>` elements; XLIFF 1.2 vs. 2.0 differences; and the metadata Symfony preserves (notes, target-language, state). Pair this page with `lint:xliff` for catalogue validation.

references.title: HTTP Cache
references.summary: Symfony's HTTP cache philosophy: rely on the standard RFC 7234 / RFC 9111 headers (Cache-Control, Expires, ETag, Last-Modified) and a gateway/reverse-proxy cache rather than reinventing caching. Lists the two models supported (expiration and validation) and the cacheable methods (GET and HEAD).

references.title: HTTP Cache - Symfony Reverse Proxy
references.summary: Documents Symfony's PHP reverse proxy enabled via framework.http_cache: true (typically only in prod). Describes the X-Symfony-Cache trace header, trace_level (none/short/full) and trace_header config. Notes when to switch to Varnish for higher throughput.

references.title: HTTP Cache - Expiration Caching
references.summary: Explains expiration caching: a response is cached for a fixed duration without consulting the application. Demonstrates the #[Cache] attribute (public, maxage, mustRevalidate) and Response::setPublic()/setMaxAge() shortcuts that produce the Cache-Control header.

references.title: HTTP Cache - Validation Caching
references.summary: Introduces the validation model: caches store responses but ask the application whether each one is still valid. Symfony returns a 304 Not Modified through Response::isNotModified($request), which compares ETag/If-None-Match and Last-Modified/If-Modified-Since headers.

references.title: HTTP Cache - More Response Methods
references.summary: Lists the cache-related Response API: setMaxAge, setSharedMaxAge, setPrivate/setPublic, setEtag (with optional weak flag), setLastModified, setExpires, setNotModified, expire(), and the bulk setCache() method that accepts must_revalidate, no_cache, no_store, public, private, immutable, etc.

references.title: HTTP Cache Expiration
references.summary: Detailed expiration documentation: Cache-Control directives (public, max-age, s-maxage), differences between setMaxAge() and setSharedMaxAge() (s-maxage forbids stale-if-error use), Expires header format, GMT conversion, and the rule that Cache-Control max-age/s-maxage overrides Expires.

references.title: HTTP Cache Validation
references.summary: Detailed validation guide: ETag-based validation (Response::setEtag and weak ETags), Last-Modified-based validation, and the recommended optimization pattern of computing only the minimal data needed for ETag/Last-Modified, calling isNotModified() early, and returning 304 before doing the heavy work.

references.title: Varying the Response for HTTP Cache
references.summary: Documents how to use Response::setVary() and the Vary header so the cache stores separate variants of the same URL based on selected request headers (Accept-Encoding, Accept-Language, etc.). Important when the same URL produces different responses depending on the client.

references.title: Cache Invalidation
references.summary: Covers manual invalidation strategies (PURGE/BAN over Varnish, FOSHttpCacheBundle integration). HTTP itself does not standardize invalidation, so applications must trigger their reverse proxy explicitly when content changes.

references.title: How to Use Varnish to Speed up my Website
references.summary: Configuring Varnish in front of Symfony: trusting Varnish as a reverse proxy via framework.trusted_proxies, X-Forwarded-* handling, cookie/caching gotchas, and ensuring consistent caching behaviour by stripping cookies in VCL when responses are cacheable.

references.title: The Cache Component
references.summary: The Cache component implements PSR-6 cache pools and the simpler Cache Contracts API based on `get($key, callable)` / `delete()`. It exposes adapters for filesystem, APCu, Redis, Memcached, etc., plus tag-based invalidation and stampede protection.

references.title: Cache Contracts versus PSR-6
references.summary: Compares the Cache Contracts API (one method, callable-based, with built-in stampede protection) to PSR-6 (two-step `getItem()` / `save()` with explicit `CacheItemInterface` objects). Cache Contracts is the recommended default; PSR-6 is reached for when interoperating with code that requires it.

references.title: Cache Contracts
references.summary: Documents the Cache Contracts API: `$pool->get($key, function (ItemInterface $item) { ... })` computes the value on cache miss, stores it, and returns it. The callable receives a `CacheInterface\ItemInterface` so it can call `expiresAfter()` / `tag()`. `delete($key)` removes a single key.

references.title: Stampede Prevention
references.summary: Describes probabilistic early expiration and locking that prevents many concurrent requests from recomputing the same expensive value when it is about to expire. Enabled automatically by Cache Contracts; tuned through `$item->expiresAfter()` and the `beta` argument to `get()`.

references.title: Cache Pools and Supported Adapters
references.summary: Cache pools store cache items together. Pools implement `Psr\Cache\CacheItemPoolInterface` (and Symfony's `CacheInterface`). The page enumerates `getItem()`, `getItems()`, `hasItem()`, `save()`, `saveDeferred()`, `commit()`, `deleteItem()` / `deleteItems()`, `clear()`.

references.title: Cache Items
references.summary: Documents `CacheItemInterface`: `getKey()`, `get()`, `isHit()`, `set($value)`, `expiresAfter($ttl)`, `expiresAt(\DateTimeInterface)`. Keys must be strings and may not contain reserved characters `{}()/\@:`. Items are returned by pools as either hits (with the stored value) or misses (with `null`).

references.title: Cache Invalidation
references.summary: Tag-based invalidation: items can be tagged via `$item->tag(['tag1', 'tag2'])` and invalidated in bulk through `TagAwareAdapterInterface::invalidateTags(['tag1'])`. Requires a tag-aware adapter (e.g. `TagAwareAdapter` decorating any pool, or `RedisTagAwareAdapter`).

references.title: Adapters For Interoperability between PSR-6 and PSR-16 Cache
references.summary: Bridges between the two PSR cache standards: `Psr16Cache` exposes a PSR-6 pool as a PSR-16 (`CacheInterface`) cache, and `Psr6Cache` does the reverse. Useful when third-party libraries require one standard but the application uses the other.

references.title: Array Cache Adapter
references.summary: `ArrayAdapter` keeps cached values in an in-memory PHP array. Items are lost at the end of the request — only useful for tests or for caching computed values within one request lifecycle.

references.title: Filesystem Cache Adapter
references.summary: `FilesystemAdapter` stores cache entries as plain files under a directory. Constructor: `new FilesystemAdapter($namespace = '', $defaultLifetime = 0, $directory = null)`. Suitable when no other backend is available; not recommended for high-traffic scenarios.

references.title: PHP Files Cache Adapter
references.summary: `PhpFilesAdapter` writes cache items as PHP files that PHP's OPcache can pick up. Faster than `FilesystemAdapter` when OPcache is enabled because cached values are loaded as compiled PHP code, not unserialized at runtime.

references.title: PHP Array Cache Adapter
references.summary: `PhpArrayAdapter` is a read-only adapter backed by a PHP file containing a static array. Designed for warm-up: build the file once with `warmUp(array $values)`, then read at full PHP-array speed. Typically chained with another adapter that handles writes/misses.

references.title: APCu Cache Adapter
references.summary: `ApcuAdapter` stores cache items in shared memory using the APCu PHP extension. Extremely fast within a single PHP-FPM worker, but not shared across machines. Requires the `apcu` extension and a non-CLI SAPI (or `apc.enable_cli=1`).

references.title: Redis Cache Adapter
references.summary: `RedisAdapter` stores cache items in a Redis server. Constructor accepts a `\Redis`, `\RedisArray`, `\RedisCluster`, `\Predis\ClientInterface` or a Redis DSN built with `RedisAdapter::createConnection()`. Supports cluster, sentinel and TLS configurations through DSN options.

references.title: Memcached Cache Adapter
references.summary: `MemcachedAdapter` stores cache items in one or more Memcached servers. Construct with a `\Memcached` instance produced by `MemcachedAdapter::createConnection()`, which accepts one or more `memcached://` DSNs and standard Memcached options.

references.title: PDO Cache Adapter
references.summary: `PdoAdapter` stores cache items in any PDO-supported relational database. Either pass a configured `\PDO` instance or a DSN; use `createTable()` once to provision the schema.

references.title: Doctrine DBAL Cache Adapter
references.summary: `DoctrineDbalAdapter` stores cache items via a Doctrine DBAL `Connection`. Equivalent to `PdoAdapter` for projects already using DBAL — provision the table with `createTable()`.

references.title: Chain Cache Adapter
references.summary: `ChainAdapter` chains several pools so reads fall through L1 → L2 → ... and writes propagate to every adapter. Typical use: a fast in-memory adapter (Array/APCu) backed by a slower shared adapter (Redis/Filesystem).

references.title: Proxy Cache Adapter
references.summary: `ProxyAdapter` wraps any PSR-6 `CacheItemPoolInterface` so it can be used where a Symfony `AdapterInterface` is required. Adds Symfony-specific features (namespaces, default TTL, marshalling) on top of an external PSR-6 implementation.

references.title: Cache
references.summary: FrameworkBundle integration of the Cache component: `framework.cache.app` and `framework.cache.system` pools, defining custom pools under `framework.cache.pools`, the `cache.app` / `cache.system` services, cache adapters configured via DSN, and the `cache:pool:clear` console command.

references.title: Using Cache Tags
references.summary: Symfony FrameworkBundle exposes tag-aware caching automatically: any pool of class `cache.adapter.taggable` (or wrapped with `TagAwareAdapter`) lets controllers/services inject `TagAwareCacheInterface` and call `invalidateTags()` through `cache:pool:invalidate-tags` or programmatically.

references.title: The Clock Component
references.summary: Decouples application code from the system clock through `ClockInterface` (PSR-20). Ships with `NativeClock` (real time), `MockClock` (frozen/movable time for tests), and `MonotonicClock` (hrtime-based). Provides the global `Clock::set/get`, the `now()` helper and `ClockAwareTrait` for autowiring.

references.title: NativeClock
references.summary: `NativeClock` returns the system time via PHP's native `\DateTimeImmutable`. Constructor accepts an optional `\DateTimeZone` so the returned `DatePoint`/`DateTimeImmutable` is in a specific timezone. This is the implementation autowired in `prod`/`dev`.

references.title: MockClock
references.summary: `MockClock` freezes the current time so tests are deterministic. Built with a string accepted by the `DateTime` constructor or a `DateTimeImmutable`. Time advances only when the test calls `sleep($seconds)` or `modify('+1 day')` on the clock.

references.title: Monotonic Clock
references.summary: `MonotonicClock` is backed by `hrtime()` and is immune to wall-clock jumps. Use it as a stopwatch — `$clock->now()` returns timestamps that strictly increase, suitable for measuring elapsed time but not for displaying real-world time.

references.title: Using a Clock inside a Service
references.summary: Inject `Symfony\Component\Clock\ClockInterface` like any service, or `use ClockAwareTrait` to autowire the clock as `$this->clock` and gain a `$this->now()` shortcut. The trait's setter is tagged `#[Required]` so autowiring works without manual config.

references.title: The DatePoint Class
references.summary: `DatePoint` extends `\DateTimeImmutable` with strict, exception-throwing parsing and additional helpers (`getMicrosecond()`, `getYear()`, named constructors `createFromTimestamp()`, etc.). All clocks return `DatePoint` instances.

references.title: Writing Time-Sensitive Tests
references.summary: Use `ClockSensitiveTrait::mockTime()` in a PHPUnit test to install a `MockClock` for the duration of the test, then `tearDown()` restores the previous clock. Tests can advance time by calling `Clock::get()->sleep(N)` or `modify('+1 hour')`.

references.title: PSR-20: Clock
references.summary: PSR-20 standardizes a single `Psr\Clock\ClockInterface::now(): \DateTimeImmutable` method. Symfony's `ClockInterface` extends the PSR contract and adds `sleep()` for moving time forward in mockable implementations.

references.title: The EventDispatcher Component
references.summary: Implements the Mediator/Observer pattern: a central `EventDispatcher` dispatches named events that any number of listeners or subscribers can react to. Listeners are PHP callables; subscribers declare their subscriptions through a static `getSubscribedEvents()` method.

references.title: Connecting Listeners
references.summary: `EventDispatcher::addListener($eventName, callable $listener, int $priority = 0)` connects a listener. Higher priorities run first; equal priorities run in insertion order. The third argument is optional and defaults to `0`.

references.title: Using Event Subscribers
references.summary: `EventSubscriberInterface` exposes a static `getSubscribedEvents(): array` method. The returned array maps event names to method names, `[methodName, priority]` tuples, or arrays of those — letting one class register multiple listeners with one `addSubscriber()` call.

references.title: Stopping Event Flow / Propagation
references.summary: Any listener may call `$event->stopPropagation()`. Subsequent listeners for the same event are skipped. `Event::isPropagationStopped()` lets the dispatcher (or other listeners) detect that propagation is already stopped.

references.title: Event Name Introspection
references.summary: Listeners can be declared with the optional `string $eventName` and `EventDispatcherInterface $dispatcher` arguments after the event object. The dispatcher passes them at call time so a single listener class can know which event triggered it and re-dispatch others.

references.title: Events and Event Listeners
references.summary: Symfony framework integration of EventDispatcher. The `kernel.*` events (`kernel.request`, `kernel.controller`, `kernel.view`, `kernel.response`, `kernel.exception`, `kernel.terminate`, `kernel.finish_request`) are dispatched by `HttpKernel` during request handling.

references.title: Defining Event Listeners with PHP Attributes
references.summary: `#[AsEventListener(event: SomeEvent::class, method: 'onSomeEvent', priority: 10)]` registers a service as a listener without the `kernel.event_listener` tag. The attribute can be repeated to subscribe to several events from one class.

references.title: Creating an Event Subscriber
references.summary: Implement `EventSubscriberInterface::getSubscribedEvents()` returning a map like `[KernelEvents::RESPONSE => 'onResponse']`. With autoconfiguration (the default), services implementing the interface are tagged `kernel.event_subscriber` automatically.

references.title: Listeners or Subscribers
references.summary: Trade-off guidance: listeners (one method, configured outside the class) keep the listener class simple and let the bundle owning the event control where it fires; subscribers (configured inside the class) keep all wiring close to the code that runs.

references.title: Debugging Event Listeners
references.summary: `php bin/console debug:event-dispatcher` lists every registered listener with its event, class::method and priority. Pass an event name to filter or `--dispatcher` to inspect a specific dispatcher (e.g. a per-firewall security dispatcher).

references.title: The Filesystem Component
references.summary: Cross-platform filesystem helpers via the `Filesystem` class. All write operations throw `Symfony\Component\Filesystem\Exception\IOExceptionInterface` on failure (typically the concrete `IOException`), so error handling is consistent across platforms.

references.title: mkdir
references.summary: `Filesystem::mkdir($dirs, int $mode = 0777)` creates one directory or an iterable of directories recursively. Existing directories are silently ignored — only failures throw `IOException`.

references.title: copy
references.summary: `Filesystem::copy($originFile, $targetFile, bool $overwriteNewerFiles = false)` copies a single file. By default the target is only overwritten when the origin is newer; pass `true` to force an overwrite.

references.title: mirror
references.summary: `Filesystem::mirror($originDir, $targetDir, ?\Traversable $iterator = null, array $options = [])` recursively copies an entire directory tree. The `$options` array controls overwrite/delete/copy-on-windows behavior; the iterator argument lets callers narrow what is copied.

references.title: remove
references.summary: `Filesystem::remove($files)` deletes one path or any iterable of paths. Files, symbolic links and directories (recursively) are all handled by the same call.

references.title: dumpFile
references.summary: `Filesystem::dumpFile($filename, $content)` writes content to a file atomically: it writes to a temporary file in the same directory and then `rename()`s, so concurrent readers never observe a partial file. Parent directories are created if missing.

references.title: tempnam
references.summary: `Filesystem::tempnam($dir, $prefix, string $suffix = '')` creates a unique temporary file in `$dir`, returning its absolute path. Unlike PHP's native `tempnam()` it supports a custom suffix.

references.title: Path Manipulation Utilities
references.summary: The `Path` utility normalizes and manipulates filesystem paths independently of the OS: `canonicalize()`, `makeAbsolute()`, `makeRelative()`, `getRoot()`, `getDirectory()`, `getExtension()`, `join()`, `getLongestCommonBasePath()`, `isAbsolute()`. Always uses forward slashes in returned paths.

references.title: The Finder Component
references.summary: Fluent file/directory search: `Finder::create()->files()->in($dir)->name('*.php')` returns an iterable of `SplFileInfo` instances. `Finder` is stateful — clone it before reusing the same instance for a different search.

references.title: Location
references.summary: `Finder::in($dirs)` selects one or more directories to search (required). `Finder::in()` also supports PHP stream wrappers like `ftp://` or `s3://`. `exclude($dirs)` removes specific subdirectories from results.

references.title: Files or Directories
references.summary: Restrict results to files (`files()`), directories (`directories()`), or both (default). `followLinks()` enables traversal across symbolic links; `ignoreUnreadableDirs()` skips directories the process cannot read instead of throwing.

references.title: Version Control Files
references.summary: By default, `Finder` ignores VCS metadata directories (e.g. `.git`, `.svn`). Toggle this with `ignoreVCS(false)`. `ignoreVCSIgnored(true)` additionally honors patterns from `.gitignore` files in the searched tree.

references.title: File Name
references.summary: Match file names with `name('*.php')` (glob), or with regex patterns (`name('/\.(php|html)$/')`). `notName()` is the negation. Multiple `name()` calls combine as OR.

references.title: File Contents
references.summary: `contains('lorem ipsum')` filters files whose contents match a string or regex; `notContains()` is the negation. Useful for grep-like searches without spawning external commands.

references.title: File Size
references.summary: `size('< 100K')` accepts comparison expressions with units `k`, `ki`, `m`, `mi`, `g`, `gi` (lower or upper case). Multiple `size()` calls combine as AND, e.g. `size('>= 1K')->size('< 1M')`.

references.title: File Date
references.summary: `date('since 1 hour ago')` accepts any `strtotime()`-compatible expression with comparison operators. `date('< 2024-01-01')` works the same way. Use `date('through ...', Finder::COMPARE_BIRTH)` to compare against creation time when available.

references.title: Directory Depth
references.summary: `depth('< 3')` filters results by how deep below the search root they sit. `depth(0)` returns only direct children; `depth('>= 1')` excludes them.

references.title: Sorting Results
references.summary: `sortByName()`, `sortByType()`, `sortBySize()`, `sortByModifiedTime()`, `sortByAccessedTime()`, `sortByChangedTime()`, plus the generic `sort(callable)`. Sorting forces the iterator to consume all matches first, so it is more memory-intensive than the default lazy iteration.

references.title: Event Names and Event Objects
references.summary: Each dispatched event is identified by a string name and carries an event object. The event object base class is `Symfony\Contracts\EventDispatcher\Event`, which exposes `stopPropagation()` / `isPropagationStopped()`. Application code typically subclasses it to carry context (e.g. an order, a user).

references.title: Creating an Event Class
references.summary: Custom events extend `Symfony\Contracts\EventDispatcher\Event`. The class name is conventionally used as the event name (`SomeEvent::class`), and the event object exposes accessors for the data the listener needs to read or mutate.

references.title: The Generic Event Object
references.summary: `GenericEvent` is a convenience event object that carries a subject plus optional arguments. Exposes `getSubject()`, `getArgument($key)`, `setArgument($key, $value)`, `getArguments()`, `setArguments(array)`, `hasArgument($key)`, and implements `ArrayAccess` so arguments can be read and written with `$event['key']`.

references.title: The Immutable Event Dispatcher
references.summary: `ImmutableEventDispatcher` wraps another dispatcher and forbids further `addListener()` / `addSubscriber()` / `removeListener()` calls. Pass it to code that should consume events but never modify the listener registry.

references.title: The Traceable Event Dispatcher
references.summary: `TraceableEventDispatcher` decorates another dispatcher and records every dispatched event together with the listeners that were called and skipped. The Symfony profiler uses it to populate the Events panel.

references.title: Sending Emails with Mailer
references.summary: The Mailer component sends emails over multiple transports configured through the `MAILER_DSN` environment variable. The Symfony framework wires `Symfony\Component\Mailer\MailerInterface` so controllers/services can call `$mailer->send($email)`.

references.title: Transport Setup
references.summary: Transports are selected by DSN scheme: `smtp://`, `sendmail://`, `native://` (uses `php.ini`'s `mail()` settings) and many third-party schemes. `null://null` discards messages — useful for `dev`/`test` environments.

references.title: Using Built-in Transports
references.summary: Built-ins: `smtp` opens a SMTP connection (DSN `smtp://user:pass@host:port`); `sendmail` shells out to the local `sendmail` binary; `native` defers to the `mail()` settings declared in `php.ini`.

references.title: Using a 3rd Party Transport
references.summary: Third-party packages (`symfony/amazon-mailer`, `symfony/mailgun-mailer`, `symfony/postmark-mailer`, `symfony/sendgrid-mailer`, etc.) register additional DSN schemes (e.g. `ses+api`, `mailgun+smtp`, `postmark+api`). Switching providers is a `MAILER_DSN` change.

references.title: High Availability
references.summary: Combine transports with the failover scheme (`failover(smtp://primary smtp://secondary)`) — Mailer uses the first transport until it fails, then falls back to the next.

references.title: Load Balancing
references.summary: The round-robin scheme (`roundrobin(smtp://a smtp://b)`) spreads outgoing mail across transports in turn. Combine with failover for both balancing and resilience.

references.title: Creating & Sending Messages
references.summary: Build an `Email` object with chainable setters (`from()`, `to()`, `subject()`, `text()`, `html()`), then pass it to `MailerInterface::send($email)`. `TemplatedEmail` (from `symfony/twig-bridge`) renders Twig templates for HTML/text bodies.

references.title: Email Addresses
references.summary: Recipients accept strings (`'me@example.com'`), `Address` objects (`new Address('me@example.com', 'Me')`) or arrays of either. `addTo()`, `addCc()`, `addBcc()`, `addReplyTo()` append without replacing previously set recipients.

references.title: Message Headers
references.summary: Email headers are accessed through `$email->getHeaders()`. Use `addTextHeader()`, `addIdHeader()`, `addPathHeader()`, `addParameterizedHeader()` for custom headers; `addPriorityHeader()` sets the `X-Priority` header.

references.title: File Attachments
references.summary: Attach files with `$email->attachFromPath('/path/to/file.pdf')`, raw data with `attach($data, $name, $contentType)`, or a stream with `attachFromStream()`. Each call appends a `DataPart` to the message.

references.title: Embedding Images
references.summary: Use `$email->embedFromPath('/path/to/logo.png', 'logo')` and reference the image in HTML with `` so the message ships the image inline (multipart/related) instead of as a regular attachment.

references.title: Twig: HTML & CSS
references.summary: `TemplatedEmail` renders Twig templates set via `htmlTemplate()` / `textTemplate()` and exposes the `email` Twig variable (with attached files via `email.image('logo')`). The CSS inliner extension inlines stylesheets so they survive email client parsing.

references.title: The Mime Component
references.summary: Constructs MIME messages used by the Mailer. `Mime` is responsible for *building* messages; `Mailer` is responsible for *sending* them. The component ships two complementary APIs: the high-level `Email` builder and the lower-level `Message` API for raw multipart trees.

references.title: Usage (high-level Email API)
references.summary: `Symfony\Component\Mime\Email` exposes chainable `from()`, `to()`, `cc()`, `bcc()`, `replyTo()`, `subject()`, `text()`, `html()`, `attach()`, `attachFromPath()`, `embed()`, `embedFromPath()` setters and produces a fully formed MIME message ready for `MailerInterface::send()`.

references.title: Twig Integration
references.summary: `TemplatedEmail` (provided when `symfony/twig-bridge` is installed) extends `Email` with `htmlTemplate()`, `textTemplate()`, `context()`, and an `image()` Twig helper for inlined images. The Twig environment renders text/HTML bodies before the message is sent.

references.title: Creating Raw Email Messages
references.summary: When the high-level `Email` API is too restrictive, build messages from `Symfony\Component\Mime\Message` and `Part` objects (`TextPart`, `DataPart`, `MultipartPart`, `Multipart\AlternativePart`, `Multipart\RelatedPart`, `Multipart\MixedPart`). Headers are built via `Headers`.

references.title: Serializing Email Messages
references.summary: MIME messages can be serialized with `Symfony\Component\Mime\RawMessage::toString()` (or written incrementally via `toIterable()`), useful for queueing, logging or debugging — and rebuilt later because `Email` itself is `Serializable`.

references.title: MIME Types Utilities
references.summary: `Symfony\Component\Mime\MimeTypes` is a singleton-style helper that returns the MIME type guessed from a filename (`getMimeTypes('jpg')`) or from a file's contents (`guessMimeType($path)`).

references.title: Guessing the MIME Type
references.summary: `MimeTypes::getDefault()->guessMimeType($path)` runs the registered guessers in order until one returns a result — typically the `FileinfoMimeTypeGuesser` (PHP `fileinfo` extension). Falls back to `null` if no guesser is supported.

references.title: The Process Component
references.summary: Executes commands in sub-processes safely (replaces `exec`/`passthru`/`shell_exec`). The recommended constructor takes an array of arguments — `new Process(['ls', '-lsa'])` — so each argument is automatically escaped.

references.title: Configuring Process Options
references.summary: Configure the working directory (`setWorkingDirectory()`), environment variables (`setEnv()`), input (`setInput()`), and timeout (`setTimeout(seconds)`/`setIdleTimeout()`) before running. Defaults apply when settings are omitted.

references.title: Using Features From the OS Shell
references.summary: `Process::fromShellCommandline('ls -lsa | grep src')` builds a process that is interpreted by the OS shell, enabling pipes, redirections and environment-variable expansion. Always escape user-controlled values yourself in this mode (or prefer the array constructor).

references.title: Setting Environment Variables for Processes
references.summary: Pass an env-vars array as the second constructor argument or call `setEnv(array)`. By default child processes inherit the parent's environment; explicit env vars override the inherited ones for the same key.

references.title: Getting real-time Process Output
references.summary: Pass a callable to `run()` / `start()` / `wait()` — it is called with `($type, $buffer)` where `$type` is `Process::OUT` or `Process::ERR`. `getIncrementalOutput()` / `getIncrementalErrorOutput()` return only the new output since the previous call.

references.title: Running Processes Asynchronously
references.summary: `start()` launches the child process and returns immediately so the caller keeps working. Poll with `isRunning()`, block with `wait()`, or use `mustRun()` after start. `wait(callable)` accepts a real-time output callback like `run()`.

references.title: Process Timeout
references.summary: `setTimeout($seconds)` aborts the process if it runs longer than the limit and throws `ProcessTimedOutException` with `TYPE_GENERAL`. Pass `0` or `null` to disable the timeout. The default timeout is `60` seconds.

references.title: Process Signals
references.summary: `signal(int)` sends an arbitrary POSIX signal (e.g. `SIGTERM`, `SIGUSR1`) to the child process. Available only on POSIX systems with the `pcntl` extension.

references.title: Stopping a Process
references.summary: `stop(float $timeout = 10, ?int $signal = null)` first sends `SIGTERM` (or the signal you pass), waits at most `$timeout` seconds, then escalates to `SIGKILL`. Returns the process exit code.

references.title: Finding an Executable
references.summary: `ExecutableFinder::find($name)` searches `$PATH` for a binary with a given name and returns its absolute path (or `null`). `PhpExecutableFinder::find()` returns the path to the current PHP CLI binary.

references.title: The PropertyAccess Component
references.summary: Reads and writes object/array properties via a string property path: `PropertyAccess::createPropertyAccessor()->getValue($subject, 'foo.bar')` and `setValue()`. Dot notation accesses object properties; bracket notation accesses array offsets.

references.title: Reading from Arrays
references.summary: Array indices use bracket syntax: `[first_name]`, nested as `[children][0][first_name]`. Trying to read an unknown index returns `null` by default; enable `enableExceptionOnInvalidIndex()` on the builder to throw `NoSuchIndexException`.

references.title: Reading from Objects
references.summary: Object properties use dot syntax: `firstName`, nested as `address.city`. PropertyAccess locates the value through public properties, then `getX()` / `isX()` / `hasX()` / `canX()` accessors, and finally the `__get()` magic method when public properties resolution fails.

references.title: Using Getters
references.summary: For property `firstName`, PropertyAccess looks for `getFirstName()`. The convention also covers camel-cased property paths (`first_name` matches `getFirstName()`). Methods must be public and take no required arguments.

references.title: Using Hassers/Issers
references.summary: Boolean accessors are tried automatically: for property `published`, `isPublished()`, `hasPublished()` and `canPublished()` are inspected after `getPublished()`. Returning `bool` is conventional but not enforced.

references.title: Magic __call() Method
references.summary: `PropertyAccessorBuilder::enableMagicCall()` lets PropertyAccess fall back to `__call()` when no explicit accessor exists. Disabled by default because it makes invalid paths look like valid ones.

references.title: Writing to Arrays
references.summary: `setValue($array, '[children][0][first_name]', 'Jane')` mutates a nested array offset. Missing intermediate offsets are created on the fly so the path becomes valid.

references.title: Writing to Objects
references.summary: `setValue($object, 'firstName', 'Jane')` writes through (in order) the public property, the `setFirstName()` setter, the `__set()` magic method (when enabled), or — for collection properties — the matching adder/remover pair.

references.title: Using non-standard adder/remover methods
references.summary: When writing to a collection-typed property, PropertyAccess prefers `addX()` / `removeX()` (singularized property name). Custom names can be declared with `Inflector` integration or by passing the pair explicitly through the builder.

references.title: Checking Property Paths
references.summary: `isReadable()` and `isWritable()` answer whether a path can be read or written without throwing. Useful when a callable composes property paths dynamically and needs to skip ones that no longer exist.

references.title: Enable other Features
references.summary: `PropertyAccessorBuilder` exposes `enableMagicCall()`, `enableMagicGet()`, `enableMagicSet()`, `enableExceptionOnInvalidIndex()`, `enableExceptionOnInvalidPropertyPath()` and a cache layer (`setCacheItemPool()`) — defaults stay strict so silent bugs are surfaced.

references.title: The Runtime Component
references.summary: Decouples bootstrapping from a specific SAPI: `vendor/autoload_runtime.php` instantiates a `RuntimeInterface` (default: `SymfonyRuntime`), resolves the closure's arguments, and runs the returned application. The same `public/index.php` works for FPM, CLI, and alternative runtimes.

references.title: Selecting Runtimes
references.summary: The runtime class is chosen, in order, by: the `APP_RUNTIME` environment variable, the `extra.runtime.class` key in `composer.json`, and finally the default `Symfony\Component\Runtime\SymfonyRuntime`. Alternative runtimes include PHP-PM, ReactPHP, Swoole and FrankenPHP packages.

references.title: Resolvable Arguments
references.summary: The closure exported from `index.php` can declare typed arguments — `Request`, `InputInterface`, `OutputInterface`, `array $context`, `array $argv` — and the runtime injects them based on the SAPI. Unknown types throw a clear exception.

references.title: Resolvable Applications
references.summary: The closure may return a `Kernel`, `HttpKernelInterface`, `Response`, `Application` (Console), `Command`, or any callable. The runtime translates the returned object into the SAPI-appropriate exit semantics (HTTP response, exit code, etc.).

references.title: Using Options
references.summary: `composer.json` can pass options to the runtime through the `extra.runtime` key (e.g. `project_dir`, `dotenv_path`, `disable_dotenv`, `dotenv_overload`, `prod_envs`, `error_handler`). These knobs configure DotEnv loading and error rendering before the kernel boots.

references.title: Create Your Own Runtime
references.summary: Implement `RuntimeInterface` (returning a `RunnerInterface` whose `run()` returns an exit code) to integrate with any SAPI. Declare it via `APP_RUNTIME` or `extra.runtime.class` and the standard `vendor/autoload_runtime.php` will hand control to it.

references.title: How to Use the Serializer
references.summary: Symfony Serializer overview: `SerializerInterface::serialize($object, $format)` and `deserialize($data, $type, $format)` for `json`, `xml`, `csv`, `yaml`. The architecture combines normalizers (PHP ↔ array) and encoders (array ↔ string).

references.title: Serializing an Object
references.summary: `$serializer->serialize($entity, 'json')` returns a JSON string. The default `ObjectNormalizer` reads public properties and getters; the `JsonEncoder` produces the output string. Context options (`AbstractNormalizer::GROUPS`, `AbstractNormalizer::IGNORED_ATTRIBUTES`, etc.) tune the result.

references.title: Deserializing an Object
references.summary: `$serializer->deserialize($json, MyClass::class, 'json')` produces an instance. Constructor arguments are mapped from the input by name; remaining attributes are set through public properties or `setX()` setters. The `OBJECT_TO_POPULATE` context lets callers hydrate an existing instance.

references.title: The Serialization Process: Normalizers and Encoders
references.summary: Pipeline: serializing runs `normalize($object) → encode($array)`; deserializing runs `decode($string) → denormalize($array, $type)`. The `Serializer` walks its registered normalizers/encoders in priority order and uses the first one whose `supports*()` method returns `true`.

references.title: Serializer Context
references.summary: The optional third argument to `serialize()` / `deserialize()` is a context array consumed by normalizers and encoders. Standard keys live as constants on `AbstractNormalizer` (`GROUPS`, `IGNORED_ATTRIBUTES`, `OBJECT_TO_POPULATE`) and on individual normalizers (`DateTimeNormalizer::FORMAT_KEY`, etc.).

references.title: Serializer Normalizers
references.summary: Built-in normalizers (priority-ordered): `UnwrappingDenormalizer`, `ObjectNormalizer`, `ArrayDenormalizer`, `ConstraintViolationListNormalizer`, `MimeMessageNormalizer`, `BackedEnumNormalizer`, `DataUriNormalizer`, `DateTimeNormalizer`, `DateTimeZoneNormalizer`, `DateIntervalNormalizer`, `FormErrorNormalizer`, `JsonSerializableNormalizer`, `ProblemNormalizer`, `UidNormalizer`.

references.title: Converting Property Names when Serializing and Deserializing
references.summary: Name converters translate between the wire format and PHP property names. `CamelCaseToSnakeCaseNameConverter` is built-in; the metadata-aware converter reads `#[SerializedName('first_name')]` PHP attributes on the property.

references.title: Ignoring Properties
references.summary: `#[Ignore]` on a property excludes it from both serialization and deserialization. `AbstractNormalizer::IGNORED_ATTRIBUTES` (set in the context array) does the same per call without changing the metadata.

references.title: Serializer Encoders
references.summary: Encoders translate normalized arrays to and from a wire format. Built-in encoders: `JsonEncoder` (json), `XmlEncoder` (xml), `YamlEncoder` (yaml), `CsvEncoder` (csv). Each accepts a context array with format-specific knobs (e.g. `XmlEncoder::ROOT_NODE_NAME`).

references.title: How to Create your Custom Normalizer
references.summary: Implement `NormalizerInterface` (and/or `DenormalizerInterface`) and tag the service with `serializer.normalizer`. `supportsNormalization()` decides whether the normalizer applies to the given object/format. Return a higher priority on the tag to override built-in normalizers.

references.title: How to Create your Custom Name Converter
references.summary: Implement `NameConverterInterface::normalize($propertyName)` and `denormalize()` to translate between PHP property names and the serialized field names. Pass an instance to the normalizer constructor to apply it.
